title: Great Product . . . Poor Performance
text: Without researching the product I did an impulse buy of the Samsung Gear IconX.  I liked the idea that I could load music and use them while I was fishing.  During their use, the left earbud was on the side facing the sun.  After about a half hour, the earbud started announcing something like "need to cool down".  It would repeat it  . . . and repeat it . . . and repeat it until I took the earbuds off and put them back in their case and in my vehicle.  The touch controls are crazy and unpredictable and I am never sure what will happen when I try to do a tap or double tap . . .  I will be attempting to return the earbuds for a refund.

title: Battery life is horrible!!!
text:
I charged new watch out of the box to 100%. Turned off all connections to make a clean test. Put the watch on the table and did not touch it. In about 23 hours the charge was 20% without actively using the watch at all or turning on the screen. It is absolutely horrible. Dormant watch consumed 80% of power.

I am wearing Huawei 6 band for a week and it is still running on the same charge, it's around 15% now. It has the same sport and health functions like Samsung watch and I paid $40 for it. It is also not cumbersome like Samsung watch.

Samsung needs to fix their battery life. Thinking about return.

ECG tracker stopped working. When I try to run it on the watch , it says "New version is available. Install the update from Galaxy App Store to continue using the app. If I click on the Update button on the watch, I get "Page not found" on the phone browser.

I've been through several support chats, calls, sms, they tried to remote to my phone! and still the issue with the watch was not solved. Every support team was trying to send me somewhere else. I spent several hours talking to Samsung support.

The issue with this watch is that it is using Samsung's own home brewed half-baked operating system. It is clunky. It is misleading. If you opened some app like browser on the watch, there is no way to close it. It is running draining power. I was able to resolve the ECG app issue on yhe watch by reinstalling Galaxy Wear app on my phone. It was just a wild guess. The watch tips and Samsung support troubleshooting was absolutely useless. This watch is a bad news.

title: Very mixed feeling about this gadget
text:
[[VIDEOID:43080b865b752bd653af5dfe533a6667]] I have very mixed feeling with this smart watch. I have Galaxy Watch 5 and I HAD Apple Watch series 6 previously. Since this was on Vine recommendation list and I just gave it a try. Apps are not stable, problems are here and there but still it's only $30. So I really don't know how to give ratings for this product. So here is what I think.

Build Quality & Design:
It has old Galaxy watch, round shape body with assumably tempered glass. The body is made with aluminum. They give 2 silicone straps gracefully. (Are you seeing this, Samsung & Apple?) For build quality, it's amazingly good for $30, and even without considering the price, it has pretty good quality.

Screen:
They say it's IPS HD resolution but well, it's nothing like major smart watches. Dots are quite visible, probably we are all get used to UHD or QHD level of high resolution screen too much. It's not specifically bothering though, but definitely it's nothing like mainstream watches. It doesn't work as smooth as mainstream watches, then again it's $30.

Comfortableness:
It is pretty comfortable, just like others. I specially like the soft silicone texture of this watch.

Apps:
So, this is the problem. So practically, even with full permission to most of the apps, something works and something doesn't. This watch has connection app and you can manage all your setting there. With full permission, call works pretty good both for dialing and receiving calls. But even with the accessibility permission for messaging, it doesn't show any incoming message nor I can send any, nor does Whatsapp. I'm not sure I'm setting this right or not, but it's true that it's not so convenient. So if someone succeed, please let me know and I will fix rating. The step tracking is somewhat unstable, so it works on and off. The health monitor works pretty well in INCREDABLY SHORT TIME (BUT shows very different number from Galaxy Watch, lol) but blood pressure measure is somewhat unstable, it fluctuates like crazy. I guess it's immature technology since Galaxy Watch 5 didn't open this function for US market yet. So I won't believe whatever number comes up from this watch.

Charger:
It comes with one light plastic charger. Again, it's $30.

Buy it if you need: budget random smart watch for your kids. I mean, it's "working" smart watch for $30.
Don't if you need: you need a real smart watch for messaging and health management. It's just not so credible and reliable.

Last note: I would give 2 star if it were more than $50.

title: Renewed Galaxy Smartwatch
text:
Refurbished Aspect of this Review:
This isn't my first time ordering a refurbished product from Amazon. I expected the watch to come with the original packaging, unsealed and possibly missing some components inside like inserts and whatnot... Everytime I've ordered Renewed it came in the original packaging... I could get over not having the box fine, (unless I was giving it as a gift.) but what really disappointed me was it didn't include the manual.

It's also important to note that you cannot find the manual in PDF on the Samsung website. They have the manuals for all their other watches, but not this one for some reason. So just keep that in mind.
I still don't know the full functionality of this watch and getting to know it has been a bit frustrating without instructions. You can find review YouTube videos that have some instructions, but you will not be shown everything...

With that out the way, the watch itself is in pristine condition. No scratches or anything. You wouldn't be able to tell it's used. The charger itself did have some wear. The lettering on the back of it is rubbed off in the middle. Not a big deal.

Functionality and Quality Part of this Review:
You know, I bought a cheap end smart watch before getting this one. I didn't get the cheapest one. It was midrange in pricing... I really thought paying so much for a brand name watch is a ripoff before I purchased one. They must work the same when they have similar specs right? NO.
The knockoff watch gave me bizarre heart rate readings. Said I took steps in my sleep, and don't even get me started on the sleep monitor. Be asleep for thirty minutes, wide awake for ten, straight to REM sleep after that for two minutes... It's a joke. The only positive thing I could say about the knockoff is it had 50% better battery life. But again, it was so useless that I wish it would run out of battery.

The body readings on this Galaxy watch are SO accurate that it's a little creepy. Plus it has features that the others don't... Like stress level! Bruh.
This was well worth the money I am very happy with how accurate it is!

One thing that surprises me, and is a bit disappointing is you cannot use the watch to answer calls when you are strictly using Bluetooth and wifi. You need to use a cell service which means you need to pay your mobile carrier more monthly to use the calling feature. With the knockoff you can just use Bluetooth. It sets your watch like a speaker or headphones so you can answer the call...
Same for texting. When I get a text it notifies me but to answer it I have to use my phone.
When someone calls me my watch doesn't ring or tell me that someone is calling. I only get a notification when I miss the call.

It's possible I am just doing something wrong as I don't have the manual... But just some stuff I wanted to share that I would've found helpful!

title: These Suck!
text:
I used them for one day and the right bud just stopped working completely. I could not find a way to re-pair it. There are NO INSTRUCTIONS that work to re-pair one or both of the buds. I went to the App Store to check on the app to see if I could find a way to get the right to re-pair and got an error message that the Samsung Buds Controller is not compatible on my phone. When I click to install it on my Samsung watch, it sits in “installing soon” mode.

As far as battery life - long lasting battery is a joke. The battery MAYBE lasts 4 hours max. I use my phone for work for at least 8 hours a day.

Don’t waste your money - you’ll end up sending them back too!

title: Impressive upgrade for a fitness user from Gear S2 Sport (3G)
text:
9/18/21: Reducing review to 1 star. Watch began bootlooping last week and I wasn't able to revive it. I opened the watch this morning to find it full of water. Ive only used the watch as directed, and it doesn't seem to be up to the task. I love the watch functionally, but ultimately if it can't meet its own product description, it is useless. After researching the bootloop issue, it seems that water ingress is a common concern amongst owners.

Skip to the "REVIEW" heading if you don't care about the factors at play that influenced my purchase.

Update #3: Just about 7 months in of daily use by a very active user. I probably use watch GPS for 10 hours a week on average, and the watch has spent probably a hundred hours in the pool. My battery is at 22% with 4 days and 10 hours on the battery. My opinion of the watch has only improved. Despite daily use in rough environments (cramped car engine bays, chlorinated pools, etc) the watch still looks brand new. There isn't a scratch on the face, and battery life is still as good or better than new. I was concerned the lack of bezel would allow the face to be scratched more, but this watch is TOUGH. I know I've impacted it on tools, concrete, and other....cringe inducing materials and it doesn't show any wear. I've taken to swimming in our 12yd pool in the backyard since everything else is closed, and even with these super short laps, the lap counter function is still dead on. It helps keep me from going crazy counting out the 142 laps that equal a mile. I'm not sure what else I can share. This is definitely one of my all-time favorite gadgets.

Update #2: Starting to regularly see battery life of 4+ days. Last two recharges went onto the charger with ~18% battery left after approximately 4d18h usage each. Given the fact that I use the watch for GPS related items almost every day I'm nervous to push it an extra day, but based on the way the battery life continues to increase slowly I'm sure I'll be there soon. I cannot express enough how pleased I am with this watch.

Update: After about 3 weeks with the watch, I regularly get 4 full days to a charge with standard use. If I use the GPS for more than a few hours over that time I might find myself needing to charge after 3 days just to be safe. After 90 minutes with the GPS on (not tethered to phone) this weekend I had used 11% of my battery. Battery life is FANTASTIC. Easily double the best battery life I saw with my Gear S2.

BACKSTORY: I've been using a Samsung Gear S2 Sport for a little over 3 years now. It's served me well, but even after a battery replacement I'm struggling to get more than 24 hours out of a charge, and little more than 90 minutes of GPS time. As an endurance athlete this won't cut it for me.

SHOPPING: When I first got the Gear S2 the smartwatch was more of a fun toy, not something I 'needed'. I've enjoyed the conveniences its brought me so much that it is now a requirement and I found myself looking to replace it with an upgrade. My minimum requirements for a smartwatch narrowed options quite a bit. Probably my favorite feature is being able to answer calls (when connected via bluetooth to my phone) through the watch itself. As someone who is always keeping busy, its great to be able to answer a phone call hands free while working under my car, on a ladder, or on a bike ride. Having a built in microphone and speaker was a must. A battery with enough capacity for a 4+ hour run or bike ride was also an absolute must for fitness tracking. Water resistance was also important, but I already take my Gear S2 off when I swim, so it wasn't a requirement. I was willing to look at other brands/ecosystems, but I have thoroughly enjoyed my experience with Samsung Health, so I looked to Samsung watches first. Luckily they offered a watch that met pretty much all of my needs.

REVIEW: As I write this review, I'm still on my first charge (55% left after 37 hours) so you may consider this a little premature. I'm writing this review because of the incredibly positive experience I've already had. I purchased the 44mm Aluminum version because the 40mm version was sold out and I wanted to take advantage of the Cyber Monday deal. I had seen in reviews that people regretted getting the 44mm because it was too large, and I do have smaller wrists. Coming from the 42mm Gear S2 I wasn't too nervous, but the idea of a smaller watch intrigued me as it would be less likely to get in the way.

Size: See pictures for how the 44mm watch looks on a tall guy with skinny wrists. I think the pictures make it look a little larger than it actually is and feels. It's about 1mm thinner than my Gear S2, and despite supposedly being about 2mm larger in diameter, when the two are compared side by side they appear to be the same size. The band is definitely suited for people with larger wrists, when I have it on the second smallest size, its just a tiny bit loose, and the smallest size is right about snug. This band would likely not work for men of smaller stature or most women. I would assume the 40mm version has a smaller band, but I cannot confirm. I am very pleased with the size and weight. At right around 30g, its half the weight of the stainless Gear S2 I came from. I was concerned about durability upon discovering its an aluminum watch, but after wearing it the build quality feels excellent.

Battery Life: The 44mm version has a battery that's approximately 30% larger than the 40mm, which was important to me in the interest of fitness tracking. I normally remove my watch every night to charge, but with my normal use it looks like I'll be able to get away with charging it every 3 to 4 nights. I'm at 55% now after 37 hours, and that includes an hour of auto detected workout, a run, and a swim. I do keep wi-fi off, I don't use the wake up gesture (watch face turn on when lifting wrist), and I keep default brightness low (but auto).

Fitness tracking: Part of what prompted me to write a positive review so quickly was my morning run/swim today. The improvements over my Gear S2 are tremendous as you can now customize the data displayed for each individual workout type. Want to see heart rate, speed, and elapsed time? Ok. Want 3 different metrics? Also OK. Totally customizable with big text that is easy to read. This watch is supposedly waterproof to 5 atmospheres and has a swim tracker, so I had to try it out. I swam 56 lenghts in the pool this morning, and without any setup or configuration the watch also counted 56. I was astonished. There is an option to set your pool lenght so it can accurately measure total distance also. The data provided is also variable: time per length, strokes per length, time per 100yd, etc. It isn't 100% accurate, but 90% of the time the information appears to be spot on. I'm a casual swimmer who is always trying to get faster, so I'm really looking forward to using this on my intense workouts. Syncing Samsung Health with Strava couldn't be easier, and was completely seamless when switching from my Gear S2 to the Active 2.

Other notes: The Gear S2 had a physical rotating bezel that sat proud of the watch face. The Galaxy Watch Active 2 has no physical bezel, just a touch bezel surrounding the display. I was (and still am) nervous to lose the protection provided by the physical bezel, and I also enjoyed being able to go right to the bezel to wake and control the watch. Thankfully, the touch bezel is just as intuitive as the physical one, with excellent haptic and visual feedback resulting in a level of control that feels completely natural, even after 3+ years of muscle memory built up from a physical bezel.

I wear my watch 24/7, and totally use it as a fashion device when attending formal gatherings. I'll generally turn the watch face to always on and chose a neat face when I'm dressed up. The Galaxy Wear app will now allow you to take a picture of your attire, and provide you with a selection of faces that match your theme. This is REALLY cool and it works exactly as you would expect it to. The beautiful design of the watch only makes me want to show it off more. They've really improved the design from the earlier generations and it feels and looks less like a tech gadget, and more like a traditional watch.

Really my ONLY gripe for this (and all Samsung watches) is the lack of 3rd party support. It is much better now than when I first got my Gear, but the Galaxy App store really feels like it is about 15 years old. Since apps aren't too big of a deal for me, I don't mind much but it did take getting used to (and eventually just ignoring) the app store and its limited options. I did find Strava, MapMyRun, Smartthings, Alexa, and other apps, but I haven't really found a need for them.

Update: After about 3 weeks with the watch, I regularly get 4 full days to a charge with standard use. If I use the GPS for more than a few hours over that time I might find myself needing to charge after 3 days just to be safe. After 90 minutes with the GPS on (not tethered to phone) this weekend I had used 11% of my battery. Battery life is FANTASTIC. Easily double the best battery life I saw with my Gear S2.

Update #2: Starting to regularly see battery life of 4+ days. Last two recharges went onto the charger with ~18% battery left after approximately 4d18h usage each. Given the fact that I use the watch for GPS related items almost every day I'm nervous to push it an extra day, but based on the way the battery life continues to increase slowly I'm sure I'll be there soon. I cannot express enough how pleased I am with this watch.

Update #3: Just about 7 months in of daily use by a very active user. I probably use watch GPS for 10 hours a week on average, and the watch has spent probably a hundred hours in the pool. My battery is at 22% with 4 days and 10 hours on the battery. My opinion of the watch has only improved. Despite daily use in rough environments (cramped car engine bays, chlorinated pools, etc) the watch still looks brand new. There isn't a scratch on the face, and battery life is still as good or better than new. I was concerned the lack of bezel would allow the face to be scratched more, but this watch is TOUGH. I know I've impacted it on tools, concrete, and other....cringe inducing materials and it doesn't show any wear. I've taken to swimming in our 12yd pool in the backyard since everything else is closed, and even with these super short laps, the lap counter function is still dead on. It helps keep me from going crazy counting out the 142 laps that equal a mile. I'm not sure what else I can share. This is definitely one of my all-time favorite gadgets.

title: BUYER BEWARE! Ticwatch Pro failed after 2 weeks and Mobvoi has AWFUL customer service!
text:
Smartwatch's have become very useful that I only take my phone out for shopping here on Amazon and watching a video/movie. I have been a smartwatch buyer since 2012 with the Sony Smartwatch. Then had the Samsung Gear in 2013, and 2014 the Moto 360. After the Moto 360, I have been waiting for a smartwatch that can use the Google Wear OS, make and receive phone calls, is waterproof, and has a solid GPS link. Before the Ticwatch Pro, I purchased the Samsung Galaxy Sport and the Newest Samsung Galaxy 46mm (Dec 2018 release) but the Tizen OS is lacking quite a lot in comparison with The Wear OS by Google. Also, Samsung's search would always mess up on me and never have the answer like Google so the Ticwatch just made sense. The 2nd screen is a awesome feature that will allow you to still monitor your steps and keep time but without draining the battery. I have not used the feature to much as I love using the Byssweather watch face/app from the Google play store. This smartwatch should keep me happy for the next couple years or until the next cool watch is released ;)
I highly recommend and give 2 thumbs wayyyyy up!

***Update January 29th, 2019
After submitting the good review, my Ticwatch Pro became a nightmare. First, going to a different screen would cause the entire watch to Freeze or lock up causing me to have to do a hard reset. Then the speaker started working intermittently. I contacted Mobvoi through emails and also telephoned. They send me a return prepaid postage and the Ticwatch Pro (in black) was picked up by UPS the next day. Mobvoi emailed me that they were sending me out a brand new Ticwatch Pro on January 24th. Well, I didn't receive the new watch or any tracking information so I called back to find out that they lied and didn't send the watch out. The guy said he was going to contact shipping and get it mailed out January 28th. Again, didn't happen!!! I email again and they email back asking what kind of watch that they were sending??? Now I have told them several times and included my name and address in 2 previous emails, but email them again the Asin # this morning. The Mobvoi company needs to get it's act together if it is going to survive.... I do not recommend dealing with them unless you like dishonest people...

title: good watch but
text:
This seems to be a common thing among WearOS watches (and Garmin). They squeeze too much into a form factor that's not ready for all of that yet. Remember early Android or iOS? You look at one of those old phones now and wonder, "How the heck did I ever think this was a nice piece of technology?". I feel like that's about where smartwatches are today.

This watch is probably one of the frontrunners (I haven't tried an Apple Watch). It definitely feels more like a smartphone than a fitness tracker. Again, this is due to WearOS. Play Store on-board, you can OK Google and chat and text and call right from the watch (mirrored off your phone). Tons of watch faces, free and paid. Some faces are super customizable, basically changing the UI of the watch (Extra menus/settings/shortcuts/toggles). It gets deep.

But all of this comes at the cost of battery. This isn't the watch's fault, it's the user's. Here's the thing - the native UI is somewhat lacking. The 2nd button has one customizable action - single-press. Holding that button opens Google Pay, and it can;t be changed. No options for 2x press. The other button does default things, but also without a 2x press option. Quick-settings have a set number of toggles, someof which are not what i need at my fingertips but without the option to change. So, a customizable watchface will give me that, but will drain battery. So to get functionality which isn;t limiting to me, I have to pay with juice.

But OK, that's quibbling. Let's talk defaults. The default has multiple fitness apps. Google Fit, as well as the Mobvoi stuff. It's kind of confusing at first, and I set 2 apps to track activity, thus double-draining the battery. That was rough. but then i decided to not use the Mobvoi apps and go with Fit (more activities and it will sync across fitness devices when you change to a different manufacturer). This experience could have been better.

The watch itself is really nice. It has an update out of the box that runs while you're going through the "welcome" stuff so the watch was really sluggish out of the box. I deduced this, but i feel like my parents would be really put off. Again, diminished first experience.

Once you get all of that out of the way, it's really nice. I am still a little confused about when the watch decides to turn the face off on its own, I couldn't find the setting to change the screen timeout. Maybe in the watchface settings, i didn't try there. I feel like this should be in "Display". other than that, it doe all the things I wanted, and then some. Being able to get notifications and reply via wifi when my phone is in the other rooms is nice for my needs. This requires the watch to be on wifi though, and it will destroy your battery in less than a day.

But, with WiFi off, along with the LCD screen feature, the battery should last me over a day, which is enough. It also has a complete saver mode, where the watch goes into just-LCD mode, and WearOS isn;t running, and the watch isn;t doing anything except being a watch. This could last days (they say weeks). My only issue with this mode is that there's no simple backlight (like in a Timex or Casio). Low-power light. The face otherwise isn't visible unless you shioe a light on it. Kinda rough if you wanted to put it into that mode overnight. It's good for outdoors battery-saving, but not for all-around use. They're almost there.

My ultimate issue is that this watch looks a little ridiculous on my smaller wrist. It's huge. it was also unable to read my heartate unless i had it too tight on my wrist in a spot where it was uncomfortable. This isn';t just this watch, same thing with garmin.

I keep trying watches but the bang for the buck doesn't seem to be there for me. Either terrible battery, sluggish, lack of features, or uncomfortable. The one really only has the comfort issue for me, due to weight, size, and desired HR position. I think my best experience so far was with the Samsung Gear Fit 2 (it's like a wide band), but that one couldn't Spotify very well - and the Pro model that does drops NFC. So I figured this T-Pro would do the trick, having both clearly available, but the comfort thing got me.

If you have a larger wrist and are used to wearing bulky watches you probably won't mind it. it's a solid piece of equipment other than being a poor fit for me. Bet battery life i've seen in a WearOS feature-rich watch.

The band it comes with is nice but I don't think it sill be nice for long. it's silicone but with an inlay of leather on the outside, making it look like a leather strap. Problem is it's thin [p]leather, and was wrinkled by the time I took it off/on a couple of times. I would consider the cost of an additional band when you're cost-comparing. I would have been fine with the silicone-only.

I guess I'm saying I'd like this exact watch and all of its functionality in a thinner and lighter form factor. I'd also like some more customizability to WearOS (not more features - jut be able to relocate and remap existing functions). I think we are a few years out from that target, and I'm hoping that manufacturers won't continue to add more functions as the batteries get smaller, then increasing battery size to meet those functions, thereby keeping the watches the same size they are right now. I think the Versa 2 may be the largest i would go, but my experience with their OS is lackluster (thought hey have good tracking). Samsusng's app store reminds me of Samsung "smartphones" pre-iPhone - lack of development and ongoing support of existing apps.

IDK. Gonna keep looking in the band form-factor but I'm giving up for a while in this form factor. Really nice device and I didn't feel ripped off for the price (like with some Samsung products). You won't hate it.

title: BOTH BANDS BROKE. BEWARE: SAMSUNG SAID THEY DON'T SELL REPLACEMENTS.
text:
Bands are VERY flimsy. When I could not find replacements on the market, I called Samsung and asked about buying a band thru them or getting a replacement band (since both bands fell apart after only 25 days of constant wear). They apologized and said that they do not replace bands; Do not have any bands to sell nor do they foresee when they will have bands for customers to buy. SAY WHAT? I asked what they expected customers to do when a band broke and the reply was: "Well, fortunately for you, you have not owned the watch long, so you can return it to amazon.

*I also own a Samsung Gear-S watch and want to point out that it still has the original band on it and it's going strong. So I am not hard on bands. The new, Gear S-2 band is flimsy junk.

title: Poor battery life, Touted health apps don't work
text:
I purchased this watch to replace a Samsung Galaxy watch 3. The reason I purchased it was to gain the health monitoring feature, things like body composition and blood pressure. Because the health-related apps only work on Samsung Galaxy phones, I purchased one of those as well.

First off, getting the watch to properly work with my google calendar took hours due to an error in the documentation. Even after getting the two to work together, the galaxy watches still chimes at the wrong times. I have my Google calendar set to chime four minutes before an event, say 8:56. With that setting, it will chime some time after 8:56. However, it no longer provides the alerts exactly when I want them. They are frequently more than a minute late.

Second, the health apps. I really wanted them. However, I am in the US and the companion app is not available. At least that is what I get when I try to use the health apps. The correct phone app will not install. As such, the health tracking apps simply don't work.

This is my second, and last, Samsung smartwatch. Considering that the features that should make the Samsung stand out simply don't work, I will be just as well served by a cheap no-name.

title: Best smartwatch on the market for non Apple users
text:
The main thing I was concerned with was battery life having tried a previous generation Samsung watch that struggled to get 18-24 hours on a charge. What I didn't want was a watch that needed to be charged every night while sleeping and only minimal need to charge during the day. This watch reaches the claimed run time and have yet to run out of power when taking advantage of the time spent getting ready for work. 30 mins of charge time adds about 20% to the watch which is more than enough to get me through the day. I only need to charge to full every other day or two with this strategy. This is with light usage so YMMV. I have the watch set to always on display and touch screen to wake up instead of gesture to limit accidental triggering. Also have GPS off unless needed to further stretch battery life. I am not much for monitoring exercise so those features don't interest me and I can't comment on how well they work.

I primarily use this for sleep monitoring, heart rate, step counting, phone and text messaging, and weather.

Phone calls are clear enough to be heard from the watch itself and the end user says I sound good on their end. I did have some issues trying to get the text settings just right so I would get notifications but got them working eventually. Samsung needs to simplify the process of setting this feature up. There too many not so obvious settings on the phone that can interfere.

Music playing.....I don't recommend this if sound quality is important to you as the audio over bluetooth is flat. Best to use the phone for this if it is nearby. Also using bluetooth audio drains the battery quickly. Having Samsung headphones/buds really helps if you own a pair. The Galaxy Wearable app automatically passes connection info to the watch for instant painless pairing. The wireless headphone function is best used for phone calling when you want more privacy.

Watch face choices....boy are there a lot! Hundreds of watch faces to choose from. A good amount are free, but most are 1-4 dollars. Not sure if it worth paying for a face especially since there doesn't appear to be a way to try them out first before buying.

Apps....again there are many to choose from to add more functionality and information at your fingertips.

The speaker can be set quite loud so no problems missing audible alerts.

Samsung is still making the user experience better, but it is still not a perfect product. I would love a way to visit the Samsung store for watch customization on a laptop or tablet to make browsing easier. Phone screens are just a bit too small for ease of navigation.

title: Samsung Gear Sport Smartwatch, Renewed
text:
I really wanted to like this watch.
It worked fine for 1st week. Then it wouldn't charge more than 40-50% total capacity.
It takes a few hours to charge, which means during that time you can't track anything.
Using the least amount of notifications makes for 2.5 days use, per charge.
The Barometer isn't accurate. I set it to manually calibrate & I have to reset my altitude DAILY & it's still off about .30-.40 inhg from my satellite fed stationary weather stations. My Garmin Instinct watch is very accurate by a varying degree of .05-.08 inhg.
You can write short SMS replies from your watch using handwriting, which is pretty neat. You can block calls from your watch also.
I really like the rotating bezel & the variety of watch faces you can get from the Samsung Wear Store, and there are a few battery saving faces available for free download.
For a used "renewed" watch, it looks brand new.

I contacted seller to exchange for a different watch to see if I can get a better one, but the 2nd one they sent the bezel looks like it was put through a garbage disposal. So its going right back.

I'm really torn between keeping or returning for full refund. The main thing holding me back is the fact this is 100% compatible with my Samsung Galaxy s10E phone. And I had numerous connectivity problems with Fitbit Versa, Versa 2 in using my new cellphone. My phone stays. I have to search further to find a compatible, accurate watch to use with it.

I can't tell you if this is worth trying or not. I may have a fluke lemon. Or I may not.

title: The FitMeh is more like it…
text:
This Fitbit is expensive with great potential.
Unfortunately, the user app is terrible and it’s a pain to setup and navigate.
It’s not accurate at tracking actual steps as even minor movement is counted.
A minor yet incredibly annoying detail is when I turn my wrist to check the time or step count it rarely ever turns on.
The find my phone function worked for a week and now it rarely sees my phone and says to “move to another location and try again.”
Hardly any good screen skins for the Versa fitbit so I’m left using the default one.
I definitely regret not getting the Versa 3 or charge 5.
Shell out the extra money and get an apple or Samsung smart watch.

title: Poor battery, inaccurate heartbeat, incoming line calls dont work, shealth bad, etc
text:
This watch is absolutely useless. I have an Apple 6 watch (and had a 5 before). Where do I even start?

1) The battery will not last anywhere close to a day
2) Heartbeat sensor is inaccurate. Sometimes it says my heart rate is a very unrealistic number which I know it isn't. It is basically useless
3) When I get an incoming call on my Line application, there is no way to answer the call using my galaxy fold 3, however I cannot answer it on my watch either, it beeps/rings on my watch but no matter where I click on the screen or buttons, there is no way to actually answer the call. So it removed my ability to answer calls at all from the Line application
4) When I get a line notification, there is a lot of swiping involved to actually read the notification
5) The apple watch allows you to have a custom info widget on the main screen, which includes support for a compass direction. The direction is automatically displayed just by raising your watch it displays N, Ne, S, whatever direction you are facing. Galaxy watch has no such ability. The compass on the Galaxy watch app requires you to do a crappy figure 8 motion to calibrate it, and it works just as horribly as it does on android phones (that is a big reason why I need a watch, because android often shows inaccurate facing direction in google maps, for 10+ years it has not been fixed- IOS doesn't have this issue at all).
6) A bunch of super annoying stuff is enabled as a default. Your watch will be vibrating all the time just to tell you that you are walking somewhere. The thing is I want the sleep function but I don't want all of the auto tracking and other annoying notifications (which no doubt make the battery life so bad). Shealth doesn't seem to have the ability to tweak enough to make this possible. If I walk 10 minutes to the train station, I get a notification that I'm walking. So I start ignoring my notifications. I want to be notified ONLY for line messages, nothing else. I turned off notifications for shealth and it still buzzes whenever I walk somewhere. I hate it, I just want to throw this watch in the toilet. I googled it and there are outdated instructions which no longer work because Samsung changes stuff around constantly. I want ONLY manual workouts, I don't want it to automatically decide I am working out just because I'm not sleeping on the couch all day. I also want to manually start my sleep tracking. There must be a way, but I can't find it. Oh, and you have to register with samsung health and give them acccess to a bunch of stuff just to be able to use the sleep tracker at all. I don't want a samsung health account but I am forced to have one.
7) The google map navigation is useless. You need to be able to see your entire route or be able to zoom out to see your entire route when driving/walking/bicyling because google will sometimes auto switch the route to something that doesn't make sense and you need to know about it. If you just blindly follow the updated route on your watch, you will run into problems, for example end up on tiny small streets on your bicycle and waste a lot of time trying to get back to the main road, etc. I could just uninstall the maps app on the watch to avoid it popping up while im navigating and want the compass shown instead.
8) There is no nosie meter. The apple watch has a noise meter which can also be displayed on the watch face next to the compasss. All the information I want is there available on the main watch face. Samsung watch faces are much more limited in the information they allow you to display.
9) custom fix watch band is fitted and made available at the Apple store so you don't need a to adjust a band, it slips on your wrist and fits perfectly. My local Samsung store doesn't have this service.
10) no voice assistant. Apple watch you hold the watch up to your mouth and talk and you have full access to siri.
11) Apple watch is smaller and fits perfectly on my wrist. I can't even tell I have it on.

I have decided to scrap my galaxy watch 4 because it is basically useless. The only function it performs correctly is displaying time and notifications (except for all of the junk notifications like auto tracking walking- Im sure there is a way to disable this but I gave up trying to find it). I am going to try having my galaxy fold 3 in wifi tethering mode constantly and having my watch connected to its hotspot, and see if I can receive Line notifications that way (I have an iphone I leave at home connected to the internet). I'm really suprised it is so bad because of all the positive reviews. I even tested it at the samsung store- you don't know all of this stuff until you make the mistake of buying it. I haven't found an android watch that comes anywhere close to the functionality of the apple watch.

title: Not for a Linux User + Other Issues
text:
"Back from whence it came."

I have the original Samsung Galaxy 46mm watch. Some of this review is comparative.

The first problem I encountered is that, to upload music, one must install a separate computer program (Samsung uploads from the Android app on the phone). There is no Linux version from Garmin and the program will not run in WINE. That rendered the watch useless to me although, apparently, one can store content from streaming services like Spotify. Nevertheless I want MY stuff; classical music that is normalized and high quality.

Another issue, and again this is comparative, is that the Samsung turning bezel has a clear advantage over swipe-only on such a small platform. In other words a Samsung watch allows one to select items by turning the bezel which can then be touched for activation.

The Garmin requires swipe-scrolling which is an adventure. In the process I found myself inadvertently selecting items as the watch has a hard time differentiating between a touch to swipe and a touch to select. Compared to a phone, this is a very tiny screen.

I found the readability of the Samsung screen to be a bit better and sharper.

I had ordered a Watch-4 directly from Samsung. The order was botched. I allowed my anger to replace my judgment. At least for me the Samsung is a far better choice. I am going to reorder.

As always, your mileage may vary.

title: Samsung is ALMOST there but one major flaw - no notification history per app
text:
*UPDATE* Lowering rating from 5 to 3 stars. Receiving notifications is a big part for me. The problem is, even though I'm using a Samsung S8+, that the watch only shows you the most recent notification per app. For example, let's say your friend messages you on WhatsApp and says text1 "Hey", text2 "Let's go eat", text3 "Where should we go?". The Gear Sport will vibrate 3 times for each notification, but when you look at the watch you will only see the latest text, which is "Where should we go?".

The only app that supports text history is the messages app, which defaults to the SMS app on your phone and cannot be changed. This is also the case with emails, where you will only be able to see the latest email you received. Same with Google Hangouts, Instagram, Facebook, etc. The watch does NOT keep a history of the notifications received per app even if you didn't look at them. That's such a huge oversight in my opinion. Even my Pebble Time had this feature.

*Original Review*

In short: this is a really great smartwatch and fitness tracker for those who thought the Samsung Gear S3 was too bulky and find all the other options in the market (Asus, Motorola, Garmin, Fitbit, and Huawei) lacking.

My needs: Receive notifications, track steps, track jogging and cycling workouts, sync calendar, send texts, control music, good battery life and be comfortable.

Warning, long review ahead.

Notifications
The Samsung Gear Sport can receive any push notification your phone receives. You can choose which ones you want to turn off and only focus on certain ones. Just because you receive the notification for a certain app on your phone doesn’t mean you need the same app on the watch. NOTE - the watch only shows you the most recent notifications per app.

Step Tracking
Tracking of steps seems to be fairly accurate. It will be accurate if I’m walking with my arms to the side or if my hands are in my pocket. It is not accurate if I'm pushing a stroller or shopping cart

In theory, this should be able to count the number of floors for every 10ft in elevation it senses but I haven’t had a chance to test yet. From the Gear S3 reviews, it doesn’t seem like it’s going to be the most accurate feature here.

Track Jogging and Cycling
Using the built in GPS, tracking my runs and bike rides is pretty accurate. I like how I can leave my phone at home, start a workout and the GPS kicks in and starts tracking me. Given that I’m a very data centric person, I really appreciate being able to go back on my phone and see all the data points from my workout, including a map of what I’ve done. Obviously initiating the workout will drain the battery quicker but if I keep it under two hours then the Gear Sport will still last me an entire day. Music without my phone, I connect my JayBird bluetooth earphones to the watch and play from my Spotify offline playlist.
Unfortunately, this doesn’t track how many times I pick up my baby who gets heavier by the day, but that’s kind of wishful thinking.

Heart Rate
The heart rate can be triggered on-demand so you can check your pulse whenever you feel like it. During a workout, it is triggered automatically throughout the workout so you know where you stand. I can’t speak to how accurate it is, but rather than focusing on the accuracy of the number itself, I like to use it in relation to where it was when I started, middle and end. For example, if the watch says my initial BPM is 70 and in the middle of the workout it’s 140, then my workout is being effective. I don’t care if it’s actually 151 instead of 140, but rather my BPM has doubled since my workout started.
By default, it is configured to take your heart rate every 10 minutes when you're idle. I turned this off since I don't really care for it.

Sync Calendar
Living the corporate life, I’m constantly bombarded with meetings followed by additional meetings. To some degree, these meetings are the vain of my working existence but a necessary evil at times. So I need a way, at any given moment, to very quickly check the meetings coming up in the next few hours. Since I’m this watch with my Samsung S8+ (and by extension the Samsung Calendar App that connects to my Google Calendar), then I can look at all my meetings immediately on the watch. I’ll also get the reminder alerts 15 minutes before my meeting starts which is a life saver.

Send Texts (SMS, Google Hangouts, WhatsApp, KakaoTalk)
Here’s the deal, if I’m getting a text from a friend and I want to respond really quickly with “OK Thanks” or a more lengthy response, I don’t want to take out my phone every single time. It’s much easier to configure a canned response that I can choose from or speak to the watch to respond, have it transcribed and once approved, send the text. This makes it really convenient in so many levels. By the way, this works with all of my messaging apps. NOTE – there is no speaker on this watch so you will not 1. Be able to get audible notifications and 2. Make phone calls.

Control Music
I’m a Spotify Premium customer, have been for quite some time. So when I heard that this watch was going to let me sync my playlists (up to 4GB) for offline play, that got me excited. But honestly, most of the time I use the Spotify app on the watch to control the music that is playing on my phone, which is being sent to Alexa that is connected to my studio monitors. Even if I’m not listening to music on Spotify and got a podcast instead, no problem, the regular music app works just fine with that. When I'm working out without my phone, I connect my JayBird bluetooth earbuds to the watch and continue listening in offline mode. NOTE – there is no speaker on this watch!

Battery and Comfort
There’s no sense in having all the features in the world if 1. The watch doesn’t last me a day and 2. It’s not comfortable to wear. This is why I’ve kept my Pebble Time for 2.5 years and no other smartwatch in the market could replace it. I’m glad Samsung has figured out that customers want to have our cake and eat it too, meaning we want all the functionality in a form factor that’s very light, thin with a battery that lasts days.

Battery wise, I can go 1-2 days on a single charge with medium use. I check notifications when they arrive (about 4-10/hr during the working hours), check for meetings (1-2/hr), control music from time to time and have one (maybe two) 1 hour workout . I do leave the watch face off until I bring it up to look at it, so it’s not always on and saves some battery.

Comfort wise, it’s light, slim and the watch face is just the right diameter. I’m 5’10” 175lb and my wrists aren’t particarly small or large. My medium sized hands (whatever that means) can wrap around my wrist so that my middle finger and thumb are touching. If that gives you an idea, at all, of how big my wrists are, I can tell you that this watch is really comfortable and most of the time you don’t notice it. I’ll post pictures compared to my Pebble Time, but they’re equally comfortable even though the Pebble is significantly smaller.

So this can do a lot more than what I’ve written about, such as auto tracking workouts, swimming, have tons of other apps installed, change watchfaces, etc. I don’t really use those that often so I can’t comment on it but I’m glad it’s there.

Build quality
You’re paying $300 for a watch it better be well built, especially if it’s going to be waterproof up to 50M and used for workouts and swimming. Good news, it is.

Design
That rotating bezel? Pure genius. I'm glad they kept it for this design as it works really well. Half the time I'm playing with the watch, I'm just rotating the bezel just for fun. The two buttons on the right side have a nice click to it. It looks nice and functions admirably.

Performance
While this has the same processor as the Samsung Gear S2 (from October 2015), this version has more RAM and the OS has been optimized a bit more. I do notice some lag from time to time, but only when the watch is doing something a bit more processor intensive, such as clearing out my notifications. But 98% of the time, it's very speedy and responsive.

OK that’s a long enough review. There’s plenty of value for me to highly recommend this to anyone looking for a smartwatch. If all you want is track workouts, perhaps consider a Fitbit or something similar. If you want an inspector gadget mini computer from the future on your wrist, this is it.

The problem is, even though I'm using a Samsung S8+, that the watch only shows you the most recent notification per app. For example, let's say your friend messages you on WhatsApp and says text1 "Hey", text2 "Let's go eat", text3 "Where should we go?". The Gear Sport will vibrate 3 times for each notification, but when you look at the watch you will only see the latest text, which is "Where should we go?".

The only app that supports text history is the messages app, which defaults to the SMS app on your phone and cannot be changed. This is also the case with emails, where you will only be able to see the latest email you received. Same with Google Hangouts, Instagram, Facebook, etc. The watch does NOT keep a history of the notifications received per app even if you didn't look at them. That's such a huge oversight in my opinion. Even my Pebble Time had this feature.

In short: this is a really great smartwatch and fitness tracker for those who thought the Samsung Gear S3 was too bulky and find all the other options in the market (Asus, Motorola, Garmin, Fitbit, and Huawei) lacking.

My needs: Receive notifications, track steps, track jogging and cycling workouts, sync calendar, send texts, control music, good battery life and be comfortable.

Warning, long review ahead.

Notifications
The Samsung Gear Sport can receive any push notification your phone receives. You can choose which ones you want to turn off and only focus on certain ones. Just because you receive the notification for a certain app on your phone doesn’t mean you need the same app on the watch. NOTE - the watch only shows you the most recent notifications per app.

Step Tracking
Tracking of steps seems to be fairly accurate. It will be accurate if I’m walking with my arms to the side or if my hands are in my pocket. It is not accurate if I'm pushing a stroller or shopping cart

In theory, this should be able to count the number of floors for every 10ft in elevation it senses but I haven’t had a chance to test yet. From the Gear S3 reviews, it doesn’t seem like it’s going to be the most accurate feature here.

Track Jogging and Cycling
Using the built in GPS, tracking my runs and bike rides is pretty accurate. I like how I can leave my phone at home, start a workout and the GPS kicks in and starts tracking me. Given that I’m a very data centric person, I really appreciate being able to go back on my phone and see all the data points from my workout, including a map of what I’ve done. Obviously initiating the workout will drain the battery quicker but if I keep it under two hours then the Gear Sport will still last me an entire day. Music without my phone, I connect my JayBird bluetooth earphones to the watch and play from my Spotify offline playlist.
Unfortunately, this doesn’t track how many times I pick up my baby who gets heavier by the day, but that’s kind of wishful thinking.

The heart rate can be triggered on-demand so you can check your pulse whenever you feel like it. During a workout, it is triggered automatically throughout the workout so you know where you stand. I can’t speak to how accurate it is, but rather than focusing on the accuracy of the number itself, I like to use it in relation to where it was when I started, middle and end. For example, if the watch says my initial BPM is 70 and in the middle of the workout it’s 140, then my workout is being effective. I don’t care if it’s actually 151 instead of 140, but rather my BPM has doubled since my workout started.

title: Cannot reccommend compared to Samsung Gear 2
text:
Having spent a few months evaluating and developing software for the Samsung Gear 2 Neo, I can't help but compare the two.

From my perspective, the most critical feature of a smartwatch is no different from a mobile phone - who is the maker of the operating system and what level of developer support does it have?

Because the hardware doesn't really matter if its a dead-end system which will never see broad support.

Even with Samsung, which is the biggest handset manufacturer in terms of unit sales and in their 3rd or 4th generation of smartwatch development with the Gear, Gear 2, and Android Wear - they can't seem to settle on a platform, and their developer support is sketchy for Gear/Gear 2.

Android Wear watches will probably be better, as they will have Google behind the OS (The Gear 2 runs Tizen, a Samsung-supported Linux with HTML5 app interface).

Likely, Apple's watch will dominate in terms of level of ongoing developer support and market share, as they will have end-end control of OS and hardware.

So where does this leave the Burg? Well, it has a couple of unique features - one being that in addition to being a Bluetooth tethered device for your Androind phone, it works with iOS as well. The other is that it is a standalone smartphone with a SIM slot.

The latter is much more interesting, as for some people with specific requirements, they could indeed not carry a smartphone and still get calls, notifications, and some additional features.

That would be helpful, except for the form factor - its twice as bulky as the Gear 2 Neo, and costs about the same. It's Dutch style aesthetic gives it a bit of latitude in being big and colorful ( the default band is light blue, but it comes with a black band as well), but not enough.

BTW, the unit does come with an aftermarket SIM, and if you want to use it as a separate account, you can just call the number and setup a pay as you go account.

As for functionality, I discourage anyone from getting too excited about app possibilities for smartwatches. For me, its greatest value is the ability to receive/dismiss calls and notifications *without taking your phone out*. I also rely on my smartphone to get me to 4-5 meetings a day *on time*, and having the reminder buzz up on my watch is more reliable than on my handset, whose ringer I may have turned off or left in my backpack.

Otherwise, even on the Gear 2, there were no apps I used with any regularity, as they were all a better experience on my handset.

title: 6 month life
text: My watch started to act up after only 6 months.  After troubleshooting for several weeks, the battery continued to get worse until it completely broke.  Samsung would not honor the warranty and offered to repair it for more than the cost of a new one.  I am very disappointed in the product as well as Samsung's refusal to back up their products.  I will not buy another Samsung watch.

title: Samsung doesn't really support this device
text:
Returning the Samsung gear 360 2017. There's a lot of reasons, but they all boil down to Samsung.
1. Lack of Mac and iOS support: as many of you know the Mac support is awful which is unacceptable on a device used by photographers seeing how popular Macs are with the photography world. And Samsung has not provided an app to view 360 videos on iOS devices, you have to upload the video you YouTube to view it as 360.
2. Lack of accessories: Samsung hasn't offered any kind of lens protection or waterproof accessories for these cameras which is unacceptable on something marketed as a action camera
3. Poor 360 support from Facebook, YouTube: even small 1 minute videos do not upload to Facebook, and when I upload to YouTube they sometimes are not 360 compatible. Seems even YouTube isn't really ready for 360 video. Samsung could solve this by offering a YouTube type service to 360 owners but they do not.

It's really too bad because I love 360 VR videos but if samsung isn't going to support their own device there is nothing I can do. The camera itself is great, it's the poor support that is the problem. Maybe in a few years they'll be better

title: Garbage product, great support
text:
Left earbud went to pot just like the other 2,000 reviews.

Living in SoCal means these are going to exposed to direct sunlight, sometimes upwards of 90 degrees F, and they simply aren't made to get that hot. If in direct sunlight for more than about 40 seconds they heat up and stop working, giving an audio warning that they need to cool down. I find this unacceptable in a product. I await the replacement Galaxy Buds and will re-evaluate, but I suspect they will suffer from the same overheating issue.

I also found that after about a month of use, the left earbud would randomly stop functioning altogether, as if the proximity sensor was deciding the bud wasn't in my ear, when it was. Also a commonly reported issue.

Pointless to talk about audio quality and other aspects when the product doesn't function as advertised.

Samsung support replaced with the newer Galaxy Buds, plus a gift card to make up the difference in price. This process took a long time for an RMA. Hopefully the replacement product can be used outside in the sunlight. If not, they will be swiftly returned for a refund and I will stop buying Samsung gear, as the company either does not test their devices properly and are using the customer as their beta testers, or they test their products but simply don't care if the product is defective before selling it. Either way, a deal breaker for me with a company.

title: Usually v2 is better, but not with the Samsung Gear 360 (2017 Edition)
text:
I was an early adopter and had the first Samsung 360 (v1, international version straight from Korea) and then "upgraded" to v2, the Samsung Gear 360 (2017 Edition). The camera is now almost unusable, and I regret selling my first version.

I won't go through all the problems. Honestly, I'm so upset and so angry at Samsung for screwing this up that I'm not going to waste my time telling them how to better engineer FORWARD rather than BACKWARDS. They really took everything about the success of the first camera and absolutely ruined it in this second version. It's stunning how they blew it. They made the new camera an awkward shape, took away the tripod and gave you an odd rubber ring to balance the camera on, took away the ability to swap batteries, and made the editing software unusable. They also just gave the camera quirks. For example, prior to writing this review, I tried to get a video off the camera three times. It kept failing for various reasons. First, a phone call interrupted the app (the app literally shut down because my cell got a phone call that I didn't answer). Second, I restarted the app and trying to stick a video and a notification came in (voice mail or text message). Again, I didn't touch the phone but the fact that an incoming message of any kind will shut down the app entirely means that you cannot save videos while ANYTHING is going on which might disturb your phone. Lastly, my phone ran out of storage trying to stitch a 3-minute video. Never mind that I have a 32 gig card and nothing on it but a few videos from the Samsung 360 (all under 3 minutes each). Processing/stitching takes a HUGE amount of space and so you have do one video at a time, delete them, then move on to the next. It's a tedious and overwhelming process for transferring videos.

For people looking at this camera, I will say three things:
(1) Get the original camera. The resolution is slightly less and it's slightly bigger, but it's a better camera overall (e.g. replaceable battery, not as top heavy, easier interface, useful but removable tripod, etc).
(2) Assume that you'll only be able to take 360 photos with these cameras-- not video. Budget-friendly 360 video from cameras like this-- sent to a smartphone-- is simply not yet ready for prime time. The files are too large, they aren't editable (see below), the processing power to stitch longer videos is too great, and the options for viewing later are too limited (e.g. I can't send them to my wife to watch on her iPhone unless I upload them YouTube so she can watch them there).
(3) Assume that you will not able to edit your videos at all. This may not seem like a big deal at first, but keep in mind that 360 video means you can't hide "behind" the camera. This means that when your record video, you get everything including walking to the camera and back to start it (or staring at your phone to start and stop it), and you can't edit out any awkward or boring parts. This makes the camera a one-trick pony: You can get short 360 video clips, so long as the timing of events and everything is perfect. It's not practical to record longer videos and then select highlights because you can't edit, trim, or splice the video. You're supposed to be able to edit using downloadable Cyberlink video software, but it's totally usable on Windows 10 and Mac. Some people have somehow been able to get it to work, but it's been my luck or chance-- certainly not by design. The software is terrible and Cyberlink has been making crap software for years. They are the company take makes awkward and bloated software packaged with some camcorders. No one ever buys their software because it's horrible. It's sort of software you get stuck with, because you have no choice. I don't mean to be harsh, but it's the truth. Their software is used in more of a hostage situation than by choice. Well, that's the case here.

Don't buy this camera. Don't even think about it.

Get yourself a first-version Samsung 360, or consider a GoPro Hero Session 5.

title: Samsung watch is going backwards
text:
I had a Samsung gear S3 (Tizian OS) from 2016, that I still miss, and I think the rotating bezel should have been kept.

Pros:
- Lots of Apps on WearOS (as it's a google OS)
- Great sensors (blood pressure, oxygen level, ECG, body composition ...etc.)

Cons:
- The Touch bezel is just a joke, not even close to the mechanical rotating one.(really you can use the physical bezel even without looking at the watch, just by felling it, thus snoozing or closing appointment notifications or an alarms easily)
- Battery life sucks, not even one full day, you have to try and extend the life by power saving and turning the screen off, topping-up mid day ....etc. , the gear S3 had almost 3 days of battery life.
- Samsung Health monitoring App to use ECG and blood pressure is not available in many countries, and even not working properly in some of the countries that have it. (many articles and workarounds you can find on the net)
- For the 44mm watch I prefer a bigger strap rather than the 20mm one. (it may be suitable for the 40mm but seems small to my liking for the bigger watches)

title: Nice but I prefer other options.
text:
I have had a few Samsung Gear watches and the Apple Watch so I will compare this with those. This is an attractive watch right out of the box but I am very disappointed in the hard silicone band. The silicone bands on Apple and Samsung Galaxy Watch are so much softer. This one is irritating on my wrist. You will surely want to purchase one of the other great options available on Amazon.

The screen is okay. It does not give a sense of depth as well as either the Samsung Galaxy Watch or the Apple Watch. My Samsung Galaxy Watch can actually fool you into thinking it's 3D. The standard watch faces do not give much information. You can purchase more but it's a hassle because the store isn't integrated into the app.

The charger is nice and small and the corded disc attaches easily to the back of the watch. It charges relatively fast.

Perusing through the menus is slow and I find it difficult to move through and find functions. It uses a standard twist knob and I don't care for that as much as a rotating bezel. Pushing the side buttons often yields no results.

Considering your other options, this watch does not stack up to the Samsung Galaxy Watch- especially for $16 more.

title: Interesting, but will get little use.
text:
I have many headsets, earbuds, and wireless versions of both. I'm always looking for the next best set. This is not it because while they are a really interesting idea with some unique features, I think they fall short of what people are looking for when they buy wireless earbuds like these.

Lets look at the features as advertised by the manufacturer (3 in 1):
• “Premium Audio” Okay, maybe. It really isn’t bad at all. I am not hearing better quality that my LG Tone Infinum or Platinum and it sure doesn’t measure up to Westone or Shure but it is good- at least as good as my Samsung Gear IconX. There is a versatile equalizer in the app with some special effects.
• “Smart Noise Cancellation” There are 29 levels of straight noise cancellation (-22 to +6) There are also noise specific modes on the app- 11 of them like “Enhance Speech”, “Airplane”, “Subway”, “Crowd”, etc.
• “Voice amplification” Definitely! But should be called “Sound Amplification”

Pros:
• It does all the things it claims but how well and how well together is the question.
• “Good” sound while listening to music.
• I can hear a pin drop in another room
• If the sound amplification is up and I’m listening to music during a walk, I can hear a car coming before I can see it.
• If using these to dim ambient noise around you, the separate noise filters work pretty well. I can’t tell the difference between all of them but there is one that includes adding white noise which I really like.
• When using them for their intended purposes (3 in 1) individually, they will successfully perform for you.
• They come with a great choice of ear cushions (the part that actually enters your ear canal) both rubber and foam.
• The app has many features for enhancing your use.

Cons:
• <<Show Stopper>> The battery life is 1.5 hours, max without a recharge in the case or case to power. I’m being generous with that time. It’s usually just over an hour and 10-15 before I get the voice warning me of the low battery. This means that regardless of the features and how great they are, you are only going to get to use them for a short while. This means they are not good for flights or any other commuter experience over an hour and they are not good for using at the office, events, hiking, etc. unless you want to take long breaks between uses.
• I can barely notice changes when using the equalizer or preset sound enhancements like “reverb”, “echo”, “bass boost”, “flange” (what is ‘flange’ anyway?)
• I need constant adjustments to keep them in my ears properly so the app will work.
• Sound amplification destroys your music experience. When I walk and want to listen to music, any wind sounds like a jet taking off in my head. If I turn the sound amplification to make it bearable, I can’t hear ambient noise like, cars, buses, or bicyclists who might run me or my dogs down. Same noise attacks if you touch them.
• Does noise amplification have to sound so “tinny”? It goes beyond high treble.
• Not waterproof or set tight in your ear so bad for athletics

So, who should buy these?
• People who want to use sound amplification or noise cancellation separately for less than 90 minutes
• People who like bleeding edge technology over total functionality
• People who want a hearing aid for a short period

Final thoughts:

There are better options for all three of the 3 in 1 features. I will likely never use these as there are better options for all three of the key features. I have a few that provide noise cancellation and great sound. They are not "wireless" earbuds but they are wireless headsets and they don't need constant adjustment and they last much longer on a charge.
• IconX and LG Tone Active are better for athletes
• There are better options for premium sound at much lower costs
• There may be better options for stand-alone hearing enhancement

But, if you need all three for less than 90 minutes, then these are YOUR new wireless earbuds

There is a versatile equalizer in the app with some special effects.
• “Smart Noise Cancellation” There are 29 levels of straight noise cancellation (-22 to +6) There are also noise specific modes on the app- 11 of them like “Enhance Speech”, “Airplane”, “Subway”, “Crowd”, etc.
• “Voice amplification” Definitely! But should be called “Sound Amplification”

Pros:
• It does all the things it claims but how well and how well together is the question.
• “Good” sound while listening to music.
• I can hear a pin drop in another room

title: Has potential
text:
I originally purchased the Samsung Gear Fit over both the Nike Fuel band and the FitBit, because of advertised capabilities. The number one thing that I wanted from this that both NIke and FitBit do not offer, is setting a goal time with a specified distance. Samsung advertised this capability, but you can only set one or the other. In addition, the distance is not accurate. I ran on a measured track for 2 miles, and the Gear Fit only said that I ran 1.7 miles. Lastly, the Samsung does not fully monitor sleep like a FitBit, you set the Samsung when you go to sleep and it starts a timer, it does not monitor you while you sleep, it merely keeps a running time.

We had high expectations for this considering it was $200, in the end we decided that we will wait until fall when the Moov is released and get that.

I'll just buy a nice Casio watch and call it a day.

title: This is horrible. DO NOT BUY. Read reviews
text:
Never have I ever had a smart watch or a Fitbit product. In 3 days of research I found this Versa would be the perfect fit for me. I decided to start the return process the same day I received it and bought a Samsung Gear Sport instead.

The size of it was great on my wrist, the special edition band was very comfortable. However, the touch screen on this only worked a quarter of the time. I tried washing my hands, doing a reboot, a hard reset...nothing worked. Sometimes the whole screen would glitch out and I hadn't even touched it.

I don't generally write reviews but I feel that Fitbit still has work to do on these touch screens. It seems many buyers have had the same issue with the touch screen not working as it should. In spending this kind of money the item should work as it says.

title: Not accurate gps, get scratched in no time.
text:
1. In less than a month glass and even watch itself - all scratched. I even sometimes put the watch in my pocket at work and still it's get scratched. It's BAD, worse than my 20 years old $10 no name China made watch! You can see just a few on photos.
I think after a year I'll have to trow it away.
2. GPS is terrible, I think it turns on every minute-two only even in tracking workout mode!!! On a map it looks like straight lines, like you teleported from one street to another.
3. Step counting - watch counts wrist movement as steps!!!!! I get like 8000 steps while standing a few hours at machine at work. My phone is waaaaaay more accurate!
4. At the same time at some point the watch tells me - you got your goal 12 hours of standing, which doesn't make any sense because it's only 12 hours since I woke up )))
5. Turn off indoor walk tracking, otherwise after a little walk at work your watch will ask if you want to start workout tracking!
6. Turn off broadcasting, or watch will tell everyone around every mile - speed and distance you walked!
7. Sleep tracking - ok. But sometimes I get up and 2 hours later it in sleep tracking mode!
8. Alarm - watch by itself adds 8 alarm set for 7:30.
9. You can't control music playing on your phone. You'll get notifications only. And if you delete notification - music stops!
10. App wont synchronize for no reason, like right now last 4 days are missing!
11. Watch can show you weather, but right now it shows me weather in location where I made a stop for a few minutes yesterday!

Okay, something good about the watch.
Battery lasts 5 days if I wear it 42/7!
It's better than a samsung watch.

p.s. I would return it, but it got scratches so bad so fast!

title: DO NOT BUY THRU AMAZON-THEY ARE NOT AN AUTHORIZED SAMSUNG SELLER
text:
UPDATE: After this watch fell apart the second time & Samsung wanted as much to repair as a new one cost, they informed me that Amazon is NOT an authorized dealer and will not warrant anything purchased from Amazon. You are taking a risk buying this watch here. You may get fake product and it may fall apart and you will be out $300. BUYER BEWARE

I really like this watch, but either mine was a defect or it's durability rating is a farce. The sensor cover on the back of the watch, which is against your wrist 100% of the time, has fallen off twice. The first about 3 months after I got the watch that Samsung repaired (after I had to argue with them and escalate the issue) and now the second 2 months after the warranty has expired. It appears this cover is secured with a gooey substance that you can see from the picture, doesn't hold very well and doesn't look like it was applied well to really hold it in place. I never took this watch swimming but perplexed how it could have a rating to 5 atmospheres if this gooey glue is all that holds the cover over the sensor. I'm not finding anyone else having this issue so possible mine was defective from day 1 but if Samsung doesn't take care of me for a replacement, this will be the last Samsung watch I purchase.

title: Missing key features, inaccurate fitness tracking- Returned!
text:
I have had two Samsung smart watches, and loved them. I wear my watch daily and only replace when the battery life gets to the point that I can't use all the functions (specifically not being able to monitor sleep because I have to charge it every night). I assumed I would get the newest Samsung watch again, but the battery life on those NEW is bad, so I felt like I should look elsewhere.

I did lots of reading of product descriptions and reviews, and felt this watch would be a good fit for me. I was very sad to have to return this within a week. Here's what led to that choice:

Pro: Battery life. That is all.

Cons:
1. Inaccurate fitness tracking- this is HUGE. For a $350 watch, with a focus on the fitness aspect, I would expect that it would be accurate, or at least close. I live in a 3-story house and after a day at home running up and down stairs all day long, it tracked ONE flight of stairs. Another day, after spending about an hour at a store walking around, I got in my car to drive home. A minute later, my watch buzzed to tell me to "get moving."
2. NO CONTACT WITH MY ALARM!!!! This was a big deal-breaker for me. I have always used my smart watches to snooze my alarms from my phone. I am a teacher, and I have silent alarms set at the end of each class period. This watch doesn't buzz when the alarm goes off, and doesn't allow you to snooze or silence the alarm. I can't always have my phone in my pocket, so being able to use and control my alarms via my watch is non-negotiable.
3. Charging- the charger for this is a proprietary weird cord. That is annoying. I prefer the wireless cradle that my last two watches have used.
4. I opted for this model because it was advertised to be able to store music on the watch so you could listen without your phone while exercising. Unfortunately, you have to obtain music from a list of specific services. I expected to be able to just copy .mp3 files from my phone to the watch.
5. Garmin Connect App is not impressive.

Overall, I am quite disappointed in this watch. I had high expectations and high hopes, but they were not met at all.

I can't always have my phone in my pocket, so being able to use and control my alarms via my watch is non-negotiable.
3. Charging- the charger for this is a proprietary weird cord. That is annoying. I prefer the wireless cradle that my last two watches have used.
4. I opted for this model because it was advertised to be able to store music on the watch so you could listen without your phone while exercising. Unfortunately, you have to obtain music from a list of specific services. I expected to be able to just copy .mp3 files from my phone to the watch.
5. Garmin Connect App is not impressive.

Overall, I am quite disappointed in this watch. I had high expectations and high hopes, but they were not met at all.

title: Galaxy Buds? More like Galaxy Duds - Did not work with Galaxy Watch
text:
I bought these Samsung Galaxy Buds Plus to exclusively use with my Samsung Galaxy Watch so the last thing I expected was for the distance between my wrist (where I wear the watch) to my ears (where I wear the buds) to be TOO FAR for the bluetooth to consistently stay connected. They stutter and make strange interference sounds as if im out of range of the audio source.

When I use my old Sony bluetooth earbuds they work flawlessly and if I use my phone instead of my Galaxy watch for the audio source these buds seem to work fine so it must be a combination of a weak bluetooth antenna from the Buds that require a stronger signal then the Galaxy Watch can produce. Its hard for me to blame the Galaxy watch though because my Sonys work fine.

title: Don't buy!  Calorie conversion incorrect!
text:
Obviously only the positive reviews come up first! The calorie conversion does not work properly. It has been wrong from the very beginning. I have sent mine back to Samsung 3 times and they finally sent me a new one and guess what? The second I turned it on it said I walked 0 steps which would be correct but had burned 405 calories!!! For example if I look at it right now at 8:30AM it says I have walked 683 steps but have already burned 547 calories. Wouldn't it be nice if that were true, no dietiing.

The people that work at Samsung don't understand that this is wrong or that you normally would've had to walk 10,000 steps to burn 547 calories if it was just normal walking. The funniest part of all this (if there is one) is when you look at the display model or even the box their example also reads like this!!!!

I previously had two of the original Samsung Gear Fits and other than the silver accent detail around the watch peeling off it worked great. If you really want to buy this please read more reviews and you will see many stating the same thing.
