title: VERY poor design, clip to hold charger to watch breaks easily. I've included a work-around.
text:
I know this is the 1st generation of this watch, maybe the new ones are better. This is the 2nd charger I've gone through for my Samsung watch. The charger "clips" onto the back of the watch with 2 tiny, plastic clips - one end is just the guide, the other has the clip which is very tiny and very thin plastic. The clip has now broken on my 2nd charger and, for the price of this watch (fortunately, I got a cheap refurbished one but this is an almost $400 watch normally). There is no way to fix the clip once it has snapped. The charger will still work, it just takes more effort - you need a 5-6" long piece of scotch tape (I suppose a rubber band could work), I fold the ends of the tape over so they're easier to pull off. With the charger in place, place the middle of the tape (sticky side to the watch) diagonally over the screen (it can only go one way or you'll cover the charger cable connection - easier to just leave the cable attached for this) and, while holding the charger tightly to the watch so the contacts are..um...contacting, fold each end of tape TIGHTLY around the back of the charger. Voila. Once it's charged, carefully remove the tape (especially if you have the glass guard applied) and you're all set. I suppose the tape could be used many times but I have cats who love to play with it, then freak when it gets stuck to their paw! Also, I don't wear my watch 24/7 so I've gotten into the habit of shutting the power off when I remove it, then I only need to charge it every 3 days or so.

The bright side is, the charger will still work with some ingenuity. The downside is, for the price of the watch AND the price of several chargers, the cheapness of these items is all too apparent. At least scotch tape is cheap! Just have a supply on hand.

title: Poor support from Samsung bad software from Samsung
text:
It was fun while it lasted. The s3 seemed to be better than the rest when I purchased it but failed miserably after just a few months.
A software update released by Samsung ruined many people’s s3 watches making them very expensive paperweights. For months scores of customers have complained and all Samsung support says is that there will be an update at some time in the future.

Initially the step counting, heart rate monitoring, gps etc... all worked great but everything stopped working after the update.

If I were looking to get another smart watch I’d probably go with an apple product. My daughter has a Apple Watch and loves it. Runs with it, swims with it, sleeps with it and all the tracking has continued to work flawlessly since she got it.

My advice to anyone thinking about getting this Samsung watch would be to look at other brands. It’s a deal breaker for me when a company ruins a product I bought and then ignores it's customers when a solution is required.

title: Device requires constant computer connection with spyware-like app
text:
So I received my Bodymedia Fit Link today. Turns out you cannot set it up with your smartphone. You have to go to Bodymedia Fit and install a proprietary, closed source app that runs in the background. It then asks you to provide a bunch of personal information that it sends to Jawbone (who bought Bodymedia).

If you want to continue using the device this little spyware app has to be kept loaded on your computer. It doesn't tell you what the app does or what it's sending. It then links this device, your smart phone, account information and everything to your credit card number and billing address.

My Pebble Watch, Samsung Gear and even my house alarm all connects via bluetooth where I can see and control what permissions are granted to the app via either the Android of IOS devices. The idea that it requires this little sync app to run on your computer and it connects all of this personal information together is either 1) calculated decision by Jawbone to gather and sell your data to advertisers on top of the monthly fee or 2) their development team is incompetent.

Oh.. and before you buy.. search the internet for NakedSecurity and Jawbone. Jawbone has a history of security breaches and Jawbone isn't exactly forthcoming about how many people are affected and what data is compromised.

If you already own this device and you can't return it for whatever reason I suggest contacting Jawbone via the Bodymedia site and complaining. There is no reason why the device can't pair with your phone and sync just your vitals without this closed source app running on your computer gathering your personal information.

title: Completely useless. Any cheap $10 set on Amazon is better than these.
text:
These would be fine, but the battery life makes them useless. When linked to your phone, you get about two hours of life out of them. You can double that by using only one at a time, but it isn't as simple as putting the used one back in the charger, and putting the other one in your ear. In order to switch which headphone you are using, you need to open up the Samsung Gear app, and switch from there. BOTH headphones need to be in your ear and have battery life in order to do this. The whole process takes about one minute to do the swap. That doesn't seem too bad, but when you are exercising and have to stop what you are doing for over a minute, it gets frustrating. If the headphone you are using dies before the swap, you CAN NOT use the other headphone, even if it is fully charged.

The battery case that holds the headphones and charges them doesn't have great capacity either. It can charge both of the headphones once, but it quickly loses charge over time. If you fully charge both the headphones and case, unplug them, and try to use them 24 hours later, all three batteries will be fully drained. So, the only option is to leave it plugged in at all times when not in use, but the documentation specifically warns you not to do this, because it will wear on the battery over time.

Also, if the case's battery dies, but your headphones both have a charge, the headphones sometimes won't turn off if you store them in the case, because they think they are still in your ear, meaning they will continue to drain their batteries.

I've used multiple different $10 cheap Chinese wireless headphones from Amazon, and ALL of them were better than this.

title: Warranty Repair
text:
Watch is well within the warranty period at 8 months old. Love the watch. Well, until today when the magnetic charger pulled the quarter size plate off the back of the watch. Warranty repair is a nightmare. Instead of just sending me the miniscule grommet they use to attach the back plate to the watch, they want the watch mailed in to repair. No time line for repair and return. Unacceptable. Perfectly good watch gone for how long? BTW, the first question that the service rep at 1800samsung asked when I indicated the plate had come off was "did it happen while you were falling". Please, even if I fell off a cliff, the back plate is between my arm and the watch and it wouldn't have come off. Part of the reason to send it in is so "we can examine the watch and make sure it wasn't mishandled to cause the damage." Ok, I get that. But if was actually broken, sending me the grommet would not fix it. I asked to talk to a supervisor who connected me to Samsung Parts, unbeknown to me so I could buy the part! Which of course, Samsung Parts didn't have, as they don't stock parts for watches! So I asked where the repair department gets parts. "Well, they have them". OK, so why can't they send me the part. "That's not how it works. You have to send the watch in". I asked the supervisor to think outside the box and contact the repair shop and just ask if they would send it. Nope, no can do. This sounds like a short discussion, but with the interminable hold times, took 2 hours. I asked to talk to her supervisor, and hung up the phone after being on hold for 30 minutes. So for the price of a small grommet and some forward thinking, I will never buy another Samsung watch or phone. Who needs this kind of hassle? My guess is if I just superglue it back on, it won't be waterproof and it will probably void the warranty. I guess I don't care as the warranty is not doing me any good.
BTW, I did not buy this on Amazon, impulse buy at COSTCO. So Amazon will not show me as a verified buyer. But is registered with Samsung.

title: There are better tablets out there than this one
text:
Review of Galaxy Note Pro 12.2
The only reason that I am giving this tablet one star is because of the effects that can be done with the photo editor. Other than that the tablet is useless. It’s too big to lug around and awkward when taking photos due to its size. It is too small to function well seated at a desk or anywhere else for that matter. The web browser is a joke. The instructions manual is next to useless. I went on the Samsung site tried Chat for support. Useless. Chat had no answer to the simple question: How to reduce the gargantuan 18 pt text size for Notepad or Action Memo. Evidently, 18pt is your smallest option. The chat person really didn’t even know what I meant. Have you ever tried to follow a white arrow on a white web page? The border on the arrow is infinitesimal.The arrow can barely be seen on a white page. Other useless offerings are the multiple home pages. How many home pages does a person need? Add to those a magazine home page where you have a choice of:
• 8 apps like email and calendar which should go on your regular home page.
• Some social media widgets. Who needs those on a separate page?
• Widgets for samsung apps samsung video and samsung watch on.
• Widgets for news, arts, technical, etc which then require Flipbook another joke.
Lastly, the camera... It takes good pictures if you feel like lugging it around and don’t shoot anything farther away than about three feet. The camera has a mode feature for: beauty face (I am not kidding...beauty face) landscape especially useless since photos from farther away than three feet are pixilated. action shot another joke. There is no standard mode. I must also mention the video camera. You can take videos but you cannot edit them as the tablet cannot download Samsung Video Editor. I did not buy it on Amazon but am a loyal Amazon Customer. I want to warn others away from this tablet.

title: Worth the money and then some.
text:
I bought this for my mother for Christmas. She would kill me if I spent the money it costs to get an apple watch or samsung gear and such. I figured I would get her a budget one or something. After doing research I found so many "fake" watches.
Then I found this one. Real AMOLED display? Cool. All the reviews were very positive. So Christmas day she opens it.
She called me last night to tell me how much she loves it. She uses it for very simple reasons and that is for fitness and to you know....tell time. She loves it connected to her phone because a couple times a week they walk (her woman's group) and they try for 3 miles. It audibly tells her when she hits each mile and how they did it (with being paired with the phone). She also wears it to bed and says she feels it is accurate about the sleep tracker.
She goes fishing with my dad late at night and they are super pro about it so "no lights!" So the other night they went out for the first time with the watch and she loved it because she could easily see the time without making noise or causing bright lights. My mom absolutely loves it.

If you want to make phone calls and read texts maybe it's not for you. want to use it for heart monitoring,l, fitness monitoring, and telling time? buy it. Ill get one for myself too.

For what you pay for it, you win.

title: Brilliant watch let down by app store,  and lack of good apps - do not buy for non Samsung phone
text:
I've owned a number of smart watches in several different varieties (Pebble, Android Wear, and now Gear), and each has their plusses and minuses.

Let me say right off that while the Gear S2 is a brilliant piece of hardware (four stars) its severely hampered by the Samsung Gear Ecosystem, poor store experience and lack of quality apps (one star). If you are happy with the initial set of watch faces and apps, then this is a highly recommended purchase. But many of the promised apps have not materialized yet and the Gear app store is full of mostly garbage apps and watch faces with no where near the quantity, consistency, quality, and versatility of Android Wear.

So, from a hardware perspective, the Gear S2 is brilliant
-The screen is a typical Samsung Super Amoled thats bright, crisp, rich, and pose efficient that just blows every other smart watch screen away.
-The moving bezel (supplemented by touch and back and home buttons) is by far the best interface on any smartwatch I've used
-The responsiveness and speed of the interface is by far the best I've encountered, way beyond Pebble and Wear
-The build quality is excellent
-The battery life is good (no where near a 5-7 day Pebble but on par with a good Wear watch like the LG G Watch R) of a full day (24 hours plus) under heavy use and up to two full days under lighter use.
- The watch is light, compact, and very comfortable on the wrist
- The wireless charging is nice but proprietary and not as compact as it could be for travel. It doesn't convert the watch to a "clock" as Android Wear does when charging. Charging speed is average
- The strap is propriatary

From a software (App store, Watchfaces, and most Apps) the S2 blows chunks
- The store is a disaster. Really only accessible from the Grear app on your Phone. Heavily segmented/compartmentalized by catagories with an almost completely non functional search mechanism so you spend a lot of time navigating it. Apps often don't specify which Gear platform(s) they support and there is no filtering by product so you mostly have to rely on screen shots (if there is a round one maybe the S2 is supported).
- There are a fair number of watch faces but they often use different customization methods and are no where near the level of realism offered by many Wear apps, nor do they tend to have the number and variety of features/complications.
- The actual Apps are even worse when it comes to quantity, variety, consistency, and support for S2. This is just trash and the store has been open for over two years so I don't see it suddenly changing for the better. Many of the cool Apps Samsung advertised on their product announcement simply don't exist yet.

And maybe there are some tricks and tips I haven't uncovered yet, but my S2 doesn't get near the number of notifications my Wear gets. I don't get any Google card notifications which I rely on heavily. The watch seems to miss notifications fairly frequently (they appear on my Wear but not my S2) or somehow they are delayed for things like Calls, Voicemails, Calendar events, emails etc. I'll give it a bit more time but if I can't get the watch faces and apps with the functionality I'm looking for, and the quantity and variety of reliable notifications, this will be going back.

UPDATE: (added one star)

- Looks like an update fixed the notification reliability issue. Now I reliably see all phone notifications on the S2. And better then Android Wear, if I dismiss one on my phone it's removed from my watch (and vise versa)
- The GEAR store is rapidly adding watch faces for the S2 (and even a watch face maker exists now). Faces are still pretty simplistic as is the maker so this and apps are still no where near the Android Wear ecosystem but I'm impressed with a lot of new S2 items in the store. The store has also gotten much better in the app/watch face descriptions about which GEAR versions are supported (although no filtering options and the search is still useless
- I've decided to keep the S2 because if its brilliant hardware and performance and the promise of more and better watch faces and apps in the future.

UPDATE: (removed two stars)

I switched from a Samsung phone to another brand of Android phone. Lost all my purchased apps and access to all purchased apps and many other apps. Only a Samsung phones provide access to purchased apps and many other apps (like UBER). While the S2 is a great piece of hardware, its nothing without a good app store - and for non Samsung phones, the app store is garbage.

title: Solid hardware let down by mediocre software. Many other choices now in this crowded market.
text:
I'm writing this from the perspective of someone who has done a lot of work in 360 cameras and their content, working with everything from the 6-GoPro Freedom360 rig, through the newer Samsung and LG cameras.

Pros on this camera: Single unit, single point of failure. Setup is simple; attach, power on, connect and record.
Cons: The mounting system is weird - you need a special dock to charge it, and there is no common mounting option on the camera itself, you need to use the included adapters. Design is overly complex, mostly just to look good. Single button, limited storage.

Connecting to the camera is relatively easy, but you need to register with 360fly, and I'd say I had trouble connecting to their service and the camera at least half the times I used it. It has the same issue on some other cameras where you want to use WiFi to connect, but also need WiFi to register. Sloppy.

The camera isn't true 360 as it of course lacks full 360 coverage. The images are not bad, but the quality is not as good as newer cameras like the Samsung Gear 360. Editing the content isn't as easy as it should be, and neither is getting the content into a common format like Youtube or Facebook 360 support.

This camera came out almost 6 months ago, and the industry is moving fast. Since it was released, Facebook started to support native 360 content capture on phones, almost making this camera obsolete.

I'm a bigger fan of cameras like the Ricoh Theta S or the Samsung - quality is better, and it is easier to use the software. With better software, this wouldn't be a bad camera, just a little behind the times. Their new 4K version is a step in the right direction, but that makes the price of this camera even harder to stomach.

title: Not Ready For Prime Time
text: I've used this watch for a few months and I find myself going back to my Fitbit Sense. The Samsung Galaxy Watch 4 has numerous problems. First among them are very poor battery life. I had to recharge this watch every day or risk having it completely discharge while on my wrist. Another issue is with the promised apps (e.g. ECG and blood pressure). They do not work....period. Both apps require you to be "outside the US" and have a Samsung phone in order for it to even be downloaded. Oh yes, there is a convoluted workaround that you can download off the web from a third party but if you have any problems with your phone or change phones you have to go through the whole process again. The blood pressure app is not accurate anyway and you have to keep calibrating the app with a real blood pressure monitor to even use it. This watch is not ready for prime time. I'm sorry I wasted money on it.

title: Refurbished Samsung Gear Fit2 Pro Watch.
text:
I actually wore out my Samsung Gear Fit2 Pro. Replaced the battery and the metal strap I swapped out on it had corroded. Watch wasn't repairable.
When I started searching for a replacement, my heart was broken. Samsung no longer makes this model.
Then I found a refurbished one on Amazon. I just love this watch. I am beyond happy.
My original Samsung watch actually saved my life years ago. The heart rate indicator actually alerted me that I was having a problem. I went to the hospital to get checked out. I was having a heart attack.
I also like the step counter. I love to see when I reach my goals.
Samsung makes a awesome watch. And it links up with my Samsung phone so well.

title: Burned and Disappointed
text:
I bought this Samsung watch recently as my previous Samsung watch was on it's last leg. My previous watch (Active) lasted a long time and had no issues.

I ended up returning the watch for a few reasons.
1) main reason is the watch triggered burn marks on my wrists. Pic included.
2) battery life is a joke. It required charge in less than 24 hours. Even on battery saving mode, it was used up super quickly.

Avoid this product at all costs. That is coming from a huge fan of Samsung.

title: Samsung Galaxy Watch Active2
text:
This watch worked well for about two months. Then, without warning, the watch just quit working. It was always kept charged; was never abused, never subject to water, or any other inappropriate treatment. Attempts to contact Samsung were useless. Their support website simply doesn't work.

All in all, this watch is an expensive ripoff. DO NOT WASTE YOUR MONEY!!!

title: Wait A While Before Buying
text:
This watch is incredibly difficult to work with, especially if you have never had a Samsung watch before.

THERE ARE NO INSTRUCTIONS!!!

You are supposed to magically figure out how this watch works. It has been very, very, very frustrating trying to figure this watch out. The more I try to figure it out, the more I want my money back.

Loading new watch faces is also ridiculously difficult. And there are very few watch faces for this watch. You might want to wait until there are more before buying this watch.

There are a ridiculous number of bugs in the software. I would wait another year before buying one of these. I am going to get a refund.

title: I needed this
text:
I have avoided a smart watch for years and finally decided to give up the fight and get one. I hate the look of the square Apple watches and mostly think they are over-priced and faddish. I wanted something that would give me the look of a traditional watch and after watching a ton of reviews on YouTube, I decided on the Galaxy Watch 5.

I love the watch but hate the band. I am not reviewing the band but it does seem like if they offer you a classy looking watch they could offer something other than the plastic bands.

The setup is extremely easy with the app downloaded on the phone. I have a Samsung phone which may have made it easier and I don't think all the functions work with other Android phones. I love all the features and integration of the numerous health and lifestyle apps. When I am sitting around the house and have my Samsung phone on the table next to me or in another room, it is great to just look at my wrist to keep up with texts and emails instead of having to chase my phone down.

I followed the tips and tricks from a couple of the instructional videos online and find that the battery has adequate life. I charge my phone every nigh so it is no big deal to have the watch off my wrist for an hour or so during the day so it can charge back up. I keep the display in the "Always On" mode except when I go to bed. I then turn that mode off and set the face to a dim display so it doesn't beam in my face when I turn over at night. Even with the Always On mode activated, the display does go to a dimmer mode after a few seconds. Twisting your wrist or using the other functions will bring it back to full brightness. The watch had a full charge on it around 10 AM yesterday and today at 5 PM it is showing 54% of the charge remaining.

Being a retired worker and family room supervisor I didn't wear a watch most days anyway unless I was going out. I am wearing this constantly except when it is charging or I am taking a shower. I find that it is extremely comfortable (except when I had a cheap 3rd party metal band installed on it that cut into my wrist). Even though the silicone band is cheesy. The body of the watch is very light weight and comfortable.

The bottom line...it has every function available that you could ever want or need and it can be dressed up to give it almost any look you like. I went with the brushed aluminum face so I can put a nice metal band on it and wear it to a senior citizen's prom, or put the silicon band back on for a sweaty sports event. (The one complaint I do have is, the spring clip on the band are a real pain to get clipped back if you are into switching bands. It took me about 10 minutes of fiddling this morning to get those tiny pins back in place. Every time I though I had them locked in, when I tugged on the band, I had the watch in one had and half the strap in the other).

It's nice to read all the reviews here, but for the best impression of the watch I found it more useful to watch the evaluation videos online, especially the tips, tricks, and suggested functions that you should install once you power the watch up.

title: Disappointing heart rate tracking
text:
Disappointed. In-acitivty heart rates are ridiculous. During strength training, it consistently rates my heart rate very low, around a third or my real heart rate. I've tried different activities, putting it instead in cardio or yoga, but the same result. Ranks my heart rate between 60 and 85 when counting my heartbeats myself puta my heart rate closer to 110-140. Heart rate is the main reason I wanted this watch, but it's terrible with this. Outside of activities, it raks my heart rate OK, but often too low or too high, such as saying my heart rate is 130 when I'm just walking at the office, or saying my heart rate is 55 when I'm sitting at my computer.

title: Bad Battery and Less than Impressed with Functions
text:
After the Samsung Gear S3 Classic battery algorithm debacle that has Samsung owners jumping ship, I thought I'd give this watch a try. I was surprised that it took nearly 6 hours to go from 6% charged to fully charged right out of the box. I was equally surprised when the battery dropped back below 10% inside of the first 5 hours of me wearing it. (And I wasn't even playing with the watch.) That night the watch was soundly in its charger all night and without even checking the next morning, I strapped it on and ran out the door to work. By the time I got to my desk and settled in, the watch was at 14% battery.

Clearly this watch has some charging/battery issues and I am returning it.

Maybe the Gear S3 Classic spoiled me with all of its functionality and its plethora of gorgeous watch faces, but this watch was less than impressive. Lag time between my phone getting an alert and that alert coming through in the form of a quick vibrate on my wrist could be up to one minute. This despite the phone being in my pocket. The watch faces available were also disappointing. And the display always seemed to be dim and not bright and clear.

Even fully functional I don't think this is a $250 watch.

My hunt continues for a decent watch and fingers are crossed that maybe Samsung will get their stuff together and unfnarck the mess they caused with the last "update".

title: Wear 3100 still sucks (not Fossil's fault)
text:
It's almost as if Fossil is competing with WearOS and its issues. They can keep trying to come up with better processors, but in the end, it's Google and its OS that drains the battery unnecessarily. After having the watch for a few months, I realized that my battery life was not on par with others' experiences, and come to find out it's the bluetooth connectivity that's draining it, despite having a more power efficient processor. Without the connection to my phone, it's an almost pointless piece of hardware. It's also laggy as hell, which is really unfortunate.

Pros include: It's got a ton of watchfaces to choose from. The size is just about perfect for my super tiny wrist (it's larger than a regular watch but not abnormally so). The watch feels premium and I like the color options. It's quick at giving me notifications and the screen is large enough to read them. Touch is pretty responsive even with a glass screen protector on it. I can share apps from the android phone with it, like MapMyHike, Endomondo, Any.do, or OurGroceries. I think the heart rate monitor is accurate, coming from a fitbit before switching to this. However I'm not sold on the step tracker. The fitbit might have been SUPER over-estimating my steps if it included my arms moving about while sitting at my desk. The Fossil might not be as sensitive, which is a good thing, but that also means that my average of 7k steps a day has been reduced to about 5k, and I'm unsure if this is more accurate or it's not including times that I walk. The watch holds the steps in memory until I look at the watchface and it wakes from sleep, then it updates my steps. So I'm not entirely sure.

In conclusion: I wanted this watch because I wanted something that would replace my fitbit for daily use and ALSO be friends with my android phone. But WearOS is just not worth the hassle. A Samsung watch would probably do everything I need, as I don't really use Google Assistant on the watch because it is slow and inaccurate. And the battery life would probably be a LOT better.

title: Terrible warrantee service.
text:
I bought the Fitbit Sense to replace my Fitbit Ionic which overheated and caused 2nd degree burns on my arm. I stayed with Fitbit mainly because of its Android compatatability, otherwise the Apple warches beat it hands down. The main features I bought it for are its steps, o2, and sleep monitoring none of which work very well.
From day one the sleep record was wildly inaccurate and undependable, as was the steps feature.
Steps uses both gps and arm motion to sense them. But can not differentiate other arm motions like raking the lawn or sweeping the steps from walking or running. Since I do a lot of work around the yard its misinterpretation of the mild exertion of everyday activities as long walks or runs gives false fitness info. The O2 sensor requires an extra $10 a month subscription and frankly doesn't work very well the watch also has a sensor that is used as an on/off switch and for other functions. From day one it was quirky and after a few days stopped working entirely.
I phoned customer service numerous times and they could not solve any of these issues. The authorized a warrantee replacement which seemed punitive. I had to wait for a return box and label, another week of transit time, assessment time at fitbit and another week for them to get the replacement to me. Please remember this watch was defective from the gitgo, and was reported to them the second day I had it.
After arguing with the rep and waiting on hold for well over an hour, I spoke to a Supervisor for another long session. The finally agreed to replace the watch immediately. It took most of a week to get it.
The replacement had exactly the same issues! I had to jump through the same hoops, only more so and will be without a watch for another week.
If you have an iPhone, buy Apple, if not try a Samsung watch, but avoid Fitbit like the plague.

Steps uses both gps and arm motion to sense them. But can not differentiate other arm motions like raking the lawn or sweeping the steps from walking or running. Since I do a lot of work around the yard its misinterpretation of the mild exertion of everyday activities as long walks or runs gives false fitness info. The O2 sensor requires an extra $10 a month subscription and frankly doesn't work very well the watch also has a sensor that is used as an on/off switch and for other functions. From day one it was quirky and after a few days stopped working entirely.
I phoned customer service numerous times and they could not solve any of these issues. The authorized a warrantee replacement which seemed punitive. I had to wait for a return box and label, another week of transit time, assessment time at fitbit and another week for them to get the replacement to me. Please remember this watch was defective from the gitgo, and was reported to them the second day I had it.
After arguing with the rep and waiting on hold for well over an hour, I spoke to a Supervisor for another long session. The finally agreed to replace the watch immediately. It took most of a week to get it.
The replacement had exactly the same issues! I had to jump through the same hoops, only more so and will be without a watch for another week.
If you have an iPhone, buy Apple, if not try a Samsung watch, but avoid Fitbit like the plague.

title: Decent smartwatch
text:
I expected too much regarding this Ticwacht E smartwatch, since all the youtube reviews talk so good about it. The truth is, I'm dissapointed. Almost everything that states it can do.. it can't do. I’ve been using it for more than a week now me (with my Huawei p20 pro). First of all lets start with the hardware: The product is good looking, nice plastic black band, very confortable, it is light weight and it combines pretty much with any look. The desing is very well accomplished. This is why i’m giving at least 3 stars.
On the other hand, the software.. this is the part where it gets frustrating to me. It took me 4 days to get it running almost normal, since I had to look for tricks or recommendation from people on youtube with similar problems. I had to restore it to the fabric settings once, after that it worked better. The battery got better after letting it die at least 3 times, now I get between 29/30 hours of battery life. Google assistant never worked properly, this is why I decided to buy this watch on the first place. The “ok Google” never worked. “Call mom” or “send a text to..” never worked Good, it always said “cant reach phone” or something, even though the watch is connected to the phone with bluethoot (and also wifi). Other major issue to me is the delay on the notification of incall coming to the phone (25 seconds at least), the phone starts ringing but the watch does not react instantly, which I think is ridiculous, this is so basic! You CAN make calls from the watch with you contatcs sync, but recieving calls is not the same, don’t know why (I looked it up online). Also, you can download many apps from the play store, but let me tell you, they are not very Good, most of them you have to open the phone in order to sync, like Spotify for example; you can't do anything if the phone is locked, you need to wake you phone, open the Spotify app on your phone and only then you can kind of play music, but on the watch you only can pause/play, go to the next song and stuff, you can't select a specific song from a playlist or an álbum (I’ve donwloaded another app call wearify, which Works better) and forget about downloading songs in order to play them without the phone. Regarding notifications like text messages, whatsapp or emails, works OK, also with a Little delay but not too bad, you can reply on the watch and most of the time is succesfull but some times it fails. On whatsapp notifications you can see emojis and still stickers.
So, overall it’s a decent smartwatch, but the wear OS system needs more work, it doesn’t feel smooth enough, maybe I’m too demanding. I recommend spend a Little more money on another watch that can run everything without having to restoure the watch or install some update or re-iniciate the phone or whatever, it all should be easier. Personally I’m considering changing this smartwatch for a Samsung active watch which is more expensive, but I think it won’t have this kind of failures.

title: Failed after 9 months
text:
I really had a love-hate relationship with this watch. The display was nice. The battery life wasn't good. Had to charge every other day, which makes it difficult to use as a sleep tracker. While sleeping, using night mode, the watch would occasionally turn on, waking me up.

I bought this watch to swim with, but like many others, found that water was what killed it. Based on other's stories about attempts to have it repaired, it appears that water damage isn't covered under Samsung's warranty, despite the watch's water resistant rating, and the fact that it has a swimming mode and a 'water eject' feature. When swimming, the watch buttons would often get hit, stopping the tracking of my swim. Not cool.

When timing my bbq cooking, I often found that the timer was inadvertently stopped. Really not cool. Overcooked steaks!

I paid $270 for this watch at a warehouse store...that works out to $30/month. Will never buy another Samsung smart watch again.

title: I LOVE this camera, but there's one HUGE FLAW!
text:
As a Samsung S7 Edge owner, I love this camera, but it has one MAJOR flaw that the end user needs to be cognitive of at all times, or the camera will be damaged (as mine was/is). I've had the camera for a about a week, and this has been my experience so far.

Out of the Box: Getting started with the Gear 360 2017 was very simple. I un-boxed and charged it, read the quick start guide, downloaded the Samsung Gear 360 app from PlayStore and I was taking photos and shooting video right away. I literally posted my first 360-degree video to Facebook within 10-minutes of connecting the camera to my Android. It is very simple and worked flawlessly. To get a little farther, I watched a couple of quick YouTube videos to learn more about the live streaming functionality, which I may never use, but is still cool if you have internet excellent bandwidth.

Pros: Again, simplicity. With my Samsung S7 Edge Android phone, I was able to snap fun photos and video and post to Facebook with little effort. The photo/video is converted from the dual-fish-eye view to the 360-degree format automatically, so it is literally as simple as uploading. The battery life is very good (held a charge for the entire week with intermittent use), and the Bluetooth connection to the phone worked flawlessly every time. As a 360-degree camera, this is really easy to use. It now works with Android and IOS (newer versions), although the Live Streaming doesn't currently work with IOS, and there may still be a lower resolution in IOS at this time.

Cons: I really didn't have a negative experience with the Gear 360 yet. Your IOS and Android phone just need to be on a compatible OS. The one main flaw was this:
- The Gear 360 is top heavy. It simply falls over if there is the least amount of instability under the base, and the rubber "stabilizer" ring supplied with it is useless (if not detrimental in giving a false sense of security). I literally have placed the camera on a slightly-less-than-horizontal surface, and it is perfectly fine for 30-seconds, then it suddenly falls over like a drunk sailor for no apparent reason. I've scratched both lenses, and here's the next big negative, there's no lens replacement kit for this camera (yet, maybe?). To repair the lens, you must currently ship it back to Korea.

Advice? If you buy this camera, buy a decent little tripod for it and take the tripod with you wherever you go. Throw the "stabilizer ring" in the recycle bin and just use the tripod.

title: Falls short of expectations especially compared to competitors
text:
The watch's square screen takes some adjustment and feels small yet the design of the watch as a whole isn't ergonomic and feels bulky. The screen resolution is also disappointing; it can be unpleasant to look at. My wrist was super unhappy with the silicone band so I had to switch it out for steel with magnetic closure which is great. I got a third party band to save cost. The pin system for band change is fumbly. The watch cannot control my phone's music or camera. Picture messages cannot be viewed on the watch. Quick replies (cutomizable preset message responses) from the watch are limited to 5. The app selection is vast when it comes to fitness but otherwise sparse. The interesting apps are not free. The Uber app failed so intensely that I had to remove it. I cannot remove Strava from the watch which is very frustrating because I don't use it.

That being said, there are seemingly infinite clockfaces to choose from. Battery life is excellent and charge time is super fast. The watch is responsive and accurate when it comes to activity recording. The sleep tracking is also more accurate than apps that use my phone's accelerometer. The move reminders are helpful without being daunting. The Fitbit mobile app is pretty comprehensive with easy setup and practical function when it comes to expanding watch data.. though I cannot get it to properly sync with MyFitnessPal.

I usually protest things that are pretty obviously marketed toward women when they don't have to be. I probably should have stuck with that inclination based on the Versa Lite's flashy colours and obvious pink tax feel. I would hope the regular Versa is more capable with it's two button functions.

I chose this instead of a Samsung watch like my partner got because I no longer have a Samsung phone. I regret it this purchase as something I live with every day. Their watch solves every problem mentioned above the band but that's an easy switch (especially since their system of changing bands is less fumbly). Their watch even cost slightly less than this one.

I stuck this out because I wanted to love it and because I wanted some space from Samsung products. I wear this every day but I even turned off notifications from my phone after a longtime glitch. I use this to count steps and tell time.

If you need a watch that tells time, counts steps, and checks your phones notifications.. then surely there is a cheaper, more comfortable, and size appropriate watch out there. If you're hell-bent on a Fitbit then get a nicer one than the Versa Life. If you're not hell-bent on a Fitbit, get another brand of wearable.

I chose this instead of a Samsung watch like my partner got because I no longer have a Samsung phone. I regret it this purchase as something I live with every day. Their watch solves every problem mentioned above the band but that's an easy switch (especially since their system of changing bands is less fumbly). Their watch even cost slightly less than this one.

I stuck this out because I wanted to love it and because I wanted some space from Samsung products. I wear this every day but I even turned off notifications from my phone after a longtime glitch. I use this to count steps and tell time.

If you need a watch that tells time, counts steps, and checks your phones notifications.. then surely there is a cheaper, more comfortable, and size appropriate watch out there. If you're hell-bent on a Fitbit then get a nicer one than the Versa Life. If you're not hell-bent on a Fitbit, get another brand of wearable.

title: It's garbage, but it's really CHEAP garbage.
text:
Understand going in: This watch is utter crap. It's garbage. It's a "smart" watch only inasmuch as it can connect via bluetooth to your phone and make/receive calls from your connected phone, or act as a very limited standalone phone with a SIM card.

However, it's only $10.

For $10, it's actually pretty damn cool. It plays music (albeit on a tiny, practically useless speaker) and can make & receive calls. For ten lousy bucks, what the hell more do you expect? The Samsung Gear 2 smart watch costs $200, and doesn't actually do a whole lot more when you get down to brass tacks. Sure, it's sturdier, and prettier, and has more flexibility; it's also 20x more expensive than this humble little $10 piece of crap that, should it get smashed, you wouldn't waste a nanosecond worrying about.

If you have an extra ten spot burning a hole in your pocket, pick up one of these crappy little watches. They're cute, and kinda fun.

title: Galaxy Watch 4 Classic - disappointing battery life
text:
Lots of features and apps, does not measure Temperature unlike many inexpensive smartwatches.
Biggest disappointment is battery life. At best, I've gotten about 36 hours, meaning you charge it every day, unlike the smartwatches at 1/5 the price which last about 6 days before needing to be charged. Charges in about 2 to 2-1/2 hours depending on state of charge when recharging.
The WearOS app is primarily used for setup, unlike inexpensive smartwatches which use the phone app for displaying collected metrics. Syncs up with Samsung Health app to record activity and some health parameters. To record an ECG requires additionally installing the Samsung Health Monitor app (a different app than Samsung Health) on your cell phone.

title: Not worth the price or the Hype
text:
I owned this watch for just over a month and in that time the price dropped $30 and what's worse is the resale was $200 less that what I literally just paid. So I took a huge loss on this watch.

Pros:
-Nice display
-Solid Build Quality
-Decent features (If you have a galaxy Smart phone)

Cons:
-Way overpriced
-Battery is Horrible. I could not get much more than a day off a charge.
-Charging - Takes almost 3 hrs to charge.
-Sensors were hit and miss.
-The O2 sensor almost never worked and always made me reposition 2 or 3 time before it got a reading.
-Heart rate sensor also was hit and miss. Especially during runs the heartrate chart would have huge gaps in it so what's the point in using it as a fitness watch if it only partially works? Even repositioning, tightening and loosening didn't help. It was an issue every time I ran.
-Newly released ECG feature and upcoming blood pressure sensor are only available if you own a samsung phone because you need the samsung health monitor app (Not the standard Health App) which is only available via the Galaxy store. So as a Pixel owner I am shut out of 2 of the main features.

Bottom line, It is not worth the price. I would recommend either the new Fitbit Sense or the Fitbit Versa 3 as they have comparable features and are much more economical. If your issue is that these lack Apps, then you should play with your phone instead. Its a watch. After losing my shirt on the GS3 I bought the Versa 3 and after about a week I am 110% pleased. Great watch, great battery and it isn't trying to be something its not.

Galaxy Watch 3, NOT RECOMMENDED!

title: Screen is dim and menus are clunky but for the price the Fenix 3 is hard to beat.  Its all about the Garmin Ecosysem!
text:
Just got this after running through several Garmin devices. I started with a Fenix 5. When I pay as much as a Fenix 5 costs I expect to be able to read it. Yes, I'm particular like that. So many people rave about how the Fenix series easy to read in the sun but it just isn't...and its BARELY readable indoors. Even if it did look great outdoors, why would I trade that for not being able to see it the other 15 waking hours of the day? Samsung looks unbelievably good indoors and looks readable in direct sunlight. Poor design Garmin. But for he price you can get this baby for now, I am fine with the display.

Also the interface is pretty clunky. Learn from Samsung. And for those user who say they prefer buttons, who cares... give us a touch screen too to navigate all the ridiculously abstract menus... While I'm at it: who the heck cares what the difference is between an app and a widget? Make themn the same thing dudes...really. Every time you have to access something you have to figure out how to get there. Who wants to work that hard with a WATCH. So I returned my Fenix 5 and bought a Fenix 3 HR Sapphire instead because for less than half the price I can live with the limitations. ...happily. Why?

Garmin's fitness tracking ecosystem is the best out there in my opinion. I have maintained Garmin and Samsung for a few years now and finally sold my Samsung Gear S3 Frontier yesterday.

Garmin:
1) Has a WIFI scale (essential and very convenient) which I use and love
2) Has the Vivofit series which I use for travel because they are tiny and require no battery charging hardware... which I love
3) Syncs with Strava (Samsung does not sync with Strava)
4) Provides lots of quality metrics, equal to Strava for Biking IMO)
5) A great product line of many choices especially for size and price

So after trying out about every product in the Garmin line I settled with a Fenix 3 HR Sapphire, Vivofit 2, Vivosmart HR, and the Index smart scale. and lived happily ever after...

title: Not as advertised
text: I was looking for a cheap smart watch and despite my research saying this was the best watch out there even when compared to ones 3 times the price, I had problems within the first hour. The battery life is terrible. It claims to last two days without a change, but I'm not confident in it lasting half a day at this point. I'll update my review at the end of the day if that changes, but right now I'm pretty disappointed. The quality seems good enough for the price point, but I'm not worried about dropping something strapped to my wrist so I don't see it as a problem. I was under the impression that it would work off of my phones data, but everything seems to require the watch itself being on a wifi network despite it not being advertised as a standalone unit like the Samsung Gear or Apple watch. It keeps wanting to update randomly which is annoying because you'd think it would cover everything in the first one so I'll be sitting around for a few minutes waiting for it to update when all I want to do is check the time. It's comfortable and lightweight, yeah. But the downsides really outweigh to upsides right now.

title: A good watch with a terrible OS - I strongly recommend not buying it...
text:
I have used Samsung Active 2 and Samsung Watch 3, so my review is a comparison to those watches.
All the good things in this watch are what comes from Samsung. All the bad things come from moving away from the Tizen operating system towards the new OS. Here are the things I hate about this watch:
- Terrible battery life -- both Samsung active 2 and watch 3 have a better battery life, since this is supposed to have a large battery I can only assume the new OS is a battery hog.
- Lack of free watch faces - Tizen OS provides hundreds of amazing watch faces for free from independent developers. Comparatively we are in a desert landscape on this OS. All watch-faces want to sell you the features. App store does not help you find the free faces, instead is pushing the super expensive watch faces (think $200 for absolute crap). You can buy subscriptions for apps that allow you select from different watch faces, but they change you just to remove the ads that make the app unusable, and then charge you for the watch faces. What a joke.
- Google play store design for Watch apps is terrible and confusing. Samsung wear app has an amazing marketplace for watch apps, but for this watch it sends you to Google play store.
This will be my last Samsung watch unless they fix a lot of these issues. I find myself going back to Samsung Active 2 and Samsung Watch 3, both superior watches to this watch. I recommend you find those on sale and buy them. They are 100x better than this watch. I hope that Samsung allows us to install the Tizen OS on it so we can salvage some value out of this watch.

title: Not as nice as a Samung Galaxy watch
text:
I had purchased a new LTE Samsung Galaxy 4 watch, but realized due to my heavily tattooed wrists, the Samsung watch wouldn't work. The sensor in the Samsung watch never thought I was actually wearing it, so it wouldn't send any alerts.

I did some research and came across this Garmin Fenix 6. Sure enough, I receive all alerts when wearing the watch. So far, it hasn't skipped a beat.

But, this watch isn't nearly as nice as a Galaxy watch. The screen on this Fenix isn't a touch screen, and you can't rotate the bezel to move between the apps. This is a push button watch. Everything you want to do, you need to push a button on the side. And the buttons don't feel that nice either. Overall, the watch works. And I'm glad I was able to pick it up on sale, but there's no way this watch is worth over $300. I wouldn't pay that much for it.

The watch is cumbersome, and like I said, it doesn't have the build quality of the Samsung. But guess what? I can't use a Samsung, so here I am. I'm going to keep the watch, and I wear it every day, but I am a little disappointed in the high price for a watch that doesn't reflect that.

I have synched the watch to my phone, but for some of the features, I need to synch it with my laptop. Again, cumbersome.

I was mainly looking for a smart watch that would work even though I have a lot of tattoos on my wrists, and I did find it. But.....if you can wear a Samsung watch, I'd buy that instead.

title: Corrupted
text:
I put this in an LG Stylo. Format went well, but the phone continuously unmounted it and said it wasn't the correct format. Restarting the phone helped, so I figured it was a phone problem. This was the first time I used this SD card, which I'd purchased more than a year earlier, never used prior.

I just pulled it out of the LG and put it into a Samsung A50. The Samsung A50 attempted to format the card but apparently something went wrong. The phone is brand new, on Android 10, fully updated. The micro SD is now corrupted. I put it into my adapter to format on my PC but it won't even format it.

Used for two purposes, wrote maybe 5-6 gigs of data of the 66 gb of capacity, and now is corrupt. I'm biased in regards to Samsung, I really love their products and will choose them over others, but this card issue and some recent quirks with a new Note 10, Galaxy A50, and Galaxy Watch Active 2 have me questioning if maybe I should be starting to look towards other brands for my needs, suchas Google phones, Fitbit watches, and PNY for storage.

title: DO NOT GET IT WET!!!
text:
Any advertisement as to this watch being waterproof is false.

My screen started acting crazy, so I contact support. After they walk me though a few things I agree to ship it in for warranty repair. It turns out they found water damage and that isn't covered under warranty; they now want over $200 to repair the watch. After pointing out the watch is advertised as able to handle water, I was told there was probably damage to the watch that allowed water in. It appears this watch is too delicate to survive my standard office workdays, much less any true adventures.

This watch has no right to be labeled Active as fragile as it seems to be. Any amount of damage, visible or not, can be enough to let water in and that will void the warranty. Warranty support was polite but not able to repair the watch. This will be the last Samsung watch I purchase.

I see a few other reviews now with a similar complaint and a price tag for repairs. Wish I had seen those before I purchased!

title: Cool Watch, But Does Not Integrate With Samsung Phone As Well As I Would Like
text:
This watch does a LOT. It provides a TON of data on the home screen and 10x that if you dig into the menus. As a fitness tracker, I think it's tough to beat and I really wanted to like this watch, but I couldn't make it do some simple tasks that I consider a requirement for a smart watch.

I've been wearing a Samsung Gear watch for a number of years. It's a mediocre fitness device, but it hits some of the high points, like steps, pulse, gps (which kills the battery), etc... but no stairs, compass, barometer, bike computer, maps, run for multiple days (not to mention WEEKS), or many other things the 6X can do. What it DOES do is allow me to manage my phone from the watch.

I get a large number of spam phone calls each day, and the Gear let's me decline those calls without digging my phone out. I also manage a lot of reminders and calendar items from my phone, and the Gear allows me to snooze them and remind me later. Unfortunately, the Fenix would not show phone calls coming in, showed text messages about 75% of the time, and only allowed me to "Dismiss" calendar reminders and alarms (no snooze).

I have devices that perform many of the functions of this watch while out on a long bike ride, hiking, or working out, but I had hoped to incorporate them into one device. Unfortunately, the 6X Pro doesn't do some of the basic tasks that I really want wrist access to, and I'll be sending it back.

My gear watch just gave me my final reminder to do my morning exercises, so I'll wrap up my review and get the Garmin watch to the UPS store later today.

title: Service beyond expectations
text:
My Samsung Gear Active 2 smartwatch bricked after a little over a year. The manufacturer's warranty was expired by a couple months and so they would not help me at all with replacement. I bought an Asurion protection plan because I'm getting too used to my devices breaking just outside of warranty.

I thought Asurion would have hidden charges, logistical hoops to jump through, and other garbage that I have experienced in the past with insurance plans. Not this! I did not have much luck with the mobile app, so I called a support representative and they were able to get me set up with minimal confusion or hassle and then they got me a replacement device at no extra charge to me. I admit I had low expectations making the purchase, but in the end I was so pleased that I told family and friends about it. Pretty strange to have such a good experience with such low initial expectations.

title: Beautiful but not for me. Consider the Galaxy Wato
text:
This is just not a great watch. Very limited apps and faces available. Bought refurbished and it had several deep scratches on the bezel. Looked too deep to polish out even though it’s obvious an attempt was made. They did the best with what they had.
I bought this piece to complete my collection of Samsung watches. I own 1 of every watch Samsung has sold to the public.
This is a beautiful watch, no doubt.
Don’t expect much in the way of apps and sleep tracking. I doubt there will be much effort put into apps a faces for this watch, but if you want a beautiful watch at a great price by all means buy this watch. Just temper your expectations when it comes to add on’s down the road.
I returned it simply because the battery on this return would not got 8 hours with it dimmed down as far as I could and still see.
So, I still have this one hole in my collection. Maybe I’ll try again and just buy one new. I’ve been spoiled by the Samsung Galaxy Watch. Consider the Galaxy Watch.

Take Care!!!

title: Usable for basic functions
text: This watch is usable for basic  functions but if you are buying this for health purposes I would look elsewhere. I believe the blood pressure monitor is unreliable and the blood oxygen is at least three points higher then my blood oxygen meter. This is  the reason I bought this watch. title: Good watch  with a weird glitch - Poor Tech Support
text:
I was looking for a smartwatch to replace my older one. I upgraded from a Garmin Vivosmart. I had narrowed the search down to the Asus Zenwatch 2 WI501Q, the Motorola Moto 360 2nd, or the Samsung Gear S2. On paper I selected the Asus, but when I looked at it in a store, it was just too big. I’m not a small guy by any means, but that watch just looked unwieldy on my wrist, so I selected my second choice the Samsung Gear S2 Classic.

I have worn the watch for a month now. Here is my review.

PROs
=====
- Very nice appearance – looks like a classy watch
- Excellent display – vibrant, easy to read in all lighting conditions, including direct sunlight
- Mostly intuitive interface, even if the rotating bezel ring is a little touchy and redundant
- The Bluetooth / WiFi connectivity works very well

CONs
- Samsung Technical support is not knowledgeable about its own product
- Curiously, the Screen Time-out does not work when the Wake-up Gesture is enabled (very frustrating)

Let’s get the CONs out of the way. First, I have to tell you I originally bought this watch at BestBuy, but returned it after chatting online with Samsung Technical Support for 2 hours, during which time they diagnosed the watch as being defective. I returned the watch to BestBuy for a full refund, and then bought the identical replacement watch from Amazon. The replacement watch had exactly the same problem – My Samsung Gear S2 would not alert me when a call came into my Samsung Galaxy S7 cell phone. This time I called Samsung, and spoke with Tech Support for over an hour, and explained the history, and it is unlikely the problem is in the watch. The Tech Support technician had me try all sorts of things, including rebooting the watch with factory default settings, which I had already done.

After I had Tech Support on the line for an hour, who was by then out of ideas, I independently came across a well-hidden system setting in the Samsung Notifications area called “Incoming Calls”. The Samsung Tech support agent I was speaking with told me he had never heard of this setting. I selected this setting ON, and suddenly the watch recognized all incoming phone calls. It seems to me this is a fundamentally basic configuration element of a Samsung device – and TWO Tech Support agents did not know about it. I really have to deduct a full star from Samsung for that one.

The second problem I am having is with the Wake-up Gesture feature. It’s concept is quite convenient, and not unique among smart watches. If the watch senses you have moved your arm to look at the watch face, it turns on the display, so you do not need to press a button with your other hand. The problem is, this feature overrides the Time-Out setting. If I set the Time-Out to 30 seconds, say, you would think the watch, having detected I am looking at it, would activate the display for a minimum of 30 seconds, and then wait until its gesture logic told it I am not looking at it anymore. But it does not do that. The Wake-up Gesture ignores the Time-Out setting, and as soon as the watch senses you are not looking at the face anymore, which could be the result of a small twitch of your wrist, the display goes blank, sometimes only a second or two after activating. It is very frustrating, because too often the display does not stay on long enough to read even the first line or two of an incoming message. The only work-around is to disable the Wake-up Gesture, which now forces me to use my other hand to press the side button to activate the display. I find this less annoying than the display going dark prematurely, and then I have to use my other hand anyway.

It might be my larger fingers, but I have trouble using the rotating bezel. I find often my fingertip drags along the face of the watch as I rotate the bezel, causing unpredictable input to the watch. Thankfully the swipemotion across the watch face accomplishes pretty much anything the Bezel can, so I do not find the bezel issue too frustrating.

With those annoyances aside, I find the watch invaluable. It is not an understatement to say this watch has changed my work day. When calls, texts, and messages arrive, I look at the watch, and then decide whether I want reply with a canned message (such as “Busy – text in 5 minutes”), compose a more detailed message on my watch using the excellent speech recognition or the tiny touch keyboard, which works better than I thought it would. Of course, I can always pull out my phone.

The Bluetooth / WiFi feature is awesome. I can leave my phone in my jacket, and be in a meeting on another floor, and my watch still alerts me any time a call or message comes in to my phone. I used to pull my phone several times an hour for to check messges and apps – now maybe two or three times a day. I completely silence my phone – no ring tone. My watch surreptitiously notifies me when anything happens, and even in meetings, people do not seem to notice I am checking an incoming message when I glance at my watch after it vibrates.

Because this watch does not have a speaker, you cannot use it as a phone, the way you can with the iWatch. My wife has the iWatch, and she both loves and hates it. If she is in a meeting, she can’t really answer the call, because then everyone in the meeting will hear the call on her watch speakerphone. But she can make calls without pulling out her phone when she is in a more private place. In my case, I can answer the call from my watch, and then retrieve the phone before giving the caller too much pause to worry. It’s a trade-off I am happy to live with.

I like the music player remote control on the watch. I use it more than the music controls on the phone itself, because I don’t have to unlock the phone interface to pause or skip the song.

I wanted an analog display that also displays the time in a smaller digital display, and the date. I found the Speedo watch face meets my liking.

I charge my cell phone every night by my bedside table, so by habit I also charge the watch every night too. The charge has never fallen below 50%.

Overall I am very happy with my watch. For me, it strikes the best balance between style, features, and performance. Samsung has to fix the Screen Time-out / Wake-up Gesture issue and improve their technical support before I would give this watch five stars.

title: NOT ok; definitely NOT worth the price
text:
UPDATED REVIEW 3/20/19:
Now that I've lived with these earbuds for almost 2 months, I realize that my original review was too hasty, and far too generous. These Samsung Gear IconX earbuds are so frustrating. At least every other day they are unusable due to a poor connection; they keep cutting out until I finally give up and just finish my workouts without them; the other half of the time they work just fine, but that's not nearly reliable enough. I've never had such connectivity problems with my Bose, or even with my ultra-cheap MPOW headphones.

Also, frequently one side or the other stops working during my workouts; sometimes a side will stop playing any sound but will keep responding to touch inputs; other times one side will stop responding to touch inputs, but will keep playing sound. Additionally, as I've noticed many other people complaining about online, the R side gradually faded out over the course of two workouts, to the point that I could no longer hear any sound coming from it. My searches online indicated that I needed to clean the little screen with alcohol, which I did. The R earbud started working fine again for a few hours, but then faded out again. Shortly thereafter, the L earbud faded down to nothing; I cleaned that one with alcohol and it worked fine again . . . for a few hours . . . before fading out again. That's totally unacceptable.

I find these Samsung Gear IconX earbuds to be borderline unusable. Samsung clearly doesn't know what it's doing, and should not have released these earbuds with such monumental problems. The only problem I have left is figuring out how/if I can return them.

ORIGINAL REVIEW:
The Samsung Gear IconX totally wireless headphones are ok overall, though very unfriendly to iPhones. They were a bit of a pain to connect to my iPhone, they don’t have an iPhone app to manage the headphones, and you can’t use certain features if you’re using an iPhone (like their variable ambient noise feature, or the ability to download music into the earbuds themselves). Virtually everybody else in the market makes their headphones just as easy to use on an iPhone as on an Android phone. I don’t know why Samsung decided to make these IconX headphones less attractive for iPhone users. It’s not like anybody is actually going to switch from the iPhone to a Galaxy phone just to be able to use more features with the IconX’s.

While I can only assume that it's easy to set up these headphones on an Android phone, the process was a bit of a struggle with the iPhone. Unlike the 15 other pairs of wireless headphones that I’ve had, which all linked to the iPhone quickly and seamlessly, it took several tries connecting/disconnecting/forgetting/connecting/disconnecting/forgetting/connecting the IconX’s bluetooth to my iPhone. I also had to keep taking the IconX’s in and out of their case during the process to try to get them to connect, and had to charge the case first before I was able to connect them.

One odd quirk about the IconX’s is that, now that they’re connected, they never disconnect from my iPhone! Whenever I put the earbuds back in their case, they remain connected. At least, the battery indicator on my iPhone keeps monitoring their charge level. They’ll stay "connected" to my iPhone in this manner for days, even though I’m not using them. One possible consequence of this quirk is that they seem to lose their charge faster. For instance, I put them in their little case on Sunday afternoon and my iPhone said that the IconX’s had 40% of their charge left. I didn’t use them again until Wednesday morning, 65 hours later, and at that time they only lasted for 45 minutes before dying. They should have gone a lot longer if they, indeed, had 40% of their charge left when I put them away on Sunday. All of my other wireless headphones disconnected from my iPhone’s bluetooth when I put them back in their case or plugged them in to charge. I don’t know what Samsung was thinking.

The sound quality of the IconX’s is fairly average. Frankly, I expected more from headphones that cost $160. They pale in comparison to the sound quality of the Bose Soundsport Free earbuds (though the Bose have their own glaring problem, unfortunately; see below). While the IconX’s certainly aren’t good enough that I would listen to music on them at home, I do feel like they are adequate for the gym, which is the only place I use earbuds.

What I do like about the IconX’s is how they fit. A lot of earphones don’t fit my earholes very well for some reason (e.g. RHA’s earbuds), but these IconX's seat in my ears quite nicely. As a result of their proper fit, they block out a good bit of ambient noise, which help them maintain a decent sound quality when you’re in a noisy environment, like the gym. This was the fatal flaw of the Bose Soundsport Free earbuds; they let in so much ambient noise that they sounded just awful in the gym; the Bose’s excellent sound quality (in a quiet environment) was completely lost as they were overwhelmed by ambient noise. Turning back to the IconX’s, one other feature that I like in terms of their fit is the little wings that tuck into the external part of your ears; the wings are fairly comfortable and keep the IconX’s in very securely (like the Bose; but very unlike the RHAs, which kept falling out of my ears onto the ground no matter which of their many eartips I tried).

The IconX’s connectivity is also decent most of the time . . . not stellar, but again, usually good enough for the gym. You’ll rarely hear the sound ping-pong back and forth between the L and R earbuds, as is a problem with some other brands (I’m looking at you, RHA!), though it does happen more on some days than on others (they've actually been terrible the past 2 days for me). My $20 MPOW earbuds have a greater range than the IconX’s before they start cutting out, but again, I think that the range of the IconX’s is good enough for most purposes. I do, however, find that the IconX’s suffer more from interference than the Bose; for example, sometimes the IconX’s don’t like it when somebody walks between me and my iPhone (which is only 5 ft away from me) or when my head is facing the wrong way. That’s annoying, but it doesn’t happen too often.

One of my other gripes about the IconX’s is their touchpads. Ugh! They are very finicky, and rarely do what I’m trying to tell them to do. They are especially problematic when I’m trying to increase or decrease the volume with a swipe up or down. Most of the time when I’m trying to change the volume they pause, then they play, then they pause, then they jump to the next song, . . . It is very annoying. I usually get frustrated and just dig my iPhone out of my backpack to change the volume. I have similar problems when trying to get the IconX’s to pause, play, skip songs, go back a song, . . . they usually do something else instead. Until they perfect these touchpads, I wish Samsung would go back to physical buttons. My other gripe about the touchpads is how I often accidentally touch them with my bicep, or even a gym towel when wiping sweat off of my face, and it pauses the audio. This happens most often during certain tricep and abdominal exercises, and when I’m doing cardio.

One more gripe before I go: the two lights on their case are entirely uninformative. The only time I’ve ever seen them light up green are when the case is plugged into a power source and is fully charged. When it’s not plugged in, even though they’re almost fully charged, both lights light up red. When they are down to half of their charge, both light up red. When they are almost on empty, both light up red. Why on earth would Samsung not have them go from green to yellow to red? Better yet, why not do what so many other manufacturers have done so effectively and have a series of lights that show you how much charge is left? I don’t get it.

The moral of my very long-winded story is that the Samsung Gear IconX earbuds are ok. The sound quality is ok, their connectivity is ok, and their fit is pretty good. I wouldn’t advise getting them unless they’re on sale, and I’d make sure to buy them from a vendor that will allow you to return them easily in case you don’t like them. Also, I think they’re better for Android users than they are for iPhone users. I’m still on the fence about whether to keep them.

One possible consequence of this quirk is that they seem to lose their charge faster. For instance, I put them in their little case on Sunday afternoon and my iPhone said that the IconX’s had 40% of their charge left. I didn’t use them again until Wednesday morning, 65 hours later, and at that time they only lasted for 45 minutes before dying. They should have gone a lot longer if they, indeed, had 40% of their charge left when I put them away on Sunday. All of my other wireless headphones disconnected from my iPhone’s bluetooth when I put them back in their case or plugged them in to charge. I don’t know what Samsung was thinking.

The sound quality of the IconX’s is fairly average. Frankly, I expected more from headphones that cost $160. They pale in comparison to the sound quality of the Bose Soundsport Free earbuds (though the Bose have their own glaring problem, unfortunately; see below). While the IconX’s certainly aren’t good enough that I would listen to music on them at home, I do feel like they are adequate for the gym, which is the only place I use earbuds.

What I do like about the IconX’s is how they fit. A lot of earphones don’t fit my earholes very well for some reason (e.g. RHA’s earbuds), but these IconX's seat in my ears quite nicely. As a result of their proper fit, they block out a good bit of ambient noise, which help them maintain a decent sound quality when you’re in a noisy environment, like the gym. This was the fatal flaw of the Bose Soundsport Free earbuds; they let in so much ambient noise that they sounded just awful in the gym; the Bose’s excellent sound quality (in a quiet environment) was completely lost as they were overwhelmed by ambient noise. Turning back to the IconX’s, one other feature that I like in terms of their fit is the little wings that tuck into the external part of your ears; the wings are fairly comfortable and keep the IconX’s in very securely (like the Bose; but very unlike the RHAs, which kept falling out of my ears onto the ground no matter which of their many eartips I tried).

The IconX’s connectivity is also decent most of the time . . . not stellar, but again, usually good enough for the gym. You’ll rarely hear the sound ping-pong back and forth between the L and R earbuds, as is a problem with some other brands (I’m looking at you, RHA!), though it does happen more on some days than on others (they've actually been terrible the past 2 days for me). My $20 MPOW earbuds have a greater range than the IconX’s before they start cutting out, but again, I think that the range of the IconX’s is good enough for most purposes. I do, however, find that the IconX’s suffer more from interference than the Bose; for example, sometimes the IconX’s don’t like it when somebody walks between me and my iPhone (which is only 5 ft away from me) or when my head is facing the wrong way. That’s annoying, but it doesn’t happen too often.

One of my other gripes about the IconX’s is their touchpads. Ugh! They are very finicky, and rarely do what I’m trying to tell them to do. They are especially problematic when I’m trying to increase or decrease the volume with a swipe up or down. Most of the time when I’m trying to change the volume they pause, then they play, then they pause, then they jump to the next song, . . . It is very annoying. I usually get frustrated and just dig my iPhone out of my backpack to change the volume. I have similar problems when trying to get the IconX’s to pause, play, skip songs, go back a song, . . . they usually do something else instead. Until they perfect these touchpads, I wish Samsung would go back to physical buttons. My other gripe about the touchpads is how I often accidentally touch them with my bicep, or even a gym towel when wiping sweat off of my face, and it pauses the audio. This happens most often during certain tricep and abdominal exercises, and when I’m doing cardio.

One more gripe before I go: the two lights on their case are entirely uninformative. The only time I’ve ever seen them light up green are when the case is plugged into a power source and is fully charged. When it’s not plugged in, even though they’re almost fully charged, both lights light up red. When they are down to half of their charge, both light up red. When they are almost on empty, both light up red. Why on earth would Samsung not have them go from green to yellow to red? Better yet, why not do what so many other manufacturers have done so effectively and have a series of lights that show you how much charge is left? I don’t get it.

The moral of my very long-winded story is that the Samsung Gear IconX earbuds are ok. The sound quality is ok, their connectivity is ok, and their fit is pretty good. I wouldn’t advise getting them unless they’re on sale, and I’d make sure to buy them from a vendor that will allow you to return them easily in case you don’t like them. Also, I think they’re better for Android users than they are for iPhone users. I’m still on the fence about whether to keep them.

title: Disappointing
text: I bought this watch over other models for the BP monitoring. What they DON'T tell you is that the monitoring only works if you also have a Galaxy phone and if you opt into a study allowing a university to collect your data. Samsung should do a better job of communicating the fine print or stop advertising the BP monitoring. It otherwise seems to be a decent watch, although step counts are inaccurate. Would rate the watch higher if the monitoring works, but if you don't care about that, not a bad choice. Oh, the Samsung Gear app is also pretty crappy. It is unintuitive and clunky. While that is not a factor in the watch rating, it is worth mentioning as the watch doesn't work without the app.

title: Don't buy an Amazon renewed product without Asurion!
text: I had many problems with the renewed Samsung Galaxy Watch I purchased. I called Asurion and they made sending the watch to them very easy (free shipping!). Unfortunately, when they returned it to me I still had all of the same issues. Going over the watch quickly it would seem fine unless you wore it for a day. Anyway, I contacted them again and again, they were very professional. When they saw the watch was beyond repair they gave me a gift card for my entire purchase price (see screenshot). I don't think I'll buy another Amazon renewed product again, but I will buy extended protection from Asurion.
