title: First died within 2 hours and second one seems very different
text: So I purchased this watch on Monday and it arrived on time (5:50 AM) the next morning. After charging completely for 3 hours, I start setting up. After I had everything set the way I wanted, it went into this weird loop and I couldn't do anything with it. I returned it and reluctantly ordered another, same watch, different box, different instructions, and different app. I thought maybe this was a newer version, the issue here is I can communicate on the online service because it's all in Chinese and there are less features on the new watch. Only a handful of watch face designs to choose from and I don't care for any of them. Steps don't seem to be accurate and other than the great look of it, the rest is quite disappointing. I suppose it's not meant to be with an off brand smart watch for me and I don't want one bad enough to purchase a Samsung watch. Beware folks, it's not what these 5 star reviewers are saying. They are either plants or were fortunate enough to get one of the few decent watches this company made.

I ordered a Samsung charger for the watch and threw the junk charger that came with this watch away. If you are having battery life issues with refurbished watch first confirm your charger is not an aftermarket one (more than likely it is). Seller shouldn't be providing Galaxy watches with aftermarket chargers that literally kill your watch battery life.

title: Stylish watch with serious drawbacks.
text:
[[VIDEOID:618dd650b9392d1ff527c5db377aba67]] I loved this watch, and then I hated it.

LOVED: *wearing* the watch, being able to *glance* at time and other information (agenda, pulse, etc.), having *flashlight* handy, receiving sms/calls.

HATED: interacting with unresponsive interface, seeing the battery drop below 20% by end of day, being offered to turn on power saving mode, which would kill the always-on display and make the watch even slower and less useful.

Pros:
+ Quality materials, and solid construction.
+ Comfortable to wear.
+ Plenty of watchfaces to choose from.
+ The screen is high quality, and adapts to ambient lighting well.

Cons:
- Large thickness. I've been hitting doors and things with it.
- No notification of any kind when Bluetooth connection to phone is lost?! If you move away from your phone - the watch won't tell you. So, you'll be missing calls and notifications.
- Paring over Bluetooth means that you can't use your phone in car + with watch at the same time.
- No WiFi status anywhere?!
- No wireless/Qi charging?!
- Many available apps are gimmicky and too simplistic. For example, calendar just shows you summary, but will not let you cancel/respond to a meeting.
- Battery life is sad... may not even last a full day. Charging overnight is a must. The claim of battery lasting 2 days is bogus.
- Painfully slow performance... could be expected from a $50 watch, but not in the $200-300 price range. Even simple built-in interfaces (upper system drawer, and lower notification drawer) are jerky and slow to react. Sometimes a couple seconds pass before something happens on screen. Instead of sliding in and out - the upper/lower panels just jump in/out unpredictably. Transitions between different screens/apps are slow too - you will stare at black screen (instead of a watchface) too often. Also, the watchfaces that show "seconds" are lagging and "seconds" jump around crazy-fast after you wake up the screen. Horrible and why?!

Overall, I'm disappointed. So much potential went to waste. Maybe I should try Samsung Gear S3 instead.

UPDATE: Running watch for a couple days without restarting - it crapped out and became completely unresponsive. I couldn't even unlock it. It was buzzing and showing strange things on display (see attached image). So, yes, this is definitely a piece of turd.

title: Love the Samsung watch, but beware of false advertising from this seller!! Not US model!
text:
Unfortunately, my review is probably too late to help anyone else. What I have now learned is too late to help myself. This seller states in the title that you are purchasing a US version watch. 2 years later, I am finding out that is not what I got.
I loved my smartwatch; the look, how easy it was to use, the many options and settings.. The battery needed to be recharged daily, but I could plug it in while I got ready in the morning and it would be 100% an hour later when I left for work. It worked great like that for 2 years until the battery started draining really fast. It would say full and report I had 1 day and 5 hrs of usage at my current settings, only to be almost dead 9 hours later. I tried changing to power saving settings but no luck. So I gave in and sent the watch back to Samsung for hopefully a battery replacement. Theres were the problems arose.
After 2 weeks of the watch being at Samsung and no response from them, I kept calling and being told my repair would be rushed as it shouldn't take that long. Finally on my third call, I was told that my watch is actually an international model and therefore the repair will take longer- and the updated charges have doubled. I called Amazon after finding out but it was 2 years and too late to do anything. The model number is not what is listed on the order and it is not the promised US version. Now that false advertising is sticking me with a service charge almost as much as the cost of the watch when I purchased it. I am so disappointed, but didn't think to check when the watch arrived. I am posting a review that maybe I can save someone else from paying for one thing but being sold another from this seller. The model number listed as advertised was SM-R830NZSAXAR and specified US model. This is false!! The model I received is SM-R830NZSATPA which according to Samsung is international and voids some of their warranties and incurs higher repair costs and time.

title: Samsung lies about their warranty and water resistance!
text: DO NOT BUY THIS WATCH. Samsung does not stand behind their warranty! I have owned a Samsung smart watch since the first generation was released years ago and never ever had an issue. My children bought this new one 9 months ago as a birthday gift to me. The past week it became stuck in an update/reboot loop. I contacted customer service and was advised to send the watch in for servicing. After the watch arrived, Samsung sent me a $220.00 bill for repair of the watch, due to my "warranty being voided". I contacted customer service for further explanation as to why my warranty was voided after only owning it for 9 months. Customer service stated that there had been evidence of previous water damage to the mother board of the watch. Which in turn caused my warranty to be voided. I am so frustrated. For a watch to promote "swim mode, water resistance and states it is submersible for up to 50 meters" and then void my warranty for water damage is just mind boggling to me. I have never swam with my watch or submerged it in water. I have on occasion worn in the shower. I can only remember on one instance where water was detected within the watch,  and it immediately went into an water expelling mode-which squirted all the water out of the watch. It would be cheaper for me to buy a brand new watch vs repair. But I will never buy another Samsung watch after this experience. Hope this saves someone else the heartache. I loved this watch and it makes me incredibly sad, but I will be switching to Garmin products now.

title: Huge regrets
text:
This is the second Samsung Gear watch that I've bought and hated. I love Fitbits, but I'm sick of spending the money just for them to stop working within 12-18 months, so I went back to Samsung. All I really wanted was a fitness tracker, which this can do. I don't need all the extra stuff, I have a phone for everything else. I'm 100% Team Samsung, but these watches are awful. Even with every single thing but the actual clock turned off, I get maybe a day and a half of battery life. I turn on the Bluetooth for maybe 2-3 minutes a day to sync my data. That's it. If you charge it before bed and put it on, you lose 15% of the battery just sleeping WITH NO GPS, BLUETOOTH, ALWAYS ON DISPLAY, OR WIFI ON. That's unacceptable.
The Samsung Health app is garbage, too. It's not as intuitive or fluent as Fitbit and doesn't sync quickly at all. The steps are way off, too. I walked in the treadmill for an hour twice last week and it only caught 37 minutes the first time and 28 the second time, while only logging about 3500 steps each time, which isn't even close to 4 miles worth of steps.
It's a nice looking watch and has a lot of features if you don't mind charging it daily, which means you'll miss out on things while it's always charging.
All in all I'm stuck with another $250 paper weight and am in the search for another fitness tracker.

title: Samsung fail
text:
"Charger Cradle Charging Dock" is a misleading name. This charger is very small and too easy to lose. I purchased a separate charger, not realizing the Samsung Gear Fit comes with the exact same ridiculously small charger and a power cord. For the 2 weeks I kept my Gear Fit, I left the charger that came with it plugged in to the power cord so as not to lose it. At one point, I did lose track of this extra charger which was still in the plastic bag it came in. The charger is also a bit difficult to unplug from the Gear Fit after it's been charged. I would have preferred a docking station-type charger. I am returning this extra charger along with the Gear Fit. Just not happy with the whole thing.
[[ASIN:B00KB1BQXS Charger Cradle Charging Dock for Samsung Gear fit Smart Watch Black]]

title: Where is my Pebble Time 2 with HRM?!
text:
Smartwatches have had a difficult journey --especially in 2016. Expected sales have been half of projected, and when billions of revenue dollars and market size are the object of investors this does not bode well. This was made all the more real by apple’s own disappointing apple watch sale figures, and even more depressing Fitbit’s poor fiscal year and necessary consolidation with Pebble (Question: is being bought a symbol of growth or rescue?).

Admittedly, watches in general have become less of a necessity with certain younger demographics tied to their cell phones and less conscious of what is on their wrist, whether utilitarian, nerdy, or fashion trendy. Of course, when we can put in our “smart contact lenses” and experience as much “overlay”, interaction, and connectivity we might desire, without looking down at anything, in the privacy of our own world and outside of the transitory “google glass” solution, the beginning of the end of smartphones is inevitable! I, for one, look forward to this Terminator style overlay, but with less red coloration, and more “Minority Report” interactivity.

So the smart watch industry continues to try to make inroads into the same demographic that might not want a watch, but would want a device that gives an active, youthful, and free feeling—more dynamic less utilitarian. “Active” is the sense that actual activity is truly measured and quantified. “Free feeling” in that you do not need to carry around your phone everywhere you go or more simply, that you do not need to look at it to acknowledge messages or phone calls (voice, how quaint!). For apple, the do it all smart phone substitute is their watch. The apple watch is nice. The screen is bright, application rich, and reportedly provides good accuracy during activities. I would not know, I do not have one. I do not want one until the battery life improves significantly. What good is an activity watch that you cannot wear continuously for at least 24 hours to track activity and sleep? That, and the price… oh lordy, the price of entry is high, even for the base models.

Enough commentary! For some background, I have/had a number of these devices.

Way back in the old days, I had a Polar watch with a chest band. These devices were (and continue to be) the go to items for accurate HRM during activity. Long on battery life, but way short on apps and connectivity in those days.

Then there was an Epson product that seemed out of their normal printer based business model. It was a basic band providing a couple of lights showing and tracking activity with a heart rate monitor (hereafter, HRM) and requiring connection to your phone for exercise tracking. It provided inconsistent HR data during certain activities (for me, stationary bike riding showed wildly inaccurate measurements), but did seem to provide interesting sleep information.

Then, a basic and early Fitbit band, without HRM, which worked OK for tracking but in the end I needed more display—why wear a watch and a band with no HRM? The variable reviews for the higher end HRM Fitbit did not prompt me to purchase one, along with a lack of an app universe and less than exciting displays.

That led to a Pebble. Notably a “Pebble Time” purchased during a kickstarter campaign. The Pebble Time has an always on color display, that only looks better outside in bright sunlight (less so inside), has pretty good 3-5 days of battery life, a great app and developer space, with acceptable activity tracking (minus HRM) and OK looks. If Pebble had lasted and carried through with their kickstarter project to provide a model like this with HRM, I would have purchased one. But no, about the same time that Pebble was supposed to carry through with that product, Fitbit bought them up –but not for their hardware acumen, but to give Fitbit a necessary app and developer community. Fitbit has not provided any solid hints they will provide a similar device to the one that Pebble did not deliver. (I do love my Pebble, and do know that the Pebble 2 with HRM is probably a great watch—but alas, no color? And no future?!).

That finally brings me to what I have realized is most important to me in any of these devices. I want a clear, colorful, and configurable display. The variety and changeability of watch faces was my initial and continuous draw to any of these watches. It needs to be accurate, and I do want HRM. It needs a proper interface on a larger screen for control and setup. It does not need to always be on, but it does need to come on when I need it. Being a Pebble fan, touchscreens were less important, but that may be changing since I got the Samsung Fit2. (Finally, he is talking about the Samsung!?).

The Samsung Gear devices, and notably the Fit2 had been on my list. I was waiting for Gear to provide apple iOS support outside of the android phones AND acceptable battery life. I was already familiar with Samsung’s gear community and my primary need for configurable watch faces. Samsung checked off iOS support in February 2017 (finally!) providing a basic app for iPhone. I started watching for Amazon Warehouse open box deals since I did not feel like spending full price on yet another disappointment. When a 50% off deal showed up (with acceptable condition notes), I jumped.

I have had the Fit2 for about a week now. Maybe not a lot of time to comment, but still:
What I like:
*Super bright display under most conditions (see below)
*Enough available watch faces to make me happy
*Screen that does seem to come on when I want it
*Good accuracy for HRM, in general and during exercise compared to my exercise bike monitor.
*The iOS app works (see below)
*The Samsung developer app is pretty nice for watch face and apps (but see below)
*I like having a touchscreen!
*The on watch touch and button based interface is mostly intuitive.

What I do not like:
*The Samsung iOS app is very basic and needs much work compared to their own android based app (and compared to the Pebble OS app).
**Additionally the iOS app is not fully implemented, not providing expected functionality across the entire Samsung GearApp marketplace. Looking for Broda or Infinity watch faces? No access provided, and the providers say directly that Samsung must provide that full access? That is very poor implementation. Come on Samsung, allow iOS users access to the full market place.
*In same area, the health tracking application needs more work with more detailed and configurable (maybe downloadable) datasets, along with potential interactivity with other apps like “my fitness pal”.
*The display is harder to read in direct sunlight, and the main display cannot be set to “always on”. (You can see an always on default basic hour:min lower power display if you choose). I need to be able to time certain operations for short periods of time over a minute but less than 10 minutes with my main face, and without flicking my wrist to bring my watch back up.
*Needs a wider app space, with yet more watch faces AND the ability for a user to create their own watch faces in the developer app without needing to register for the Samsung marketplace and developer space (I just want to create watch faces and upload them to my watch without putting them up on the Samsung GalaxyApps seller space. And so far, Samsung has been non-responsive for my own submissions—stalled in “registration” status. What gives?).

What I am neutral on:
*The looks. It is less watch and more sports band.
*Battery life is average, with my use, at best 2-3 days.
*Perceived durability. Everything seems solid so far, but I am wary based on surrounding reviews.

What I would recommend:
Buy one—even at the retail price, it seems a good value! Even more so if you can find an acceptable open box from Amazon Warehouse to try. You may like it, especially if you are looking for an alternative to Fitbit or apple devices.
Also buy a screen protector with your Fit2 like [[ASIN:B01G4HI3ZQ Samsung Gear Fit2 Screen Protector, IQ Shield LiQuidSkin (6-Pack) Full Coverage Screen Protector for Samsung Gear Fit2 (Gear Fit 2) HD Clear Anti-Bubble Film - with]]...There are a number of examples of cracked screens out there--protect your watch from the beginning.

What do I really want? My color Pebble Time 2 with HRM! Oh well. 4/5.

*In same area, the health tracking application needs more work with more detailed and configurable (maybe downloadable) datasets, along with potential interactivity with other apps like “my fitness pal”.
*The display is harder to read in direct sunlight, and the main display cannot be set to “always on”. (You can see an always on default basic hour:min lower power display if you choose). I need to be able to time certain operations for short periods of time over a minute but less than 10 minutes with my main face, and without flicking my wrist to bring my watch back up.
*Needs a wider app space, with yet more watch faces AND the ability for a user to create their own watch faces in the developer app without needing to register for the Samsung marketplace and developer space (I just want to create watch faces and upload them to my watch without putting them up on the Samsung GalaxyApps seller space. And so far, Samsung has been non-responsive for my own submissions—stalled in “registration” status. What gives?).

What I am neutral on:
*The looks. It is less watch and more sports band.
*Battery life is average, with my use, at best 2-3 days.
*Perceived durability. Everything seems solid so far, but I am wary based on surrounding reviews.

What I would recommend:
Buy one—even at the retail price, it seems a good value! Even more so if you can find an acceptable open box from Amazon Warehouse to try. You may like it, especially if you are looking for an alternative to Fitbit or apple devices.
Also buy a screen protector with your Fit2 like [[ASIN:B01G4HI3ZQ Samsung Gear Fit2 Screen Protector, IQ Shield LiQuidSkin (6-Pack) Full Coverage Screen Protector for Samsung Gear Fit2 (Gear Fit 2) HD Clear Anti-Bubble Film - with]]...There are a number of examples of cracked screens out there--protect your watch from the beginning.

What do I really want? My color Pebble Time 2 with HRM! Oh well. 4/5.

title: Buy from Samsung not from this seller!

title: My Galaxy Active watch died for the third time in less than a year and a half.
text: I got my Galaxy Active through Best Buy, not amazon. It was still under manufacturer warranty so I was able to get it repaired twice through the local samsung repair department. Now it is out of warranty, and I did not purchase the expensive extended warranty so I guess I am out of luck. I enjoyed my Active watch while it was operative but am disillusioned with the Active watch and will look elsewhere if I decide to get another activity tracker smart watch

title: Very nice try but....
text: Nice design, style, band but functionality not close to my Gear 3 Frontier.  I really wanted to like this more than my Frontier, really.  Out the box its sexy, clean and big like I wanted.  Under the hood after Google sync, its just not as functional as my Samsung, and due to Samsung battery life I was rooting for TicWatch.  The screen is nice but not as nice as Gear 3.  Functionally it does not have the interface or depth to compare to Samsung Gear yet.  Its interface feels not as connected as Samsung Gear 3 Frontier.  Maybe after a few versions it can compete or maybe forge its own "30 day usage" niche.  For me, smartwatches are more about the "smarts" than "30 day usage", otherwise I'd wear my regular watch.  But hey, good attempt and very nice design.  I love the silver faux dial face and black silicon/leather watch band!!!  Serious connection issues!!!!

title: Magnificent with a few frustrations
text:
These Sony wireless ear buds are my favorite tech item. I absolutely love so much about them from the sound quality to the compactness to the battery life. But they are flawed enough that I do not think they deserve five stars. 9 out of 10 if it was possible.

How can this be? Read on.

(Programming note: I tested these headphones with a useful Spotify playlist called "Songs to Test Headphones With" -- I also listened to my regular music for months and months with these headphones because I enjoy them so much and because it was so hard for me to write this review)

Sound quality:

Everything begins with the sound quality. The Sony WH1000XM3 over ear headphones have this magical, immersive sound that makes you rediscover music and luxuriate in sound like nothing I've experienced before, despite having listened to some very good mid-range headphones before. The problem with the WH1000XM3 headphones is the size. They are large over the ear headphones. That is what makes the WF-1000xM3 earbuds so incredible. Sony engineers have somehow managed to get 85% of the sound quality of the WH1000xM3 headphones into small ear buds.

If sound quality is important to you and you need earbuds you can stuff in a pocket, these earbuds are absolutely exceptional. If you travel everywhere with a bag and this is not an issue, consider the WH1000xM3 over ear phones.

There is absolutely no comparison to lesser earbuds like the Samsung Gear Icon X--the Icon X sound like scratchy AM radio after listening to music on the Sony earbuds.

Bluetooth phone calls:

The quality of bluetooth calls leaves a lot to be desired. In a quiet place, sure its fine. But in any kind of noisy place it is almost impossible to have a call. The aforementioned Gear Icon X do a better job. Sony seems to struggle with bluetooth generally.

Bluetooth connection:

The earbuds independently connect to bluetooth, which is great, but I suspect they have some issues in terms of bandwidth/transmission, because they seem to work flawlessly in my home but out and about they frequently drop. Perhaps a more technically minded person has more insights.

Battery life:

Absolutely stunning. Not only have I never run out of charge on the battery case despite being tremendously lazy about charging it, but I've never run out of charge on the buds. This would doubtless change if I wore them on an international flight, but for ordinary daily use, you'll be hard pressed to have battery issues.

Fit and comfort: I don't love the fit of these buds. They are a little bigger than the typical earbud and always feel a little awkward when you are walking around. It's important to find a positive fit for both noise cancelling and sound quality, so this may take you a little work. They also are not great earbuds to work out in as they don't tolerate sweat well. That said: they are without a doubt the most comfortable earbuds for long duration listening I've ever used. I even fall asleep wearing these earbuds and wake up hours later with no discomfort. Really exceptional.

Noise cancelling: Actually really fantastic BUT Sony has insisted on making the default settings less than full noise cancelling, maybe because they are worried about people walking around and getting run down by cars they don't hear. To take life into your own hands, you have to go into the Sony app and set everything to max. Once you do, the outside world is very effectively blocked out. Yes, you can still hear murmurs, but it knocks everything down twenty decibels. This is amazing for plane rides and other persistently noisy environments. The only problem is that people cannot see the earbuds in your ears and sometimes will end up yelling at you, frustrated that you cannot hear them.

About that app:

It's a little finicky but absolutely essential to gain control over your earbuds. If I wasn't able to turn off the touch controls on the side of the earbuds, they would be a 2 star product for me. But because I am able to go into the app and control this, the earbuds are much more useful.

Ditto some other quirks: if you pull one ear bud out while on a call, the other ear bud persists. If you pull one ear bud out while listening to music it pauses. You can change this in the app as well. I am not sure which way I prefer, but its nice to have the option.

You can also let the app analyze your ear shape for a supposedly better quality sound. I haven't gotten around to doing this.

If you drop one of your earbuds, Sony has your back by providing a blinking light. This light is only active if the earbud doesn't sense any pressure around it (ie its not in your fingers or your ear). This is pretty clever.

Missed opportunities: I do not know why manufacturers insist on putting controls on ear buds. I never use them in a positive fashion and I just end up mistakenly doing things while adjusting my earbuds. Instead, why not make the battery case a remote control? I would also love to see a special edition of the case decked out like a Sony Walkman. Wouldn't that be cool?

Overall, I think these earbuds are fantastic. You get truly exceptional sound quality--a balanced and immersive experience that sounds great for everything from classical music to hip hop and rock in between, truly hearing all the details of your music--in a package that fits into a jeans pocket. That's pretty amazing. Add to that very good noise cancelling and excellent battery life. True, the bluetooth calling leaves a lot to be desired and occasional bluetooth drops may frustrate you, but pound for pound, these earbuds are the champs.

Recommended!

title: Do not buy for Dexcom integration
text:
There's 3 of us here, fairly technically inclined. After about 6 hours between us trying to get Dexcom to show as a watchface on this we all gave up and I bought a Samsung. This was the cheapest Garmin that was on the list as being Dexcom compatible (on Garmin's site). We need no other features so I cannot speak on anything else about this watch. May be harsh to grade so low over one feature, but I don't think they should be able to advertise as Dexcom compatible if you need to be a scientist to get it set up.
My $20 knockoff can count my steps and push my notifications. I needed Dexcom compatibility. This ain't it.

*Dexcom is a continuous glucose monitor that gives real-time readings to diabetics.

title: Totally Broken OS/Features locked for Non-Samsung Phones
text:
Love the Watch so far. Having been a prior Apple guy, I hated the square derpy look of the apple watch. Also as small as an issue as it may seem, I hated that the apple watch didn't have an hourly chime. Also the Apple watch faces, although nice, weren't nearly as customizable as other brands. The Galaxy 4 classic checks most of the boxes for me. The display is fantastic. Functionality is pretty good with a non-Samsung phone, although kind of a D move by Samsung to restrict the EKG/Blood Pressure to Samsung only phones. There is no indication whatsoever by Samsung that some features are restricted to Samsung only phones. I discovered this only by reading obscure forums. Samsung openly advertises that this watch has EKG and Blood Pressure monitoring, but nowhere do they tell you you have to have a Samsung Phone. They should be sued. Come on Samsung you aren't apple, and for the price, you'll just push people to Fossil, especially when Gen 6 comes out. The thing I dislike most about the watch, are the bands. They are so cheapy!!!! It fits weird, feels weird, the band stays don't stay, and the band pushes the watch up off the wrist in a weird oval shape. 8 dollar watches at Walmart have better bands. Samsung either should've included no band at all, or charged an extra 10-20 bucks for better bands. My wife has a Galaxy Watch 3, and hers came with a nice leather band that looks and feels so much better. You would never guess that my watch is the newer, more expensive version. Order yourself a band before buying the watch to avoid disappointment, otherwise your first impression will leave you wanting to return the thing.

3 Week Update:
Still enjoying the watch enough although there is one thing that is really driving me nuts-non Samsung watch faces or watch face apps (such as Facer) do not synchronize well. For example the steps counters are always 500-1200 steps off; the heartbeat counter is always wrong, the weather information is always a day off (and sometimes wrong location). Even the time is wrong with the always on display (the seconds either freeze or show 00). Since I don't have have a Samsung phone (thus don't have access to the Samsung app store), this means if I want accurate information on my watch face, I am limited to the 15 or so (totally lame and ugly) faces that come stock with the watch. Seriously who wants a creepy cartoon emoji waiving and pointing at you? Or how about a face of cats as**ole? The watches that come stock are awful plain and simple. I continue to just use third party faces even with inaccurate information. But then I have to ask, what's the point of a smartwatch if the information being displayed is wrong? Two stars now Samsung/Google.

4 Week Update:
Knocked down to 1 star. The OS is just overall totally broken. The health tracking app is a complete joke. I've tried many times to use it for runs and walks but it continuusly pauses. I've turned off auto pause but it still doesn't register anything. For example today I went on a half mile walk. After I finished the walk, I checked my stats on the watch app where it said I walked 17 steps, at a distance of 0.00 miles, with average speed of 0.0 mph with a max speed of 3.1 mph... HOW DOES ANY OF THAT MAKE SENSE? Also whenever I'm driving, it constantly says "good job you did it!" Thinking I stood up or started a workout when I totally didnt. And again going back to my earlier issue, the watch face shows steps at one number, the watch app shows a second number, and Samsung Health app on the phone shows a third... The watch face says I'm at 102 heartbeats while the app says 109. This watch is totally useless for health tracking. It's totally useless for accurate for weather information, it's totally useless for accurate time keeping... About the only thing it does is look pretty and send notifications. For 379$ however, my Casio looks better, shows accurate information, and I don't have have to worry about battery life. Maybe the watch works better paired to a Samsung phone, but on non Samsung Android phones, nothing works right. Maybe a future update will rectify some of these issues, but these are major issues that should've been addressed before release. I recommend holding off on any Wear OS3 Watch until Samsung/Google can get all the kinks worked out. Seriously did they even Beta test the OS or just merge Google/FitBit/Samsung OSs together and hope for the best at launch?

3 Month Update:
Yep, way overpriced for a watch with locked features and buggy OS. Should've gone with Fossil. Same battery life, better looking watches, same features. I feel conned.

title: A review from the perspective of a long-time Apple Watch user
text:
I've recently transitioned from all-Apple devices to the Android ecosystem. One of my biggest "worries" about this transition was getting a replacement for my Apple Watch, as my impression of Android-based wearables wasn't positive. While the Apple Watch is by no means perfect, the user experience is, generally, excellent, and the build quality of the Apple Watch is outstanding. After doing quite a bit of research for an Android smartwatch, most reviewers seem to recommend this model, the Galaxy Watch 4, or the Fossil Gen 5 Smartwatch as the clearest front-runners for best Wear OS devices available at the moment. For perspective, my use-case for a smartwatch is to be able to tell time, get notifications, and do some degree of fitness tracking, though the latter is relatively less important to me. I ultimately went with the TicWatch because of the crazy advertised battery life and my strong aversion to getting sucked in to Samsung's own ecosystem within Android, which seemed to be necessary to get all of the functionality out of the Galaxy Watch without having to do some hacking that I wasn't interested in doing. Some of the reviews on Amazon and elsewhere had me quite worried about this device. After daily-driving it for nearly a month, though, I'm EXTREMELY impressed and very happy with the purchase. If you have similar trepidation, read on and buy without regret.

THE GOOD:
- I think the build quality of the TicWatch is actually quite good. Yes, the bulk of the watch body is plastic, but it's a solid, high-quality plastic that looks and feels like a premium device. I really haven't noticed missing the all-glass-and-metal design of the Apple Watch. Some reviews derided the TicWatch as looking like a "toy" because of the plastic body, which I absolutely disagree with. Despite the $300 price point, I'm not at all disappointed with the build quality. It's not as sleek as the Apple Watch, but the TicWatch is significantly lighter, and the beautiful chromed surface of the Apple Watch eventually gets scratched to hell without some kind of protective case. In contrast, I haven't had that experience with the TicWatch so far. The steel bezel seems to resist scratches well, and I haven't noticed any blemishes on my unit so far despite wearing it nearly 24 hours/day.

- The battery life on the TicWatch is INCREDIBLE. This is really a total game changer compared to the Apple Watch, which had an awful battery life that required daily charging. On the TicWatch, I have all monitoring enabled, all notifications enabled, and am using the default screen options (keeping the primary AMOLED display on for only 5 seconds after the last interaction and using the LCD display at all other times except when a new notification comes in). With this setup, I run the battery down by 30-40% each day. Right now, for example, I last charged the TicWatch to 100% ~30 hours ago and still have 67% charge remaining. At this rate, 2-3 days of battery life is no problem, and daily charging is no longer a necessity. I will say that the charger kind of sucks - more on that below - but the battery life is outstanding.

- I love the dual-display setup on the TicWatch. I do wish that the LCD had a bit more functionality - for example, it'd be nice if there was an indication that there are unread notifications and displayed a pulse reading - and had some degree of customization options (which I know would be limited by the technology), but it's otherwise outstanding. The backlight looks exactly like a classic, "old-school" LCD watch - think Timex's Indiglo light. If you need to get to the AMOLED display, a push of one of the crowns gets you there. This was a genius decision by Mobvoi, and the battery life improvements make this totally worthwhile and something other smartwatch makers should take notice of.

- Performance on the TicWatch is excellent. Screen transitions, animations, and opening apps are all snappy. The experience doesn't feel at all janky, and it's largely comparable to my experience with the Apple Watch. If, like me, you're worried about how this device will perform, don't be - the user experience is excellent.

- One of the common complaints that I read about the TicWatch was having to rely on the touchscreen for all input - in other words, the lack of rotating bezel or crown to scroll the screen. I actually haven't found this to be an issue in day-to-day use. Sure, a functional crown would be nice, but the screen is large enough that scrolling on the screen directly isn't difficult. At this price point the lack of a scrolling crown does seem odd, but I don't think the use of the device is hampered in anyway by its absence. YMMV.

- As a recent immigrant from Apple devices, I've actually found the Wear OS experience to be decent (at the time of this review, Wear OS 3 isn't available). I do think Apple's UI is superior and easier to use, but I don't find the Wear OS experience to be so absolutely lacking or terrible as to be a problem. YMMV, I suppose, as I know Wear OS is frequently lamented as being neglected by Google. As someone new to the Android ecosystem, this isn't evident to me.

THE MEH:
- To get all of the functionality out of this device, you'll need to use Mobvoi's apps. I will say that basic fitness readings and other data collected by Mobvoi's apps do seem to integrate pretty well with Google Fit, which I prefer to use. Google Fit doesn't collect everything, bit it's good enough for me: pulse readings, sleep time, and step counts. For anything else, I use a shortcut on the watch face for Mobvoi's apps so that I can check specific parameters if I want (like the pseudoscientific "stress" readings). Unlike other reviewers, I haven't had any issues with inconsistent step counts - everything is more or less consistent across the watch, Google Fit, my calorie tracking app, and Mobvoi's apps, though it does take a bit of time for the data to be pushed around. That said, Mobvoi's apps need some work. They clearly look and feel like poorly translated apps with a design language that seems out of place to my American eye. The data in the Mobvoi apps are slow to update, even when forced to do so. I don't frequently use the Mobvoi apps since I prefer Google's interface, but the suboptimal experience here is unfortunate.

- The charger needs some work. It works well most of the time, but like some reviewers have mentioned, I've occasionally had issues with the watch presumably being connected to the charger but not actually charging. I've gotten into the habit of checking to make sure that the watch indicates that it's charging before leaving it to charge. Charging is also slower than I'd like, as many reviews have mentioned - it takes about an hour to top off the watch after being used for a 24-hour period. I suppose that's not terrible, but it seems a lot slower than the Apple Watch, which can nearly fully charge in that period of time. The absence of magnetic charging is a bummer - I'm not a fan of the cable-based charging, which reminds me of my Pebble watch that I had back in the day. I hope that the next model will improve on this point.

THE BAD:
- The only negative point included in most reviews of this device that I actually agree with is that the haptic motor sucks. The motor is totally garbage, and I can't believe that Mobvoi presumably tested this device and stuck with the motor anyway. As many other reviewers have mentioned, I hear the haptic motor more than I feel it. This absolutely needs to be fixed in the next model - at this price point, the inclusion of this garbage motor is really inexcusable.

In summary, I'm generally quite happy with the TicWatch, and I have no regrets about the purchase. I think it's fairly priced for what you get, the experience has, in general, been solid and comparable to my experience with the Apple Watch, and it performs exactly as I would hope a smartwatch would. It's not a perfect device, but the quirks and annoyances don't actively interfere with its usability, and none of them are deal-breakers for me. If, like me, you like the idea of the insane battery life but are worried about some of the negative reviews you may have read, take the plunge and go for it. I've been surprised by how positive the experience has been, and I'm a believer in Mobvoi now. Highly, highly recommended!

title: Great for biking and some activities, but the built-in optical heart rate monitor disappoints -- as does it size and weight
text:
If you want a fitness tracker, and want to track your sleep, etc., and do some light exercising infrequently (like twice in January and then give up), this is NOT your watch. Go get an Apple Watch, or a Samsung Gear, and be happy. On the other hand, if you're a fitness freak and really want the details on your workouts, this *might* be for you. Read on.

OK, still here, good. What this watch is good at: Great barometer, GPS, data tracking, external sensor flexibility, and fit/finish. What it's just OK at: user interface and web experience. And the ugly: the built-in heart rate monitor.

OK, there's a ton behind that last paragraph. I'm an avid sports electronics nut, and have a variety of Garmin and other brand sport watches, bike computers, power meters, etc., etc. I have to charge ELEVEN things at the end of a bike ride. I'll try and distill two weeks of hardcore testing down to a few paragraphs.

The best experience I've had with the Suunto is while riding a bike. It pairs with my bike power meter (unlike Garmin which arbitrarily hobbles all but their top end watches from this basic functionality). It pairs with my [[ASIN:B00JQHTJS2 Heart Rate Monitor]]. It pairs with my bike's speed/candence sensors. It just bloody well works, and works brilliantly.

It's also great for hill walking. The barometer is really top rate. And the mapping function is really great, too. It's too big for running, and I'm a big guy (195 lbs/88 kg, 6' / 183 cm)... I'm slow enough as it is; I don't need further hindrances!

The worst experience: using it as a fitness tracker, or tracking heart rate during the day or especially at night. The thing is just too darned heavy, the built-in optical HRM doesn't work for me (I think it's a function of weight; the heaver the device the more it's affected by movement and the harder it is to separate said movement from heart rate measurement), and Suunto's app and website don't really let you get at past data in any decent way. Oh, yeah, and unlike my [[ASIN:B0711LKZKX Nokia Steel HR]] or [[ASIN:B074KBWL9J Vívoactive 3]], it's BIG and UNCOMFORTABLE while sleeping.

More on the heart rate issue: it would often report an insanely high heart rate (like 160 bpm) when my real heart rate was 110. It seemed to be reporting a mashup of cadence, actual heart rate, arm swinging, whatever... It would settle down and get accurate, and then go crazy again. Other reviewers report similar issues, and one reports that after a few weeks of "getting to know him" it settled down. Mine hasn't settled down. I just wear my Scosche when exercising with the watch. The built-in HRM is useless. Bah.

The first two pictures show a size comparison between the Garmin, the Suunto, and the Scosche HRM. "One of these isn't like the other" and is way too darned big!

The UI is a bit fiddly. My top complaint is font size. For such a big watch, it tries to cram too much info on a screen – and as a result uses a font too small for me to read easily, e.g. when I get a notification of an e-mail or text message. The font is ESPECIALLY bad when I'm running, etc. I don't need to read a novel; just give me the basics and I'll be happy!

Oh, and I had a *terrible* initial experience with the watch. It bricked itself about an hour into my use of it. I had to send it back for repair. The good news is that the person on the phone was completely gracious about it, and within seconds I was set up with a paid UPS sticker to return it. The bad news was that it took a few weeks to get it back (OK, it was over New Year's, but still...). No real info other than "reloaded software."

Why four stars then? Because for the use cases where it works, it's GREAT, Simply GREAT. It's just not a Swiss Army Knife (or even a Finnish Army Knife :-) like the Garmin watches. I'm glad I own it, but the Garmin is still my "daily driver" activity watch. If you're looking for a great watch for biking (and you have a power meter), walking and like big ("I'm important") watches, then it's five stars, easy.

Disclosure 1: I haven't taken it swimming yet. I plan to, but it's January and even in San Diego the water is a bit chilly.

Disclosure 2: I work for a sister company to one that makes smart watches. I don't work with this folks, and am writing this review as an individual.

title: Not a good product for health programs when synced through iPhone
text: I really want to buy a Samsung Gear Fit2, I really do, it seems like a great deal for all the product features.  However after doing significant research I realized that while the Gear Fit 2 now has an app on iOS (S Health Gear Fit App), unfortunately it cannot still share its information with other apps.  And the only way to sync is using that app (you cannot sync with PC like Fitbit).  Which means that my health program through work (a nationally recognized one) that works just great with my Fitbit, and Apple Health, cannot gather this info from the Gear Fit 2 making it completely useless for the purpose I want to purchase it for.  Hopefully Samsung will continue moving in the direction of giving the iOS app the same features as the Android App so that third parties can glean the information, until then I'm purchasing a Fitbit Charge 2 or Blaze (begrudgingly).

title: $600 heartbeat sensor...
text: Features surrounding heartbeat sensor are endless. That's all it does well. The apps are spotty at best. The build quality leaves a lot to be desired. I've owned several Samsung watches over the years and they've set a high bar that this very expensive Garmin doesn't live up to. The lack of touch screen, wireless charging, inexpensive band and even the packaging are a disappointment. Additionally, I expected a more seemless integration with other Garmin devices. Trying to get this to send HB info to my Edge 830 bike computer was not intuitive and I feel like they missed an opportunity for a seemless experience. I should be able to hit start on one and have everything sync up. In all, I have a lot more heartbeat data than I did before, but for this price I expected more.

title: I cannot recommend this product, lacks stable Bluetooth connection when running.
text: I bought this as part of my commitment to getting back into shape.  I have a different set of Bluetooth headphones that I've used for at least 5 years and surprisingly, I've actually used those more than these because the Bluetooth connection on these new GOgroove headphones are sh*t.  I have a Samsung gear 2 smart watch that I use for my runs and such and it pairs up quickly, but.......I'm constantly losing the connection every time I move my wrist, which is all the time.  It's as if there is a pause in the songs every 5 seconds or so and it doesn't do this with my other headphones.  So it seems that in order to keep your Bluetooth connection with these headphones you need to remain still and within a couple feet.  Although the sound is good and the fact that it's sweat proof, I haven't really had the chance to notice it's benefits because of the lack of a steady connection.  I wear a 7 1/2 ball cap, I have an 18 inch neck and short ear canals and the fit is okay.  The adjustable ear ports are nice, but they don't seem to want to stay where I put them, but it isn't a deal breaker.  The Bluetooth connection is though, therefore, I will probably be sending these back as soon as I can get some time to do so.  So yes, I am very disappointed with the product and I'm even more disappointed with the fact that now I have to spend my even more valuable time to run to the post office to return these.

title: Not So Smart Watch
text:
Well, I'm spoiled. I was attempting to purchase a lite version of the old Samsung Gear 2 Classic, to temporarily replace that truly classic smart watch.
This "Tinwoo Eclipse" just isn't that smart. I like the Eclipse name, but the Google Play Store doesn't offer the app I wss instructed to install. Too many discrepancies.
Nothing intuitive about it, unless you simply want an analog watch face displayed on a screen, with no easy route to sample the few available watch faces without another account/membershipin the Tinwoo club.
I suppose that I'm just an old geek, but this new smart watch is lacking by my standards. It won't really function in the described most complex, (limited & simplistic), mission, without the new owner "creating an account" in the "pcloud". I just won't do it.
I won't share my personal info, my contact info and allow all those new cookies, so the I can gain access to a better users manual.
The included instructions, nor the downloaded copy provided instructions that match the available icons in the watch's own settings screen. I do want to pick a digital watch face, as offered. I couldn't find the icon which was said to lead me to 14 total (very similar) watch faces.
This might be good for some folks, but it won't work for me. I'll settle for a classic Casio digital watch with a more modern watch face and a ten year battery, until I find a real smart watch which I can afford.
To top it off, I spent the biggest part of an hour waiting for a chat response, from Tinwootech's web chat, but never got even a out to lunch message. Pretty disgusting for a chat window that claims in big print; "We reply immediately", no joke.
Good luck

Not So Smart Watch. Well, I'm spoiled. I was attempting to purchase a lite version of the old Samsung Gear 2 Classic, to temporarily replace that truly classic smart watch.
This "Tinwoo Eclipse" just isn't that smart. I like the Eclipse name, but the Google Play Store doesn't offer the app I wss instructed to install. Too many discrepancies.
Nothing intuitive about it, unless you simply want an analog watch face displayed on a screen, with no easy route to sample the few available watch faces without another account/membershipin the Tinwoo club.
I suppose that I'm just an old geek, but this new smart watch is lacking by my standards. It won't really function in the described most complex, (limited & simplistic), mission, without the new owner "creating an account" in the "pcloud". I just won't do it.
I won't share my personal info, my contact info and allow all those new cookies, so the I can gain access to a better users manual.
The included instructions, nor the downloaded copy provided instructions that match the available icons in the watch's own settings screen. I do want to pick a digital watch face, as offered. I couldn't find the icon which was said to lead me to 14 total (very similar) watch faces.
This might be good for some folks, but it won't work for me. I'll settle for a classic Casio digital watch with a more modern watch face and a ten year battery, until I find a real smart watch which I can afford.
To top it off, I spent the biggest part of an hour waiting for a chat response, from Tinwootech's web chat, but never got even a out to lunch message. Pretty disgusting for a chat window that claims in big print; "We reply immediately", no joke.
Good luck

title: Lackluster
text:
I'm a nerd so I may be biased, but here are gripes:
-Initial setup required a 30 minute charge straight out of the box. It then took another 30 minutes to "download" something onto the watch...assumably just initializing and interfacing with my phone (LG G4).
-The wrist strap appears to have texture in product pics. This band is flat as Kansas. And boring looking. Cheap. I feel I need big muscles, a tank top and shaved chest with sunglasses and a flat brimmed hat to wear this thing right. I don't say "Bruh" enough.
-You CANNOT make/receive calls from the watch - only texts.
-When using the 'always on' display, it seems to default to a watchface that I didn't choose.
-You cannot view emails, Facebook, and other App messages from the watch - only the brief descriptions and a nice thumbnail preview (if a picture is included).
-I took the watch off the charger at 7:30am. It's 2pm and my battery is at 43% - with minimal use. 2 day charge my butt.

I've been through few different smart watches, this is just another lackluster disappointment. This ranks behind the Samsung Gear 2.
Though it has Android Wear OS (which is better than Tizen IMO), this is missing:
-optical heart rate monitor
-built in sleep monitor system
-camera
-ability to read/write emails
-general ease/quick setup

Not a fan of this watch and it hasn't been 24 hours. Don't waste your money if you're a techie.

title: Battery Life Issues (with no solution from Samsung!)
text:
UPDATED REVIEW
I loved this watch (and gave it 5 stars) when I first got it. And even for the first few months. The last 2 months have me rethinking the entire "smart watch" concept. The battery life, which used to be great, has rapidly gone downhill. When I look at the app on my phone to see what it eating the battery, it is Samsung Health. I've followed various steps to disable the activity tracking to the limit that the apps allow - and even with that, it is below acceptable performance. I used to get close to 2 days out of a full charge (although I do set it on the charger each night to be safe). Recently, I'm at 50% after 8-9 hours. I've reset it multiple times (which is a chore itself - while it backs up, it doesn't restore all of the settings requiring me to go through and reconfigure it each time), which seems to help for a few days. Samsung does NOT allow you to remove the S-Health app from the watch. I don't use the app, and find it offensive when it is eating up the majority of my battery life and I'm powerless to stop it.

Definitely won't be buying another Samsung smart watch after this.

ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW (Week post-purchase)
I tend to be a bit skeptical about non-OEM refurbished devices, and this watch exceeded my expectations. It arrived "like new", with no visible scratches or scuff marks. Unfortunately I didn't take pictures of it upon arrival (I've already scratched it myself through normal wear, which to me makes it all the more impressive how it arrived!) It works great - I'm able to get 2 days out of a charge without putting it in "low power" mode. I haven't connected the 3G/4G with my carrier yet, and have it connected to a Samsung Galaxy S5 via bluetooth - but I love the ability to capture voice memos without pulling my phone from my pocket (and that it transcribes them immediately and shows them on the screen - which is great for short-term notes and quick reference!)

I wasn't sure how much I would really use a "smart watch", and didn't want to spend $300+ to find out. This is a great product at a great price for anyone else looking to learn more about wearables.

title: Stay Away from Samsung Gear Cameras
text:
Unless you have a Samsung Galaxy phone or tablet, stay away from these products. Samsung does not provide full compatibility with industry standards, you can not stitch the images in other than proprietary software, puts strict limits on Mac computers and you have to use Samsung memory chips otherwise it doesn't work!
Besides that, the camera is NOT water resistant, least proof. The camera stopped working after some outdoor shots, and sent it in for servicing under warranty. The service called me back saying that the camera was working properly. Glad for that, but the camera had quit on my on a trip!!!! That's why I sent it!
Besides this, the camera is surprisingly unreliable taking video. In a recent special trip, the camera did not record half of the video time that I was using it.
I'm giving up on this piece of whatever. Don't spend your time on this.

title: Does NOT work with Chrome OS
text:
They fit well and sound great on my Google Pixel 2 XL phone. But be aware that they will pair with a device only through the Samsung Gear app -- which is why they will NOT work with my Pixel Slate tablet. Although the Slate is a Chrome OS that runs (most) Android apps fine, Google Play tells me that it is not compatible with the Samsung Gear app. When I try to pair the Buds to the Slate directly through the Bluetooth menu, I just see the word "connecting..." cycling endlessly.

A chat with Samsung support confirms that the Buds will connect only through the app, and that there are no plans to make the app compatible with Chrome OS.

title: Good Value
text: Great value for the money. It does not track sleeping very well at all, at least not for me who works overnight. I have the option to manually edit the amount of sleep but I don't understand the point of doing it when it won't be able to tell me if it was deep sleep or any other details. It literally shows me as being up for weeks at a time even though I sleep during the day. Good for workouts and simple functions such reading text messages or seeing who's calling without taking it my phone. Battery life is insane and I still love it and wear it ahead of my Samsung watch.

title: Look elsewhere!
text:
Worst wireless bluetooth I've ever encountered.
I currently own and use the LG HBS 730 and based on reviewed, I thought this one might be better. I was dead wrong
Cons:
1. It doesn't' connect as easily as other bluetooth headsets and good luck if you try to connect it to a table without NFC
2. Flawed design. I did not find a power off button on this thing, it has no magnetic clip for the ear pieces, so you just have to leave them to dangle about and arduously force them into the holder, buttons are less intuitive than on the HBS 730.
3. It doesn't have intuitive announcements like the HBS 730 and many others I've tried.
4. Call quality was abysmal
5. Audio quality is a joke
6. It's also not as comfortable to wear and the HBS 730 and many others I've used.

Pros:
Hmmm... Can't really think of any.

This is likely the very worst around the neck bluetooth headset I've encountered and only recommend it to those who like to throw away money. I would rather pay for others I've tried (HBS 730, Samsung Gear Circle, LG TONE INFINIM, Rokit Boost SwageU, HBS-800, SNEER® "iSport" Series Premium 2014, SNEER® "iSport" Series Premium 2014, Motorola Buds SF500) that accept this one for free.

BTW, the overall best of all of them has been the HBS 730

title: Not great...
text:
I bought this to charge my Samsung Galaxy Watch Active 1 and Buds Pro on my nightstand. Save some room with less cords. The first disapointment was taking it out and seeing a black cord with a white stand. Just a cheap choice and looks terrible. Upon placing the watch on the stand it was very obvious the magnet is weak at best. I could blow my watch off the stand if I wanted to. Not even comparable to the oem charger. That said, it did connect and sit there fairly easily.

By far the biggest dissapointment was after charging for maybe 15 minutes I took the watch off and it was very warm. It does not do this with the oem charger. I would not feel comfortable leaving it on there overnight which is why I bought it. Buds charged ok. The green light seems to just be on whenever something is on it and I'm assuming connected. It did not turn off when at 100% which is annoying at night for me. There is one light for each charging pad. There were no instructions about what the lights mean included.

Overall, with the above the stand is less than ok (ok being 3 stars) in my opinion. Give this stand a stronger magnet, coordinating cord, add more function to the lights, and better power/wattage management and you would get 5 stars. However, now I'm just thinking I should return and pay more for the Samsung Duo pad.

text: This is hard to depict I'm photos but I wish I had seen this in another review. I am a former Samsung Galaxy watch owner. When it crapped out I decided to try this inexpensive smart watch. title: Don't see myself buying a Galaxy watch ever again
text:
I felt like I'd gotten pretty savvy about Samsung promising features they didn't fully deliver on, but I guess not. All sorts of things they advertised that weren't released - or were delayed over a year - in the US. Want to monitor you blood pressure? Wait for it, wait for it.......okay, hope you have a cuff so that you can calibrate it and recalibrate it constantly. If not, you're out of luck.
Took the occasional shower with it still on, went in the spa 2-3 times with it, and then I noticed it wouldn't power on or charge. Days later, it powers up then says it needs to shut down because it is too hot - even though there is zero warmth to the touch. Not sure of the connection, but as a sports watch this has been nothing but pain and failure. I won't even get into the short battery life or the fact that if you forget to turn on airplane mode over even a 2 hour flight you'll basically end up with a nearly dead watch when you land.

I know it's an older watch now and costs about a third of what i paid for it, but I still wouldn't do it.

title: Good smart watch, not a good Garmin
text:
I got this to replace my vivosmart hr +. The band was chewed up and I wanted a larger screen. After getting this,I am disappointed and regret it. The vivosmart has clear legible screens to read your total distance for the day, total steps, total stairs , total active minutes, plus notifications. This does not count your total distance for the day, it's hidden in separate activities list. Most of us started out with a Garmin for distance tracking. Also the fields for the rest of your activities are cramped and hard to read. I can't even really tell what I am looking at like I did with the vivo.
As far as a smart watch it's great, you can read text messages in color easily. The watch is attractive. But I am thinking of just getting a vivo band to replace and either return this or have to use two watches. Which defeated the purpose. Just not what I wanted from Garmin, I could have just gotten an Apple or Samsung watch and kept the vivo.

title: A real disappointment
text: I am a very happy owner of both a Galaxy watch 3 and Galaxy watch.  When I saw the price of the newest Samsung watch and saw that it was running Wear OS 3 I thought they just made a good thing better, I was wrong.  I bought the Galaxy watch 4 44 mm, which means it doesn't have the physical rotating bezel.  The bezel it does have is bad, I have to be a contortionist to make it move and when it does its hard to get it to stop on the app I want.  Its not the only way to get to the apps so I won't bother using it any more.  The rubber watchband is just plain bad, after wearing the watch for a short time I realized I didn't want to struggle with it every time I put the watch on so I ordered a replacement band.  And Wear OS 3, where are all these great apps I keep reading about?  Its really not a big deal, Tizen and Wear 3 both have all the apps I really want or use but really, saying the app store is so much bigger when it fact there's nothing special there is no selling point to me.  So, I give it 2 stars because I'm hoping its as reliable as my old Samsung watches and zero stars for Wear OS 3 and the wristband.

title: Heath and Exercise not good.
text:
Software almost there, watch has died 3 times after having 50 percent charge, just loses all charge, never goes int 45 day mode on its own, not sure if it even has that much time if it did, other wise works good. It is 10 times better than the amazfit watch I tried, used to have galaxy watch, is better than that but can't beat twist ring.
On second watch, heart rate on hikes works right about 50% of the time, distance off by 25% under on trail hikes. Sleep is pretty lame too, my Galaxy 2 and Garmin watches never had these problems, the Movi exercise apps are really bad, and charts really suck too. the google app gives much better info graphs.
Need turn off most function to get 3 days of life, settings for face coming on always resorts back to off.
Heart rate freezes sometimes while hiking, but watch heart still working poorly at same time.

Unable to find decent apps for exercise and heath for this watch. I will not trust these guys doing reviews of these watches any more.
Update the google fit software works for exercising, I guess just that Mobi stuff is bad, I give watch a strong 4 stars now.
Update 3 weeks later, well back to 1 star, tic health was tracking heart rate and altitude during hikes , now nothing but distance, time ,and map.

Have had watch over 6 months, heart rate function still not reliable, sometime it works , sometimes its way off, will never buy a google product again, It used to be fun using my old Galaxy 2 watch, this ruins the experience of a digital watch. Samsung or Garmin for sure.

title: Not so stupid, I am teachable - I am so STUPID!
text:
Uodate 2/09/2027
The picture below shows my easy and cheap fix for the watchband. I just did not want to buy a new wrist band that might or might not fit my tracker. I bought 20 ponytail/braid bands at the drugstore for $2.00. I doubled one over the band and it holds my band snugly and is easy to get off and on.
Update 2/05/2017
The snow has melted quite a bit, and I found my Gearfit 2 just inside the pasture fence where I throw hay to the horses. Fortunately, my 1,200 pound Tennessee Walker had not stepped on it. I brought it up to the house, put it on the charger and reconnected it to the Samsung Gear app my phone. So, after 8 days of being buried in the snow with night temps in the upper teens, my Gearfit 2 is working just fine. I am going to order a different band for it, but until it comes, I have taken a bread bag twisty tie and secured my band. Samsung needs to fix this issue and not rely on the cottage industry to pick up the slack. So many people are not as lucky as I was today to find their watches.
Now on to performance. I do really like this tracker and maybe because I live out in the country, I am more tolerant of glitchy electronics and intermittent internet connections. #satellitemegalagtimes #thankyouelectriccoopforrunningfiberoptics The issues I have with the tracker are: 1. it does not register my stair climbing faithfully. Sometimes it does the house stairs. Sometimes it doesn't. It almost always gets the elevation change outside from house to barn - equivalent to two flights of stairs. 2. The step count on the Gearfit 2 does not match up with steps on my Samsung Galaxy S5. The phone always shows more steps. 3. The exercise choices do not match up with the phone. I have horsebackriding on my phone app but not on the tracker. The GPS works well out here in the boonies. I like the many options in settings. I turn off all notifications because I want to prolong battery life - I go two full days and still have battery - and I really don't want to know who is calling, texting, on FB while I am out walking in my gorgeous frozen northland or asleep. Yes, it has a few issues and I can deal with the ball park number of steps and stairs. It is good for my moderate OCD to not have things perfect. I am grateful that I found it right where my husband said it would probably be. <3 Thank you St. Anthony. ;)

1/29/2017 Man, oh man am I angry and do I ever feel stupid! I researched the different types of fitness watch/monitors carefully. I have probably been looking at different ones for a year now. Everything about the GearFit 2 was great. I liked the look, the options, the fact that I didn't have to crane my neck and wrist around to read the data. It synced beautifully with my Samsung Galaxy phone. It kept accurate steps, sleep, heart beat. In a word, it was FANTASTIC. You may be wondering why I gave it one star. IT'S THE WRIST BAND!! My lovely easy to use fantastic fitness aid is now out in the horse barn, hay barn or under 3 inches of new fallen snow. I read the reviews and the warnings about the watch falling off people's wrists and disappearing. I also read that Samsung had improved it with some type of keeper to prevent that. There is a loop that you pass the band through before fastening it into its slot. IF THAT WAS THE FIX, IT AINT' ENOUGH!!! My lovely, I-told-you-so applefitbit friends will certainly get a charge out of this if they find out. I don't care what people say, I'm just really really angry and feel that I have been cheated by Samsung. Most of their products are good, but I will not be buying another GearFit2 or any other GearFit. They are too expensive to have them slip off an disappear. I will count my own steps and assume that my heart is beating and that I am really sleeping at 4 am. Grrrrrrrrrrrrrr.

title: BUYER BEWARE NOT WATER RESISTANT!!!!
text:
I am so frustrated!! I will not ever purchase another Samsung watch again!!! I just got my new watch in June if this year... it would not hold a charge for longer than 1 hour... I had to turn all settings off except for a digital watch face and the step counter to get it last almost 4 hours.. then it stopped working all together... I sent it in to Samsung to have it repaired under the 1 year manufacturer'swarranty. Today I got a bill for $220 because they said it's out of warranty.
When I called to see why my watch that is less than 60 days old is out of warranty... they told me it has water damage and that this voids the warranty... This watch is a sports watch made to go 50 meters under the water!!! I have not worn this watch in the water!!! I dont even have a bathtub in my house!! I have a handicap accessible shower!! The only water it would have come into contact with is rinsing a dish to put in the dishwasher!!!
When I asked what could I have done " in my house because we are in COVID-19 lock down!" to have compromise the integrity of this watch that is supposed to withstand going 158 feet under water? I told them I believe it has to be a manufacturing defect that would allow water to get into this watch. They said that the technician (that I am not allowed to talk to nor can they talk to) said it was not covered under warranty due to customer use and there is nothing I can do about it but pay it or cancel it!!!! So after spending 450 on a watch I now have to pay 220 on a watch that has never worked!! BUYER BEWARE!!!

title: Seriously do not recommend
text:
Well, I enjoyed the watch until it somehow got water damage. This will happen to you, too, if you happen to shower or wash dishes with it on. Water damage is not covered by warranty and will cost more to repair than the watch is worth.

In terms of usability: probably 3/4 stars. The apps aren't that great, but the watch itself looks nice. It has nice fitness tracking, as expected (be careful about sweating, though, wouldn't want it to get a nano-meter of sweat in it). I expected to use it for more than fitness but didn't necessarily find myself doing so.

Never buying a Samsung watch again, despite having one of their phones.

title: It gives her life control but ..Battery is terrible!!.
text:
My sister is diabetic and has a Samsung phone like the mister and me. Here's is a low end non expensive model. It works for her and she tends to drop or lose them. We have high end expensive phones and watches. She want to track her sleep and steps. Dr says good idea. But even she knew not a $$$$ smart watch. Thank you Amazon. This is perfect. It allows her to do what our watches do for less. The only issues she has is the cord is way too short and battery life is not even good. She can't sleep in it because after wearing all day walking the hall half the day battery dies. The mobile app was easy for the mister to sync but if the battery does not improve with use it is not going to work. I can't return passed warranty. But .. she is getting frustrated and That makes me angry. I know she is older but I take care of her and when she is unhappy I am 😠
Update...battery is not getting better so it took away a star and she is complaining about the strap and it got wet while washing dishes and it shocked her. I may have to but her a Samsung watch. Which may be too expensive. SO DISAPPOINTED!!!!

title: Maybe not the worst, but you get what you pay for
text:
There was only a few usable features. Anything that required a connection to the phone consistently bugged out and lost connection, if it could connect at all.

I was able to receive notifications every now and then, but they would come in groups. I couldn't get the calling or outgoing texting to function at all. There is also a severe lack of face options, and the bluetooth connection drops pretty much any time you leave the room without your phone.

I looked all around Google and couldn't find any news, so I e-mailed the the V Wave [phone app] company about any potential updates coming. After a few days I didn't hear anything back so I just returned the watch.

[I went ahead and just paid twice as much to get a renewed Samsung Galaxy Watch. You can get a lot of really good watches really cheap because they're renewed. I've had 2 phenomenal experiences with virtually new renewed phones, and while the renewed watch I received had some issues remaining from the last owner, I'm currently exchanging it and the seller is helping to ensure the next one is up to the previous standard.]

Some Positives:
-Simple things like the calculator work.
-Step counter and oxygen reader may be accurate.
~~They were within the margin of error before I returned the watch, and I didn't compare the other measuring tools. Didn't bother to test anything else because I got it mainly for the purpose of a watch and means to answer my phone from another room.
-Time is accurate after pairing with phone.
-The stock watch faces aren't the worst.

title: Battery life is poor
text:
While light and generally easy to use, I didn't find the menus very intuitive on the device and had to repeatedly look up what icons meant when I was trying to do set up and use it initially. The watch face options aren't attractive and you have no option to download new ones as I could on my gear fit 2. It wouldn't have been so bad if I could have just had a plain watch face that didn't make the hour and numbers different colors maybe.

Ultimately my problem was battery life. I was looking to swap out my Samsung Gear Fit2 which would last me approximately 3 days depending on whether I let it auto track my walking or if I tracked it myself which used GPS. I opted for the vivosport because, like my gear fit 2, it had built in GPS and because it was so small. However, after the first 24 hours of using it (from a full charge) I was down to 60% of my battery. I had set gestures to only during activity and locked the display so I wouldn't drain battery from the backlight. I had also turned the display down in brightness. I had used it for a 3-ish mile walk with GPS on which was around an hour of walking, tracked my activity for an hour workout that morning (no GPS) and otherwise had just worn it around.

Ultimately I returned it within a few days for another Garmin device that had the same smartwatch battery life but more than 1.5x the life while using GPS. I haven't regretted switching to my Vivoactive3 once.

title: Great value for the money! (updated)
text:
Update (10/11/21)
After enjoying this watch for a few weeks, I started noticing some issues:
- The app and the watch stop syncing. The app shows the watch as connected, and yet, there is no data from the watch appearing there (see the attached photo).
- The watch started to turn itself off at random times. When restarted, it resets all the data (HR, BP, Steps), so it looks like you haven't done anything all day. Coupled with the app that doesn't sync often, your entire day of health tracking data is lost.
Based on the abovementioned issues, I am removing a star from my previous review.

I needed a smartwatch that would double as a fitness tracker, as my older Samsung Galaxy watch is too big and bulky to wear at the gym. When this MBHB arrived, I was pleasantly surprised with the array of features and options it offered. Here are the pros and cons of this device, based on my experience owning it for a few weeks.

PROS:
- Size and Weight. The watch is very light, even with a metal bracelet. The size is just right, not too large, and not too thick.
- Battery life. The battery life is great. I go for at least five(!!) days without charging the watch, and it is impressive at any price range.
- Features. The watch is packed with features. For me, the health and fitness features are the priority, and it doesn't disappoint. You get blood pressure, heart rate, and blood oxygen monitoring. Those measurements are assumed accurate as they fall within my typical range. You can set the watch (via the app) to measure your vitals at certain time periods automatically and get a report at the end of the day. There are numerous sport modes, a steps counter, and a calorie burned counter. The watch can display your text messages, you can answer phone calls, and even dial numbers from the watch if you desire to do so. You can even use it to play music, in case your phone is charging in another room. You can set the watch to remind you to drink water or move after certain time periods.
- User Interface. The UI is actually very intuitive. Some other watches I have seen on Amazon were not that well designed. This one has large icons that are self-explanatory and just easy to use. You can change watch faces and even download additional ones via the app, but some of them are not very aesthetically pleasing (but it's a matter of taste). I've attached some photos.

CONS:
- The App. The "Funkeep" app is not too bad, I've seen a lot worse from Chinese developers. It syncs with the watch and keeps track of your daily vitals, and you can connect with Google Fit. I still feel it could be a bit more polished, with some better-translated labels.
- The bracelet. I absolutely dislike it. It is awkward to put on, and at one time, it came undone and the watch went flying. Luckily, there was no damage to it. I will probably replace the bracelet with a rubbery bracelet, something more appropriate for a fitness tracker.
- Waterproof rating. I wish I could wear this watch while swimming, it would have been a home run. I saw some other inexpensive fitness watches offering this option.
- Sleep Tracker. That feature just refuses to work for me. I wore it overnight multiple times and got no sleep data. I checked the settings, and there shouldn't be anything preventing the watch from gathering the data. That was one of the things I wanted to measure but cannot.
- Unknown brand and quality uncertainty. You're not buying Apple Watch or Fitbit, so there are no expectations of quality and longevity. I have never heard of the MHBH brand and am not sure what to expect in the long run.

Overall, I am satisfied with this watch. It offers a lot of health/fitness tracking features, it is easy to use, and it is a good value for what you get. I would definitely recommend it, despite some of the cons.
