title: Great product with some downfalls.  Still needs work.
text: While the concept was well executed, I feel this product was not ready for every day wear for the average consumer.  The glasses are pretty bulky.  They are heavy on the face and caused pain across the bridge of my nose while wearing them for long periods of time (+/- 30 minutes).  The stems were tight on my head causing discomfort.  Vuzix tried hard to make these look and feel like Oakley glasses.  They were not stylish enough in my opinion to go out in public without being laughed at.  They concept was great though.  The picture and clarity were great, though a little distracting and hard to see while driving.  Video playback from Netflix was not great even connected over WiFi.  The unit I received had a hard time staying connected to my phone even if it was right next to me via bluetooth.  It also lost connection with the included bluetooth earphones.  Apps ran slow and some messages did not appear on the glasses until much later after receiving them on my phone or watch.  I was using a Samsung Galaxy S10+ and have a Galaxy watch both newer technology and no reason why these glasses lost signal regularly.  I really like the concept, would like to test their newer products for the average consumer in the near future.  Ultimate I decided to return these to Amazon only after 2 days because they were not comfortable, I could not see wearing these publicly and the connection issues.  They are a good product, something I could get behind once some of the aforementioned issues were addressed.

title: Made my wrist ache
text: I absolutely loved this watch but it made my wrist ache so I had to return it.  There is something about the sensors they use that after a few minutes of wearing the watch, the ache would start in my wrist and then up into my hand.  I took it off, rested for a period of time and tried it again - with the same result.  I know it's specific to this watch because I currently wear a Samsung Galaxy watch3 and have no discomfort.  The reason I got this watch was because I have a Pixel 3a and the Samsung watch does not fully integrate with my phone, I need a WearOS watch.  Amazon customer service was wonderful and I am able to return the watch for a full refund.  I'm bummed.  I really liked the watch's functionality and integration with my phone.  The only two things I didn't love about the watch:  1) The plastic case - because when it vibrated, it was loud due to the plastic housing.  2) The software didn't have an activity monitor that reminded me to get up and move.  I use this every day with my Samsung watch.  But I would have been very happy to keep the watch if only it didn't make my wrist hurt.

title: 10 faces outta 10 ))))))))))
text:
Firstly, I didn't get this at Amazon, but someone else had a huge sale on them. I was compelled to add this review.

At the price that ?????? sold this watch, 5 stars.
CONS: At the listed retail price, I may have taken a star off, but read to the end. I don't care for the two finger swiping, and endless tapping on things to activate, don't activate, or do or don't deactivate. Menu seems to be really confusing and esoteric, or not intelligent. I have big fingers and did get the bigger 44mm watch, but with two fingers, you cannot see what you are swiping to or from. These are all minor gripings. AND it talks too much, haha.

PROS: Doesn't quite sound like a 5 outta 5 star review so far does it? People are so jaded today, and complain about technology that didn't even exist a few short years ago. Enjoy and marvel at things folks. We always had phones, but today they are computers, and people find a way to nit pic inconsequential things. Look at the big picture. The watch that Samsung has put together is a masterpiece. Sure it isn't perfect, but who wants a Timex or Casio watch now if you own this Samsung Smart Watch? I'm not going to get into all the things it does, because you could write a book on it.

The watch faces that come embedded in the watch are truly great. You can download plenty more free faces too. It will take you a while to find them and refind them. I haven't paid for one yet. I put a couple in my list just to demonstrate the watch to my friends, and has flashing neon colored lights and info galore. Many of the faces have bio signals too. I was really looking for a watch, but I think it is making me healthier, why? Many of the faces tell you to get off your BU** and move around, or what your heart rate is currently beating. I've notice that eating a little healthier, especially less fatting meats, my BPM(beats per minute) have plummeted by ten most all day, and around 60bpm at wake up in the morning. No stressed out wake up heart rate. It is making a believer out of me. There are all kinds of activity and monitor based apps, alarms, phone, music, texts, emails, Facebook, Messenger, and much more. I own a Samsung Note 8 phone and BAM, and meshes with this in a second.

The physical watch itself, is stunning. I got the all black one. Way cool looking. Worth the price all by itself!! The watch face pops up and it is a 'WO THAT LOOKS UNREAL'!! Don't be fooled by the bland yellow stock face they show you, stunners await. The watch band is the best watch band you'll ever put on yer wrist. All the Casio-Timex watches I ever owned, the watch band barely fit my big wrist, and always on the last notch. This strap tucks under the band so it never is flopping out. The notch I use is far from the end, and the band is very high quality soft rubber that is easy to strap on. The strap, I'd rate it higher than the watch itself. There is also a litany of watch straps you can buy too, but the shipped band is incredible. Costco also bundled an extra watch charger. Here's a big tip and is a hint at most folks biggest complaint. So many complainers, so little time. Everyone gets this watch and turns everything on at once. Battery life for that scenario is about a day or less, and then the complainers start in. I learned right away from reading the complainers. I programmed the watch to sleep or go dark in ten seconds, maybe five. Turned the volume down, and shut off most of the monitoring apps.. Most of these things, most of you won't use, so save battery life. The people that use all the apps for jogging and monitoring, just need to realize that charging it is a way of life, or using it for jogging and not every day use. After deprogramming the thing, I get several days out of a charge, and it is still talking at me and doing some monitoring. I laugh when it keeps telling me to get up and move my body while watching sports for too long. Watch can also be set for watch only, which would last for who knows how long on a charge. Don't complain, just buy it, and enjoy the wonders.

I had a Sony Smart watch a few years back and kept breaking the one tiny piece of plastic on it that cost $00.002 cents and ruined the strap or the watch or both. This Samsung edition cost twice as much as that first smart watch and does ten times the features and looks ten times better at $150 on sale. Wait for the sale price and get one, because you might as well enjoy what technology is inventing these days, before we are gone. Don't let the world pass you by.

title: Great Smartwatch but not suitable for Blue Collar Work
text:
I've had a Fitbit since 2012. My units have never fallen apart, just charging issues after years of use. I used my last one with a Samsung Watch until it died then decided to get this to replace both since the Samsung Watch wasn't as robust as the Fitbit units have been as far as exercise tracking.

I found it fits well, the battery life is pretty good considering I have all the options on for monitoring and it gets maybe 2-3 days. Charging is SUPER fast I think, couple hours and you're good to go. The guided relaxation is pretty neat and everything (or course by design) works best with Premium service.

Today, (17 months after purchase) the screen popped off the base about 75% and was like an accordion. It's very sad because my Samsung took a beating and only had some scratches. And that lasted me about 4 years and I was a lot harder with it.

I'm not paying to fix it because anyone is going to say that it's not working or you have to replace the screen so I just threw on some super glue and I'm calling it a day. I can't confidently recommend this unless you have a desk job and don't go DIY crazy in your house.

title: Exceptional rugged fitness watch
text: So I had several Fit bit bands prior (Charge 2, Charge 3 and Versa) and  Galaxy Active watch. Neither of the Charge bands could be seen in daylight and I had horrific app connectivity issues when trying to sync. The Versa was better. I gifted that to my MIL when I got the Galaxy Active. I liked that interface BUT 2 day battery life was not acceptable. I looked around and found the TRex Pro and dug the rugged look and marketing of long battery life. I easily get 20 days between charges. This to me should be a minimum. The Zepp app has never had an issue on my Samsung Galaxy phone and is detailed. All updates have been seamless and NEVER a sync issue. I like that I get notifications and can choose to respond on my phone or not. I liked mine so much I bought a second one for our son and he absolutely digs his.

title: Awesome Buds for Certain Users.
text:
I think most reviewers that give negative reviews on these completely miss the point with these buds and dont understand the product. The top bullet point on the Samsung product page says "Go Phone Free." The second bullet point is "Simply Tap to Control." I dont know, but it seems to me from the top two bullet points that Samsung primarily intends these to be used without a phone. As it turns out, that is exactly what these are and where they shine.

If you really think about it, not having full, robust, and total control from a phone - Android or iPhone - is irrelevant. Not being able to upload songs directly from an iPhone is irrelevant. The best way to upload songs to these is from a PC, i.e. without a phone. Not being able to have "full" control from an Android or iPhone is irrelevant. The best way to control these is from the touch interface, i.e. without a phone. Not being able to use Bixby on an iPhone is irrelevant. Bixby needs internet and ties you to a phone, which is what you are supposed to be getting away from. If any of your complaints are I wish it would work better with my phone, then your complaint is irrelevant. You are supposed to get away from the phone with these.

Having said all that, the sound quality is above average for ear bud style headphones. The sound is crisp and clean whether via bluetooth or onboard music. The bass is lacking, yes, that is true. But, compared to other similar type headsets, it is at least on par. Overall, the sound is 4/5 compared to other ear buds I have tried.

Bluetooth wireless range is the best I have seen in similar products. I have had cheap wireless earbuds that disconnected 3 feet away. These are full on bluetooth range. If they are cutting out, its the other end of the Bluetooth connection at fault. Its not these earbuds, they are solid. You can transfer songs via Bluetooth, but I believe it has to be one at a time. This is tedious. Again, you are better off using the PC. Connection range and strength 5/5.

Control via hardware is fine. It does take a little practice, but it is better than adequate. Again, the whole point is to get away from the phone. I do wish you could control the songs onboard via a watch. You can stream from a watch and control that just fine. I do not think it is a big ask to be able to control the onboard songs via a watch (certainly from a Samsung watch). Part of the pitch with these is that they are workout ready. I dont want to have my phone when working out (although you can control the onboard songs via the phone). However, I do wear my watch (also pitched for workouts). Only makes sense that the Icon X and (Samsung) watches play better together. I hope they add this feature in the future. Controls 4/5.

Battery life is more than adequate (2018 version) for the intended use. These are not meant to be all day wear. These are meant to be for occasional use. Primarily these are for workouts. When kept in the included charging box (which is highly recommended), these are good for 7 days or more of workouts without having to recharge the box. In this context, no one has a legitimate complaint about battery life. All week battery life for workouts is really way more than anyone can ask. Battery life 5/5.

Comfort is really going to depend on your ear. Everyone has different ears. I found that the ear canal part was fine out of the box. I did change the other piece to the smaller set. I think they fit rather well, again, considering that these are for shorter term use. Comfort 4/5.

The onboard storage is waaaaaay more than needed. Honestly, you dont want more than a couple hundred songs on board at most. I find that 60-75 songs work best. Again, I want this experience to be simple and easy to use without the phone. Too many songs can be a burden to scroll through and find that one song you want to hear right now. Do yourself a favor and dont try to load your entire library on board. Just keep your favorites on the buds. It is better to have bulk storage on a watch (or phone if you want to have that on you for workouts), as it is much easier to find and play songs in a large catalog on these. Storage 5/5.

Final thoughts. If you understand this product and how to best use it, it is hard to deny that Samsung has hit a homerun with these. They have it where it counts. If you are an audiophile, these are not for you. If you want to have and control huge libraries of music, these are not for you. If you want all day every day wear, these are not for you. If you insist on doing everything from a phone, these are not for you. But within a certain space for a certain user, these are the best on the market. Yes, they are pricey. I feel that these should be closer to $100 mainly because these definitely purpose, and not something you will want to use all the time in every situation. Overall 4.5/5.

title: This watch is underwhelming.
text:
Holding this watch in your hand, it feels cheap.
The faces you can choose from are awful...
Out of 30 faces maybe 4 were cool.
Faces are extremely tiny, hard to read.
The band is a super cheap stretchy rubber.

It functions. Tracked steps and the GPS worked.
Sleep tracking didn't work right.
If you have an apple phone don't waste your time and money, just get an apple watch.
If you have an android phone, get a Samsung watch simple as that.
Don't waste time and money on third party crap.
Pass on this watch it is underwhelming.
Keeping it in hopes that an update will make it better.

title: Known issues that Samsung refuses to correct
text: 6 months ago I would have given this watch 5 stars...now not so much...and not because of any of its features. It is a great watch, but it has issues that Samsung refuses to correct. I bought my watch in December 2019. This summer the heart rate monitor cover came off. Samsung fixed it that time under warranty. In September it came off again and now they are trying to charge me for the repair though it is still under the 1 year warranty. You should know the back is held on by what can only be described as a rubber cement type product. I have never showered or swam in my watch and if I have gotten it wet it has been immediately dried off. If you google the issue, you will see this is a common complaint and Samsung seems to take screwing over its customers and its course of action on this instead of investigating a better solution. It's pretty sad since I was considering getting a Galaxy watch 3....now I'm not ever certain I won't be getting rid of all my Samsung products.

title: A million times better than Fitbit
text: I initially bought a fitbit for my son, but returned it after a day because it was so awful. I looked online for an alternative and found the Galaxy Fit 2. I have had other Samsung watches, but I didnt know that they had recently come out with the Fit 2. It was less than half the price of the fitbit and so so much better. The only thing my son would like is to be able to change the watch band. As it is still new we couldn't find any - but we'll keep looking. I'm waiting for it to go on sale again so I can buy one for myself. Very pleased!

title: Trash Watch.
text:
This watch was a major disappointment! I "upgraded" from a Samsung Gear S2 Classic that was purchased 4+ years ago to this and I was completely disappointed! My S2 Classic was able to outperform this watch in every aspect! Lets start with the battery life... Its complete Trash! Wouldn't last me a whole day, barely half. Where my S2 I was able to get a fulls day charge with moderate use (same usage) Moto 360 by the time I got home from work It was close to dying on me. There is really no apps that are able to be downloaded and to reply to messages using the watch is a pain in the butt! I honestly am going to return this watch, I went ahead and purchased the Samsung Watch 3 and can honestly say I don't regret spending the money on that even if it is an extra $200 which I got it on sale for $365 Price matched from Walmart thru Target. If you have the funds purchase the Watch 3, you're welcome! If not, buy youself a used or even new older Samsung Model, its light years away from what this is capable of...

The only nice thing I can say is the brown leather strap is pretty nice and also nice that they provide two sets of straps, regardless its not enought to persuade you to buy it.

title: Not worth it if you're planning to save a few bucks, don't take the chance with a refurbished one.
text: I bought this refurbished one, the watch started to turn off unexpectedly every few hours, which I found suspicious, later on, it didn't start unless being charged, until eventually it turned off every 10 minutes and stopped turning on again. Charging the device made the screen studder and went into a boot loop. I managed to fully reset it, but it started doing the same a couple of hours afterwards. Apparently, several other users have the same issue, others start to experience the issue after several months. My suggestion is to avoid the risk, and get the newer Samsung Watch instead, as a new frontier 3 can still have these issues after a couple of months down the line, with no fix in the horizon.

title: Missing essential functionality - Not enough meat on the bone to justify the price.
text:
Missing some essential functionality - its obvious people like me are going to attach this camera to moving vehicles of various sorts. There is nowhere on the camera to attach a safety rope/lanyard/string... none... and what is worse, none of the included mounts have the little nub that goes in the hole next to the 1/4 standard camera mount.... this means when it vibrates, there is nothing to stop the camera from turning and unscrewing from the mount. The only mount that has the nub is the piece of plastic in the packaging that you throw away. Guess we can just hope it doesn't vibrate loose because you wont attach a lanyard to it. I expected more. For $350 less I could have had a Samsung Gear 360 and 128gb MicroSD. For $550 this should have included 128gb MicroSD and a 2nd battery. There is no way to power the device while driving down the road because that requires opening the side door. This also means no attaching an external mic while driving down the road because on a motorcycle the side door has to be open. = There just isn't enough meat on the bone to justify this price. Instructions are seriously lacking and/or inaccurate.
I cannot believe I can't attach a safety cable to a $550 camera... that's just pure engineering blunder. I figured the software would make a difference and get rid of some of the stitching weirdness you see in Gear 360 videos.... it does not! This camera doesn't use EIS stabilization in VR Mode either.... It does not over heat that I can tell, otherwise I think Samsung Gear 360 makes more economic sense based on capability and price.

title: So useless it ridiculous
text:
I know it's only $200 so I'm not going to critique this watch too much for stuff like it's lack of speaker. I have a Samsung galaxy s20 so I thought getting a Samsung watch would be a good idea, but it turns out that Samsung watches don't have Google apps so you can't download Google maps which makes navigation pretty hard and you still have to look at your phone. Bixby is trash, but I don't need to remind anyone of that and since this isn't a wear os watch you can't use Google assistant and get stuck with Bixby who is incapable of even turning up the volume on your phone which is bothersome because I thought my Samsung galaxy s20 was what this watch would be best with. I consistently have issues with changing the music too such as I cannot pause songs, but I can click play which makes it really awkward when you try to turn off your music, but it won't turn off. This really disappoints me because if I had spent the same amount I spent on the galaxy watch and phone pair instead on an apple phone and an apple watch I'd have a far better watch set up, Samsung is essentially telling us if we want a good OEM watch we have buy apple.

Update: at first I thought it was a one off, but it wasn't. Every time I control Spotify on my watch it breaks the Spotify app on my phone and I have to close Spotify like 4x and restart my Playlist in order to fix it, mind you this is on a galaxy phone with a galaxy watch... But besides that I went on a long bike ride with this watch and I realized that it has no use other than potentially tracking your workouts, but not the map of your workout. There was no Google maps so I had to take out my phone to navigate(even the navigation pro app I downloaded in order to fix the lack of maps didn't do a better job than Google speaking the directions from my phone and thus was promptly turned off). the music control on my watch caused the Spotify on my phone to stop responding so I just listened to whatever played. I will be returning and purchasing a $4 digital eBay watch because it will tell the time just the same as this watch,it is ridiculous how useless this watch has proven to be.

TLDR: even if you have a Samsung galaxy phone you will still see many different issues with this watch so I do recommend that you get a wear OS watch because it allows for far better functionality and the ability to download Google maps.

title: NOT a fitness tracker
text:
This device should not even be called a fitness tracker. It is more of an extension of your cell phone that has a few VERY basic fitness tracker functions on it. However there is no app for tracking or analyzing your data which makes this device pretty much useless as a fitness tracker.

The Samsung Gear Fit Manager app to make the thing work would not work with my phone. I had to buy a 3rd party app from the Google marketplace to even use this thing. It seems like Samsung wants you to have a Samsung phone if you want to use the Gear Fit.

This device is great if you want to read and send texts or e-mails from your wrist. But if you're looking for a full functioned fitness tracker, this is not it.

title: Disappointment
text: This watch worked great when I first got it. oct 2016 by mid apr 2017 I got an error message "Android wear is not responding" and the watch froze up. I tried everything to get it to come back on. after a few internet searches I found that this is a common issue with this watch. It is a software flaw and you need to send it in to get it fixed. I charged it all night and the next morning it came on but within 5 min I got the error again. This continued throughout the day. The next morning after a full charge again I was able to get to the menu to disconnect from phone and factory reset. That seemed to correct some of the issue. I didn't load any of the apps and just run the minimal. I still get the error once in a while but by clicking on "OK" it seems to restart android wear now and isn't freezing. I wrote a note to Ace Easy who I bought it from but received no response. I think I know why the manufacture discontinued this watch. I would not recommend this watch. I would look at Samsung gear or gear fit. disappointed

title: Expensive, Good, & Takes Some Getting Use To
text:
When did finding earphones to play music get so complicated? Remember the good old days when Sony earphones were just ten bucks? It was wired, fit in one’s ears, and did the trick. Now, it is just endless options and many of them are overpriced and aren’t even that great. I’ll keep my review short.

I have the Samsung S9 Plus & the included wired AKG tuned (Samsung branded) earbuds are fine, and I actually like it, but the wire has cotton fabric on the outside to make it less susceptible to getting tangled. However, when it does get tangled, it is actually very annoying to get untangled. Plus, it clings to clothes way more than usual earphones made of plastic/rubber.

I got these and I am happy with it overall, but it does take some getting used to. They fit great in the ear, appear well made, and look futuristic and sexy (unlike the Apple ones that are white, and look unattractive, like something flaccid). The sound is fine, but i do wish it was slightly a smidge louder with maybe a little extra bass. Please keep in mind the earbuds automatically start consuming battery once it is out of the charging case, so one has to be really careful on they how store these before actually putting them to use.

Pros:
Great design
Decent battery Life
Case doubles up as protection and charges the earbuds without a cord (functions as extra portable battery pack)
UBS C type charging port for quick charge
Functions has a solo MP3 player without a phone
Can be used for talking and making phone calls
Samsung Gear software already preinstalled on Samsung phones
Ten mins of charging gets a full hour of music play

Cons:
Takes some getting use to
Super small and paranoid that it can get lost at any min without delicate care
Battery starts working as soon as they are taken out of the case
No On/Off button
No USB C charging brick included (For this price tag it should have come with one)
No extra case (For this price tag it should have come with one)
Sound could be slightly louder with extra bass (Just a small smidge)
Expensive and overpriced
Amazon included this with 40lbs of kitty litter and item came smashed

title: Battery life is not great, good watch though
text:
Update... I am not sure what changed, possibly a software update did it. But I started getting much better battery life out of this watch. I just got in the habit of charging it every night and using super conservative power settings for pretty much everything, and before it was a struggle to make it all day in a charge. And then all the sudden it wasn't an issue anymore. Now when I put it on the charger at night if sometimes still has 80% battery left.

So a typical Samsung mobile device. Good hardware, bad software. But I updated my review to not be so critical because I don't have anything to complain about at the moment.

My original review below.

I bought this to replace an older Galaxy Watch that lasted me a few years. I was initially very happy with this watch.

But then after about 3 months, the battery life started going way downhill. When it was new, it could go 2-3 days before it needed to charge. Now, it can't even make it 12 hours before complaining about low battery - and that's with every possible power saving feature on. GPS is off. All the features that turn on the screen are disabled - the only way the screen turns on is by me pressing the power/home button. And even with the "battery saver" mode enabled, now today it is down to 5% battery after wearing it for 14 hours and barely even turning the screen on.

This model is crap when it comes to battery life. Stay away!

title: Great watch... if you don't use the features you originally bought it for.
text:
This journey started when my FitBit Charge 2 watch stopped working after 3.5 years; even worse when a hand me down Versa 1 didn't even last a month (didn't survive light dishwashing at home and its suppose to be waterproof!?!?) so I had to some smartwatch shopping for these past few months. Picked this up in November when there was a sale. As an Samsung Galaxy smartwatch user over the years, my selection was boiled down to the FitBit Sense, Garmin VivoMove 3 hybrid and this Samsung Galaxy Watch 3. Burned by the Versa 1 experience and bad reviews of the VivoMove 3 at the time, I went with the Samsung Galaxy Watch 3. Picked it mostly for the android compatibility, steps, sleep tracking, blood oxygen and blood pressure monitor features.

I've been using it for 2.5months now. To date, it still doesn't have the Blood Pressure Monitor feature. The Blood Oxygen monitor seems to match my pulse oximeter that I bought online but I wish it periodically automatically runs the tests. The steps and sleep tracking feature seems to working but I don't have a second device to compare it with. Battery life is good, as long as you keep the watchface off which is annoying and still something to get to use to. Flicking your wrist doesn't always turn the screen on. If you keep the watch face on, you can get about 24hrs out of it before you fully drain it.

During the pandemic, it didn't get much action or daylight unless I'm out doing food errands or coming in to work (as needed). While charging, I've inadvertently dropped it a couple times to a wooden floor from a very low height or got banged against the wall accidentally but it doesn't get scuff which is a good thing. My other pet peeve with this watch, just like my old Moto 360 watch, is how much it sticks up away from the wrist that it ALWAYS gets caught with the sleeve of my shirt, jacket, you name it. I normally do not have this problem my Seiko analog watches, including dive watches.

My biggest issue with the watch aside that it still doesn't have the blood pressure monitor feature, is the EKG reading app. It cycled through at least 30 attempts today just to get a reading, and yes, I was not moving. Very frustrating and annoying. I then proceeded to take my blood oxygen and stress reading afterwards and it came back with no stress. Now I know that feature is not working as well.

Also for an expensive watch, it doesn't come with a fast charger. I would rather have it come with a fast charger and a cheaper, more practical silicon strap than a leather strap. It would be nice besides being able to do on-demand reading of blood oxygen, stress and EKG that it could periodically automate to create a trend and not have to stop and worry about it.

title: A noble attempt, but ultimately falls short of excellence
text:
After getting bored with my Apple Watch Series 3, I tried a bunch of smart watches. The short conclusion is that the only thing that rivals the Apple Watch is the Garmin Fenix 5 or the 935. Nothing comes close. I have tried Samsung Gear 3, Fitbit Ionic, Suunto Spartan WHR Baro, Suunto Spartan Trainer and the Huawei Watch 2.

Huawei Watch 2 could have been a great alternative if it did not use Android Wear. Android Wear 2.0 (Oreo) is a complete drag still. In fact, at this I am fairly certain Google is going to put Android Wear out to pasture soon. The OS really struggles on the watch and the battery drain (even in Smart Power mode) is much higher than spec-d. This may have something to do with being paired to an iPhone as Android pairing apparently draws less power. The Huawei work out app is simple but pleasant to use and the display is good but not as good as the samsung gear s3. What Huawei did as a smart move is to use the analytics from First Beat and that gives them an edge. The sleep tracking feature (part of Oreo update) also works well. Suunto Spartan Trainer and WHR Baro are both good but the trainer suffers from a catastrophically poor display and backlight. The WHR Baro suffers from a very inaccurate and inconsistent heart rate sensor. Suunto used to use First Beat licensed software for computing calories but no more. Their in house calorie estimation is not as accurate.

After going through all these watches in the last 3-4 weeks, I would have to say, I underestimated how good a watch the Apple Series 3 is. The battery life is phenomenal, the user interface is intuitive, Siri works great ("Hey Siri, let's do an indoor bike ride") and the display is bright and easy to read. Where Apple fails for a serious endurance athlete is lack of power meter support (on the bike) and not detailed enough analysis of workouts. Although you can use RunGap to upload your workouts to Strava. Sleep tracking on the Apple Watch is decent using Autosleep app.

If you are an Android user, I would go with either the Samsung Gear Sport (S3 is HUGE) or better yet with a Garmin 935 or 735XT. Skip the Vivoactive 3, or the Fenix series (weight)

title: Not a bad watch for my 1st smart watch
text:
I am writing this review having never had a smart watch before (except 1 VERY BAD and CHEAP smartwatch, that lasted exactly 3 hours).

This Samsung Watch 4 40mm watch is almost everything I wanted a smart watch to be.
Setup took a LOT longer than I thought it would.
I had to update the watch through my phone using bluetooth, which was horribly slow.
I also had to update my phone to be able to access the watch updating properly (I think it was 3 apps for the phone to be able to update the watch, which took an hour), then I had to download and install a few more apps for my phone to be able to mod the watch and locate it if stolen and get watch faces.
Then there was the setup for each and every app that the watch needed, with tutorials (which I needed, since this is the first REAL smart watch I have owned).
All through bluetooth.
I think it took me 3 to 4 hours to get the watch, and my phone, totally updated to use (on my phone, I uninstalled anything I didnt need, or use, or want, long before getting this watch, so I had a LOT of apps to download and install from samsung just for my phone. If you have left your samsung phone with all the apps still on it and updated it all the time, then it will take you much less time than it took me).

I like EVERYTHING about this Watch 4, except 2 things:

1. Bixby. Piece of junk software that I dont need, dont want, and there is NO WAY to turn it off or get rid of the icon, or even disable it. I accidentally hit it 5 to 6 times in 2 days. Samsung, PLEASE at LEAST let us users be able to disable Bixby.

2. The band, or strap, or whatever its called. It would be great, IF it didnt make you put one side of the band UNDER the other side to lock it in. This pulls my arm hair, pinches my skin, and creates a bump under my wrist. Terrible, terrible design for a watch band. Its too bad too, because other than that, it is a comfortable watch band.

Other than those 2 issues, the watch is great.
After using it for a few days, and having Battery Saver on (which does 2 things. It makes the battery last for days. And, it also unfortunately disables a couple things I actually use, so I have to turn it off to use things I want, then turn it back on to save battery, which isnt as easy as it is on a phone).

The charge time is really, really fast (even during the 4 hour setup, which made the watch go down to 21%, it charged fully in just under 1/2 an hour).

I am disabled and have a lot of health issues, and I can use ECG and all the other health apps including sleep, during the day and night, and save the results to my phone which makes a chart for a month of all the results, which is a super feature, as I dont need multiple devices anymore, nor do I need to write results down anymore. To be honest, the ecg, bpm, pulseox, etc, is the main reason I bought the watch. And, the results are pretty close to what the Dr.s offices record, so its pretty accurate.

God forgive me, but I like being able to switch watch faces also. I wish samsung had a LOT more free watch faces, and I also wish they made it so that Ads dont play, but I love being able to go from stainless, to gold, to black, from analog to digital, to being able to actually customize where everything is displayed, and what I want to remove from being displayed. It is so much easier to look at my watch than to log into my phone to see the weather, or calander, or whatever.

Oh, and another thing I dont really care for, but is my fault, is the way to scroll. Never having had a smart watch before, let alone a samsung smart watch, I did not know that you scroll with the side of the screen. I keep launching apps I didnt mean to because of the scroll feature. I should have gotten the Watch 4 Classic, but I didnt know the wheel actually scrolled through everything until after I got the regular Watch 4. So, thats my bad, not samsungs fault.

I would definitely reccomend the Watch 4, especially for those with health issues. This is the best watch I have ever bought in my life. If you are an android samsung user, definitely pick one up.

title: Dont waste your money and buy the samsung watch instead.
text:
I really like the user friendliness and look of the watch. I dont like the dials on the side, they poke my wrist sometimes. The battery life sucks...lasts maybe a day if barely used. Works well with my Samsung phone. The metal scratched within a week of using. It charges kind of slow and it connects to the charger by like a magnet lying face up. The charger does not come with a block.

I ended up getting the new samsung rose gold watch as a gift a couple weeks later and it blows this out of the water. Samsung battery lasts me about 2 full days depending on how much its used, sometimes a little longer or a little less (min 1 1/2 days and max about 2 1/2 to 3).

title: I decided to sell them and buy a Samsung gear circle which is use everyday and love. However
text:
I wasn't going to post a review as I bought these several months ago and did not care for them at all. I decided to sell them and buy a Samsung gear circle which is use everyday and love. However, since Jaybird emailed me, and asked me specifically to review them, I figured I couldn't let them down.

First, they are way way over priced. If you want a set of bluetooth headphone that are okay but cost about $100 more than they should, then buy these.

Next, the sound is just mediocre. I don't understand the rave reviews about how great these sound, they really don't sound that good. I guess after you spend almost $200 on something you expect them to sound great so people want to justify their purchase and convince themselves they sound amazing. Again, just they sound okay.

And, if you have smaller ears they simply will not stay in, trust me, I tried. YMMV.

Bottom line, overpriced, mediocre pair of bluetooth earbuds. Nothing more, nothing less....period.

title: Borderline junk
text:
After reading all the reviews and watching youtube videos on this product I decided to buy it. That was a big mistake! I compared the Garmin 735xt to my Samsung Gear Fit 2 and I was very disappointed with the Garmin. This watch reminded me of a 70's era digital watch. I have no idea if the item I received was a legit Garmin product but, it seemed very cheap. The price its being sold for on here keeps bouncing up and down and to be completely honest. I wouldn't pay $5. for this watch.

title: Not a smart watch, definitely a tracker, but exactly what I needed.
text:
Let me first say that I am not a smart watch person. I had the Samsung Watch before and absolutely hated it. I was very resistant to switch from my faithful Charge 3 to a new device that seemed similar to a smart watch, especially one that clearly had features that hadn't been fully sorted out yet. At the same time, some of these features, like the 24/7 heartrate monitoring and improved data for sleep monitoring were definitely a draw.

I haven't had this device for months yet, and may update this in the future if I notice different things, but I will say if you go into this with the idea that you're buying a health tracker, you'll get about what you expect. This is not a fancy smart watch and if you want all the music features, additional apps, and things of that nature, this device is not for you. It doesn't function like a smart watch near at all. The music player is limited to Deezer, which I haven't tried. It allows you to navigate Pandora and Spotify on your phone through the device, which is literally the only feature I wanted a smart watch for when I was teaching dance, so for me it's perfect. Almost everything else focuses on your health.

Other reviews have commented on the press point to activate the watch being problematic, but I have had no issues. It doesn't always come on when I turn my wrist to face, which was the same issue with all my previous Fitbit devices. However, the press point to turn it on is basically the same idea as my Charge 3, so maybe that's why I have no issues there.

Not all the newly available metrics have obvious uses. The additional sleep data is very much up for interpretation, but if you keep a log with how you feel when you wake in the morning, any correlation becomes obvious. While I don't know how to interpret this data myself, it is something I've mentioned to my doctor and we will be reviewing on my next visit to help give us a bigger picture of what contributes to my poor quality sleep and other sleep challenges. This is one of the reasons I opted to upgrade when my Charge 3 was still working great and perfectly serviceable.

In additional metrics, we should talk about that EDA scan that's mentioned in all the reviews, but no one seems to understand. I'm actively trying to train myself to make use of that feature. A nifty little thing I've noticed, EDA responses don't appear when I'm under high stress, but they do appear when I've experimented with using the quick scan feature as a non-visual timer for deep breathing in the middle of a anxiety attack. While I don't know if this is reliable, it is giving my medical team more information to try and figure out what's going on, so this may have useful implication in the future.

Another note on the EDA quick scan, as I haven't done any of the guided sessions, it gives you information on your heartrate variability in comparison to your baseline, which I assume is taken from your sleep baseline. Below baseline indicates high stress where above indicates a relaxed state. For me, this has become a useful tool to make sure I'm getting enough exercise, which directly impacts my depression. I know some people out there say "just make sure you get XYZ amount of exercise every day." For me this is giving me useful data on how much and how often is enough to get that number up. Will a run or yoga in the morning be effective for the day? Does an evening routine impact the next morning? It's quantifiable data that I can use as a cue in building my own routine.

I also want to mention the other feature that sold me on this device, the 24/7 heartrate monitoring. Before the pandemic (and became increasingly difficult throughout the pandemic) I had taken up running. One of the things my training app asked was my heartrate during my run. Only problem was I could not accurately get that data as my Charge 3 needed me to remain stationary for an accurate heartrate read. While my heartrate on a run may not have been a necessary detail to include, not having that heartrate often meant exercises were missed and other activities that possibly should have flagged a response didn't. My active minutes have dramatically increased as a result of having more accurate heartrate monitoring. I am also getting a much more accurate look at my activity levels and my calorie intake versus calories burned. This constant data is a lot more useful for anyone who is monitoring their health during exercise, trying to get an accurate calories in/calories out record, and anyone trying to get an accurate picture of what their activity level is throughout the day beyond simply steps to include things previous trackers may have missed. It even picked up on 4 minutes of cardio levels while I was doing an excited sword demonstration for my kids while homeschooling. Is that 4 minutes useful time? Probably not, but it's good to put in my list of activities Fitbit wouldn't have noticed before, but logs now, which also includes dance and other movement exercises that weren't enough to trigger my Charge 3 to identify them as exercise. It was disappointing that 2 hours of dance 3 days a week would often only show up as maybe 30 minutes each day, and sometimes not at all. Those same practices (with an admittedly shorter 30 minute cap due to lack or stamina, thanks pandemic...) are being picked up in their entirety, even if my heartrate elevation is small, but enough to push it into registering in one of the "zones". This has helped a good deal in getting an overall portrait of my health and activity levels, which I'll be bringing to my doctor on my next visit.

The only thing I have found is I haven't been able to get 6 days of battery life out of it, generally landing at 4-5 days. For me this isn't an issue as I don't wear my device to shower and have had a habit since my original Charge HR (way back in the day) of popping it on the charger whenever I shower. This may also be why I don't have any effect from the device or band as my wrist gets regular breaks.

If you're primarily looking for a smart watch and just want something with some extra features, this will not be the device for you. While the extra health tracking is a great draw, it loses a lot of the functionality my old smart watch had, the notifications for things like texts don't always come through (which is fine for me because I don't want that) and the pay feature isn't fantastic. Again, that doesn't matter to me because it's a feature I don't intend to really use anyway, but had to test it out. The apps are limited, and options for music and taking calls aren't wonderful.

On the other hand, if you're looking for a device to give you detailed health information, to accurately log fitness activities, and increased metrics for sleep, this may just be the device for you. It's the best fitness tracker I've seen so far, and this is giving my medical team some new information to work with, which may or may not be useful in the long run, but looks promising. That EDA quick scan had potential for people who suffer clinical anxiety or even PTSD as it can be used to cue deep breathing, and the resulting data may result in creating a better profile of what's going on during an attack, or at the very least can result in creating a cue to take specific actions in a way that simply feeling in the moment cannot. Not all of this data will be useful for everyone, but it could be useful for someone who needs this kind of data to help understand their sleep patterns, anxiety levels, how activity, even in small amounts of a few minutes here and there, impacts their mental health, or even just someone who wants an accurate profile of their physical fitness throughout their exercises.

I was 100% resistant to the idea of yet another smart watch, but in the end I'm happy I made the switch. The added information I've gained has given me a lot more understanding of what's going on with me. I now have clues as to why nights I get plenty of quality sleep (according to previously going on just my sleep score) I wake up feeling tired, and I'm seeing a correlation with sleep temp particularly and low quality sleep. That 24/7 heartrate has been huge. I can now actually monitor my activity level instead of trying to track exercise my Charge missed (because I never turn on exercises prior to start as I never remember or can't figure out which fits best), and result in trying to figure out a routine that way. Everything is logged now, making it simple and accurate. The EDA scan is becoming a tool to manage my anxiety, and while the metrics may or may not be useful, it does cue activities which are useful, so even if it's no more than placebo effect, the end result is valuable.

What you will get out of this device will be very personal and it depends on how you use it, and what you expect to get from it. The Fitbit Sense can be a useful tool for you and your medical team, or even you and your trainer, depending on your goals. This device is definitely not for everyone, and the price point does not make it worth it for every user, but if you're looking for a tool to track your health, this one seems to be the best on the market to date.

title: Samsung Gear Fit Smart Watch
text: I have had 2 of these now, and both have failed after only 18 months' service.  They stop working and constantly reboot until the battery runs out.  I wear mine all the time but protect it from water damage, charge it daily with the recommended charging pieces, and don't drop it.  I think they should last longer for the price. While they last, they are great! It was easy to set up and use, the numbers are large and there is space to tap when needing to change something.  I have not found them to be long lasting!

title: Not worth full price - Novel for 360 pictures and video, don't expect high quality imagery.
text:
Full disclosure, I purchased this used on Ebay for about $60. This is my second 360 camera. I got one on a whim that was deeply discounted ($30) on amazon. It was cheap. And the images were bad. but it was fun. I "splurged" on the Samsung Gear 360 because I thought it would give me better images and video. So far I'm not terribly impressed. I'm just barely convinced I got my money's worth at $60. There's no way I'd pay $100 or more for this camera and be happy.

Pros:
The Bluetooth pairing with my Galaxy Note 9 was pretty easy
The interface is easy to use
I think 360 videos and images are fun.
You can create wide angle shots by using front or rear cameras separately.
It has a threaded tripod mount

Cons:
The image quality is grainy and fuzzy.
The video is just barely acceptable - don't expect sharp, vibrant images.
The camera itself is top-heavy - you're going to want to mount this on a selfie stick or tripod, or risk having it topple and scratch the lens housing

title: Battery life leaves something to long for
text: I purchased this after seeing several reviews that raved about the battery life on these watches. I was very disappointed after giving this a shot myself. Typically, I didn't get even a day's use out of the watch before it died. I wasn't using it for anything extra, either. I would typically only use it for notifications for my phone and then the step monitor. Because this didn't have the battery life I was hoping for, I will be returning the product and going back to my samsung gear.

title: Samsung Electronics Galaxy Watch 4 40mm Smartwatch
text:
I had high hopes for this watch. But after 2-3 hours and it refusing to set up no matter what I do, I'm over it. I can't sign into the app or the watch. Complete disastrous waste of my time. Maybe they didn't test it or who knows.

title: Short battery life, short band, no actually free watch faces
text:
After my Samsung Galaxy Watch Active 2 sustained water damage from less than a meter of fresh water, I had intended to purchase a Pixel Watch, Fitbit, or Garming smartwatch, but Amazon had a great deal on this 40mm Galaxy Watch 4 I couldn't turn down. I won't go into the positives too much, as there are numerous reviews talking about them, but it is far superior to the Active 2. The animations are smoother, the app compatibility is much greater due to using WearOS and not Tizen, the health features are better, and software support is until 2026. However, there are several negatives which I will list.

First is battery life, which is about 36 hours with the always on display set to off and a non-animated dark watch face. This is pathetic considering the Active 2 lasted over 2 days easily with the same settings. This means you will have to carry the charger with you everywhere if you are going on an overnight trip, as it is not QI compatible with all QI chargers, especially Anker brand.

Second is the watch faces. On previous Galaxy watches with Tizen OS there was a wide selection of free, nice watch faces. With the move to WearOS there are very few free watch faces, a good 99% have restrictions unless you pay, and due to the way WearOS treats watch faces, which are full-fledged apps, instead of just watch faces, app developers have access to much more of your data. Also, the number of included Samsung watch faces is few, even fewer than previous Galaxy watches. This negative will apply to all WearOS watches currently, however, but Samsung could have included a larger selection of first party watch faces considering the price of the watch. I will say that updates have added some new watch faces, like the Ball Watch Face, but it's not useful or desirable vs the kind of paid watch faces which have millions of downloads, and the Pixel Watch has many more included faces.

Third is the watch band. This version is for S/M wrists, not M/L wrists. This is not listed in the description.

If you can get this watch for under $140, and you don't mind charging it every day, and you don't mind using the included watch faces or paying for one, then you won't be disappointed.

title: Good looking watch, nice capabilities, but horrible battery life (only applies to 40/42mm models)
text:
I saw that these have the capability of taking blood pressure (although that's not yet available in the US) and decided that it would be worth "upgrading my Samsung Galaxy Watch 3 to this. That was a mistake. I have the 41mm Watch 3, so decided to get the 42mm Watch 4 classic, because I like the stainless steel body and rotating bezel. With the Watch 3, I would charge it while showering in the morning, then wear it all day, through the night for sleep tracking, and while exercising in the morning. It always had between 40-50% battery life left at that point. That translates to between 30-40 hours of battery life.

I got the Watch 4 Classic and made sure to have all of the exact same features enabled and disabled, and it never made it through the night. I would get it to 100% while showering, wear it through the day, and by the time I went to bed it would be down to 30-40% battery life or less. I estimate that I was getting about HALF of the battery life from the 42mm Watch 4 that I was on the 41mm Watch 3. That meant I could use it while exercising, or use it for tracking my sleep, but not both. I would have to charge it over night then use it during the day only. Either that or I would have to take it off and find a time to charge it during the day, which is also less than useful.

I thought that maybe I had WIFI or GPS on and they were eating the battery, but I checked and not only did I have those off, relying of the Bluetooth connection to the phone to provide those, I changed heart monitoring to every 10 minutes rather than continuous that I was using on the 3. I also didn't turn on any of the new features, such as snore detection, on the new watch to be able to get a fair comparison. I returned it and purchased the 44mm Galaxy Watch 4 (not classic) instead. I prefer the look of the classic, but the 46mm just seemed too big on my wrist. With the larger battery in the larger watch I'm getting about the same 40 hrs life that I got in the smaller Watch 4.

I wouldn't recommend the 40/42mm models, but the 44/46mm versions work well. Which to get is primarily a matter of taste, since the operating specs are exactly the same.

title: Took a chance with a lesser known brand & regret it
text:
I ignored tons of lower reviews when deciding which watch to go with & I regret it big time. Should have just went with the FitBit

Wrist Gestures - When I shake my wrist to wake/view my watch, 85% of the time it does not react. This causes me to have to press the button on the side to see the time, step count, or even see my notifications. While this may seem trivial, the other smartwatches I've had (Apple Watch & Samsung Gear) have both reacted to a simple flick of the wrist & when I do have a notification, it shows up when I glance at my watch.

System Update - System will not update for some reason. I get a message stating that the system could not update & when I attempt to retry, i get a little dead android guy & have to manually restart.

Going to attempt to contact support & will update my review if this changes.

title: Stay Away - A Pixel User
text:
How does Google let a product like this exist? Where to start:

1) Good luck getting up an running with this watch. It takes at least a day for all of the updates and 'bugs' to settle down.
2) Navigation is a mess. App take too long to start? The screen will lock and you go back to the start screen. Good grief.
3) Want to use the GPS? You'll be waiting a half hour for the standalone GPS to connect.
4) No offline playback with Spotify. Why even bother?

It does look decent, but that's as far as it goes. All of the YouTube or other reviewers must have used a superior model to what I have here, because my 6 year old Samsung Gear Fit runs circles around this. I wouldn't pay $25 for this let alone over $100.

title: Insane Battery Life
text: I owned Samsung watches before, but as soon as the warranty ran out, they stopped working. It really stings to lose hundreds of dollars. This watch, on the other hand (pardon the pun,) is amazing. It does everything they say it will and then some. I've only had it for about 2 weeks, but everything is working great so far. I would recommend it to anyone that is tired of paying 3 times as much for a watch that does about half of what this one does.

title: It will suck all your energy/time out of you and offer you frustration in return
text:
On Samsung forum, other people also reports that Samsung Gear watches keep disconnecting from their phone. It happened to me twice in 10 days. It disconnects every 2 or 3 days. The first time, I have not realized that it was disconnected until 4 or 5 days later. As a result, I have lost my exercise data for 4, 5 days.

What took me some time to try and did not help:
I tried to turn off the bluetooth on phone, and then turn it on. Did not help.
I tried to turn off the bluetooth on gear and turn it on. Did not help.
I tried to reboot my phone. Did not help.
I tried to reboot gear. Did not help.
I tried to unpair the gear and phone, turned out that is not possible.

If I am not mistaken, unpairing means factory reset. And I had to factory reset my gear and pair again -- this is very annoying as it takes a lot of time and all the exercise data since the disconnection are not backed up. Even restoring backup and re-installing all the apps updates takes some time. And you always have some manual steps to make the gear exactly the same as before the factory reset.

This is super annoying...deal breaker for a smart watch. And to the comment above, I will not factory reset my phone! There is nothing wrong with my phone -- factory resetting (and restore + manual adjustment) a smart phone takes HOURS!

I have always been reluctant to trying Samsung smart devices. The Gear Sport is my first attempt to try out Samsung. It did not disappoint me in disappointing me. Luckily, I did not start my journey with Samsung with its expensive Galaxy phone.

title: Great battery life, but otherwise terrible design and worth less than 1/4 the price
text:
Quick Summary:
The audio quality is only as good as true-wireless earphones less than 1/3 the price, connectivity is middling and requires multiple special apps, the physical buttons are stiff and from a low-end product 2 years ago, the charging case doesn't work well and is very hard to get open, and the fit of the earphones in the ears is only slightly above average. However they do have a lot of features, the passive noise isolation is good, and they have extremely long battery life for true-wireless earphones. I can't speak to the voice-pickup and voice-isolation of these, which seems to be an off-target feature for true-wireless headphones and their primary claim of quality.

Fit & Feel
----
The earphones themselves are a bit on the larger side for true-wireless earphones, but that's the trade-off for the battery life they provide. They also don't stick out of your ears like you're the Communications Officer from the Original Star Trek like Bose and Senhauser products do. They do fit into the arches of your ear very well and comfortably, and seat incredibly solidly. As long as you have the correct ear-tip that actually fits just into your ear canal as instructed, they fit solidly into your ear with no worries about them coming loose or falling out even under vigorous exercise.
The ear-tips are a bit oddly formed though relative to the industry standard. They have a more trapezoidal-shape to them rather than a partial-sphere-shape, and it's noticeable in how they feel within your ears. I'm not sure if the ear-tip shape is supposed to provide better passive noise isolation, but it has no noticeable difference for me, and it actually causes extra pressure within the ears. I've found that the best measure of in-ear headphone quality is how comfortable your ears feel during extended wear, and these Jabras have come up a bit lacking. I've tried some other true-wireless headphones and been able to wear them completely comfortably for 3+ hours sitting at a desk, at the gym, or on a run. With the Jabras, my ears start feeling a bit pressed on the inside at around 1.5 hours and I feel like I need to take a break from them to let my ears relax. I've heard others call this feeling an "itchy" feeling in the ear, and that also describes it well. This is roughly what I get from middling-quality in-ear headphones made by any manufacturer and not what I'd expect from a product anywhere near this price point.
These earphones also emit a slightly concerning heat from the inner side of them when in use. It almost feels like the battery inside each earphone gets pretty warm during use, enough that it's noticeable around the area of the earphone body where the battery contacts are located. I thought I was crazy at first, but I took them out to check, and the inner side of them was definitely noticeably above skin-surface temperature. It's not hot enough that it's uncomfortable, just noticeable and worth mentioning. It very well might be related to the "in-ear detection" feature that can be used to auto-pause since I don't see any visible sensors on the inside of the earphones other than the over-sized battery contacts that might be doubling as a resistive sensor.

Controls
----
The control scheme on these earphones is pretty much bland-standard for any Bluetooth device, you have an up and down button on one earphone, and a multi-function button on the other. The up and down button primarily control volume, but on a long press do next and previous. The multi-function button is largely a play-pause toggle, or when a call comes in it's a tap to accept and double-tap to reject button. If you're not on a call, you can also double-tap to toggle the HearThru mode, and long press to trigger the voice assistant. With an up and down button on an earphone that may be oriented at a different angle for every person, they've smartly added little tactile tick marks to indicate where the center of the up and down areas are. However instead of the buttons truly being oriented in something approaching an up and down, they're oriented along an upper-back to lower-forward diagonal that will never vary enough from individual to individual to actually approximate up and down or forward and back. Furthermore, standard control organization generally associates the same functionality with up and forward, and with down and back. This mixes that up so it always takes a second to think about whether you're pushing an "up" button or a "back" button when you press the upper-back button, buttons that would normally have exactly oppposite effects.
Unfortunately they seem to have tried to shy away from the capacitive/resistive touch buttons completely with this product, instead using stiff physical buttons. While this makes it less likely you'll accidentally press a button, it works very poorly for true-wireless headphones. Unlike most headphones, true-wireless headphones have no structure to resist you pushing the earphone deeply into your ear when you have to push a physical button. With stiff buttons, this problem is even worse. These earphones are clearly made by someone who doesn't understand that at all, because pushing any of the buttons at all requires enough force to be distinctly uncomfortable both in your ear canal and the area outside your ear. Double tapping actually becomes nearly impossible because each press moves the earpiece so significantly out of position and into the flesh of your head, that your finger motion is more than that necessary for a key on a standard computer keyboard. Releasing the button enough to then press it again while trying to blindly maintain your finger's orientation relative to the earphone is extremely difficult as a result, which is the very reason all modern manufacturers use touch-sensitive buttons that require minimal finger motion to tap and double-tap.

Battery Life & Connectivity
----
The battery life on these true-wireless headphones is hugely beefy compared to all other products in the market. Unfortunately that's not as big a benefit as most people would think, and it seems to largely be a necessity for support of some of the "unique" features they provide. Most true-wireless headphones have a continuous battery life of about 2-4 hours before they need to be recharged, depending on the product, the volume they're used at, the distance from the phone, and other various specialty features they may have. Listening to music sitting at a desk with my phone next to me and HearThru mode disabled, I've had these last 6 hours and still report 25% battery life remaining. However the HearThru mode that plays ambient noise thru the earphones to allow situational awareness seems to have a huge impact on battery life, doing the same with HearThru enabled put me down to 25% battery life in just 3 hours. However, considering this puts them on the upper end of the industry standard battery life even when a battery hogging feature is on, that's quite impressive. I haven't test what the impact to battery life is if the step counting mode is also turned on, or if you're mostly making phone calls instead of listening to music/podcasts. I also haven't tested the impact on battery when you're about 6 feet away from your phone on average and occasionally walk 20 feet away from it to grab something (like when I go to the gym). However the existing tests do seem to indicate a very respectable battery life could be expected.
Connectivity quality of these earphones is unfortunately a bit shoddy, though not as bad as budget true-wireless devices from years past. The earphones do consistently and unerringly connect to each other, which is much better than some products, but seem to have issues with the connection to the phone. This might be an effect of how they've been forced to setup their connection with the phone to support some of their "unique" features, but it's generally unacceptable. Like almost all true-wireless earphones, one of the earphones is a master device and one's a slave, with the slave earphone connecting only to the master earphone while the master earphone connects to the slave and whatever other devices your "headphones" are connected to. Like most Bluetooth headphones in general, this requires putting the "headphones" (the master earphone) into a pairing mode, then searching for it as a new device on your phone. Also like most nice Bluetooth "headphones" that support being paired with multiple devices simultaneously, once paired the "headphones" will maintain a prioritized list of devices with preference given to the most recently paired. Given the connection issues between the master earphone and the phone however, this process can be rather onerous. Almost 50% of the time I've used these earphones I've had to forget the device on my phone and repair it to access the "advanced" features it provides. While I've never had any issues that required re-pairing when just using them like regular headphones, any voice assistant functionality, HearThru toggle, or EQ setting changes (I've never tried the step counting), have required re-pairing the devices so the Jabra apps recognize it properly and provide these functions. This appears to occur because Jabra actually requires two apps be installed to use the extra features of their earphones, one app to modify the settings and one app to intercept what your earphones send to your phone so it can determine if that should modify your settings, toggle the customizable voice assistant, etc.

Sound & Audio Quality
----
For such a high-cost device, the playback quality on these earphones is suprisingly mediocre, even among true-wireless headphones. The customizeable EQ settings allow you to add a little more bass or treble, or whatever you feel might be lacking in default output of most headphones, but even cranked to the extremes of the EQ range, none of the differences seem hugely different from the default flat EQ. In comparison to low-end Bose wireless headphones (not true-wireless Bose) that cost less than these, these Jabra earphones sound completely standard rather than anything approaching high-end or premium like they claim. They do do a decent job of passive noise isolation, but once again it's only about average. A number of other average in-ear headphones, wired, wireless, and true-wireless, all have unnoticeably different passive noise isolation. This includes devices that are less than 1/6th the cost of these Jabra earphones.
It seems one of Jabra's major claims with these is that they provide premium levels of voice isolation with these earphones for when you're talking in a call. I'm not sure why they've decided to focus on this, since true-wireless earphones aren't really intended for this purpose and are notoriously bad at it during the best of times. I personally have no use for this, I'll switch to my Plantronics wireless headset that gives me 10x the quality for 1/2 the price if I need voice isolation from the mic. In fact, them implementing the isolation via 2 mics in each of the earphones working together seems like an odd choice, since most people jumping on a call where they'd need the voice isolation from the mic probably don't want to fiddle with independently putting two separate devcies in their ears and will likely want to put in only the single master device so you can better hear yourself, which won't give you as good of voice isolation. Furthermore, the focus on voice isolation from the mic seems to have strongly negatively impacted the HearThru feature that seems much more useful to me.

"Unique" Features
----
Alexa as a voice assistant. Unless you specifically want Alexa as your voice assistant, this actually makes the product worse. The Bluetooth standard has a common way to trigger the native voice assistant so almost any Bluetooth 5.0 device can do it without an app being required on your phone. Jabra is trying to make it configurable to select between triggering your native voice assistant (Siri or Google Assistant) and Alexa. This means they have to use and intercept a non-standard Bluetooth command from the earphones that gets interpreted by the settings app to trigger the right one. Given the extreme connectivity issues with this intercepting application, it means that instead of simply losing the ability to use some of the highly specialized features they provide, you're also losing the ability to trigger the native voice assistant when the connectivity fails. If they weren't supporting Alexa as an optional voice assistant, they could use the standard Bluetooth 5.0 command that would always trigger the native voice assistant.

HearThru. A great idea, allowing situational awareness by replaying the ambient noises without delay so you can effectively cancel the passive noise isolation. This isn't a unique feature to Jabra, other products like the Samsung Gear X have it also. Unfortunately Jabra's focus on voice isolation from the mics seems to have severely degraded this much more useful feature. The audio that comes thru when HearThru mode is enabled is tinny, slightly staticy, and quiet. It's bad enough that the feature is barely useful, only serving to let you hear particularly loud noises that would normally be reduced to minor background noises by the passive noise isolation of the ear-tips. Most importantly, this doesn't work well enough to enable it and actually have a conversation with someone without feeling like you can't hear yourself, meaning it doesn't implement Jabra's "Hear yourself in calls" feature for real-world interactions.

Hear yourself in calls. If you're actually trying to use both earphones while in a call, you are going to have problems with the passive noise isolation making your voice sound weird to yourself. It makes a lot of sense to replay your voice in your own ears in this case. If you're also going to have heavy voice isolation from the mic, it also makes a lot of sense to supply that audio instead of all ambient audio while you're in the call. This seems like a minimum necessary feature if someone is actually going to use these earphones for making calls.

Auto-pause on removal. An interesting feature that doesn't rely on connectivity with the app. Since the earphones only have a button capable of pausing on the right ear, this makes a lot of sense for the left ear. Since the button on the right ear is stiff and hard to press, it also makes some sense for the right ear. This feature could be made completely unnecessary if the buttons were less stiff and a way to pause were provided from both earphones like most products have.
