this requires putting the headphones the master earphone into a pairing mode then searching for it as a new device on your phone also like most nice bluetooth headphones that support being paired with multiple devices simultaneously once paired the headphones will maintain a prioritized list of devices with preference given to the most recently paired given the connection issues between the master earphone and the phone however this process can be rather onerous almost of the time i ve used these earphones i ve had to forget the device on my phone and repair it to access the advanced features it provides while i ve never had any issues that required re pairing when just using them like regular headphones any voice assistant functionality hearthru toggle or eq setting changes i ve never tried the step counting have required re pairing the devices so the jabra apps recognize it properly and provide these functions this appears to occur because jabra actually requires two apps be installed to use the extra features of their earphones one app to modify the settings and one app to intercept what your earphones send to your phone so it can determine if that should modify your settings toggle the customizable voice assistant etc sound audio quality for such a high cost device the playback quality on these earphones is suprisingly mediocre even among true wireless headphones the customizeable eq settings allow you to add a little more bass or treble or whatever you feel might be lacking in default output of most headphones but even cranked to the extremes of the eq range none of the differences seem hugely different from the default flat eq in comparison to low end bose wireless headphones not true wireless bose that cost less than these these jabra earphones sound completely standard rather than anything approaching high end or premium like they claim they do do a decent job of passive noise isolation but once again it s only about average a number of other average in ear headphones wired wireless and true wireless all have unnoticeably different passive noise isolation this includes devices that are less than th the cost of these jabra earphones it seems one of jabra s major claims with these is that they provide premium levels of voice isolation with these earphones for when you re talking in a call i m not sure why they ve decided to focus on this since true wireless earphones aren t really intended for this purpose and are notoriously bad at it during the best of times i personally have no use for this i ll switch to my plantronics wireless headset that gives me x the quality for the price if i need voice isolation from the mic in fact them implementing the isolation via mics in each of the earphones working together seems like an odd choice since most people jumping on a call where they d need the voice isolation from the mic probably don t want to fiddle with independently putting two separate devcies in their ears and will likely want to put in only the single master device so you can better hear yourself which won t give you as good of voice isolation furthermore the focus on voice isolation from the mic seems to have strongly negatively impacted the hearthru feature that seems much more useful to me unique features alexa as a voice assistant unless you specifically want alexa as your voice assistant this actually makes the product worse the bluetooth standard has a common way to trigger the native voice assistant so almost any bluetooth device can do it without an app being required on your phone jabra is trying to make it configurable to select between triggering your native voice assistant siri or google assistant and alexa this means they have to use and intercept a non standard bluetooth command from the earphones that gets interpreted by the settings app to trigger the right one given the extreme connectivity issues with this intercepting application it means that instead of simply losing the ability to use some of the highly specialized features they provide you re also losing the ability to trigger the native voice assistant when the connectivity fails if they weren t supporting alexa as an optional voice assistant they could use the standard bluetooth command that would always trigger the native voice assistant hearthru a great idea allowing situational awareness by replaying the ambient noises without delay so you can effectively cancel the passive noise isolation this isn t a unique feature to jabra other products like the samsung gear x have it also unfortunately jabra s focus on voice isolation from the mics seems to have severely degraded this much more useful feature the audio that comes thru when hearthru mode is enabled is tinny slightly staticy and quiet it s bad enough that the feature is barely useful only serving to let you hear particularly loud noises that would normally be reduced to minor background noises by the passive noise isolation of the ear tips most importantly this doesn t work well enough to enable it and actually have a conversation with someone without feeling like you can t hear yourself meaning it doesn t implement jabra s hear yourself in calls feature for real world interactions hear yourself in calls if you re actually trying to use both earphones while in a call you are going to have problems with the passive noise isolation making your voice sound weird to yourself it makes a lot of sense to replay your voice in your own ears in this case if you re also going to have heavy voice isolation from the mic it also makes a lot of sense to supply that audio instead of all ambient audio while you re in the call this seems like a minimum necessary feature if someone is actually going to use these earphones for making calls auto pause on removal an interesting feature that doesn t rely on connectivity with the app since the earphones only have a button capable of pausing on the right ear this makes a lot of sense for the left ear since the button on the right ear is stiff and hard to press it also makes some sense for the right ear this feature could be made completely unnecessary if the buttons were less stiff and a way to pause were provided from both earphones like most products have

title: Every feature I wanted.
text: Short answer: I bought this after being a Fitbit user for almost seven years. Fitbit products have gotten more and more unreliable. My Sense would not stay connected to my Galaxy Note 10+ for more than a few minutes, and the Sense was the only real replacement for the Ionic after they stopped selling that watch.  The Galaxy Watch 3 does everything I wanted it to do, and stays connected to my Note 10+ almost intuitively! I have yet to try and connect the watch to LTE via Verizon, but should be trying to reach Verizon soon to do so.  The running GPS is great. Having my Samsung Buds connect to either my phone or my watch for workouts is even better, and so easy! Much better than trying to connect my buds to the Fitbit for sure!  If you don't care for Apple products and need something a little more reliable than Fitbit, this is the answer.

title: SNAKE OIL!
text: I'm at minute 52 waiting for someone at Samsung's 24/7 (!) desk to provide an answer as to why my Galaxy Watch Active 2 suddenly went black after only 4 months of abuse-free functioning. Adored wife bought this for me for my birthday to assist me in keeping tabs on my heart rate, but that function counts only beats-per-minute, which I can do myself. As for the remaining apps, I'd rather have had a GPS mapping & backtracking function, as well as tide and altitude monitors, since sleep quality and caloric intake are not high on my list of priorities in a wristwatch. Still, the watch does tell time and date, and the Bluetooth capability makes it easy to find if you misplace it, as well as notifying you of calls/messages in the unlikely event you should ever be away from your cell phone. Probably great for those who live continuously amidst concrete.

title: Good Budget Smartwatch - Setup/App Not So Great
text:
Let's start off with a quick statement about this watch. I purchased this on sale for right around $35, so my expectations were pretty low to start with. If you are looking for high quality and a lot of features, this is not a watch for you. Also I would like to mention that I am using an Android Phone (Samsung S22). With that in mind, here is my review, Pros/Cons first then a brief summary:

Pros:
- Price: If you can pick this up on sale, this is a good deal. I was able to get mine for $35, which was about 50% of the price. I feel like I could work in this and not worry about breaking it like I would a higher priced watch.
- Batter Life: The battery seems to last quite a while between charges. I'm going on about 4 days now without charging.
Comfort: The smaller face on this watch makes it a bit more confortable than others I've used. The band is soft and curves well around my arm.
- Looks: This does not look bad for a cheap watch. You can tell that the band is not high quality, but as far as looks, this is not a bad looking watch.

Cons:
- Functionality: This has very few features. If you are wanting something that you can type on, talk through, play games on, ect, then this will sorely disappoint. It has the basic health type features (heart rate, O2, sleep monitor, steps...stuff like that). It has the ability to receive text and weather, but you really can't do anything with it other than view.
- App/Setup: This is the biggest gripe from me. It was a pain to set up compared to some of the more expensive ones. First, the app is very basic, with little to no help getting things working. My weather did not work initially, it would crash the app. It started off in C when I wanted it in F. I thought I could change it in the watch...no you change it in the app by clicking the temp. I finally got that working only to find my messaging wouldn't work. After messing around with all of the various permissions, it started to work. Definitely not something that I had to do with my Samsung watch.
- Movement detection: There are times when I go to move my arm and the watch face acts as though I want to change it (it gets small and vibrates). Not sure what that is...probably something I could turn off in the app.

When I first started to set this up, I was pretty disappointed in it. The weather did not work, the watch settings were very basic, the settings were set up in a way that I really did not like, and the app was not very friendly. The setup is not user friendly at all, but once things are set up, the watch works great! All of my features are working now, so I shouldn't have to really get back into the app unless I want to change my watch face.
The features of this watch are limited, unlike watches like the Samsung and Apple brand, but you also pay for those features. Me, I like simple. I have a phone that I can use for things like games, texting, and calls. This has just enough for me to monitor when I need to. If that is not what you're after, pass this one up.
I would recommend this if you can pick it up for under $40. It is my opinion that this is a good budget watch if you want something you are not afraid to break or replace. I'm giving it 4 stars, with the note that this is taking out the trouble with the app and only focused on the watch functionality. I've rated this in the app store already.

title: Disappointed in some features not correct/working.
text:
I started out giving this watch five stars, but have downgraded it to three stars because of overall functionality. The mileage walked was never correct when compared to the step stride and known distance walked. The message notification worked for two days and then failed. Three reinstalls of the app and three watch restarts couldn't solve the problems. The 24/7 customer service means getting a response about 24 hours after asking for help. The "suggested" help was limited to very basic suggestions. Sure wish it had worked.

After looking at Samsung watches, this watch was a great alternative for soft and functionality. The manual is not very intuitive when it comes to resolving setup issues. title: Not a great being for the buck...
text:
After owning a previous pair of bluetooth headphones (JAMS Transit) and accidently misplacing them, I figured it would be a good excuse to see how well the True Wireless headphones were - I feel like I should have waited a few more months. While the sound quality is great There is much to be desired.

The software/app that is supporting them is useful for configuring the headphones, but when trying to get the Samsung Gear app along with the S Health app took a little bit of fiddling. I finally got it to work, but noticed at the end of my first workout (freeweights and machines) it labeled it as Walking and gave me on option to change that.

Another nice attempt to implement was the Ambient Sound option so that you could hear your music as well as what was happening around you. The only problem is that most things sound like you are hearing them through a tin can. while your at the gym, this feature could (figuratively) make your ears bleed when mixxed with the sound of clanging weights.

My final observation and my most disappointed one - the battery life. I'm a big samsung fan and enjoy their products, but I should have read more reviews on these before buying them. Even on a full charge I cant seem to get more than an hour or so out of them before the Low Battery notifications come up. The container they are stored in also doesn't help hold any kind of charge, so if you get them ready and don't use them for even a couple of days, don't plan on using them very long. My previous JAMS could last 8-10 hours on a full charge and they were a 3rd the price. Like everyone these days, battery longevity is critical. I know they are a True Wireless system, and maybe it will take a little more time for them to develop better quality battery life, but I can't help but rate it based on what you have to pay for it.

title: Battery Life is the AWFUL and Samsung Customer Service even worst
text: From the get-go, battery life on the Galaxy Watch 3 was the pits. I tried everything - turning and off apps - with no satisfaction. I would have to wear this watch with all APPS OFF to get more than 24 hours battery life; otherwise I sometimes charging (2.5 hours at a time) several times a day. I purchased the watch directly from Samsung (BIG MISTAKE) and contacted then after 2 or 3 weeks of struggling with the watch. The person I spoke with would not guarantee that my issue was covered by warranty (it is) and tried to direct me to Best Buy for service even though the only Best Buy that services this watch anywhere close to me is two state and a 6+ hour drive away (12 hours total)  - a drive which undertaken in the middle of a pandemic would have required me to quarantine for 14 days in the state serviced as well as my home state on my return. The customer service rep I "chatted" with would not connect me with his manager, would not give me a phone number where I could speak with someone directly, would not send me a copy of our chat transcript (had to copy and paste it  into another document). I am still struggling with the watch and battery life - a situation made worst everytime an update changes my settings. I cannot dis-recommend this watch and Samsung enough BUT if you are not convinced, then  be sure to purchase it from Amazon as the Customer Service  is MUCH better and returns far easier to make.

title: Not Accurate.
text:
I'm going to keep this review simple, I have been using smart watches for years, I have used fitbits Samsung watch apple and other generic brands; But this has worst performance. I wore it normally like my other smart watches, for three different days and all day and night... The first day it only register 200+ Steps, and 27 calories i walk 9000 to 10,000 daily, the other two days wasn't that much different. The heart rate didn't register until day three. Maybe it's a defective product, but other people have reported similar issues....that if it doesn't happen immediately like mine did it'll happen sooner or later.
Pro: it's lightweight, colorful screen, beautiful easy to read, instructions are straightforward and very easy, the app works great
Cons: Steps, calories are not accurate, sleep and heart rate didn't even register.

title: No water resistance, at all.
text:
The watch functions as advertised, and for the first two days I was very happy with the purchase. Then I accidentally wore it in the shower for about 2 seconds before remembering to remove it. After that the watch was completely ruined, I could not use it even as a normal analog watch. Luckily it was in the Amazon return period, so I returned it and got a Samsung Galaxy watch instead.
Don't buy this, because eventually you'll accidentally get it wet, and then it will become a paperweight.

title: TRASH! Not really effective at being wireless
text:
Update* the right earbud now only holds a 10 minute charge before warning low battery then shutting off in a minute. Absolute garbage.

Planned on using these for working out and leaving my phone at a fair distance... that's not possible. The bluetooth connectivity works a MAXIMUM of six feet and that's with intermittent cutting out. Even with my phone in my pocket. And the phone is a Samsung S9+ before I got these buds I had been using a set of skullcandy bluetooth neck bands. Even those have a range of over 50 feet! The selling point is wireless? I was willing to accept a mediocre SQ, which they are 3 out of 5 in musical reproduction. Pairing was simple enough. Battery life is as advertised. But, why bother releasing something that can't free the consumer from having to hold the phone close? I plan on returning and ordering the Samsung Gear IconX 2018, at least I know personally from 3 friends that they work all the time.

title: One out of two I own died within first year
text:
Good sound quality and battery life, but one out of two pairs died in less than a year. JLabs replace them without problem, but still 1 out of two died within a year. I had better expectation for that price.
Also outside signal drops a lot when you simply turn your head left or right. I was using them during periodic walks/runs. Signal transmitted from Samsung Gear 3 watch on the left hand while receiver is actually behind your right ear (basically your body is blocking light of sight while no walls around to bounce signal). I am sure in the building signal is more reliable.
Two stars only for durability.

title: Disappointing - poor job tracking steps
text:
I prefer the Samsung/Google platform to Apple, so I was excited to hear about the Galaxy Watch Active when I decided it was time to upgrade my trusty Fitbit Alta, which I had worn for 3 years. I preordered and picked mine up the day that BestBuy shipped them to the stores, so I've been using it daily for about 3 months, and I'm disappointed.

First the good - it's small, light, round, and comfortable. I rarely notice that it's there. I also like that there are many watch faces available for a reasonable price.

Unfortunately however, it does a poor job of tracking steps, which is the primary reason I bought it. The Galaxy Watch Active only records steps taken with swinging arms. This means that steps taken while pushing a grocery cart or carrying something in my arms are not recorded. After 3 years of wearing a Fitbit, I know my habits, and how many steps I take in an average day, and the Galaxy Watch Active records approximately ⅓ fewer steps daily. Disappointing.

And, forget about tracking flights of stairs. I'm lucky if my watch records 1 flight daily, and I regularly do at least 10.

Also, I expected the Galaxy Watch Active to be capable of engaging in phone calls, like my husband's Apple Watch. Not so. While I like that I can reject calls from my wrist, when I answer there, I am directed to my phone. There is no audio on my wrist, so what's the point of being able to answer there? Pointless feature.

In addition, I was deceived about the blood pressure monitoring capabilities of the Galaxy Active in the pre-sale marketing hype. I'm still waiting on that in a stable and reliable format.

And also, I do really wish that the Galaxy Watch Active had the EKG function, like the Apple Watch. My health insurance is no longer covering that in routine physical exams, so it's pretty nice to be able to run it in the doctor's office on the Apple Watch. I also wish that I received high stress warnings, like on the Apple Watch, encouraging me to breathe mindfully.

Incredibly annoying is the bright green light that constantly flashes against my wrist while wearing the watch comfortably loose. It shines in my and my husband's eyes at night, in movie theaters, at shows… it's quite distracting. The Apple Watch doesn't do this.

Not as important, but noteworthy, are the dreadful (Bixby) voice to text and texting capabilities of the Galaxy Watch Active. While acceptable on my Galaxy phone, they're barely tolerable here. I expected a similar experience device to device.

And I should mention that while it was quick and easy to replace the watch band of my Fitbit Alta, this is quite cumbersome. The band cannot be replaced quickly or easily. Even with my small fingers, working with tiny pins is tedious. I have to use needle nose pliers and tweezers. Poor design.

Finally - battery life. I have never been able to get 48 hours from a single charge. I must charge every day. But on the bright side, it usually only takes about an hour to bring it back to 100% from 50-60%, so that's not too bad.

I really wanted to love this device, but a step tracker that doesn't accurately track steps is pretty disappointing.
full_text:
Disappointing - poor job tracking steps. I prefer the Samsung/Google platform to Apple, so I was excited to hear about the Galaxy Watch Active when I decided it was time to upgrade my trusty Fitbit Alta, which I had worn for 3 years. I preordered and picked mine up the day that BestBuy shipped them to the stores, so I've been using it daily for about 3 months, and I'm disappointed.

First the good - it's small, light, round, and comfortable. I rarely notice that it's there. I also like that there are many watch faces available for a reasonable price.

Unfortunately however, it does a poor job of tracking steps, which is the primary reason I bought it. The Galaxy Watch Active only records steps taken with swinging arms. This means that steps taken while pushing a grocery cart or carrying something in my arms are not recorded. After 3 years of wearing a Fitbit, I know my habits, and how many steps I take in an average day, and the Galaxy Watch Active records approximately ⅓ fewer steps daily. Disappointing.

And, forget about tracking flights of stairs. I'm lucky if my watch records 1 flight daily, and I regularly do at least 10.

Also, I expected the Galaxy Watch Active to be capable of engaging in phone calls, like my husband's Apple Watch. Not so. While I like that I can reject calls from my wrist, when I answer there, I am directed to my phone. There is no audio on my wrist, so what's the point of being able to answer there? Pointless feature.

In addition, I was deceived about the blood pressure monitoring capabilities of the Galaxy Active in the pre-sale marketing hype. I'm still waiting on that in a stable and reliable format.

And also, I do really wish that the Galaxy Watch Active had the EKG function, like the Apple Watch. My health insurance is no longer covering that in routine physical exams, so it's pretty nice to be able to run it in the doctor's office on the Apple Watch. I also wish that I received high stress warnings, like on the Apple Watch, encouraging me to breathe mindfully.

Incredibly annoying is the bright green light that constantly flashes against my wrist while wearing the watch comfortably loose. It shines in my and my husband's eyes at night, in movie theaters, at shows… it's quite distracting. The Apple Watch doesn't do this.

Not as important, but noteworthy, are the dreadful (Bixby) voice to text and texting capabilities of the Galaxy Watch Active. While acceptable on my Galaxy phone, they're barely tolerable here. I expected a similar experience device to device.

And I should mention that while it was quick and easy to replace the watch band of my Fitbit Alta, this is quite cumbersome. The band cannot be replaced quickly or easily. Even with my small fingers, working with tiny pins is tedious. I have to use needle nose pliers and tweezers. Poor design.

Finally - battery life. I have never been able to get 48 hours from a single charge. I must charge every day. But on the bright side, it usually only takes about an hour to bring it back to 100% from 50-60%, so that's not too bad.

I really wanted to love this device, but a step tracker that doesn't accurately track steps is pretty disappointing.

title: So Many Missing Features!
text:
I'm not sure I would buy this watch again. My past smart watch was a Samsung Gear Fit2 and I have to say it had so many more functional aspects than the Versa. I can't believe that Fitbit hasn't integrated some of the features that you'd automatically expect it to have. And when you comment on the Fitbit website about the various missing features their comment is "We'll take this into consideration." However them taking it into consideration doesn't ever translate into action. I've been waiting for the Versa to be able to silence phone alarms since I bought this product months ago. Notifications and vibrations are not constant. When you set an alarm on your Versa - You get three short vibrations and then it automatically snoozes for 9 minutes! These three short vibrations aren't enough to wake me up so I always have to have a backup alarm set on my phone.

The fact that you can't store watch faces drives me bonkers! I love changing my watch face with the season, but with Fitbit once you change your face you have to go searching through the hundreds of other faces just to get the one you used back. No favorites, no saving faces to the Fitbit app. Very poor user features. This is another issue that Fitbit responded back with "We'll take this into consideration". But still nothing changes.

While the watch is nice and functions as a step tracker sometimes - I'm not sure how accurate it is. I went on a bike ride yesterday - set the watch for bike ride yet the watch clocked me having walked 11,000 steps. I didn't step one foot off the bike in over an hour yet it clocked me walking 11,000 steps.

In a nutshell - I think Fitbit as A LOT of work to do on this model. I wouldn't recommend buying it until the issues are fixed.

title: NOT the Smartest watch

I'm in the medical field so I know my fair share about HR, Sp02 and sleep data..

Watch is paired to my Samsung note 20 ultra

title: Tried to like it, but ended up hating it
text:
I am shocked at how horrible this watch is. I was using an Android Wear watch (the Samsung Gear Live) before picking up the Gear S on this weekend, and just 3 days later I'm more-than-ready to return it to T-Mobile. Here are the several issues I've encountered:

Notifications: first, and most irritating, the watch is completely worthless if you want to use Hangouts and Gmail as your SMS and email apps. You can get notifications for new messages, but you can't respond to them (see below under "Apps" for more info). And, the notifications you do get are very messy: emails run on and on; you can't tell where one message ends and another begins, and you have to swipe around a bunch on the interface to see everything. Swipe here, tap there, swipe there again. Not very user friendly, or quick.

Voice control: S Voice is SO BAD. Coming from using "OK Google" on the Android Wear watch, I'm used to being able to basic things like set reminders and check sports scores. You simply cannot do that with S Voice. If I want to check sports scores I would need to pull up the Opera Mini web browser (not included by default) and Google search it using the mini on-screen keyboard. (If you want to get technical, I could press a button on the keyboard that would allow me to use voice-to-text with S Voice... but seriously?)

Apps: There are some cool standalone apps on this device -- some cooler than some you'll find on Android Wear. Yelp is great, and Opera Mini is pretty nifty. But, on my Android Wear watch I had Trello, Evernote, IFTTT, and others that are NOWHERE to be found on the Gear S. Also, app integrations with the apps on my phone are horrible. Nearly every time I tell my Gear S to open a notification on my Note 4, it will open the appropriate app, but it will rarely load the actual "item" that the notification was referencing. Also, Samsung wants you to use their default Messages and Mail apps, otherwise you can't interact with the notifications. Nope... not doing it. I have already gotten accustomed to using Hangouts with my Google Voice number -- I like the fact that I can text people from within Gamil on my computer, and already had all my friends switch to my Google Voice number in their address books. I am not switching back.

Battery life: my Android Wear watch claimed to get a day's use out of it, and it does. The Gear S claims to get 2 days. It gets 1 day, if I use it at all. If you don't use it, it might last 2 days -- today, for example, I've touched it hardly at all because I hate it. After 9 hours it's at 75% -- so, 2 days might actually happen!

Call forwarding: also, there is the issue with forwarding calls from my Note 4 to the watch. It does work, but every single time my watch disconnects from the Bluetooth connection with my phone and engages the cellular antenna, I get a very large error on the watch's "home screen" saying that Call Forwarding cannot be enabled. I called T-Mobile support, who was unable to figure out why I am getting that error; they referred me to Samsung support who was also unable to figure out why I am getting the error. Very frustrating! And dismissing the error (which pops up every few minutes) requires me to tap on the watch.

Hardware: the one thing I like about it. Actually, since I have a big wrist, I do really, really like the physical design of the watch. It fits perfectly on my wrist and the curve is very nice. The strap is the better than the Gear Live (it has a metal clasp). Also, the charging cradle doubles as a battery so you can actually charge the watch while away from a power source. Super nifty! Too bad the software is completely awful!

The screen: it claims to have an "always on" screen. I call B.S. The screen is always on... if you flick your wrist toward your face. For comparison, Android Wear watches are TRULY always on, meaning, they always show the time. I thought this was REALLY deceiving of Samsung in their marketing because I expected that the watch would always show the time, even when it's not pointed at your face. About 20% of the time when I turn my wrist to look at the watch, it doesn't recognize that motion and therefore won't show me the time. Very annoying. At heart it's a watch; I want it to show the time. Other than this, the screen is very nice and the colors are fantastic.

Navigating the software: you must use your finger. As I alluded to above, voice control is not this watch's specialty. It is very much tied to your finger via swiping and tapping, and the menu system is not the most intuitive. It's not horrible, but it really does feel like you're using a miniature computer on your wrist. "User friendly," "quick," or "easy" are not key words I would use to describe it.

Despite my best efforts at trying to like this watch, I just ordered a new Android Wear watch (I'll be trying the Sony SmartWatch 3 this time, because the Gear Live kept dying on me and the Google Play store kept having to replace it), and I'll be taking the Gear S back to T-Mobile as soon as the SmartWatch arrives.

their address books i am not switching back battery life my android wear watch claimed to get a day s use out of it and it does the gear s claims to get days it gets day if i use it at all if you don t use it it might last days today for example i ve touched it hardly at all because i hate it after hours it s at so days might actually happen call forwarding also there is the issue with forwarding calls from my note to the watch it does work but every single time my watch disconnects from the bluetooth connection with my phone and engages the cellular antenna i get a very large error on the watch s home screen saying that call forwarding cannot be enabled i called t mobile support who was unable to figure out why i am getting that error they referred me to samsung support who was also unable to figure out why i am getting the error very frustrating and dismissing the error which pops up every few minutes requires me to tap on the watch hardware the one thing i like about it actually since i have a big wrist i do really really like the physical design of the watch it fits perfectly on my wrist and the curve is very nice the strap is the better than the gear live it has a metal clasp also the charging cradle doubles as a battery so you can actually charge the watch while away from a power source super nifty too bad the software is completely awful the screen it claims to have an always on screen i call b s the screen is always on if you flick your wrist toward your face for comparison android wear watches are truly always on meaning they always show the time i wrist to look at the watch it doesn t recognize that motion and therefore won t show me the time very annoying at heart it s a watch i want it to show the time other than this the screen is very nice tapping and the menu system is not the most intuitive it s not horrible but it really does feel like you re using a miniature computer on your wrist user friendly quick or easy are not key words i would use to describe it despite my best efforts at trying to like this watch i just ordered a new android wear watch i ll be trying the sony smartwatch this time because the gear live kept dying on me and the google play store kept having to replace it and i ll be taking the gear s back to t mobile as soon as the smartwatch arrives

title: DO NOT BUY!
text: I bought this for my husband as a Christmas gift. He was torn between this and the Fitbit Charge 2, and now he regrets his decision. The Gear Fit 2 just stopped charging one day. When we contacted support, we were told that we have to ship the device in to a service center and they will hopefully have it back to you in 10 business days. If you have an Apple watch, you can take it to the Apple Store for troubleshooting. If you have a Fit Bit, they troubleshoot and if they cannot resolve they immediately ship you a replacement. From my additional research the majority of Samsung Gear Fit 2 customers indicated that when they ship it in for service, they tell them that the charging issue is due to corrosion and is not covered by warranty. So basically if you sweat wearing a fitness device, it is out of warranty. This experience has proven to me that I will never purchase another Samsung device.

title: THIS IS THE ONE
text: I must have sent back a half of dozen smart watches back to Amazon. I had the Samsung gear 1, 2, and 3. The battery life was not good and must be charged daily. Not this watch. I went on vacation for over three weeks and came home with 50 percent charge left. It does everything you want and only has to be charged once a month or less. You can always see the time on the face but if you twist your wrist, it lights up. I"m a senior so I chose a large numeral screen. This is by far the best watch I have as a daily user. THIS IS THE ONE

title: pimptastic headphones (not so much)
text:
Updated review after a couple weeks use:

It is ok. But lately it's been dropping signal even when I'm holding my galaxy note 4, so its not because of distance. And the note 4 has the latency software you say works with these headphones. Btw I've never had Bluetooth latency, not even on a cheap 20$ pair of Bluetooth headphones.

It has really bad switching between my iPad air and my note, to the point that its not useful. Check out the Samsung gear headphones to see a really well done multi machine connection setup, its seamless, so I know it can be done right and cheaply.

It barely works on my iPad, every time I start a new show it drops audio but not the connection so I have to turn them off and on, or reconnect in settings. This is unacceptable.

It's got great battery life, and its got decent sound, but it's not pro anything. Its Bluetooth and so I give these a lot more room for failure because Bluetooth cans are not being made in as much quantity as jaybird knockoffs, but maybe that'll change.

Worth 100 bucks? Maybe. It does what you say it does, just barely and not very well.

For example my 30$ samsung gear headphones are better quality wise in every way, but they're not cans. They connect to all devices equally and switch fast and seamlessly.

I doubt seriously your pro edition is any better than your 50 dollar option, my guess is its exactly the same quality and same issues.

So while not being horrible, they're not exactly awesome. And I feel cheated for getting the more expensive option when they're most likely the exact same as the cheaper edition, I mean if they were worse you couldn't actually use them.

Old review right after I got them:
I just got these and they came fully charged, I have been jammin' out all day!
connected to all my devices, and it does switching really well, which is one of the main reasons i got this.
I don't know about the low latency stuff, but my phone has the apx stuff and while I have never noticed lag in audio on Anything I've ever owned that does audio, that could just be because I buy quality when it comes to my ears ;).

Update: So apparently you have to use the provided usb cable to charge these. i don't know what is different about them, but the charge light would shut off after a couple seconds, which kinda sucks.
but on the other hand, I've been listening to these for 3 days and they've only been charged for like 10 minutes. So now I'm going to have a full charge and we'll see how long these last on a full charge.
If the seller sees this:
what kind of usb cable is this? usb3?

title: Wanted to love it
text:
Wanted to love it, but get this I broke TWO the first day I owned them. This post is a bit of a ramble I apologize for that.

I work in a shop and the screen just are not durable enough. I broke the first one within an hour and the second one 6 hours into the first day.

I have the Original Samsung Watch and the new 46mm Galaxy 4 (https://www.amazon.com/Samsung-Electronics-Smartwatch-Detection-Bluetooth/dp/B096BHFLVD/ref=sr_1_2?keywords=galaxy%2Bclassic%2B4&qid=1639943826&sr=8-2&th=1) and BOTH have held up. The original Galaxy watch is 3 years old even scratched up to heck , but the screen never broke.

For some people the all glass top is not going to be durable enough. Dont drop the Sense on the ground face first or it's over, or hit it the edge of a counter or even with me the pressure of using a sander not even hitting the screen physically cracked the screen. I never touched the face!

I love the app, the only thing I hate is that there is no way to get accurate steps unless the arms are moving. This is an issue with all the arm watch and trackers I k ow of, but Fubit should come out with a thin little strap for the ankle that works in conjunction with the Sense. All if the companies wrist trackers should actually offer some type of ankle add on to get accurate steps for the people that use treadmill desks. I walk 8 to 10 miles a day, the Fitbit on my ankle(Inspire 2) is dead on accurate and matches the miles on the treadmill. The Fitbit sense tracked about 1 mile while I used my treadmill desk typing, that's a 7 mile difference on that one test day. That being said my Galaxy watches nor my kids Apple 7 watch do any better, none of the wrist watch trackers, count the steps correctly unless the watch is on the wrist while on a treadmill anyhow.

I wish Fitbit, Samsung or any of them came out with a strap or device as an add on for the ankle for us treadmill desk walkers.

As far as the heart and sleep data the Sense is decent, but nowhere near the accuracy nor data given from my Whoop. That being said the Whoop is better than the Samsung Galaxy 4 and the new Apple 7 as well. Of course there is no way to get the Whoop data into the Fitbit or Samsung app. The sleep is pretty good on al the devices the whoop is just far more consistent and gives more data. The Whoop band takes heart beat 100 times a minute, the Galaxy Watches, Apple and Fitbit sense 1 time per about 10 minutes by default going to 1 time per minute, but anything more than 1 time per minute heart beat check in Fitbit, Galaxy and Apple 7 eat the battery to wjere it would not get you through the night. So Whoop wins hand down for serius sleep and heart beat tracking.

So I sit here today with a Whoop on my arm(for best sleep and heart data of them all), a Samsung Galaxy 4 Classic(46mm) on my wrist(for overall email, text, phone calls and Samsung health data) and the Fitbit Inspire on my calf(for accurate steps) and I must use 3 different apps to get all the info I believe is as accurate as device today allow.

At least the Fitbit app and the Samsung Heath app both take in the mynetdiary( My favorite food tracker ir calorie counter) food intake and both also take my daily weight in from my Yunmai scale directly with no manual input.

I also have own the Kardia 6l for ECG EKG(https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B07RQW6SD5/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_search_asin_title?ie=UTF8&psc=1) and it's so much better than any of the trackers. The Galaxy Watch and Fitbit Sense are mere toys for EKG ECG compared the the Kardia 6 L. Of course I wish the Kardia info went into the Fitbit or Galaxy app like the scale and food info does. I also have the OMRON Platinum Blood Pressure Monitor(https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B07RX8WQ4K/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_search_asin_title?ie=UTF8&psc=1) and though the Kardia EKG and Blood pressure share one app I wish both these monitors information both automatically went into the Samsung Health and/or the Fitbit apps too.

You can see from my perspective for total Health data we are still far away from a one app, one device solution and I get that. Hardware will take years more to get to one device fits all that is as accurate as separate devices. My main issue is the app software. If the companies would at least play nice with each other for the apps we could be closer to that overnight. If they would just allow data to be shared with every companies apps and let us choose health apps based on form factor( even paid) and not force us to use multiple apps.

title: Wasn't for me.
text: I didn't like this watch. I bought it when it first came out and it was no way worth $350. If the regular Apple watch costs $89 to make then this LG watch probably cost about $49. I see after a couple weeks on the market the price has dropped to $320. I don't think it is worth more than $150-$199. I didn't like how the wrist band holder sticks out especially on the outside of my wrist. The wristband looks like it goes under the watch with these two prongs sticking over your wrist and I have pretty thick wrists. Other cons, sometimes the notifications came up, sometimes they didn't, sometimes they would just randomly disappear. I also lost connection with in home wifi and Samsung Note 4 on a regular basis. Heart rate monitor ranged between 53 and 99 within a couple minutes without changing activity or wrist placement. Step counter doesn't work as well as my cell phone. Smart phone also has a much better Heart rate monitor. If I do get a smart watch in the future it will be a full watchphone with sim card such as the Samsung gear S or anticipated LG LTE so I can leave home without my phone. I speculate LG watches work better with LG phones and Samsung watches better with Samsung phones, google phones/watch etc. Not really interested in paying exorbitant prices for flash memory via iphones and overpriced wrist bands for apple devices. Not too excited about buying another device that pretty much duplicates what I have in my pocket and have to recharge it every night. Yeah it's nice to get "quick glances" every once in a while without pulling your phone out which for me was about 5% of the time but in the end not really worth it. It just contributes to more ADD.

title: Could not charge reliably
text:
I tried this out with a Samsung S21 Ultra, If I placed the phone just right I could get it to charge laying down. If I tried standing the S21 Ultra up, I could not get it to charge... even after extending the stand. I tried this with and without a case, both failed. Standing up doesn't work well anyway with this charger because there is no lip to keep the phone from sliding off the charger, and with the case on it's even worse! However, I could inductively charge Soudcore Liberty 2 Pro+ earbuds without a problem.
I don't have a Samsung watch so I tried an Apple series 3 smartwatch and Iphone 8+, neither of those devices could be charged. The listing mentions these devices are not supported, so this did not affect it's rating.
To add to this product's useability issues is the USB port. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. I could get it to work for a little while, and then it would stop. I would turn off the light and hit the USB button and my phone would charge through USB, and then stop a minute or two later.... about 5 mins later it started charging again?!?! In short, the USB is a fail!
This product is simply too finicky to recommend. Standing the phone up is a complete fail... laying the phone down takes some manuevering to find just the right spot. It also worked with my case back on while laying down and no problems at all with my ear buds. These two items saved this charger from a one star review! I don't have a samsung watch, so I will give the benefit of a doubt that it works. The USB was also a fail. The light worked, but the light and USB buttons are not back lit, tiny black buttons on a black base are hard enough to find in the light forget about finding them in the dark when a light is needed!
Because this charging station mostly works under right conditions, it barely passes the 1 star mark and gets rounded up to 2 stars!

title: Not an android watch, for better and worse.
text: I've had a few samsung watches, they're nice but the battery life is short and they break too easily. This watch has less features and doesn't sync with any of the Samsung health stuff. The operating system is simple and there are only a handful of faces available. Still, it keeps time, displays notifications, allows call rejection, counts steps, keeps track of heart rate,and the battery lasts for days. It's a steal for the money. It seems like it's a fairly high quality build. It does charge on my Samsung wireless watch charger as well as it's own charger. I don't even know how long the battery lasts, because I've never let it run longer than 3 days on a charge. In this sense, it's way better than every Samsung watch that I've owned which all were lucky to make it through 1 day.

title: Sound absolutely horrible
text:
Update: Couldn't get them to sound any better, AptX was enabled (and prompted by) the Android phone. Seller refunded me fully right away. So I added another star, mainly because these work but are terribly basic and I will not use or recommend them for ANY purposes. I can only compare this to call sound quality (NOT vo-lte), although I am positive these were activated properly (refer to Aptx above).

P.S. I am not in the least an audiophile, but I do appreciate music and decent sound. My other comparisons are the above TT sport earbuds (same tier), Sony MDR-100ABN (not nearly in the same category) and MEE Audio M6 (wired) which all give different sound and are great for me.

So a very quick first impression:
They sound horrible - metallic and unbearable, like cheap dollar store wired earbuds. I tried this both with an Android Xperia phone and Samsung Gear Fit2. Steer clear. I have an aging pair of TaoTronics (BH-06) and they sound far superior.

This isn't detailed or a long usage review, I just had to post this because these are extremely disappointing, even for a 20-30$ product.
I usually try not judge a product on a single merit (or lack thereof), but these I can't bear to listen to so they default to null for me.

title: Beautiful watch, but it's functionally falls short..
text: First let me say that this is a beautifully crafted watch. I really loved the high end look and feel of this watch. Unfortunately it falls short in functionality. Bixby freezes up or says "something went wrong, try again later". Even with help from Samsung, bixby never worked as intended.  Android assistant is not supported on this watch. If you want to search an answer to something, you have to pull your phone out... The built-in weather app constantly has problems updating, even after tweaking the settings. Sometimes the watch would not wake up when twisting your wrist to get info. You must use samsung message to get message notifications and be able to reply. It does not support Google message which is much easier to use. Battery life is questionable. Sometimes it did well and sometimes it would chew up 65-70% in one day with light use. A premium samsung watch should integrate flawlessly with their phones and Android/Samsung software, unfortunately this didn't happen. As much as I loved the look and feel of this watch, I had to return it.

title: Bad Battery Life. Not worth it in my opinion
text:
This is my first smart watch and tbh i think spending over $100 for a watch is VERY expensive let alone $230ish.. So I thought I would give it a try because I needed a decent watch that had silent alarms and can give me notifications. Just as an FYI I work in an office and I am not that active so I thought this watch would last two days for sure. Here are my experiences:

On 100% life without changing the settings (out of the box) it was really bad. Lasted a little longer than half of a day
on 100% life changing all the settings to OFF or minimal (some you couldn't turn completely off)and using the battery optimization tool in the beginning of the day lasted about a day in a half maybe if i was lucky. I literally was using it as a watch that was it no calls or anything...

I am currently having it returned because I cannot do anything on it without the fear of the battery running out less than what was stated and thus not a fun/worth having for a $230 watch... It just isnt worth it.

A friend recommended me to go for just the basic older version Samsung smart watch. It is bigger and bulkier but if it allows me to use it without having to worry every half day that it will just loose all the battery then I am willing to make that compromise.

title: Image quality at par with others, functionality is subpar
text:
UPDATE:
I used the KeyMission and the Samsung Gear 360 in an Emergency Room scenario video yesterday. Did not use the app to control either device. I just used the apps to change settings and then to eye-ball perspective once they devices were mounted. After that, I manually fired the cameras each time.

Footage: KeyMission footage was more pleasing -- better color rendition, better tones. The Samsung footage was flat in comparison -- usable, but noticeably flat. Exposure was similar between the two. Sharpness is similar between the two. Stitching for objects closer to the camera is a problem for Nikon, but wasn't detrimental to the project. For other uses where objects along the stitching line that are less than 3 feet away are important will be a problem.

Functionality: The KeyMission worked fine. No hiccups. I could tune in via the app between takes if there was time -- and it did take 30 seconds to log into the app and connect each time. The Samsung took maybe 20 seconds to connect each time.

Battery: The KeyMission lasted an hour. Sporadic use of WiFi. Maybe 35 mins of actual footage. The Samsung lasted 48 minutes before it overheated and I swapped in a cooler battery to keep going. So, while I can't compare battery life, the overheating issue for Samsung is almost the same kind of problem.

Post-Process: The Nikon files came out of the camera already stitched and read to be edited. The Samsung files needed to pass through the Samsung phone to be stitched. That took hours. I was able to take the files off the Nikon card and cut a quick one minute sample file for others to see within 30 minutes of the shoot. The only thing that would have made this better is if I could have done it all on a mobile device rather than having to lug a laptop onsite.

So, I've upgraded my review to four stars based on the above experience and plan on keeping my Nikon and using it in place of the Samsung.

ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW
Bought the 360 at BB yesterday. First thought: pairing to an iPhone 7 and a 6 were equally challenging. I burned through a fully charged battery trying to get it right. It was a matter of doing the steps over and over until it worked; making sure after each failed attempt to make the iPhone forget the Bluetooth and wifi. But in the end, I have it paired to both phones.

Second, once paired and in use, I learned that no matter what you intend to do, you need to turn camera on and then in the app, select Remote Photography in order to make the camera start its wifi network. Even if you are just wanting to review images on the card via the app or change settings, you have to go into Remote Photography first. No network is initiated if you go into the other modes first. Takes 15-20 seconds. And be ready for the connection to drop if you are inactive even for a couple seconds. Or if you go into another app. The Samsung Gear 360 connects no matter what the task and holds for longer.

Third, forget trying to download video to your smartphone. It just errors out, no matter the file size. The Samsung Gear 360 gets it done no problem; too bad it only works with a Samsung phone.

Photos straight from the key mission are not recognized as 360 by Facebook, whether you upload straight from SnapBridge, from iPhone's photo app, or via the Facebook app. Photos from Samsung Gear 360 are recognized by Facebook as 360.

SnapBridge will only upload the 2MP file to Facebook from within the app; it errors out when you try to upload the original.

The desktop software won't run on my Windows 10 machine. Refuses to install. But that's fine since the files straight off the micro-SD card are already stitched and ready for YouTube. Haven't tried other VR video outlets yet. But this is one advantage the key mission 360 has over the Samsung Gear 360. All files from the Samsung camera have to pass through a stitching process if you take the files straight from the card. And since I edit in Adobe Premiere and inject the pano metadata manually anyway, I don't really care to use Nikon's software.

Photo image quality so far is the same between Samsung and Nikon. Video image quality is also close but right now I'd give the low light edge to Nikon (images are soft but not as noisy as Samsung) and the stitching quality for near-distance items are goes Samsung. Daylight or well-lit indoor shots, no straight advantage by either. But the fact there's exposure control on Nikon I think would be great for difficultly-lit situations. However, it's buried deep in the menus and there's no ISO control, which Samsung has.

So, after one day, can I say Nikon's $500 device is worth the money versus Samsung's $350 + a Samsung phone (if you don't own one)? Well, if you own a Samsung, no it's not. If you plan to have a desktop workflow, yes it is. But if you are hoping for a mobile device workflow, I need to do more testing.

Samsung has its functionality problems too and I'll see how Nikon holds up. For example, the Samsung Gear 360 overheats and shuts down after 9 minutes of shooting at the highest video resolution. It also forms condensation inside the lens of the dew point is right. And the round ball shape makes accidental photos and videos possible because you can't rally grab anywhere safely.

The only thing that would have made this better is if I could have done it all on a mobile device rather than having to lug a laptop onsite.

So, I've upgraded my review to four stars based on the above experience and plan on keeping my Nikon and using it in place of the Samsung.

ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW
Bought the 360 at BB yesterday. First thought: pairing to an iPhone 7 and a 6 were equally challenging. I burned through a fully charged battery trying to get it right. It was a matter of doing the steps over and over until it worked; making sure after each failed attempt to make the iPhone forget the Bluetooth and wifi. But in the end, I have it paired to both phones.

Second, once paired and in use, I learned that no matter what you intend to do, you need to turn camera on and then in the app, select Remote Photography in order to make the camera start its wifi network. Even if you are just wanting to review images on the card via the app or change settings, you have to go into Remote Photography first. No network is initiated if you go into the other modes first. Takes 15-20 seconds. And be ready for the connection to drop if you are inactive even for a couple seconds. Or if you go into another app. The Samsung Gear 360 connects no matter what the task and holds for longer.

Third, forget trying to download video to your smartphone. It just errors out, no matter the file size. The Samsung Gear 360 gets it done no problem; too bad it only works with a Samsung phone.

Photos straight from the key mission are not recognized as 360 by Facebook, whether you upload straight from SnapBridge, from iPhone's photo app, or via the Facebook app. Photos from Samsung Gear 360 are recognized by Facebook as 360.

SnapBridge will only upload the 2MP file to Facebook from within the app; it errors out when you try to upload the original.

The desktop software won't run on my Windows 10 machine. Refuses to install. But that's fine since the files straight off the micro-SD card are already stitched and ready for YouTube. Haven't tried other VR video outlets yet. But this is one advantage the key mission 360 has over the Samsung Gear 360. All files from the Samsung camera have to pass through a stitching process if you take the files straight from the card. And since I edit in Adobe Premiere and inject the pano metadata manually anyway, I don't really care to use Nikon's software.

Photo image quality so far is the same between Samsung and Nikon. Video image quality is also close but right now I'd give the low light edge to Nikon (images are soft but not as noisy as Samsung) and the stitching quality for near-distance items are goes Samsung. Daylight or well-lit indoor shots, no straight advantage by either. But the fact there's exposure control on Nikon I think would be great for difficultly-lit situations. However, it's buried deep in the menus and there's no ISO control, which Samsung has.

So, after one day, can I say Nikon's $500 device is worth the money versus Samsung's $350 + a Samsung phone (if you don't own one)? Well, if you own a Samsung, no it's not. If you plan to have a desktop workflow, yes it is. But if you are hoping for a mobile device workflow, I need to do more testing.

Samsung has its functionality problems too and I'll see how Nikon holds up. For example, the Samsung Gear 360 overheats and shuts down after 9 minutes of shooting at the highest video resolution. It also forms condensation inside the lens of the dew point is right. And the round ball shape makes accidental photos and videos possible because you can't rally grab anywhere safely.

title: Bad Experience with Bluetooth Only Watch
text:
First, I bought the Bluetooth edition of this watch and tried everything to get it to work with my phone but it would not connect. I have a brand new Samsung S21 and the Samsung Galaxy Watch 4 40 mm Bluetooth would not recognize the phone or vice versa.

Articles abound on the internet to fix this issue - wipe Bluetooth settings, reset or factory reset watch, try older versions of the Galaxy Wearables app. None of these worked, except factory resetting my phone, which worked for about 6 days before failing again. The watch stopped staying connected and stopped detecting sleep and health indicators. The battery also died extremely quickly.

I took a chance and bought the 44 mm LTE edition of the watch because I loved the feature set of the previous one so much. It paired immediately with my phone, and has stayed connected since. The LTE is making the difference. It costs $5 more on my plan to add the watch, but it's worth it. I have more features enabled on this 44 mm edition and it stays charged through the day without the need for recharge.

I also bought a Fitbit Charge prior to both of these. It would not reliably detect sleep or other health indicators. Fitbit recommended wearing it on the inside of the wrist or not telling it you're going to sleep so it will auto detect what you're doing. This only worked some of the time.

The Galaxy Watch gives the same indicators as the Fitbit and the sensor is more accurate.

title: Questionable quality
text:
I've worn smart watches for the past 7 years. This is my first Samsung watch. I haven't had any issues with the various other brands I've used, but this watch strap is causing a rash with oozing welts. I clean the strap on a regular basis and don't have especially sensitive skin, so I don't understand why this is happening. Since I've been wearing silicone bands for the last 7 years without any issues whatsoever, I suspect this band is not just silicone. The part that's causing problems is the very end of the strap that tucks under the buckle. I did contact Samsung through support, and they wanted me to send in my watch without any guarantee of how long it would take for me to get it back, nor a replacement to use while they have my watch in their possession. I declined, as I need a watch on a continuous basis. I'm disappointed in the quality of this watch and the poor customer service.
Oh, and I get 1.5 days of battery life, so I have to charge it daily. I know this is due to the settings I use, but my Fitbit with same settings went 3 days on one charge. Not what I expected from Samsung.
Looks like I will be locating a different style of strap, and when it's time for a new smart watch, Samsung will have dropped down several notches on desirability scale.

title: Battery Life was questionable
text:
I bought a Galaxy Watch Active 2 during pre-orders a few years ago, I really enjoyed the watch until (I'm assuming) a water mishap rendered the touch screen iffy at best. I don't need the latest and greatest so I figured I'd be able to buy a "like new" Galaxy Watch 4 for way cheaper instead of waiting for the Galaxy Watch 5 to be released for (I was assuming) a higher price.

My main concern with the watch was the battery life, it seemed to under-perform compared to my Galaxy Watch Active 2 when using the same settings and watch face I used on my previous watch. Besides the ECG (which was supposed to be included in the 2) it didn't seem like there were all that many improvements. That's not a huge issue to me because I really just need a watch that does some smartwatch basics.

The design on the 2 is a bit different than the 4, not necessarily in a bad way, but the band... I absolutely hated! The way they added the bit to contour right against the watch face made it look quite bad and fit weird on my small wrist. The contour is squared off on top and bottom of the band, so if you have a small wrist like me (I'd really consider my wrist average for a female) the watch face takes up most of the top of your wrist and the watch band needs to go almost straight 'down' to wrap around my wrist, however, due to how the bands contour is squared off, the squared off edge ends up catching on the body of the watch and either you have to squish it which makes it go at a legitimate 90 degree angle exposing the side of the contour making it look weird and pinched on the wrist, OR it gets stuck on the face and looks like the band is bulging off the side of the watch face, all of which looks awkward on my wrist.
I feel like this could have been easily resolved with rounding off the bottom of the contour on the band. If i were to have kept it i probably would have trimmed up the bottom squared off edge to a more rounded finish to let it more more freely on my wrist. The contour itself looks nice on a larger wrist like my husbands but its just not visually appealing of very functional for a small wrist.

The combination of all these factors led me to return the watch, I feared that the "used" factor could have played some part in the battery's questionable performance. I made the decision to return the watch, wait for the announcement of the Galaxy Watch 5 and weigh the pros and cons of re-buying the 4 in "NEW" condition OR spending more and getting the brand new Galaxy Watch 5. Once they announced the Galaxy Watch 5 (about 10 days ago) I compared the pricing, specs, etc. .... Ordering through Samsung, during pre-orders, I was able to use a promo code to get $50 worth of accessories on top of the wireless charger duo (which is also available with amazons pre-order of the watch) but what pushed me to go through Samsung for the pre-order was their trade in deal (trade in ANY functioning smart watch for a minimum of $75 up to $165(I'm trading in an old fitbit for $75)) In the end I was able to get the 'latest and greatest' for almost the exact same price as the Used- "Like New" watch I returned.

title: I really love the watch, but the battery life is horrible.
text:
I switched from a Fitbit to the Samsung Watch after my Fitbit simply kept having connectivity issues. I really liked it and had a few of them over the years. But I will say the Samsung Watch is much nicer overall, except for the battery life. I knew the battery life was a known issue prior to buying it however, but I always put my watches on the charger when showering. So it hasn't been a huge problem, but I only get maybe around 25-26hrs in total before it shuts off. As far as the functionality, it does pretty much as you would expect from a mainstream smart watch. Tons of apps, tracking, push notifications, watch faces, etc. You can also easily make custom faces using any photos, and adjust the type of data the face displays. The dial also spins and allows you to scroll through varies menu's. Tapping, swiping and the side buttons also give some extra ways to interact with it. And as mentioned it supports push notifications, so like most watches all you have to do it flip your wrist up to see message, answer phone calls etc. It even has full texting capability with it's tiny keyboard. Although... that's not something you are probably going to use a lot. Charging wise it charges pretty quickly, so that at least helps balance out the battery issue.

I have been pretty happy with it overall.

title: All around disappointment and the lack of polish at this price is a slap in the face.
text:
Missing 360 data:
This one is huge, they did not make the camera write the data to the image to make it so websites display the image as a 360. You have to edit the meta data on each image before uploading. Both Google and Samsung Gear VR could not read the images until this data was manually added. This was such a basic thing, every other 360 camera does it. It would just take a few lines of code in the firmware to do this but yet they left it out.

Build:
Build quality seems great. The device has good weight to it and feels solid. During my testing I did not run into overheating but some have reported it. The buttons on the device are not intuitive so you will have to read the manual. Having replaceable lens protectors is great and a reason I would never buy the Samsung gear 360.

Image quality:
If it is overcast forget about it. You need direct sun for this device to produce anything that does not look like mud. There are tons of compression artifacts and there is no sharpness to anything in the images. Even in well lit rooms it preforms poorly.

Software:
AWFUL- I am using a Note 4 and the connection drops every time the application is not in the foreground. If the screen locks the connection is lost. I have to reboot the camera to get the connection back. The software also does an awful job of displaying images. They look extremely distorted in all of Nikon's software (mobile and desktop). No testing went into this software. The camera also takes a photo every time I turn it on.

Image Settings:
Extremely bare bones. With how poor the images are you will be looking to try to tweak the settings but you can't. I don't know how Nikon could be so far behind the competition in this area. There is also no HDR setting to help deal with the poor performance. The most worrying thing about the lack of HDR is how easy it would have been for them to implement in camera, it was just lazy.

Stitching quality:
The stitch is rather large and uses a blur to mesh the front and back images resulting in some noticeable areas of blur. In my testing even when keeping well above the recommended distance between the camera and subjects the blur line was visible on areas that contained details. It was less noticeable outside with things very far off in the distance.

Video:
Don't expect "4k" quality when talking about 360 videos since the image is stretched all the way around. I went into this understanding that and it still looks awful. The lower frame rate on an action cam is also odd. For $500 you would think they could put enough horse power in there to pull off a full resolution 30 FPS option. They also did not include a h.265 recording option. I don't think I could show anyone the resulting video without some degree of embarrassment.

Sharing:
Their sharing platform is a joke and not worth using. 360 degree images don't work out of the box so it is a pain to share them the right way on better sites.

Final thoughts:
DO NOT BUY. They rushed it out or just did not care. The lack of the most basic features and missing meta data shows how little effort went into this device. Also never hope for a firmware update to fix problems after you can no longer return something, once they have your money they don't care.

Nikon, you took a dump in a box and then charged me $500 for it. I have used Nikon my whole life and this feels like a betrayal.
full_text:
All around disappointment and the lack of polish at this price is a slap in the face.. Missing 360 data:
This one is huge, they did not make the camera write the data to the image to make it so websites display the image as a 360. You have to edit the meta data on each image before uploading. Both Google and Samsung Gear VR could not read the images until this data was manually added. This was such a basic thing, every other 360 camera does it. It would just take a few lines of code in the firmware to do this but yet they left it out.

Build:
Build quality seems great. The device has good weight to it and feels solid. During my testing I did not run into overheating but some have reported it. The buttons on the device are not intuitive so you will have to read the manual. Having replaceable lens protectors is great and a reason I would never buy the Samsung gear 360.

Image quality:
If it is overcast forget about it. You need direct sun for this device to produce anything that does not look like mud. There are tons of compression artifacts and there is no sharpness to anything in the images. Even in well lit rooms it preforms poorly.

Software:
AWFUL- I am using a Note 4 and the connection drops every time the application is not in the foreground. If the screen locks the connection is lost. I have to reboot the camera to get the connection back. The software also does an awful job of displaying images. They look extremely distorted in all of Nikon's software (mobile and desktop). No testing went into this software. The camera also takes a photo every time I turn it on.

Image Settings:
Extremely bare bones. With how poor the images are you will be looking to try to tweak the settings but you can't. I don't know how Nikon could be so far behind the competition in this area. There is also no HDR setting to help deal with the poor performance. The most worrying thing about the lack of HDR is how easy it would have been for them to implement in camera, it was just lazy.

Stitching quality:
The stitch is rather large and uses a blur to mesh the front and back images resulting in some noticeable areas of blur. In my testing even when keeping well above the recommended distance between the camera and subjects the blur line was visible on areas that contained details. It was less noticeable outside with things very far off in the distance.

Video:
Don't expect "4k" quality when talking about 360 videos since the image is stretched all the way around. I went into this understanding that and it still looks awful. The lower frame rate on an action cam is also odd. For $500 you would think they could put enough horse power in there to pull off a full resolution 30 FPS option. They also did not include a h.265 recording option. I don't think I could show anyone the resulting video without some degree of embarrassment.

Sharing:
Their sharing platform is a joke and not worth using. 360 degree images don't work out of the box so it is a pain to share them the right way on better sites.

Final thoughts:
DO NOT BUY. They rushed it out or just did not care. The lack of the most basic features and missing meta data shows how little effort went into this device. Also never hope for a firmware update to fix problems after you can no longer return something, once they have your money they don't care.

Nikon, you took a dump in a box and then charged me $500 for it. I have used Nikon my whole life and this feels like a betrayal.

title: Wow!
text: I have a Samsung Gear S2 which I love but the screen is too small and the battery life is too short. Because of that, I decided that I was going to order a watch with a bigger screen and longer battery life but didn't want to spend $300.  I did a lot of research and in the end bought this watch because of the positive reviews and I am glad I did. Now don't get me wrong, this watch does not have nearly the same amount of features as the Samsung but it is exactly what I need. All I wanted was a watch with long battery life that could also shows all my text messages, phone calls, emails and notifications. This watch does that and more, it also shows amount of steps, hours of sleep, heart rate etc. And on top of that it is waterproof and the battery life for me has lasted longer than a month. Also, the screen is large, clear and the watch is very nice looking with a nice band. It is somewhat limited on watch faces but they have added new watch faces every week and there are probably over 50 options. Considering the price, the features and it's great appearance, this watch is a no brainier and I would recommend it to anyone!

title: A great Wear OS smartwatch
text:
Update 2019-11: Suddenly the watch isn't charged well, the charging speed was very slow. After googled on the Web, I found the charger issue at the Mobvoi's community forum. Fortunately, my 1-year warranty has still alive, so I created a support ticket. After a few days, they responded to me and sent a brand-new charger. It works very well, I haven't measured but it seems the charging speed increased.

----

It's my first smartwatch, so I can't compare with other smartwatches, but I'm satisfied. After purchased I concerned the weight, but it's lighter than my expectation. It has beautiful display and nice battery life. It has 2 days life in normal use.

There are official replace bands but we can use other bands for Samsung Gear S3. The bundled band looks too formal to me, so I replaced with this band https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B073RFQXPB/

title: Love the Watch Hate the Battery
text:
I Love my Samsung watch but the battery life isn't the best.
The apps work well. You can customize which apps you want and get rid of those you don't. You can always add them later if you change your mind. I don't have to have my phone on me all the time which is great. I can answer calls, which I have done. I can see text messages come in real time. It's a great smartwatch. I just have issues with the battery life. One of my friends limits the apps he uses on his Galaxy watch and gets 2 sometimes 3 days on a single charge. I've done that but prefer to have all the apps I need at the ready, PLUS have a fantastic battery life on my smartwatch.
Fix this issue Samsung! I won't ever use anything Apple. Just fix this please.

title: Not really a smart watch.
text:
I've had this watch for 4 days now. This is my first "smart" watch. I'm not too impressed with it. It tells time really well. The heart rate monitor is fairly accurate. The step counter, not so much. On day 1, I was sitting on my couch playing PS4, and I had taken over 1000 steps just sitting there according to the watch. So I suppose if you want to feel like you've gotten some physical activity done, then this is the watch for you.
It's pretty useless without the phone app. You can't even set an alarm from the watch itself, and there is no audible alarm. If you get too far away from your phone, it won't show weather. The camera shutter opens a camera in the phone app, not your main camera app.
By the way, as I've typed this review, the watch says I have taken 500 steps while sitting still.
I'm going to just buy a Samsung watch.
