title: Battery life changes to 2 hours within 7 months of ownership.  Dont buy!!!
text:
UPDATE. Its WORSE
Samsung says, they dont influence how their electronics respond when they get older (No software changes to force you to upgrade to a new device). Such as Apples forcing people to buy new Apple products. My Samsung Gear S2 is a perfect example of Samsung sending downloads to influence the behavior of my watch, warranting me to want to buy a new one. I have had my S2 watch for 2 years now and I've posted on this site before. Every mid summer (June July) and around the November to January timeframe, Samsung will release an update for the watch. Within 5 days after you complete your update, your battery will drain and last no more than 2 hours. I thought I had found a solution by cutting off the watch before charging. Then around Christmas, a dreaded update that has made my watch useless once again.

I love my personal Samsung phone and I have a Iphone for work in which I do not like but I'm afraid that this endeavor has pushed me over the edge. Two years of wearing a fake watch that has no power is Korny. The Gear is so much better than the Apple Watch but Samsung refuses to help me with a solution.

My suggestion to you all is, if you must buy this watch, get it from Best Buy with a 2 year warranty and make them replace it every year when the battery starts to die as it will. When Best Buy begins replacing these every year from hundreds of thousands of people, this will cause Samsung to resolve the problem or Best Buy stop carrying the product because of its inferiority.
------------------------------------------------------------
Great watch. The battery life is HORRIBLE. I actually bought my S2 from Best Buy approximately 7 months ago but I'm so pissed at this problem that I have made it a point to let every electronic medium(email, blog,website, etc) know about this watch so you wont spend the $300 I did. Within the past 2 months, i have had to factory reset my watch 3 times (along with removing the gear software from my phone twice) which causes you to loose all of your downloaded apps and configurations for the watch. They tell you to backup your data but what sense does that make if your watch has a software issue. If you re-install the backup (that might have a software issue), you reinstall the problem. Therefore, I havent backed up any of my watches information since after the 1st reset. Within 3-4 hours everyday my watch goes to 15% then dies.
Heres my configuration
- I only have 20 apps that send info to my watch each day.
- My brightness setting is at 4 out 10
- I use bluetooth and home wifi for connections
the watch looks brand new with no damage.
Samsung offers absolutely no help nor resolution except resetting the device. Im in sales and this watch is critical to my very busy lifestyle. I am contemplating dropping my Galaxy S6 and Gear S2 for Apple products and let Samsung go. But, I really love my phone and it sucks that the Apple watch is not compatible with my phone. I am so angry that I wasted this money for a device that doesnt work. These should be pulled off of the market. Google "battery life samsung gear s2," and see the litany of complaints then make the right decision to not get this watch

title: Meh
text: I got this watch after trying the Galaxy Watch and getting frustrated with Samsung's lack of app selection and bloatware. Wear OS has a much better selection of apps, but honestly the OS isn't as clean. As for Hardware this watch was a real disappointment. I understand the plastic case for cost, but the OS ran slow as hell and would regularly stall out and glitch. The heart rate monitor was all over the place and never displayed correctly when I used Watchmaker to show it on the watch face. The battery life was also pretty disappointing. It can make it through a whole day if you turn everything off (Wifi, Always on display, etc) and rarely use it, which kind of defeats the whole purpose of having the thing. Overall I can't say I'd recommend this watch to anyone.

title: You can't go wrong with this watch
text:
I got this watch to replace my Samsung Galaxy Watch, the main reason was the battery, I was so tired of having to charge my watch every day, I had to install TWO applications in my phone to have my watch working, besides, Samsung was trying to shove down my throat the Samsung Health, Samsung Pay and Bixby, long story short, I just throw it away, it sucked.

This watch was "cheaper", I got a solid 20 days of battery, it tracks what I need and no more, sleep, hearth rate, steps and notifications, the watchfaces aren't that much but are good enough, you might get more from an expensier device but this watch is the perfect balance between features and price, the design is sleek and simple, light and it offers exactly what I was looking for.

title: Not wear os
text:
Update: I've been using it for basically a week now. If I turn things off and turn down the screen I can eek out a day before I need to charge it. Touch bezel is pointless. It works but it is easier to just not use it. Unfortunately I don't have a Samsung phone, cause it just won't do certain things without one. My son has the active 2 with a s21 and it does more than my 4. Sleep detection is awful. I've kept track for some time now with my Amazfit, which was hit or miss. For most of this week I've worn both watches to compare. It's been unbelievable how different numbers are between the two. Samsung is much higher on footsteps and sleep time. 50% higher at times. And I'm still not happy about the Tizen, I mean "wear os" software on this watch.

The watch came yesterday, but I already have some strong opinions. It is not a wear OS watch. What it is, is a Samsung watch that may use wear os as a starting point but puts a heavy skin on top of it. I cannot use it with wear os on my phone. So in other words I'm stuck with Samsung apps to run the watch. Absolutely not what I want. Second, the battery life is a joke. The watch came in the box, flat dead. I charged it fully right before bedtime, next morning at 4am it's at 40%, that means I'll be charging it twice a day to use it as I see fit. Plus when I woke up the clasp on the watch had unbuckled itself.
Not sure if I'll learn to live with Samsung instead of Google. But not happy at the moment. It kinda reminds me of the Amazon fire phone, yeah I could get google apps, but it still wasn't google android. Same thing here, yeah I can get get google apps but it's not wear os.

title: * UPDATE * UNUSABLE BATTERY LIFE! * 3 hours = Dead *
text:
UPDATE: I want to love this watch... but it is now an insanely expensive paperweight.

After having this watch a little over a month now, the battery CANNOT last more than 3-4 hours no matter what I do. I have turned off the screen to only wake up to touch. i ahve turned the brightness to the lowest setting. I have turned off all the sensors including location, heart rate, wifi, motion detection , etc... as well as uninstalling almost every app. I am not using the watch to perform any function other than TXT notifications now, so the only thing that is turned on is bluetooth and txt notifications.... and yet the battery still dies in less than 3.5 hours every time!

I find this to be totally unacceptable in a watch advertised to last for days with many more features turned on. Im not even using 4G or wifi at all! This is nuts....

*** MOBVOI - Please refund my money and I will gladly ship this paperweight back to you.***

ORIGINAL REVIEW: Everyone has different needs and expectations when it comes to these kinds of gadgets, but I believe this is the best all around smart watch for android available to date. I bought this Ticwatch Pro 4G/LTE to replace my Samsung Galaxy watch. My main gripe with the Samsing was no support for google apps. The Ticwatch can do everything the Samsung can, but the TicWatch is running Wear OS, which means seamless integrations with messages, google maps, google fit, keep notes, and importantly, google assistant. Wear OS is not perfect, but the ability to utilize the same google apps I use every day on my phone is the entire point of having the watch in the first place.

PROS:
google apps integration
good battery life
4G if needed
dual screen is awesome
style can dress up or be casual

Overall, the the TicWatch Pro 4G is the best all around smartwatch available for android users.

Also, I have dealt with Mobvoi customer service on
several occasions now and they have always been super prompt in resolving my problems. I wouldnt hesitate buying any product from them in the future because I know they will stand behind it 100%

title: I wish there were more stars
text:
I've had a Samsung gear S3 for 6 years, but the battery was going bad and I didn't like the features or price of their Galaxy Watch 4. After looking and doing my DD I came across the Amazfit GTE 3 Pro. It tracked y steps perfectly, which the Samsung did to (at twice the price) and also gave me five times more exercises to track, great weather, alarms (I use three daily plus two on weekends) and accurate heart rate.

Oh, here's the kicker: While I got 4 days on te Samsung gear S3, I got 12 days, yes full 12 days battery on the Amazfit.

I'll see how this holds up over time and amend my review if I think it's necessary.

Great smartwatch, especially for the price.

title: Disapointing
text: I do a lot of research on  items that I am looking to purchase. After spending a bunch of time looking this watch over and reading many reviews I decided to make the purchase. There are several things that should have been mentioned in other reviews but were not. The watch is very good looking and feels very solid. I dont like things that are light and feel 'plasticy'. I have had this watch for several months now and its pretty nice. There is a severe lack of instruction that comes with it though. Also, it seemed to take hours to get it set up meaning there were a ton of updates that needed to be downloaded. The leather band that came with it has proven to be very durable and looks really nice. The clasp however tends to grab hairs on your wrist. My biggest complaint about this watch is that it doesnt stay connected via Bluetooth. I wear it on my left wrist and my phone on my right hip. The watch constantly disconnects for no apparent reason. Trying to reconnect has proven to be painful and so aggravating! Sometimes the phone (Gear Manager) will show the watch as being disconnected but the watch will show that it is connected. Sometimes its the other way around. Sometimes the watch and phone will try to pair and you have to make sure the code is correct on both devices then tell it to connect. VERY frustrating! The magnetic power cord connection while it seems to be pretty slick, does not stay in place well. The actual end of the cord is narrow and moves side to side on the back of the watch not making connection if moved any. Finally, there are waaaaay to many steps that need to be taken to get the smallest thing done. For instance just to shut the watch off you have to go through 5 screens to get to the check mark to shut it down. In fact when you go to charge it, it turns back on. Little things but when other watches provide these features, why cant Asus? I had the Samsung Gear 2 Neo before this watch and loved it. When I had to get a new phone I did not go with another Samsung so I can no longer use it. So I might go ahead and get a different phone so I can sell this watch and go back to my trusty Neo, then sell this one. So, nice watch, not dependable.

title: Robust Update To The 4 but Redesign Requires New Charger Accessories
text:
Update 11/22/2022 (Three months)

The disc charger fails to magnetize due to the strap pressure pushing the watch head off the magnet.
This watch is very poorly designed. Also, the BP function was disabled rendering the ECG function worthless. You need both simultaneously.

Original Review
This watch is an upgrade to the 4 and a sidegrade to the 5, and this watch has undergone a major design. It is more durable from the materials included. The head has better glass and is more resistant to hard treatment. There is ECG if you can use Samsung Health Monitor (only available to devices that can download from the Galaxy Store), and while it is not medical device accurate, it does a decent job of detecting arrhythmia although tuned toward false positives. The Samsung Health integration is good, and the watch so far is better at sensor detection, though the app at the time of writing (September 2022) had a bad update and needs work.

Unfortunately, there are issues with the wristband design. The wristband does not lie flat, so the head will not actually lie on most wireless chargers, even the Samsung ones. I have a Samsung Wireless Charger for the Galaxy Watch 4 and my S19, and this watch unfortunately will not lie flat on it (as pictured). As of right now, there is not a branded charger from Samsung outside of the included USB-C disc charger that works without taking the wristband off. This is quite the design flaw, and I am surprised that it was allowed to go out in the state without a compatible charger sold with it.

This watch is a nice upgrade for ECG (arrhythmia detection) and for more risky activities with a modest battery life increase. If you have multiple products in the Samsung ecosystem, I would hold off until Samsung gets an upgrade for the wireless charger onto the market.

title: No luck with the turning bezel
text:
After having gone through a coupIe cheaper smart matches, I paid over $200 for this watch in July 2019, figuring that I'd be getting a superior product. I have a Galaxy phone, so a Galaxy watch must be the "real" deal, right? Wrong. First issue..from the getgo, I found it difficult to get a grip on the bezel in order to turn it, in order to move from screen to screen. So, I bought a stick on bezel ring on Amazon, and this bezel ring provided a better grip.
Then at some point, it became very difficult to turn the bezel at all. It was so bad that
I resorted to spraying some silicone lubricant on it. Just a touch....I was afraid the silicone might mess up the watch. That solved the problem, and the bezel was now unstuck and all was well.
Recently , all of a sudden, when I turn the bezel, nothing happens, most of the time. I'll get a text message. I look at my watch quickly, and before I'm done reading the message, the message disappears. The message only appears for a few seconds. This is normal. If the watch was functioning correctly, no problem. Turn the bezel to view the message. But now, I have to fight with the bezel, turning it and turning it, and maybe eventually, it'll change to the next screen. I've ordered a much less expensive watch to replace this. It seems that there are a lot more reasonably priced smart watches available now, with decent reviews, than a couple years ago. I'll see how the new watch works out when I get it. I noticed that Samsung still offers the turning bezel design on is latest version of this watch. Interesting. Maybe I'm the only one who has had issues with the turning bezel.
Other than the bezel, the watch worked fine and looked great. I got a lot of compliments on it. It measured my pulse accurately. Other than my pulse, I never paid any attention to any of the other health related mumbo jumbo, like counting steps or when I'm sleeping. If I need a watch to tell me whether I'm getting proper exercise or whether I'm getting enough sleep, I have other issues. I want a watch to give notifications especially at work, so I don't have to pull out my phone. It did that just fine. But the turning bezel, although it seems cool, has got to go, in my opinion.

It seems that there are a lot more reasonably priced smart watches available now, with decent reviews, than a couple years ago. I'll see how the new watch works out when I get it. I noticed that Samsung still offers the turning bezel design on is latest version of this watch. Interesting. Maybe I'm the only one who has had issues with the turning bezel.
Other than the bezel, the watch worked fine and looked great. I got a lot of compliments on it. It measured my pulse accurately. Other than my pulse, I never paid any attention to any of the other health related mumbo jumbo, like counting steps or when I'm sleeping. If I need a watch to tell me whether I'm getting proper exercise or whether I'm getting enough sleep, I have other issues. I want a watch to give notifications especially at work, so I don't have to pull out my phone. It did that just fine. But the turning bezel, although it seems cool, has got to go, in my opinion.

title: Broke way too fast for $400
text:
I've had this watch for 5 months.

I wore it on a hike and it broke. I don't know why... it was very hot that day. It did drizzle rain but it didn't pour rain... but it's supposed to be water-resistant anyway. I didn't wear it swimming on the hike or anything.

The watch face turned black after about 30 min. I thought it had just died or something. So I put it in a secure pocket in my backpack.

I went home to charge it and restart it... it began the restart and never finished. So I sent it to Samsung to repair it (it's supposed to be under warranty).

They want $200 for the repair. I don't even know why it stopped working... what a cheap crappy product. It was under warranty, but they said that the warranty is invalid, but didn't give me reasons.

I'm sorry, but a "fitness watch" that broke on a routine hike after 5 months of use, then they want me to pay 50% of its original cost to fix it? It's not worth $400 if it's going to be cheaply made and just brake. Oh well, won't go with a Samsung watch again.

I don't recommend spending $400 on this product. It could easily break and they don't have your back on repairing it.

title: No audible alarm
text:
I really wanted to like this watch but after playing around with it for a couple of days, going through the owner's manual step by step and Finally getting it to pair with my Samsung watch after 3 or 4 attempts and reboots and deleting and reinstalling the app - I have to return it.

My wife went from having No watch to the Apple Iwatch 7 which is probably the best smart watch out there and I was envious, but couldn't justify spending $1,000 for a watch, when my old-school Casio Data Bank I've been wearing for 30 years does everything I've ever needed a watch to do.

I want a watch that tells me what time it is when I look at it. I don't want to have to shake it and activate the lift-to-lit feature - which I had to do with this Amazfit watch. I want a watch that beeps when the alarm goes off - whether it's the alarm to wake me up in the morning or the countdown timer I use regularly to tell me the laundry's done in the laundry room. The Amazfit vibrates, which I can barely feel.
I like what an other reviewer said - who ever heard of an alarm that doesn't make any noise!

The watch faces are okay, but I really wanted the watch face shown in the ad. Couldn't find it in the watch face "catalog" on the app. Don't know where they got this picture.

All the other features on this watch are probably great for workouts and health monitoring, which I think would've been great to have, but first and foremost I wanted a timepiece.

I've had 4 Casio Data Bank watches over the last 30 years so maybe I'm stuck in the past, but the past Works. And if it works - don't fix it!

I spent a few hours last night googling smart watches with audible alarms and will probably have to break down and spend a few hundred dollars at the very least, IF I want to upgrade and join the 21st century, but for now - I'm okay having a watch that shows me the time every time I glance at it and beeps when I need to be reminded of something!

This watch is probably great for a lot of people, with Alexa built in, and heartrate and stopwatch and weather, and incoming text notifications (even if you can't actually read the text or make calls like you can on the $1,000 I-watch!) and everything else, but it's just not for me.

title: Huawei vs Samsung Gear S2
text:
I just sold my Samsung Gear S2 and purchased this watch. Wow! Night and Day!

1. Samsung OS (Tizen) is crap: few apps, not intuitive, not customizable. Huawei uses Android wear which is not perfect, but much more intuitive
2. Huawei's implementation of the features are logical and useful: ie notifications, receiving and replying to texts, etc... Samsung's...not.
3. Bigger screen on the Huawei means I can see the text!
4. Packaging is amazing on the Huawei. Feels like a Rolex.
5. Samsung band is cheap - Huawei is high quality leather
6. Samsung forces you to use the "S Health" fitness app which is crap. With Huawei you can use Google Fit.
7. Gear S2 has lots of Bloatware that you can't remove.

There is no comparison between these watches. Get the Huawei...period.

title: Not suitable for swimming
text:
Don't purchase the Samsung Galaxy Active2 watch if you intend to use it for tracking swimming workouts. The warranty states - "Standard Limited Warranty does not cover defects or damage resulting from accident, misuse, abnormal use, abnormal conditions, improper storage, exposure to liquid, moisture, dampness, etc." It should actually say defects or damage resulting from normal use and normal conditions because that is exactly what happened to me.

I bought the Samsung Galaxy Active 2 after researching smartwatches. I specifically searched for “best watch for swimming.” There were several results providing a list of watches for swimmers. The Apple Watch was usually the top choice but since I don’t own an iPhone, that’s not an option for me. High up on most of the lists was the Samsung Galaxy Active. It seemed like a good option.

Shortly after that, Samsung released the Active 2. The reviews raved about how great the watch worked, the style, etc. So I bought one. The promotional material online from Samsung included using the watch for swim workouts, both pool and outdoors which is what they refer to for open water swimming. A detailed review praised the watch for its ability to count laps and detect strokes.

So I’ve owned one since October’19 up until a little more than a week ago. I used it for my swim workouts and it worked. It wasn’t perfect. It does an average job of stroke detection-you can’t rely on it accurately identify the correct stroke (I’m a former division 1 swimmer, so my strokes are good). It also can’t count a lap if you are using a kickboard. Other than that, the lap counting is pretty good. I swim 5000yds, 5 or 6 days a week. That’s 200 laps and the watch usually counts it correctly.

I was happy with the watch. Then our pools closed due to the pandemic. The main pool I use during the summer recently announced they will not open this season. I decided to buy a wetsuit so I can swim in the lake.

The wetsuit arrived about 2 weeks ago and the manufacturer recommends testing it for size by going for a swim. They allow returns/refunds within 30 days. I tested the suit the day after it arrived. At our nearby lake. I also wore my watch and set it to track outdoor swimming. After I finished my swim I noticed the screen on my watch was blank. I pressed the button to disable water lock mode and it didn’t respond to any button presses. After I got home, I contacted customer service and described my problem. They verified it was under warranty (which doesn’t expire until October’20) and provided instructions and a shipping label for sending it in for repair. I sent it off the next day. Several days later I got an email saying the watch was received. Then a few days after that I got an email message from Samsung support stating that my watch is not under warranty. I thought it was probably a clerical error, so I immediately called the # on the message. After speaking with someone they told me it probably wasn’t covered because of water damage. I argued that the watch was rated for 5ATM blah, blah blah, to no avail.

For those of you thinking about buying this watch for swimming, don’t. It will eventually fail and it won’t be covered under warranty.

For more information about this, do a Google search for "Samsung watch warranty useless" and you will find many people who've had this exact same experience.

title: Save yourself the headache. Don't buy these
text:
I regret getting these so hard. I got 2 pairs off indiegogo, meaning to gift one pair, but these are terrible and I am stuck with crappy, expensive bluetooth earbuds now. Pairing wasn't too bad, but the sound quality is only ok, for the price I expected better, but here is a list of reasons NOT to buy these:

1. The battery life is not super great. I get that these are completely wireless, and therefore the battery life will not be super strong to begin with, but these are don after 2 hours or so and then will need to be charged, which leads me into the next issue..
2. I have pulled these out of the charging case after leaving them in for a few days only to be greeted by a low battery warning 10 seconds after I put them in my ear.
3. These earbuds will prioritize being on and being paired with your phone for sound vs actually charging. Whether they are in the case or not, they insist on being paired with my phone. Additionally, these will randomly connect themselves to my phone while in the case as long as they are within range of my phone. It's super annoying and there is no way to stop it. You will spend more time trying to turn these off and stop the automatic pairing of these with your phone.
4. They will randomly unpair when you are using them or the sound will cut out.
5. The soundpass option is terrible. It works, but only for the immediately surrounding area; I can hear my hair brush against the microphone, but not a conversation that is happening in the same room. Additionally, the soundpass option will overpower whatever you have going on in your headphones, My music will become ultra quiet while my hair against the mic is super super loud. It's obnoxious. These don't really noise cancel, so it's not too big of an issue overall as I just opt not to use it anyways.
6. These don't stay in your ears super well. I'm on crutches and they've fallen out of my ears while I was moving a few times. I haven't tried the earhooks, but they won't stay in super well on their own.

I own two other wireless bluetooth earbuds, both of which I find higher quality than these.
1. The Mpow T3, which have decent sound quality, and are cheaper. These are really easy to use, but they do not have an ambient noise option. Pairing is easy, as are the controls. I can't comment on battery life as I haven't used these until they were currently drained yet and I have temporarily misplaced these, but these are decent for less money.
2. Samsung Gear Icon X- I just got these and I love them. I also haven't used these until they are drained since I got them last night, but so far I am impressed. These also have a ambient noise option and you can control how loud ambient noise is coming into your ears. They pair really easily, and you can also load music directly on these. They pair once you take them out of the charging case and the sound is good. They are definitely more expensive though, but worth it compared to these pieces of junk.

title: Hoped the replacement would solve my battery problem because I loved the watch :(  It didn't!
text:
I love this watch --- but--- The battery is only lasting 4 hours with ambient on and 6 hours with it off. I also can't turn off the wrist activation even though I reset to factory conditions twice. I like the watch enough that I am exchanging it for another just in case the problem is with the battery in this particular watch. I also own a Samsung Gear Neo which I really like but the looks of the available faces and the over all appearance of this watch is so much classier. It is the difference between having a sports watch and a dress watch, but when the dress watch can't make it through dinner that is a problem. I will update this review after I get the replacement watch and see if it last longer. I can live with just a day, but not 4 or 5 hours.

update: I really wanted to love this watch. It looks nice, the functionality is great the available apps are good and it works well...almost!
I got my replacement watch three days ago and like the last one (I didn't mention this) it had outdated software that needed updating. Sounds easy, but the first watch took three attempts because I ran out of battery power in the middle of the updates (even though I charged to 100% before installing) and my second watch continued to show me an error message "[PUBLIC] Full OTA to LXA44B" I went on line to determine how to correct this and was given one suggestion that didn't work and another that suggesting taking the watch apart.

I love technology, but I am not a techie. I just want the watch to work and work for a reasonable amount of time. I have no doubt that in the future these types of complaints will dissapear. I am over 50 and remember the same problems with PC's and with Cell Phones. But for now my solution is to upgrade to a better product. I didn't want to have to spend $400 on a new watch but I am going too. That brings me to why I am going with a Huawei Device instead of the Moto 360 2nd Gen. I have seen hundreds of reviews (even 5 star) that complain about the battery life for the Moto 360 and Motorola did not heed the comments and did not improve the battery power. The Huawei Smart Watch is not only higher rated and has a AMOLED screen most of the reviews stated that even with heavy use they got a full day out of it. That is all I ask. I don't need 10 days or 5 days, I can put on the charger when I go to bed and night, the same way I do with my Cell Phone. What I can't do it put a watch on at 7am and need to charge it at 11:00am. This while keeping the ambient off on the MOTO 360.

Again! The watch was great (not the replacement, I couldn't get that one to even work) and I so wanted to keep it. I loved it! It just is too much trouble.

A quick note on Amazon's service. Never have a found a company that takes care of those that are loyal (I have over 180 orders with Amazon since 2009 and am a Prime Member) as well as Amazon does.

They made the replacement easy! They made the return easy, and they gave me a credit for my trouble (throwing in a $9 accessory on my new order). Not because I yelled and screamed but because I had a question on how the credit would work since I had put it on 6 month no interest and had another small item on the same order which would not qualify on it's own for the interest free offer. Like so many people I refuse to carry any credit with interest charges. I used online chat to work this out and the Amazon rep was fantastic.

I don't know that Amazon has the best prices (as long as they are close) and I don't care. What I do know is I always have peace of mind when I order from them.

title: Only OK
text:
You would think that I would know about Samsung fitness and health trackers by now, but apparently I am a slow learner. I already have a Gear Fit Pro and a Galaxy S3 Classic in addition to my new purchase of a Galaxy Watch 3 to go with my Samsung S10 phone. They have all ( including the Galaxy Watch 3) had the same problem with heart rate tracking during exercise. If I believed the numbers, my heart is racing out of control everytime I do much of anything. All 3 have "Always on" , but it is the kind that turns the fitness tracker into a watch not the kind that keeps the fitness tracking on. I have a Fitbit Surge for that. I wish that Samsung and others would give me control of battery life..

I bought the Galaxy watch 3 because it has ECG, blood oxygen, and blood pressure even though it only does those on-demand and not during sleep. The sleep function is pretty pathetic, but I do not care because I have a device under my mattress to monitor my sleep and a fitness band to monitor heart rate, blood oxygen, and blood pressure as well as sleep. The ECG requires a separate app but works quickly and well. I can usually see what is happening within a few seconds. In addition, there is a complete recording available in the Samsung Health app. That is important to me because I have occasional bouts of afib that I cannot feel. I resent that Samsung requires a certain level of phone security to use the ECG. That should be my choice. Blood oxygen is measured using the Samsung Health Monitor app, and seems OK. Blood pressure measurement is a real setup and measurement nightmare. I have successfully set it up twice and uninstalled it twice (yes, it requires still another app). All three of these items (ECG, BS, and BP) operate in different ways and cannot be set up to automatically operate at night.

Here are a few more random thoughts. Many people complain about the battery life, but it seems barely adequate for me. The price is too high for what you get. The Watch 3 seems much lighter than the Gear S3. I found it more acceptable to wear during exercise even though it is large.

I have not decided whether to return the Watch 3. I would definitely keep it if heart rate tracking during exercise worked. I keep hoping that Galaxy will get their act together.

title: Horrible Battery Life!
text:
When I purchased the Samsung Watch5 I thought that it would be a better watch than the Samsung Gear S3 Frontier watch that I purchased a few years ago; but sadly, it is NOT. My Frontier watch has to be charged every 23-24 hours and the Watch5 has to be charged every 46-48 hours. Sooo inconvenient and disappointing for a watch that’s sooo expensive.

The Watch5 is beautiful and the band is rather comfortable and easy to use for a silicone band but the battery life is horrible. Although I love my Frontier watch and I really like the dressy look of the new Watch 5, what’s the use of having a great looking watch that has such a short battery life? The other problem is that you get two chargers with the Watch5 but no wall plug in which to provide power. Samsung should have included one charger and a wall plug because who needs two chargers and no way to use them? I do not recommend purchasing this watch unless you’re just going to wear it for a few hours and put it away to charge for another day.

I recently purchased a Health Smartwatch 3 from Spade & Company and I can wear that watch for 8 days before I have to charge it and at a much cheaper price. Maybe Samsung should get together with this company to find out what their secret is for a longer battery life.

title: I found it frustrating to use since it is not very compatible with other systems.
text:
It's a great tablet if you don't need to use it for any google apps or files-music-photos were hard to sync if at call. I couldn't get side loading to work. I wanted to consolidate all my books-or add my other book apps but to no avail. I don't object to paying for items but the Amazon store apps were limited and charged for many apps; especially ones that were so similar to ones I have for free (with ads/in app purchases on my Galaxy tablet and my Samsung smart watch. I did like the smooth access to Alexa and I was able to get easy access on my Netflix, Hulu & Pluto apps.
Pros: Good battery life, has micro card slot and show mode. Nice hd display; offers an audiobook app but with a monthly fee AND OF COURSE Alexa.
I will say the support team is usually excellent to work with and while I 'm not in the habit of returning, upon my returning the Fire 8 tablet, While I was traveling was stressful.  I wanted to give more stars but the shipping fiasco gave me a headache.

title: I wanted to like it...
text: I really wanted to like the Samsung Gear Fit2, but I can't.  I received mine new in box, and it wouldn't charge.  I checked the Samsung forums, and found out this was a known problem.  When the device is fully charged down, you may have to charge it in your computer first, then use a wall plug.  Nothing in the docs said that.  But that did not solve my problem, so I went online with tech support.  First, they told me how to charge it.  Plug it in to the wall.  Set the watch on the charger.  ARGH!  I know they have to do that, but it is so frustrating.  Then they asked if it was water damaged.  I had told them I had JUST OPENED THE BOX.  Then they said the only thing I could do was return it.  Thanks, Samsung.  Great customer service.

title: Do you REALLY need more?
text:
While my Citizen does a great job of keeping time and has solar to power it, it does not do a great job with the date. It only knows 31 days for each month, so I'd have to adjust it every month with 30 days. Eventually I just stopped adjusting it. Now THIS watch, while not the levels of an Apple or Samsung smart watches, is more of a "bright" watch. It has what I NEED in a watch, shows 24h time, display time AND day correctly, never have to adjust for day light saving time, great battery, good build, good looking, show weather, show notifications from phone, allow for multiple faces or I can import a background and place the time in a couple of places.
I also bought this on sale since I thought it might be a toy but turns out it is not. It also comes with an additional screen protector and proprietary cable. The watch is very light and has very good battery life. It does have some limitations such as you get a limited number of faces, and most are meh. While I like that I can import a picture for a customized background, you can't change the font styles and the color options are limited, and you are limited to where you can position the time. The watch has other features, like stopwatch, steps counter, count down time, but I rarely use those. I like the shake to wake up feature, but it works when it wants to, not every time. I like the rugged look and it is sturdy, after wearing it until now, I don't see any scratching other than on the screen protector and when you see the watch, you really don't notice the scratching. I really like this watch and am thinking about turning in my more expensive one. You do need to install the Da Fit app on your phone to pair it to the watch. Overall, I'm very happy with this watch.

title: OS change from Active2
text:
UPDATE 10/17/2022: I've dropped the rating from 4 to 3 because of the OS issue. Today, Android informed me that it needed to install an update. Little did I realize that when I did so, it would completely reset the watch. For a terror-filled half hour this evening, I seriously thought that the steaming offal pile that is Google was going to make me re-purchase the watch faces I'd downloaded when I originally set up the watch, as it was impossible going through the Play store to simply zero in on the apps I've downloaded. I don't know when that changed either, but where it used to be easy to view my downloads, that feature is gone too. I've been using Android products for >10 years, so I think I know my way around the Play store. The sad thing is, I really want to like this watch. There are a lot of good things about it. The switch from Tizen to Android OS, though, is a serious problem. I don't know if it's big enough yet to make me switch to the other major brand of phones and wearables, but I'm seriously considering it after this last debacle, especially since I'm up for a new phone with my carrier. Google has the anti-Midas touch lately. Very disappointing.

Size-wise, this (40mm) watch is comparable to the Active2 that I needed to replace. I didn't realize that the OS had changed, so instead of buying/downloading watch faces through Samsung like I have been, I'm now stuck getting them from Google. Samsung had a far better selection, and there are way too many candidate faces that come up in the search that look good, only to get the, "Sorry, this app is not compatible with your device!" message. Hopefully this will change soon, but so far, it's the biggest disappointment.

Also, battery life through the first couple of days has been remarkably short. Plan on charging it before you can wear it; mine came completely uncharged. For the time-between-charging issue, I think I just need to find my settings and turn off what I don't need (WiFi etc.) to get longer life out of it.

Also, it's impossible to tell from any of the pics online which size bands you'll need if you don't like the one Samsung includes. From what I've been able to tell, all of the Galaxy Watch 4 models use a 20mm band. I had to go to Best Buy to look at actual watches before pulling the trigger on the Prime Days sale for this watch. Thankfully, all the ones I recently bought for the Active2 fit the GW4.

On a positive, the screen resolution is improved vs. the Active2.

Long story short, still getting used to the watch, but so far, I'm liking it. There are enough things I don't like at the moment to keep me from saying that I love it, but I'll update this review over time to let you know how I've adapted to it. This review is only after about 36h wearing it so far.

text: This is watch is not a very good smartwatch. Maybe I'm just spoiled because I had a Samsung watch before. But the price was cheap so I decided to order this one you get what you pay for. I don't think the blood pressure readings are correct they're way too low for me according to my regular blood pressure monitor. It also does not give me emails. I also sometimes do not get phone calls alerts letting me know I'm getting a call. I'm not very happy with it but for the money I'll keep it.

title: Poor Quality, Misrepresnt features
text:
I bought Samsung after having Fitbit Versa for 4 years, because I wanted to have better communication between my cell phone and the smartwatch. talk and text options intrigued me to switch(sadly).
I reviewed the Samsung and loved all the amazing features, but now, I think that was too much expectation from this company.
Every single features that Samsung claims you will have on your smart watch is simply not true, I checked a few here:
1- It is not water resistant: Samsung have swimming app on the watch, claims you can swim and take shower and it is "IP68". The playing with words here is like a joke. First of all they do not trust their watch and their Guarantee doesn't cover if water goes inside the watch. first of all why water goes insidr an "ip68"? Please, check IP ranking and feel the pain.
2- Sleep tracker is just fake, I sleep 5 hours each night , my Watch records 7-9 hours. the graphs is also nonsense, not even close to any sleep waves you can read in a book.
3- Step counter. it is not accurate .Samsung watch and cell phone each have significant difference. turn of the blue tooth and compare the numbers,(don't let them to connect an pair for a few min).
4- SPO2: is very in accurate. at least 5-10% difference between a Pulse oxymeter and this watch. Enough to cost a life!
5-Heart rate: is non-sense. I ran the other day my HR was 170/min for 2 hours (I am still alive). I checked with a blood pressure machine and it was 75/min 10 min after activity. so, the App calculates energy expenditure based on your activity an HR. How? I bet ecg app would be the same. just recommend you to go cal your cardiologist.
All and all on the paper you see everything that you want to have , but practically you have nothing but a huge bill to pay, very disappointing!!!!
I don't recommend waste your money to buy this low quality watch. you will find much better watch with this money. This is my last Samsung ever!

title: Decent First Effort By Samsung
text:
***NOTE: My review previously incorrectly stated that IconX earbuds do not work with iOS. They do pair with an iPhone and other iOS devices and play sound without any issues. Touch playback controls work, however, there's no support for fitness features. I apologize for misreading the spec sheet without actually trying them out with an iOS device.***

I am an avid runner and a martial artist and have always been on the lookout for truly wireless earbuds that would allow me to fully engage in my workouts without worrying about them falling out or getting displaced. Thanks to Samsung, I have finally found them! But it's not to say they're perfect or without compromises. I should also mention that my previous go-to earbuds were Jaybird X2 and I will compare the two below in some areas... On to the main points...

Build Quality & Design: solid, hard plastic. They look like they are built to last. Solid case that doubles as a charging dock and has a battery of its own that is good enough to recharge the earbuds about 3 times. A clever move by Samsung given the battery life is one of the IconX's major weaknesses.

Fit: The earbuds fit exceptionally well. I have medium sized ears and ended up using M-size tips and L-size wings (there are 3 pairs of each included). They never fell out or came loose during running, heavy bag workout or anything else I threw at them. Easy to put in and take out of your ears as well. Exceptionally light. Colour me very impressed, Samsung!

Look: While visible in your ears, they're not sticking out too much. I like the look of them far more than my Jaybird X2.

Compatibility: Only Android 4.4+ is officially supported per spec sheet, though they do pair with iOS and play sound as your regular Bluetooth headphones. I had no issues whatsoever connecting my Samsung Galaxy S7 to it and listening to music/podcasts via Bluetooth. Samsung Gear app found it instantly and connected without any probs. Also successfully tested with an iPhone 6 for music/podcast playback.

Controls: I have mixed feelings about touch controls. In casual use, they're simple and quite reliable once you get used to them, but can be finicky during workouts when you're moving and your hands are sweaty. All in all, they work pretty well. I like the fact that either earbud responds to touch commands. The automatic pairing/start once you put them in and the auto-stop once you take them out are nice touches as well.

Activity Tracking: This is where we get into some negatives... IconX fails miserably as an activity tracker. It cannot track distance accurately due to lack of GPS. It told me I've run 2km after completing a 4.5km run which is just cruel. :) The heart rate monitor, as is the case with many other trackers, is unreliable. I really wish they'd concentrated their efforts on sound quality and audio player features instead... speaking of which...

Sound: No issues with sync between the earpieces or sound cutting out as reported with some other similar wireless headsets. Sound quality is pretty good for a Bluetooth headset. It can be described as "warm" with bass present but not overpowering. Highs and mids are somewhat lacking but not to the point where you won't enjoy your music. Jaybird X2, in comparison, does sound a little better but not by a huge margin.

Noise Isolation: Superb! They cancel out all the noise around you which can be a good or a bad thing, depending on what you're doing. There is an "ambient sound" mode that allows you to hear the surroundings, but it is a bit weird as the sound is fed through the built-in mics.

Music Player: IconX have 4GB of internal storage to store your audio files (each earbud has 4GB capacity and audio files are copied to both during transfers, in case you're wondering). Unfortunately, the built-in music player is very limited. You can jump back and forth between tracks, randomize their play, pause/play and that's about it. No skipping, no playlist support which makes them terrible for podcasts or any similar large audio tracks. You do have an option of streaming podcasts directly from your phone but that diminishes the already limited battery life -- its single biggest weakness...

Battery: Obviously you can't have something this light without compromising battery size... I generally found the battery life claims by Samsung accurate. 1.5 - 3 hrs depending on enabled features. My average workout length is 1.5 hrs and I use them as a standalone MP3 player (with Bluetooth / activity tracking off) which works quite well. Streaming Bluetooth will drain the battery very quick, you'll be lucky to get 1.5hrs out of them.

Another major issue that I've found with the related to battery life is that they drain battery when left unused (in case or otherwise) for a day or two. There are many reports of this on various boards, including Samsung support forums. I've contacted Samsung support about this issue and they say this is not normal behavior and the earbuds should not be discharging *while in the case*. Still not decided whether to take them in for repairs or exchange at Costco where I bought them, but I will update the review with regards to this problem.

Overall, a decent first effort by Samsung. Given the fact this is a first truly wireless headset from a major manufacturer, I am willing to accept some compromises. I took off 2 stars for the rather useless activity tracking features, the limited playback capabilities and the battery life issues. I am sure the next iterations will bring the much needed improvements and I am curious to see what other manufacturers come up with.

title: Honest review
text:
Firstly, this is an honest review by an actual customer. The watch is solid, heavy, and feels premium. Apps are the standard stuff. The keyboard is useless and there is no option for a qwerty keyboard. I did read that if your willing to jump thru hoops, you can install a Google based keyboard. (I tried and it's froze watch, so i factory reset). Not much in way of downloadable apps. Battery life is a solid 2 days.
Now on to health app. Primarily I bought it for sleep tracking so I could move my fitbit charge 3 to my ankle for accurate step count. The sleep app on fitbit is considerably more accurate than the Samsung Watch 3. Same with steps count. It would be nice to be able to upload fitbit step count to Samsung, but it's not possible. You can only manually add distance. (Anyone that says otherwise is not being honest).
The main point I dislike about the watch is the band clasp (I have the titanium watch). The clasp doesn't stay closed and I must snap it closed several times a day or risk dropping the watch. I don't wear sleeves or anything to catch on it to pull open. I've even made it loose on my wrist thinking the way I bent my hand to wrist was causing it, but it wasn't. It's a poorly made clasp.

text: The watch itself is nice, easy to use and setup, faces can be changed or customized, getting around the different screens and functions is easy as well. The only thing I use this watch for is the alarm, and steps.  Right now I do not have it setup to receive notifications of text messages or emails.  And I have the GPS function turned off.  I will turn off the weather as well, in case that is adding to the draining of the battery.  I will come back and update whether this helps or not.

title: MISLEADING description - have to return
text:
In order to set up this Samsung Galaxy Watch4 with my provider, I needed its IMEI. I searched the box and the back of the watch in vain, and realized that despite the ad's "fall detection" and

"Leave your phone behind while staying connected — call, text and stream music, all from your wrist with LTE connectivity.... "

Surprise! - this watch does NOT have LTE. Either the ad is misleading, or I got the wrong watch.

With fall detection limited to a proximity of five meters of my phone. I must return the one I was hadn't even finished unboxing.

title: Buyer beware, Seller tried to "Buy" a 5 star review from me in exchange for a full refund!
text:
Seller tried to bribe me with a 5 star review in exchange for a full refund, 3 days after it was delivered to me.

Now I question If these other "perfect" reviews were bribed and took the bait, just to get a free watch because it is a "new model" as seller tried to say.

Buyer beware. I won't be bought, so I told Seller to not email me again, and I would give it a fair review after I have had time to use it.

I have a Samsung Gear 2 watch as well, it is old but it was free. It does not have the features this one does, so for the price thought this was worth checking out.

Heart rate monitor and blood oxygen are off compared to a real blood/oxygen medical grade meter I have to compare them too. It is a few units off every time.

Miles walked seems to match up with actual GPS distance that is verified accurate, so that feature seems to be accurate.

I tried using the sleep monitoring function, and also do not believe the results it shows can be measured by simply wearing the watch at sleep, no way it can monitor all those measurements simply by your pulse at night, sleep studies would need to be measured professionally to Guage accurate measurements of sleep cycle, not this cheap watch.

I haven't used the synch to music feature, or taken a call from the watch yet, I mainly got this for the separate phone app features and the activity modes my Gear 2 watch doesn't have.

Battery life seems OK, with all the features turned on and using it daily, I am getting about 2.5 days worth before recharge. I am sure if all features were turned off, it could last a week perhaps, but not if you plan to actually use the watch features.

As others state, if you are in a activity mode, you cannot get out of the mode and go to another feature of the watch, or it "stops" monitoring the selected activity. Multiple times it has done this while walking and wanting to go to another screen of the watch.

Band feels cheap, I don't expect it to last long with daily use. I have had my Gear 2 for 5 years now, use everyday, still using original bands that were on it when I opened it.

It is a stylish looking watch for what it is, but you get what you pay for, it is a basic $45 no name watch.

YRMV, but the shady business practice of this seller turned me off. Wont be buying from them again.

Buy at your own risk.

Thanks

title: Not bad, but not the best.
text:
I started with the Charge HR. It had great life, lasted for years, was slow to update data to the app, was small and simple, but left me wanting more data gathering capabilities.

TLDR: I would wait for the Samsung Galaxy Watch 4. It will have Sp02, glucose, blood pressure, every feature the Sense has, and have better features that are an extension of your smartphone. The Apple watch comes in second but doesn't offer what I want, especially at the price.

I want a health tracking watch that provides me with expansive health metric tracking over time, sleep tracking, and smart time wake-up capability. It is a bonus to me if the watch works as a good extension of my smartphone (i.e. for notifications, checklists, navigation, etc).

The Sense is leaps and bounds better than anything I have had before but it again still leaves me wanting more.

1. I would like all time Sp02
2. I would like blood pressure monitoring
3. The watch already collects and processes all of the data, and I paid for the watch, so I don't like the idea of having a subscription on top of the cost. Luckily, they don't pay-wall too many metrics.
4. The watch integrates terribly with the smartphone. There are essentially no apps that support the watch so the watch is basically it's own device. The only app that I found was useful is United Airlines. This would give you flight information and allow you to display your boarding pass. NO other airlines offer an app so you are extremely limited. 4a. Lets say you want to have a simple shopping checklist on your watch so that you don't have to keep unlocking or looking at your phone. With the Sense, it is hopeless. The only workaround is an app that allows you to write yourself a text file using your smartphone's text app, then copy the file into your fitbit app. You must write in specific formatting, otherwise it will not work. Too much of a hassle and it could be so much easier.
5. Not all fitbit apps are optimized or will even work with the Sense as they are written for older fitbits. In addition, there are several apps that are clear knock-offs of apps maintained by real companies. I am surprised that they haven't been taken down for copy-write claims. In addition, I appreciate having apps that are backed by companies and not just apps that are created by some high-school student in their basement. The apps that are professionally developed have less issues and present much less of a security concern.
6. The screen isn't terribly responsive. I find myself being a little frustrated sometimes trying to swipe right to navigate the watch. The other swipes seem more responsive and consistent.
7. Wear this in the shower or rain and every drop of water has the potential of taking over your smartwatch! The watch thinks you are touching it and my watch was moving around all of the screens, apps, and changing all of my settings. It looked like someone was remoting into my watch! The fix would be to temporarily disable the rotate-wrist-to-wake-up screen feature every time it rains or you want to take a shower. It would be nice if I could just hold the side button for 3 seconds to lock the screen or something.
8. The fitbit app seems underdeveloped and hacked together but it mostly works. Surprising that they app hasn't changed much in the last 6 years.

The good:
1. The ECG/EKG is awesome! Fitbit wouldn't let me use the ECG at first which was extremely shocking but I was able to eventually set it up after watching a youtube video. Essentially, Fitbit thought I was in a foreign country and wouldn't let me use the ECG.
1. The watch tells me sleep information really quickly after I wake up. This info is very helpful for me to decide if I should try to go back to sleep. I've been struggling to manage sleep for decades, so any help is appreciated. The older Charge HR took 20+ to tell me how I slept, which was pretty useless.
2. The watch is actually useful and better than nothing. I will ditch this thing as soon as the Samsung Galaxy Watch 4 as soon as it is released.
3. From what I have seen, Fitbit has the market cornered on sleep tracking and analysis. If you buy any other watch, it would seem that you might be giving up some of this expertise, algorithm, and other technologies.
4. It's a good size as I don't like thick watches or large diameter watches.
5. Screen quality is good. Battery life is good.

title: It's a Time Sponge, not a Time Piece
text:
I've had this quirky frustrating piece of junk for over a year and a half now, paired with a Samsung S7, and I must say it has been way more trouble than it's worth. It's cool when it works, but that's a rarity. I bought this TicWatch to replace a Samsung watch that I had lost. The Samsung was easy to use and never gave me a problem. But after losing it I thought I'd try another approach. I liked the TicWatch features, especially the dual layer screen, especially since even with all the features (which I do use when I can get them to work) it's still a wrist watch, and I like being able to just see what time it is at a glance, without lighting up the room. I got a kick out of all the features for the first couple of months, but then it started acting up. It would start doing weird things or sometimes just freeze altogether. I can't tell you how many reboots and complete resets I've had to do, and all the research I had to do in online forums just to figure out that a reset was needed and how to do it. And apps like stop watch or pulse are useless because the screen doesn't stay active long enough to time anything or take your pulse, and there is no way to get it to stay on longer without adding an app that messes up other things. Over the past year and a half I have found this thing to be completely unreliable - even just to tell time as it freezes up at the worst moments. Keeping it going for all this time has soaked up more of my time than the thing is worth (thus the term "Time Sponge"). My recommendation as a relatively long term user? Pass on this one. Keep shopping. Or, if you have a Samsung phone, get a Samsung watch. This thing just isn't worth it.

Update - six months after writing the above the watch no longer connects with my phone. I've updated the apps on the phone, factory reset the watch, double checked Bluetooth ... nothing. Phone just sits there saying "trying to connect", and watch says " connect Wear OS to watch". This watch is now only good for one entertainment purpose - put it out on the street and watch cars run over it! Utterly useless piece of junk!

title: Bought because other review sites said this is the best 2018 Wear OS watch.
text:
My Motorola 360 v2 battery died and the watch is over 2+ years old now. I researched a good Wear OS watch and the TicWatch Pro was recommended.

Things I like:
2nd LCD screen for basic info.
Wear OS options

Things I dislike:
Quality of screen is inferior to the 2016 Moto 360 v2. Screen pixels visible, contrast lacking.
Size. The watch is bulky compared to the Moto or even the current Samsung Gear watch.

I originally bought a Samsung Gear watch but realized they do not use Wear OS. They use a Samsung watch system called Tizen and hence I bought the TicWatch Pro . But now after almost a month, I've decided I'm going back to the Samsung watch until Google releases a better Pixel watch.

I really wanted to like the device but the aesthetics aren't great, screen quality not as good as Samsung or Moto watch from 2016, and OS sometimes still runs choppy as Wear OS has not seen new processors in watches in over 2+ years.

Wait for a better Pixel watch from Google or go with Apple or Samsung.

title: WEAK Battery & UNRESPONSIVE Screen
text:
The battery lasts 2 days, if that. My wife bought a cheapo Made In China $30 HR monitor watch with all the bells and whistles and that give a week of battery!

Secondly, the screen is EXTREMELY unresponsive to touch. So much so as to make an easy decision to return. Spending far too much time trying to "activate" a touch response is exasperating!

A subpoint ding is also that the screen is TOO DIM in direct sunlight. It gets completely washed out. You have to palm a shadow over the screen to see. When jogging outside, this is frustrating to the point of "realistic conditions" UNUSABILITY! Compared to my Samsung Galaxy direct sunlight comparison, it's a day/night comparative difference. Amazfit should have increased the brightness/nits to resolve such a no-brainer issue! It shows you engineers can be smart while being extremely dumb (sorry, had to say it!).

Thirdly, I primarily bought this watch so I can control my Android phone music player from the watch. Although there is a feature advertised to do this (in Settings), this is FALSE! Customer Service replied to only one of my emails regarding this and their answer was a copy/paste from the small user manual. There is NO way to control your phone's music player or any app with a player, like Spotify/Google Music.

....BTW, in my search for a watch that does things I need, I found TONS of SUPERIOR watches of this ilk on AliExpress and at super prices WAY BELOW this watch! It was an eye opener what's out there from Asia! To give you a jump start on this stuff, check out Zeblaze Thor 4, Kospet Hope to name an extreme few of the army of watches that blow this Amazfit out of the water (and cheaper!!).

EDIT: After using the watch before my return, I raised my star rating from 1 to 3. The battery life has substantially improved to 5 days turning everything off like bluetooth. Secondly, I figured out that there IS a remote music player!!! But for this to work you FIRST need to turn ON your phone's player. This then activates a player HIDDEN on the Home Screen, which you then need to swipe UP to see a basic control scheme: FF/RW/Pause/Volume Up & Down. The fact that this ISN'T IN the manual or website is horrendous! I will still be returning the watch as the screen is very difficult to use with finger touch--too much time putzing around. Secondly, the screen washes out in the direct Sun really badly versus screens from other brands with higher brightness/nits, like the Samsung Galaxy watch--a day/night difference!

title: Don't buy this watch if you need to monitory your heart rate
text:
I had a Galaxy Active 2 watch. A year ago the Samsung Health App stopped displaying your heart rate over time. Instead they give you a bar graph that is specifically designed to have no useful information. The watch still displayed a plot of your heart rate since mid-night and there is an App that can transfer your Heart Rate data to Google and Google will display it.
For the Samsung Galaxy Watch 5, they have totally removed the collection of heart rate data. You can't even use Google to display it.
Literally, the only way to monitor your heart rate over time is to check every few minutes and write it down. Instead of giving you your heart rate plot they give you a bar graph the looks almost the same when you are sleeping as working out. Intentionally of no value what so ever.

title: Subpar connectivity, poor reminder system, and bad software. Better options exist at this price!
text:
As of January 2020: As a fitness tracker, this does all right -- it tracks your BPM, steps, sleep, and can automatically detect certain exercises. It doesn't have a GPS to track distance traveled, which other products in the same price range have (Galaxy Watch Active 1 and Apple Watch Series III), however, that never really bothered me. The five-day battery life is great -- but I think that comes at the cost of robust connectivity and a lower-res screen.

But it's advertised and priced like a fitness tracker + smartwatch, and this is where it falls short.

The Alexa implementation is a total gimmick. Fitbit does not tell you this, but you can't store more than 3 (!) reminders at any given time. Deleting reminders requires you to log out and log back in to Alexa on your phone. Furthermore, the watch relies on remaining connected to your phone to use Alexa -- but bad connectivity issues make it so that you often have to take your phone out of your pocket to use voice commands on or sync your watch.

Options for applications are sparse and there is no option to add easy-access widgets. Even basic features like syncing with your online calendar or providing regular and robust notifications from your phone are either buggy or lack key features. Simple things like having a checklist require a third party app that is semi-functional.

On the other hand, the Samsung Galaxy Active 1 or Apple Watch Series III (which cost the same or are cheaper) get the smartwatch/organizational features right. For instance, the Galaxy Active 1 stores virtually unlimited reminders, has customizable widgets that can function as calendars, checklists, workout routines, health, etc, and has much better Bluetooth connectivity for notifications or updates. Unlike the Versa 2, the reminder, calendar, and checklist features just work (as they should!). The battery life on these watches (~2 days) is not as good as the Versa 2 (~5 days), but I've found 2 days to be plenty. Putting the Galaxy or Apple watches on power save mode essentially reduces them to the level of functionality the Versa 2 has, and that makes the charge last significantly longer (~3-4 days). On top of all this, both these watches do just as well as the Versa 2 for health tracking (if not better!).

In conclusion, I just can't imagine favoring this watch over other obviously better options on the market. I ended up trading this watch in for a Galaxy Active 1 and haven't looked back ever since. If you own an iPhone, the Apple Watch Series III would be a great option too.

Subpar connectivity, poor reminder system, and bad software. Better options exist at this price!. As of January 2020: As a fitness tracker, this does all right -- it tracks your BPM, steps, sleep, and can automatically detect certain exercises. It doesn't have a GPS to track distance traveled, which other products in the same price range have (Galaxy Watch Active 1 and Apple Watch Series III), however, that never really bothered me. The five-day battery life is great -- but I think that comes at the cost of robust connectivity and a lower-res screen.

But it's advertised and priced like a fitness tracker + smartwatch, and this is where it falls short.

The Alexa implementation is a total gimmick. Fitbit does not tell you this, but you can't store more than 3 (!) reminders at any given time. Deleting reminders requires you to log out and log back in to Alexa on your phone. Furthermore, the watch relies on remaining connected to your phone to use Alexa -- but bad connectivity issues make it so that you often have to take your phone out of your pocket to use voice commands on or sync your watch.

Options for applications are sparse and there is no option to add easy-access widgets. Even basic features like syncing with your online calendar or providing regular and robust notifications from your phone are either buggy or lack key features. Simple things like having a checklist require a third party app that is semi-functional.

On the other hand, the Samsung Galaxy Active 1 or Apple Watch Series III (which cost the same or are cheaper) get the smartwatch/organizational features right. For instance, the Galaxy Active 1 stores virtually unlimited reminders, has customizable widgets that can function as calendars, checklists, workout routines, health, etc, and has much better Bluetooth connectivity for notifications or updates. On top of all this, both these watches do just as well as the Versa 2 for health tracking (if not better!).

title: This "refurbished" Watch is just a "Heavily USED" under a different name.
text: I am disappointed. Its being sold with Amazon's "Stamp of approval", but this is BAD. No original box NOR are any of the accessories. The accessories are "Wish.com" level items. The watch had a residue on the bezel and scratched all over the metal and a scratch on the back/bottom of the watch. I managed to clean the residue [it was like masking solution or something]. The replacement band is NOT genuine leather and is super cheap. The device got really warm upon charging, and I think [I hope it doesn't though] it may have issues with overheating in the future as I read others have had. I paid extra for the Mobile Data version over the BT version [MISTAKE] this was MY mistake though because I have a pre-paid device [metroPCS] and I did not know it was not compatible unless you have a "contract" plan... again My mistake for not researching more. The bluetooth connection is a bit wonky as well. What I mean is, I have Bluetooth headphones and also Samsung Gear IconX earbuds. The JBL headphones can connect to 2 devices at one, but it will not connect to my phone and watch. May be Working as designed, but i assumed I'd be able to save tracks to my watch and listen or switch to my phone [Samsung S21+] and listen to Spotify or Audible. It Doesn't seem to like that at all... If I try, when listening on the watch its SUPER choppy and seems to go in and out of connection. When i disconnect it from the phone it plays cleanly. I don't have this issue with any other BT device my headphones has been connected to simultaneously. Although, again, this may be WAD. I could be doing something wrong. It's a nice watch, Would have loved one that was "more" clean, and newer, but as Samsung no longer sells this watch online and I didn't have an extra $300+ to spend on a newer model, I turned to Amazon... Ugh... it's what I get for going cheaper. SAVE your money and Save up for one directly from Samsung...

title: Do not buy this watch. It will break after a year!!!!
text:
DO NOT BUY THIS WATCH!!! I bought this watch for my husband on Jan 2017. He lived and raved about this watch. Then recently the face popped off and the arms were acting weird on the face. Luckily i thought the warranty that i bought from Amazon would help. I filed a claim with Assurance. I was told that they would not fix the watch since it is called physical damage.

My husband didn’t even do anything to the watch!

So, spend your money on an Apple Watch or a Samsung watch. DO NOT BUY THIS PIECE OF CRAP.

This is not the first time a garmin watch has failed. Shame on us for believing in their product.

title: BAD products and BAD services
text:
One warning for the people using Samsung products. BAD products and BAD services.
I bought this Samsung Gear S2 Smartwatch Classic from Amazon last December 2015, sale price is $330 and for sure I had chosen US version because I’m living in US. So last 2 weeks, somehow the plastic to hold the bezel on the screen of the watch broken, it caused the bezel to fell off, it was not my fault, I think the watch still under warranty and I called Samsung for repair it.
1st time they asked me the invoice and the serial number, email and telephone and address, I sent them the copy of invoice and the UPC code had the serial number on it, Samsung don’t answer me, I have to call them back again, this time they said my serial number is not valid, they asked me 4 days to verify the serial number, Samsung don’t answer me again, after 4 days I call them back, this time they said my serial number was not valid and they refused me to have service. So I wrote this review for the people like Samsung’s products like me, think back carefully before by the Samsung products. My watch was $350 dollars, I used only more than a month and then go to the trash can.

title: Not worth the cost
text:
Like other reviewers have said, this watch gets 2 1/2 days before the battery needs to be charged, MAX. Less than a year after purchase, it started dying in one day. Then it started working properly again and I was getting two days out of one charge. However, once it started working again, the step counter stopped working...two days after the warranty ran out. My previous watches had been FitBit and I never got more than a year out of them. I guess it's the same with a Samsung. At least this model. I'll still use it. I never cared about the step counter. I wanted it to tell time, give me notifications, and give me my heart rate, which it still does all of that. I can't say I won't buy another Samsung watch since my husband's (different model) has lasted for years. I just wouldn't recommend this one.

Edit: One month after the warranty ran out, I found that the heart rate wasn't working anymore and the Bluetooth barely works. I definitely cannot recommend this watch. I'm letting it die and shopping for something else.

title: No Flaws
text:
I am a professional photographer. I received this as a promo when I purchased my Galaxy Note. I do not get paid or compensated for this review. I have read a couple of reviews and they are misleading. First off, the software for Windows/Mac is NOT hard to find. It took me 5 seconds to find it. Just Google "Samsung Gear 360 Camera" choose the official Samsung site. It takes you straight to the Gear's product site and immediately to the right in the grey box it says "Requires MicroSD memory card to capture and save content.
For Windows/Mac software, click here "
NOT HARD.
Also top heavy? Maybe if you put it on an uneven surface. I was on top of the Arch de Triumph, put this (without any artificial support), on a piece of the original Arch outside of the fenced area on top of it in windy conditions, took many photos without so much as touching it. It did not fall. In fact, using my phone as a remote utilizing live view on my phone (AWESOME) I was able to control the iso and speed settings, and captured some wonderful photos (regular stills, 180s and 360s)!!!! This thing is AWESOME!!! NO Flaws, so long as you are able to use simple tech. I mean it's 2018, everyone should know how to use this simple tech by know, or at least be able to download the software that is made available easily.

Get outside and take some photos!!!! So much history, so much nature to see!!!!

title: Sensors Totally Inaccurate, Great Battery Life
text:
I use to have a Samsung watch and tried this brand after my Samsung broke. I was really disappointed in the step-count of this watch in particular. It's totally off and is way higher than it should be. According to my phone, I've taken 256 steps so far this morning. According to this watch, I've taken 729. That's a huge discrepancy. Even before I got out of bed this morning, my watch said I'd taken 76 steps. It makes me question how accurate the other readings are (heart rate, O2 levels, sleep monitor, etc.).

If you're not too interested in the fitness aspect of this watch, the battery life is great and lasts days. It's also easy to change the bands, and there are a lot of inexpensive bands options on Amazon.

The phone app is cheesy, and the interface is "okay". You can tell when reading things on the app or watch that it was translated from a different language. The grammar and punctuation aren't the best. I think this makes the watch feel cheaper because of this. It might not bother some people, but it's very distracting to me.

title: Very compressed files.  Poor resolution.  Easy to use
text:
I was sorely disappointed in this product. It has so much going for it, but in the end, it seems the developers decided that they were going to limit compatibility and make sharing to facebook the only thing this thing does well. Low resolution in 360 is absolute rubbish.

Things that are awful
1. The jpgs are 6 *megabytes* in size. For a 360 photo? That's far too much compression.
2. unable to change file type or resolution
3. stitching is not seamless and HAS to be done using Samsung gear 360 software which has limited functionality
4. No raw image data saved or accessible

Things that are ok
1. the form factor is cute
2. it is easy to set up and use
3. it looks nice on the phone
4. quick charging

On the other hand, the battery life is excellent.

title: Great for 6 months . . . now unpredictable to the point of uselessness.
text: The BOTTOM LINE: After less than one year, the battery-charging and watch-discharging have become so erratic that I cannot count on any of the many features even being available.  Coming from the generation before computers existed, I struggled to figure out all of the many functions and capabilities of a "smartwatch."  Much as I became proficient with computers, it took me longer than younger colleagues to learn many of the multiple-button-pushing algorithms of the Samsung Galaxy Watch Active 2.  What completely disappoints me is that my watch no longer charges OR holds a charge in ANY predictable way.  I simply have elected not to use the watch for anything more than a silent wake-up alarm and I then take it off and put it on the charger until the next night (hoping it will be charged but often finding that it has not).  Where can I seek assistance?

title: You get what you pay for
text:
Had this device for just over six months and miss my Samsung GearFit 2

1) GPS takes forever to connect.
2) GPS is very inaccurate.
3) HR monitor kills the battery (you can turn it off).
4) If you turn off the HR it will no longer perform sleep tracking.
5) Counts steps and stairs while you are typing on a computer/eating/driving... really anything that involves your arms.
6) if you do not move your arm while walking i.e. hold a coffee cup, you are not moving.
7) First time I got this "water proof" watch wet the screen stopped working.

So I would say look at something else.
