title: Information on your Wrist
text: I previously had a Samsung Gear s3 Frontier, when it died after 4 years, i purchased a Fitbit sense.  My sense fell apart after a little over year.  After contacting fitbit for warrantee information they informed me it was out of warrantee and offered no solution.  I decided to purchase a refurbished Samsung Galaxy watch 4 after reading the reviews.  I love my watch, the battery (although not near as long as the fitbit sense) gets me thru a full day without a charge (something my gear s3 struggled to do).  Sleep tracking, steps, heart, ecg, stress, and oxygen measurements are all great.  I even downloaded a few apps on my watch that I couldn't get on the fitbit.  So far I love my watch.  Only thing missing from my galaxy watch4 is google assistant but i read where that is coming soon.

title: Liberty Plus might be the better route to go with Zolo
text:
Having no wires is really neat but overall these earbuds have become an annoyance. The silicone sleeves for fit adjustment end up really hurting my ears after a short while. The battery does not last as long as the competition - 3.5 hours vs 7 hours on the 2018 Samsung Gear Icon X. There is no way to set the EQ of these through an app like my Jaybird X3's. The sound on these is decent but not great - I tend to hear a soft crackle or other artifacts. The worst annoyance I have is keeping them off. I will turn them off and hear them power down. Then I place them in their case for charging and 30 seconds later or so I see that my phone is pushing audio through the ear buds again. I have to keep going and turning them off and delicately placing them in their case so that the door of the case doesn't touch their on button. It keeps happening over and over again. I might be interested in looking at the Liberty Plus model when it's available as I hear some of these issues were addressed and they come with a better case. Unfortunately, I can't help but feel let down by Anker on these and I have A LOT of great Anker products.

Update: After about 3 days of trying these I have now experienced the issue others are complaining about - the left earbud cutting out. Also, today after taking them out of the charging case and using them for about 20 minutes, the battery started to die on me. I'm guessing they did not make a good connection in the case and did not recharge correctly. The case was fully charged. I think this product has great potential in the next iteration but, as of now, there's a lot of room foro improvement.

title: Screen wouldn't respond to button after 3 weeks use
text:
I have a Samsung s5 phone so I thought a samsung gear fit will be a reasonable choice.
I had it for Christmas present and wore it for 3 weeks till yesterday, the screen wouldn't respond to the button. Then the battery drained in 7 hours from 100% to 5%. I reset it and hope it will recover but in vain. Fortunately my husband bought it from Amazon and I was able to return it.
I liked it before it broke (not even 1 month!). It recorded my activity accurately. I didn't need my phone everywhere to record my steps. The battery lasted days before recharge. I might have been unlucky to receive a defective one since other people don't seem to have the same problem. Very disappointed, I am trying other brand this time.

title: Tickin Me Off
text:
My original Galaxy Watch died after almost 5 years. I didn't realize how good that watch was until I started looking for a replacement. Based on the reviews on Amazon, the Tickwatch sounded like a good option. Within 24hrs it was clear that it wasn't for me.

1.) The secondary display triggers all night long waking me up. I turned on theater mode which turns off the display. Unfortunately in this mode, YOU CAN'T TURN OFF THE ALARM!!! Seriously, it's a known issue discussed on the Mobvoi forum for the past year.
2.) Second night, I tried essential mode. I'd lost sleep tracking, but at least I would get some sleep and have an alarm. WRONG! In essential mode, the alarm is disabled. Again, this is discussed on the forum, with no solution from Mobvoi. They have the ability to turn off essential mode, but apparently that also don't work reliably.
3.) Third night, I tried just covering my watch under the sheet. That resulted in the watch getting stuck in bootloader mode. I presume I hit some combination of buttons throughout the night.

Minor issues:
1.) Like almost all smart watches there is zero crown to protect the display. The display will get scratches and probably cracked in long term use. It seems to be universal, even Samsung is dropping the crown on the Galaxy Watch 5. Just a bad design.
2.) Battery Life was just ok. 76%-6% in 48hrs.
3.) Heart Rate never seemed to work. It only captured a couple data points each day.
4.) Secondary Display is bright. Primary display is dim and low contrast.

It's being returned.

title: Just say no if you have a Samsung Phone
text: I truly tried to like this watch.  Really.  Maybe it was not having Wear 3.0, idk.  Maybe it was the fact that I couldn't have it tell me my calories, heart rate and distance thru my headphones.  Maybe because it was the fact that I had to download numerous other apps to get it to 'semi' communicate with my phone (Note 20 Ultra) the way the Watch 4 did...but just barely.  Maybe it was because in reality, with what I need to be running on the watch 24 and 7, the battery was no better then they watch 4.  It also could have been that the Mobvoi app never truly synced with Fit.  Pretty badly actually, like missing my daily workouts.  And the proprietary charger?  Good luck with keeping that on the watch.  Yeah, no, this watch isn't for me.  Maybe it is for you, give it a try, but be ready to sit down for hours trying to set it up the way it takes MINUTES to do with a Galaxy Watch.  I cannot recommend it.

title: A necessity for the IconX but overpriced and does not last
text:
The foam tips fit perfectly on my Samsung Gear IconX (2018). Due to the short earbud nozzle of the IconX (and typical of most truly wireless earbuds to allow smaller charging cases), standard foam tips will not fit the IconX--these fit perfectly and sit securely on the earbud since the earbud core is 'recessed'. The product works much better than the stock silicone tips that the IconX comes with. The better seal improved sound quality noticeably. Unfortunately despite this, I cannot recommend this product.

Although this is set of 3 tips, it still cost $20 (not great value). Each pair has lasted about 3 weeks of near daily use before tearing. Once the foam tears, attempting to continue using them causes irritation in the ear canal. I'm currently on my last set after ordering these late February/early March this year.

TL;DR: Although the foam tips do their job, the durability is poor and disappointing.

title: Physically a great watch, but had to return...
text: I'm editing my initial review, because after having this watch for just under a month, I decided to return it. I have a Samsung Galaxy S6 and I was having CONSTANT connection issues. I did every troubleshooting thing I could do and I still couldn't get it to stay connected throughout the day. My phone would be within FEET of the watch and I would look down and see that it was not connected, probably 5-6 times per day. It became such a hassle that I couldn't justify paying so much for something that just wasn't consistent. I've had the Samsung Gear S for about a year now and it is a top-notch piece of equipment. I don't like the physicality of it like I do the LG Urbane, but it has never given me an issue with connecting and works exactly as it says it should. I'm bummed this watch didn't work out for me, because I really did like what it looked like on my wrist.

title: Good and...
text:
...well, some of the design is just plain stoopid.

I previously had the Samsung Gear Smartwatch V1 and moved it on, ready for the next gen device. This is pretty close to being next gen, but not completely. My biggest gripe is the watchband and the way Qualcomm has elected to design it into the device the way they have - the good people at Qualcomm tried, but this is asinine. If you do not know, you size the band and then physically cut it to a length before the device is wearable. If your wrist gets smaller or the person's wrist you subsequently sell the device to when you done is smaller, no prob - the other way, hmmm... no bueno ...and there is no replacement for it - pretty much it's a one-and-done 'adjustment'. Dumb, dumb, dumb...

The good parts so far include being able to control apps on my S5 with it, like the Google Play music app and I like the flexibility of the Mirasol display - low power display, backlit when you want it to be or need it to be Really simple to change the display relative to what's happening or how you want to use it. Replying to text messages using Nuance is pretty neat too - probably better than the Samsung Voice app.

Are there other aspects I like? time will tell. More apps will make it more useful for sure, but the watchband issue - really lame. Expected better from a company that prides themselves on superior engineering.

title: Battery life is way too short.
text:
I love the watch but no matter what they say about the battery, it needs daily charging. I hate that. For a while I added a new face to it. Great idea but with the new face, it might mean I charged in the morning and in the evening. A lot of neat apps work with this watch but again, if I use them it might mean a double charge. Can't believe Samsung didn't have the foresight to realize that after their Galaxy Watch. Now even the Watch Active 2 has a small battery. Not sure what I might do. I'm testing now with black background and no functions but phone and text. What a waste of a good watch. I'm wondering now how they can say it'll last 2 or 3 days. If you use it, it won't.

12-5-2015 A little less than 6 months later, I'm thinking about switching to a different watch. Sometimes I find I need to charge the watch during the day to get it to last. The battery is terrible. I also hate the daily briefings that pop up in the morning and evening -- more battery usage and I can't figure how to turn it off. I've followed all the directions but doesn't work. I would not buy this again. Maybe it's time to go back to Garmin.

title: Not the greatest smart home tool, and probably won't be for a while
text:
I got this because the Pixel watch isn't coming out for a while *tears*. So far the Samsung watch is...ok (keep reading).

The design is great, even on my tiny tiny rist. I wish it was charged right out of the box.

The apps & widgets are a challenge!! There are very few good apps available in the store. It's REALLY hard to find a 4 star app. Gmail, Google Maps, Netflix, or Nest apps are not available yet. There's a Google assistant patch (GAssist.net), but you have to be somewhat tech savvy to install it. There is a 3rd party $2 Alexa assistant app. Be prepared to spend money on good-ish apps. This watch isn't going to be a great smart home tool right out of the box, and probably won't be perfect for quite some time.

title: Not comfortable, subpar build quality
text:
This was my second attempt at wireless earbuds after purchasing a pair of Cowin KY02. I bought the Otufan earbuds based on the pretty good reviews and the ability to control the volume from the earbuds, which is not a feature found on all wireless earbuds. I found the Otufan to be bulky in my ears and despite trying all earpads provided in the box, I could not get one to fit properly. Since my primary use for the earbuds would be during exercise, I had to return them. The controls are as advertised and the volume can be adjusted from the earbuds. Too bad they did not fit.

7/27/19 edit: after trying other options from other brands and facing other issues (fit, pairing problem, charging issue), i decided to spend $30 more and get a pair of refurbished Samsung Gear IconX. DO NOT WASTE YOUR TIME WITH CHEAP EARBUDS. The Samsung are night and day as compared to all $30-$40 no name earbuds I have tried. They fit nicely, offer a lot of fitting options, have a great battery life, and are supported by an excellent software.

title: Disappointed...
text:
I am super disappointed in this watch. I decided to upgrade my Fitbit to a smart watch so I could do more and initially purchased the Samsung Gear S3 Frontier (non-LTE). Great watch, annoying that it does not run Android and because I don't have a Samsung phone (I have a Motorola Droid Turbo 2) I was losing out on some of the features because they weren't compatible, specifically the voice commands and ability to text. I decided to look for another watch that would be compatible with my phone but has the features I loved on the Gear S3. I loved the ability to use it as a bluetooth speaker for my phone, to play music on it, I loved the heart rate monitor.
I decided on the Huawei watch and can't be more disappointed. The HR monitor is not continuous which is a PITA and when it does take a reading it is completely incorrect. I have tested this by taking my heart rate using a medical grade HR monitor simultaneously as well as manually taking my HR on the opposite wrist. In addition the sleep tracking is horrible. It only tracks sound but not movement.
The speaker is virtually useless. I can not use it as bluetooth speaker any longer because no one can hear me and I can't hear anyone clearly even with the volume all the way up. The speaker volume is significantly quieter than any other sounds the watch makes. On the S3 I regularly used it to talk on the phone while doing things around the house where I normally couldnt hold my phone (folding laundry,emptying dishwasher, etc).
In addition although it has a speaker and you can control spotify you can not stream spotify to the watch which is just annoying because again for the same reasons as above I used that feature all the time.
Other features that the Gear S3 stands out is the rotating crown makes it super easy to switch between screens, I am constantly swiping away notifications just to get to the menu screen on the Huawei to switch to an app (HR, step tracking, etc).
The upside to the Huawei is the super fast charging although it only works if you line it up perfectly, you can not just drop the watch on the charger, I often have to pick up the watch and charger to assure the pins are lined up before it will start charging.
I am unfortunately stuck with the Huawei watch though because even though I used a Prime seller (Maxproductsales) to purchase it they want to charge me a return stocking fee as well as the cost of original shipping since I received it with free shipping initially and I am responsible for returning shipping. This would put me out about $75ish.
I am hoping that when the Androidwear 2.0 upgrade reaches this watch that it addresses some of the issues I am having but I have almost considered just going back to my Fitbit at this point since at least it accurately tracks my steps, HR, and sleep.

title: DO NOT BUY GEAR 360
text:
Important update!!!
Dead after 6 weeks. Got a insta 360 air which costs double ($120) what I paid for this ($60) but is 10x is much better.
DO NOT BUY SAMSUNG GEAR 360!!!!
was okay while it lasted.
_____________________________________________
I'm enjoying using it with my friends. It has several drawbacks that the cameras costing 4 times as much don't have but to me this is a toy and $400 is too much to spend for a few giggles.

I don't have a Samsung phone, so I don't get to use the app. It would be nice to review footage without connecting to my PC but it is what it is. I seem to have enough setting control with the buttons.

I wish it had self-levelling, it's a pain to have to hold it exactly level all the time. I'm going to stick a bubble level on it lol.

The image quality is bland (I'm being kind) but the results are a fun novelty. Only worth it if you have techy friends. High quality available elsewhere.

title: Not necessarily Waterproof
text:
*updated 11/11/2020 I revised my rating to one star. If it were possible to make it zero, I would.

I've only used this housing a handful of times and the latch is now broken. Adding that experience to the poor optics and the leaky gasket, there really isn't a single reason to purchase this. Not one. It was a complete waste of money. I would avoid this housing.

I live on a lake and spend a great deal of time in and on the water. I've sacrificed more than my fair share of GoPro cameras to the lake gods while out playing. It gets old doing that so I decided to do everything I can to keep the rest of my equipment in good working order. That includes purchasing floats or enclosures. After reading many reviews I can't say I had high hopes for the Underwater Housing Case for Samsung Gear 360 Camera. After using it yesterday I fully understand the lack of enthusiasm for the product. Here's why:
I carefully placed my camera inside the enclosure, mounted it on a tripod then lowered the assembly into the water adjacent to my dock. The depth was about three feet, which is considerably less than the advertised ability to withstand depths of 30'. After an hour I retrieved the camera and inspected the housing. While there wasn't enough water inside to cause damage, there was a significant amount of liquid inside. If it had been underwater much longer I think there would have been enough to short out the cam. I may try adding a semi-liquid applique to help the gasket seal a little better.
My expectations were exceeded in regard to picture quality. Many had rated the enclosure poorly for diminishing the optics. Aside from a prominent seam, I think that it did okay and am content with results.
I would probably buy the enclosure again with the understanding of its limitations. No dives to 30 feet or spending more than 30 minutes or so under water. With that, I wish there were a higher quality enclosure for the 360.

title: Great activity tracker but not a great battery life
text: I was so excited to get this activity tracker since I just got the Samsung Galaxy S7 phone. The Gear Fit2 also sends notifications, texts, track the weather. When I received the Samsung Gear Fit2 the battery was at 15% and took 1 1/2 hours to fully charged. I was able to get a interactive face for it in the app store for free but realized that my battery was dead within a day. The recommended battery life is 3-4 days and mine last 2 days max with the brightness on the lowest and the do not disturb on. I do not feel like it tracks my steps accurate. I like that I can track the weather, water intake, coffee intake, alarm, time walking/running. I gave it 4 out of 5 stars because of the battery life and step accuracy. Besides those 2 things, the Samsung Gear Fit2 is Great! #insidersgearfit2

title: although it has its good points. Some notes
text:
I’ve had my Samsung Gear 360 for about 2 months (since 9/28/16). I was excited to get it. My first experience with it is a bit disappointing, although it has its good points. Some notes:
• It is not going to do the job for professional panorama photographers. I took 2 shots of the same room 3 minutes apart under identical conditions. I did one using Google camera (the cell phone app that allows you to take 360 panoramas (or “photospheres” as Google calls them) and the other with the Gear 360. What I noticed:
o The exposures were much better in the Google Camera panoramas. I guess that is because google takes many pictures and each of them is exposed for the lighting in that direction. In the Gear 360 photos, some areas were very dark and other overexposed. This was true even when I used HDR (which helped but still wasn’t very good). I am frequently taking photos where one part of the photo is so dark I can’t see important parts of it and other parts are overexposed. If there are no big differences in lighting, then this wouldn’t be a problem.
o The Google photos had lots of stitching errors due to the fact that I took them handheld. I have used a monopod with the Google Camera app to keep the camera in one position. That helps a lot, but there are still lots of stitching errors.
o The Gear 360 photos had a noticeable stich, but only at one juncture, i.e. where the 2 fisheye photos joined. Others have mentioned this also.
o I used a tripod to take pictures at something close to eyelevel. The tripod is in the picture. If you use the 3 small legs attached to the camera, you will have the camera sitting on a table or on the floor, neither of which is great.
• The camera does not come with any software that would allow you to view the panorama on your computer. That’s a pain, since your photos look better on the computer screen and that’s where you’ll want to keep them over time. 3rd party apps are available to view on your computer, but Samsung should include that with the camera.
• It does have a panorama viewer for the phone.
• A charger is not included but is required. The instructions say you must charge before use. But there is no charger with it. It says you must use an approved charger, but it does not have any listing of an approved charger. Not a Samsung charger or any other.
o If you try to buy the product from Samsung directly, they don’t list a charger for it. And they don’t make clear that it is something you need to buy (otherwise you can’t charge your camera).
o FYI, a Samsung phone charger will work. Samsung confirmed that is OK.
• It comes with a tiny quick start “manual”. Not a full manual. You can get a more extensive manual online but again, finding it was hard. Google did a better job than Samsung in locating Samsung’s manual.
• No micro SD card included but is required. When you buy the camera, Samsung’s website offers the option to buy a micro SD card but does not make clear that it is required.
• The manual says to download the ActionDirector program go to the [...] but a search on the site for “ActionDirector” finds nothing. I eventually found the program (by a Google Search as the search on Samsung’s website found nothing).

Conclusion. I was excited to get a camera that would take 360 panoramas in one shot, but I was disappointed in the results. I don’t use it as much as I thought I would. I will be looking for a better camera.

title: Too good to be true
text:
I'm so disappointed in this camera. From the start it was nothing but problems. It doesn't "require" you to use the Samsung Gear app to control it, but in order to get a live preview of your footage or picture and easier access to controls, you need the app. No problem, I thought, I have a Samsung Galaxy Note 4. Not the newest phone, of course, but I figured I already have a Gear VR that works with my Note 4 so this shouldn't be an issue to get whatever apps I need. Wrong. The official Gear app wouldn't install on my phone from the Play Store. So, I consulted the internet, and found an altered version of the app that WOULD work for my phone. That app worked fine as far as controlling the Gear 360, no issues.

You also need to install Samsung's Gear 360 Action Director on your computer to view and edit the videos. Two thumbs down, the files are in .MP4 so I don't understand the need to download Samsung's program just to view the videos. It sucks that Samsung makes you jump through unnecessary hoops.

So moving past that, what else is wrong with the camera? My main issue was that the videos wouldn't save properly. I primarily used the Single Lens mode, as I bought this hoping for it to be a bargain GoPro replacement, it turned out to be too good to be true at around $80. No matter what I tried, the videos would load as literally 2 frames per second. It looked like someone took a picture or two a second and called it a video. The audio loaded normally. The videos would look fine as previews on my phone, but as soon as I tried to look at them on my computer, 2 frames a second per video at the most. My computer isn't high end up but can load other videos without a problem. I recorded the videos (in varying resolutions and using both the front and rear camera) on a brand new Samsung Evo Plus Micro SD card so the SD card isn't the issue. I recorded 1080p 60fps video from my phone to the SD card just to be sure and the playback was smooth, no choppiness and the 60fps recorded as advertised.

I'm tired of dealing with this faulty device so I sent it back for a refund within a day of having it. It's a shame because this piece of equipment looks relatively high quality, just delivered unusable footage in my case. 2 frames a second is not acceptable.

title: DO NOT BUY THIS CAMERA! NOT EVEN CLOSE TO BEING READY FOR THE BIG SHOW. Probably the biggest mistake of Nikons corporate life.
text:
I am so ticked off right now about this camera, what a colossal waste of time! I bought this at best buy on pre=order. Thank God I can take it back within 14 days for full refund. I have owned two previous 360 cameras. One generic Panovision for $160, the Samsung Gear 360 and now this basically useless camera. To start, the App is PAAATHEEETIIIC! That's if you can even get it to work! I can get it to work on my old LG G3, but not on my S7edge? It will hook up through Bluetooth, but not WIFI (which is required to see the camera view on your LCD screen). For me the biggest turn off is that you can only see the view when you are NOT recording. Who's the moron that thought that was something to leave out? I want to to be able to see the view during the shoot in case things change (like people or the environment) w/o turning off recording. That is a serious problem and a deal breaker for me. You have very limited ability to make adjustments to the camera as well, you cannot adjust many shooting parameters. I will say, it is very well built and feels sturdy in the hand which I initially was quite excited about initially when I first opened it up. But, it was all down hill after that. The image quality in a mildly lit room was HORRIBLE. The big problem here is that Nikon placed $500 price tag on an action camera to compete with Samsung Gear 360 which has been on the market for at lease a year so it's not like they didn't have time to make it a significantly better camera with all around better specs and make sure the APP works consistently, but they didn't. I also spent a good hour on the phone with customer service to see if they could help me get it to connect to my Galaxy S7, but even though she was nice and did try, we couldn't get it to work. She did let me know that she had not been given any training on this camera before yesterday (release day). That tells me that they didn't bother to test this thing on most popular phones in the world to make sure it would connect! If you cannot get it to connect to your phone, then even if you just want to go and press the record button and wing it, you cant really know if you have the thing set to the correct parameters for the environment. This was my first Nikon purchase ever, I have use mostly Sony, some Cannon in the past so I was hoping for Nikon to impress me, but they have made me realize that they don't take the technology part of this business seriously. This is a camera that relies completely on your phone/tablet to work properly and Nikon basically just ignored that entire part of the equation. Shame on you Nikon, you have embarrassed yourself. Hope you have room in your warehouses for all the returns of these overpriced and under tested cameras. The one star is for good build quality and uneducated, but friendly customer service.

Update on other people's reviews. I keep seeing people compare this b to the Ricoh Theta s and occasionally say it's better, I don't have a Rico, but I do have the Samsung Gear 360. The NKM 360 is not even playing in the same balm park as the Gear 360. Samsung camera is a quality piece of equipment, MKM 360 is joke played on consumers who don't know how good 360 video can look.

title: Garmin failed.
text:
(Owned for two months) I purchased my Garmin Forerunner 235 at REI. I returned the first watch I bought due to syncing issues and overall software issues, freezing and not keeping the time and so on. The second watch that I received did the same thing but in my view worse then the first watch. I tried to contact Garmin with the first watch multiple times and the second watch with a very unfriendly defensive Tech Team.
Let me say I really did like the watch and wanted it to work that's why I returned the first one and gave them a try again figuring it was a firmware issue but the problems continued occurring I gave it two or three passes when it was having problems and finally I came to the conclusion why in the world would I have to deal with this after paying $330.
Once Garmin figures out the software issues and can have a fitness tracking watch that actually will keep a correct time and not freeze once a day they might be able to have a place in this crowded Market but for now there is way too many Fitness bands and smart watches out there to deal with a non-working product from Garmin.
My second Garmin watch was returned yesterday and I purchased the Samsung Gear S2.
I thought I did extensive research before purchasing the 235 but it might be one of them things you have to experience for yourself, just make sure if you purchase it you get it through a company with a good return policy.
My girlfriend has a Garmin that I bought her at the same time and she is having firmware issues as well so I truly believe it is Garmin.

title: Won't buy another Samsung device EVER!
text:
Bought this to work with my Samsung note9 & galaxy watch. When I used third party earphones I had issues with them not connecting or remaining connected - especially with the phone. I had hoped that by getting Samsung earbuds this would fix the problem. Unfortunately, the quality of the products in this 'environment' can not be overcome.

Customer service is deplorable. After talking to several different agents, I know first hand that the quality is all over the board. There's 'Patrick' who told me he had to transfer me to a 'Super Agent' to get the issue resolved. Repeated 'disconnections' when I asked to speak to a supervisor.

Returned the buds! Will NEVER buy another Samsung product.

Unfortunately, I've been extremely disappointed with the quality of Samsung. Both the watch and the phone had severe quality issues. Samsun

title: Lack of apps, lack of compatibility, going backward in design aesthetics, usability - RETURNED TO AT&T
text:
Samsung really screwed up by not using Android Wear instead of Tizen.

1 - Bought the 3G/4G enabled version from AT&T.
2 - Got it home, connected to Huawei Honor X2 (Mediapad 2) since other Huawei devices are supported per Samsung's site
3 - Opened Samsung Gear App store on phone and...nothing. Literally no apps were listed.
4 - Contacted Samsung - They said 'uninstall app store and reinstall and if that doesn't work it's compatibility'. Great, can't install apps.
5 - Used nephew's Edge to connect and review apps. HORRIBLY LACKING SELECTION; no Pandora, Nine, etc. So, doesn't really matter that my selection is literaly ZERO on my Huawei; because the apps that I need aren't available for anyone.
6 - Even if I were to install, I think the watch would have made me reset in order to reconnect to the Huawei (had to do a light reset in order to even connect to a SAMSUNG PHONE. My God.
7 - Overall, the screen is small - the Gear S was a much better design and easier to use. The device looks like a clunky and cheap Chinese knockoff you can get for $40. Actually, the cheap knockoffs look better than this plain jane watch. Really.

My primary need for the watch was to be able to have a backup standalone way to make phone calls; right, the only serious contender are the Samsung watches; the Neptune Pine is ridiculously huge and the LG Watch Urbane 2nd edition was recalled and canceled by LG. Hmmm.

This was a really disappointing foray into smartwatches. No independent connectivity = fail, Tizen = fail, primarily beacuse YOU HAVE NO ACCESS TO ANDROID APPS. Who the heck is developing for Tizen? All I can say is "Tizen = LOL" Right.

Returned.

title: Works as expected and less expensive than most of the competition
text: My wife wore a Fitbit for about a year. The bands would only last a few months and then we had to struggle to get the band release to work to install a replacement. The plastic finally broke to the point that a new band couldn't be installed. About the same time the screen stopped working. The whole thing gave the impression that it was a throwaway device. She then moved to a Samsung Watch which synced to her Samsung phone. I was interested in something that had a combination of the features of the two but at a reasonable cost. This watch fits that niche perfectly. It's constructed much better than the Fitbit having a metal case with easily swappable standard bands. The battery lasts a whole week where my wife needs to charge her Samsung watch every other day. The Runmefit app that pairs with it has most of the features that similar apps have and syncs easily with my Samsung phone. I work in a hospital and was surprised to see how closely the blood oxygen and blood pressure indicated by the watch matches those taken by dedicated medical equipment. I'm pretty tough on watches but this is holding up well. There are still no scratches on the screen and the band is as good as new. I may replace the included rubber band with a cloth or leather strap but that is easy to do as it's a standard 22mm band.

title: 2nd Generation as bad as 1st
text:
I didn't purchase the 2nd Generation from Amazon but because the 1st generation band I purchased died, Pivotal sent me a second generation band (twice). I have reviewed the 1st generation band and have included that review below. Same review for both bands. After four bands, I'm done with these tracker until Pivotal figures out how to manufacture a good product.

__________________________
I am done with Pivotal until they get their manufacturing figured out. Although the company was fairly responsive (as long as you use the power of Twitter) the product is a piece of junk.

I went through 4 bands in less than a year. Each band would last about a month and then randomly fail. Each time I would then have to send it back to Pivotal so they could evaluate it and send me a replacement. It cost me about $10 each time I sent the band back and would take about 4 weeks to get a replacement.

When it worked, the band was fairly cool. It doesn't do all the things that the much more expensive Fitbit, Samsung Gear, etc. do but it does what most people need (tracks steps and distance). The app is finally getting to the usable point and has some neat features.

This is a nice idea and way cheaper than the competitors. Pivotal really needs to lock down their manufacturing and start delivering a product that works.

title: Buy something else
text:
I bought this watch as an upgrade from my Amazfit Bip S (actually two units due to what I thought was a defect). I had high hopes but was very disappointed. While I regularly got 3 weeks on a single charge from my Bip S, I was only able to get 2.5 days on this device with the same usage and without the always on display. I realize the GTS 2 has different hardware that causes more battery drain, but I was hoping to at least get 5 days as most of the video reviews said was standard. Although I did feel the screen was an improvement, this watch offered little additional functions to justify paying three times what the Bip S costs. The Bluetooth call receiving did work but I prefer not to speak on a speaker phone letting everyone around hear my conversation. Note that this watch can only receive calls not dial, thus it was not particularly useful for me. Almost any watch, including the Bip S can pick up a call to be taken over a headset or handset. Replying to messages would be more useful but this watch can't do that. With the use of a 3d party phone app, the Bip S can at least send quick replies. Even that is not possible with this device because of its more "complicated" firmware.

The watch I bought was advertised with Alexa built in but clearly did not have that and customer support said it was still a work in progress--six months after launch. This was not in and of itself a deal breaker for me but the false advertising did not improve my impression of this watch. What was a deal breaker was not being able to load music onto the device. That feature is definitely present on the watch but my transfers were frequently interrupted and I ended up spending an hour and a half to load just five songs. Without onboard music storage, this watch is way overpriced once you consider the GTS 2 mini is almost half the price has everything but the onboard music storage. This watch is also still lacking remote camera control which is mind boggling considering there are $30 watches available on Amazon with that feature. At this price point and with a battery that really only lasts 2-3 days, this watch is substandard. For the same price or just a little more you can get a Fitbit Versa (2 or 3), Samsung Galaxy Active, Samsung Watch (refurbished), Ticwatch (various models), and multiple other WearOS devices. All of these other devices have more features and functionality than this GTS 2 and are a better way to spend $150-$200. If you don't want a full featured smartwatch and really only need to receive notifications, then go with the Bip S, Bip U Pro, or GTS 2 mini. They are much more affordable and have better battery life. As for me, I'll be switching to a Fitbit.

title: No customer support for Samsung smart watch
text: My S8 Smart Watch was great until the battery died. There was no way to get it replaced. Because we ordered online from Amazon, there were no local certified repair services available. When the Samsung support center was contacted, they required the serial number of the watch in order to proceed with any repair, but because the watch was completely dead, the watch could not be connected to the phone and the serial number could not be located. I threw the watch in the trash.
full_text: No customer support for Samsung smart watch. My S8 Smart Watch was great until the battery died. There was no way to get it replaced. Because we ordered online from Amazon, there were no local certified repair services available. title: Poor battery life and terrible interface
text:
I bought this as part of a pre-order Samsung credit for their S22 phone. As I had to return the phone due to quality issues, I had to decide if I wanted to keep the watch and pay full price or return it. I ended up returning the watch as I did not want to further encourage Samsung's bait and switch tactics they used with the S22. The tactic was promising you the "best trade in value" for a pre-order and then promptly increased the trade in value by $250 and refused to honor the difference!

But, on to the watch. I am coming from an Amazfit GTR 2 which is a terrific watch for basic functions. It doesn't take calls or have full control of our phone or have apps that you can download. But, it has sleep tracking, blood oxygen tracking, etc. AND a multi-day battery for half the price of the Galaxy Watch 4.

After using both, I actually found the Amazfit to do a better job at sleep tracking. That surprised me as I though the Galaxy watch would have had better sensors, but that was not the case. Eventually, the poor battery life was driving me crazy as well. With the always-on setting disabled, I managed to get 2.5 days out of the watch. With it on, I got less than a day. In contrast, the Amazfit got my 7 days with AOD on and 12 days with it off.

Charging the Galaxy watch was also annoying as it regularly took over 90 minutes to charge. The Amazfit? 30 minutes. How is it possible that the Galaxy watch needed so much more time when it has a similar sized battery??

What this watch does well is integrate with the Galaxy Wear app on your phone which is far better than the Amazfit app. It also is better at controlling some aspects of your phone including notifications and music playback. So if that is what you are looking for, this is the better choice.

Overall, I'd recommend the Amazfit GTR 2 or new 3 over this.

title: Not for US Samsung phones; does not work with Samsung Pay
text:
So, here's the thing: this is technically my second Samsung Galaxy Watch. I first bought an open-box at Best Buy for about $309 ($338 with tax) before returning that after seeing this sold for $243 ($266 with tax). I thought I was getting $72 back to myself by returning the Best Buy bought version and purchasing this one.

Ended up being a bit of a mistake.

You see, even though this watch sold here is still officially by Samsung, it's not compatible with all Samsung phones worldwide. If you take a look at the images attached, the back cover is written in Spanish versus English, implying that this product was not originally meant to be sold within the States. The problem here is that some apps that are available where I live may not be available in where this specific make is meant to be sold in. Because of this, apps like Samsung Pay would not work on the smartwatch despite my phone having the app installed. And that's just one app. I didn't bother attempting to utilize the watch when one of the apps I required most was not compatible with the watch. I believe it has much to do with the system of the device that limits its capabilities, but that's just a guess.

Unfortunately, I'll be returning this watch despite the better price. Although less expensive, I suppose I don't mind paying to have full ability to access everything I want than being given error messages on apps not compatible with the watch. For those residing in the US, I recommend just purchasing this in a store based in the country unless you don't mind apps like Samsung Pay not working on the watch. If you live outside the US, then maybe this might work better for you and your phone than it did with me.

Side note: All apps on the open-box from Best Buy worked with no single issue. I also have the latest Samsung Galaxy model as of today (Galaxy S10), so it's very unlikely my phone was the issue.

title: .... where do i start?? .. oh how about.. DONT WASTE YOUR MONEY LIKE I DID!
text:
ok.. im going to start with stating i have spent 3 weeks with Garmin support and had the camera replaced once.. final determination by Garmin is that its normal operation. if i did not have $300 in accessories invested.. i would demand a refund.

1: the camera goes into thermal shutdown (overheats) WAY to fast.. recording times are as followed
5.7K 360 - 30min (to be fair. this is advertised to overheat in 5.7k mode)
4K 360 with GPS, Bluetooth, and WiFi on - 50min-70min
4k 360 timelapse with all sensors off - 3 hrs
1080p one lense timelapse - 3.5 hrs
1080p one lense with sensors on - 2 hrs

2: i have the vehicle constant power mount. this mount DRAINS THE BATTERY!!
if you take the battery out; the mount supplies plenty of power to the camera.. with a battery in; the battery will drain like normal and shut the camera down after 1-2 hrs.
3: the software cannot stitch 360 pictures... WTH?
after 6 months of dealing with Garmin engineering what they came up with is that the camera is operating as designed..
so it is designed to be used:
outdoors, out of direct sunlight (but its low light performance sucks.. so you just need to create a shade just over the camera itself), in a strong wind to keep it cooled off, for a mazimum of 30-60 min with a 2 hr cooling off period between clips.

its an $800 waterproof paperweight!

an $800 camera that is WAY below even the $150 Samsung Gear 360 in quality.

save your money and buy ANY other 360 camera. or at this price point.. buy 3 or 4 of the other cameras and still save money.

title: Garbage - don't buy a Samsung Galaxy Fit2!!
text:
Exactly one month after purchasing my Samsung Fit2, it has completely stopped working. It will not turn on and will not sync with my phone. Troubleshooting with Samsung Support USA was a disaster because they kept confusing my device with the similarly named Samsung Gear Fit2, even after I provided both the model number and my device's serial number. They asked me to press buttons or try functions that this device does not have. There is no way for me to reboot this device - not even one of those little "press the button with a pen" on the back.

Ultimately Samsung SUpport told me that I must return the product so they can diagnose it, but I will lose all my data when they start diagnosing it. I would have had to pay for shipping. Also, only *after* sending it in for repair will they decide if I should get a new device, a refund, or if they can repair & send back my fitness band. They could not give me a timeline for how long this would take.

I cannot stress enough how terrible Samsung Support USA is - they gave me one heck of a terrible customer service experience.

I am sending this back for a refund through Amazon instead. I am so unhappy with how quickly this device broke and how there was literally nothing I could do to troubleshoot and fix it at home. Shame on Samsung for selling such a garbage product. All I wanted was a basic fitness band to help me with phone notifications, help me track my sleep, and help me track my workouts. All I got was a frustrating headache.

title: PLEASE READ BEFORE BUYING!!!!
text:
NOT THE WATCH FOR FITNESS TRACKING..... I've wanted this watch for awhile now and I'm so sad it was a disappointment.

The watch itself is beautiful and has awesome features.. I wanted an (upgrade) from my Samsung gear... I run daily so I know how many calories, on average,I burn in a certain time span... This watch SEVERELY misses the mark when it comes to calories burned... when I run for 20 mins I burn an average of 400 cals (approx) 100 cals every 5 mins... after a 20 mins run with this watch... it only counts 177cals burned which is ridiculous for 20 mins w/ heart rate of 150...

I decided to keep the watch because I like how it looks and the other features like being able to take calls, the full texting capability and the vibrant screen etc.. I have not tried it in water and don't plan on it... its just upsetting that its not up to par when it comes to the fitness aspect..

If your wanting a watch for fitness ... this is not it AT ALL..
Save your money!!!

title: Would not recommend as workout tracking watch.
text:
I tried it for about 3 weeks and had to return it. As a health watch it did not live up to the hype at all. While I did love the look/size, customizable features, Bixby routines, and integration with samsung phone, it was not what I was looking for to replace my Fitbit Blaze. After a couple of days I realized that the step tracking and heart rate was WAY off. I went into the Samsung Health app and updated height, etc and decided to try a couple more days. That did not fix the issue so I got in contact with the Samsung customer service where they essentially told me I could try rebooting it completely but it would likely not fix the problem. I rebooted the watch and sure enough the problem remained. I decided to wear alongside my Fitbit for a couple days and the difference was huge. On the Samsung watch my HR was 77 on the Fitbit (and manually taking) HR was 54 and stepwise Samsung read 3000s for the day while Fitbit read 8000s. If I tried taking my HR on the Samsung health app in phone, it was accurate and would sync to accurate HR on the watch, but who wants to do that daily when the watch is marketed to do it automatically?!

If I was just looking for a smart watch to integrate text messages, music player, weather, pay, etc. this would be a great watch. Simply not what I was looking for to replace my current watch, I might look into the new Fitbit Versa as their health monitoring programs seem much more accurate.

title: Samsung Galaxy Watch Active is a NOT SO great health monitor
text:
I particularly like the features monitoring my heart rate, and automatically registering my hiking and cycling activities. The only problem is that with recent update it has stopped monitoring my sleep habits. Don't know how accurate this was, but it was nice to view the relative times of REM, light and deep sleep.

This device has proven very durable. I wear it at all times, except when taking a shower, or swimming. The battery lasts about two days, before it needs to be recharged. Certainly not as long as a Garmin device I had, with a battery life of 5-6 days.

****I have now had this for just over 5 months, and several of the functionalities have ceased to work.**** Perhaps it is pilot error, but so far I have not found the corrections to the following: Quickly twisting my wrist no longer turns on the watch face. There no longer is an alarm when sitting over an hour, or when the battery runs low. The sleep monitor function does not work with Samsung Health. Sleep times have to be manually entered if I care to track hours of sleep. What does work is exercise and step tracker, and hear rate monitor. For about $200, would expect this device to work longer than 5 months. Very disappointing.

title: All show, but no go.  Great screen, poor fitness tracker
text:
My never ending search for the perfect Smartwatch / Fitness tracking hybrid continues. So far I have tried in order..LG G Watch R, Samsung Gear 2, Asus Zenwatch, Fitbit Surge, Sony Smartwatch 3, Sony Smartband Talk, Striiv Fusion and now the Samsung Gear Fit.
First the good. The screen is so nice. Brilliant color, clear and crisp. All notifications work, including Google Hangouts, Emails, etc. Only issue is that is a pain at times to delete them all. It takes a few taps. Android Wear does it much better and easier. Battery life is ok for me, 2 -3 days. The price at $119 has come way down and now it is a very good value. The heart rate monitor when tested seemed fairly accurate.
The bad: This is a fitness band that can’t track daily calories burned. You can only track calories while exercising or walking. You burn a lot of calories sleeping or even breathing, but Gear Fit does not track them. So you can’t really tell how many calories you can eat to balance it against daily calorie burn. Also, natively they Gear Fit does not track gym workouts. Only Walking, Running, Cycling and Hiking. But they do have an integration with Endomondo which does a decent job of tracking my Crossfit workouts for calories burned. But Endomondo does not integrate with S Health where the calories burned through walking, running, etc or stored. So you really have to use 2 apps to tracking your active calories. And like I said you still will be missing your other non-active calories burned. Sleep tracking is manual and does not give you much info at all. Just when you are motionless or not. There also is no Smart Alarm to wake you when you are in a light sleep. You can use the cell phone alarm and the Gear Fit will pick it up though.
Overall: This will probably be returned too. Comfort is great. Notifications are great. Android Wear is better for notifications as you can respond to Google Hangouts texts via voice or auto text response on the watch. With the Gear Fit you can only use the canned response for true SMS texts. I use Hangouts so this doesn’t help me. The screen is the best of any fitness band or smartwatch I have tried. But the poor set up of fitness and sleep tracking to me is a show stopper on a Fitness band. Based on all the Smartwatches/Fitness Bands I have tried here is my current ranking from best to worst for my needs.
8. Sony Smartband Talk – Good for calorie tracking. Sleep tracking in automatic. The talking aspect on the band itself is nice. But notifications would not work consistently on it. They would come initially and then stop. No idea why. Sony Support did not know either. Also, the e-Ink display is impossible to read in low light and the text is very small.
7. Striiv – Ranked higher than the Sony Smart Talk Band because it is half the price. Notifications are ok, but the fitness tracking aspect is lousy.
6. Samsung Gear 2 – way too big to work out with. Tizen is cumbersome and not as easy to navigate as Android Wear. Camera and speaker on the phone for call is nice. Also, can’t respond to google hangouts texts with Tizen
5. FItbit Surge – Notifications are limited to SMS and Call Alerts. No email, no calendar, no google hangouts, etc. It is even cumbersome to navigate to them when you do get SMS texts. It is good for tracking gym workouts and heart rate. Auto Sleep tracking is nice, but the data doesn’t give you much info. Black and white screen is not as nice as other color ones or as clear as the e-Ink on the Smart talk Band. Great battery life. Never did use the GPS. Continuous heart rate monitor is nice, but it really underestimates it during the workouts.
4. Asus Zenwatch – Looks nice, but too nice to work out with seriously. Also, don’t like the awkward way it measures hear rate. See Sony Smartwatch 3 below for Android Wear Review.
3. LG G Watch R – Better battery life and heart rate monitor than the Asus Zenwatch. Cheap band. Also, too big and bulky to workout with.
2. Samsung Gear Fit – Beautiful screen! Very good on notifications. But the fitness tracking aspect is lousy. Can’t track daily calories in total. Link with Endomondo saves the device for being down to #5 on the list.
1. Sony Smartwatch 3 – true Android Wear Smartwatch. Can respond to Google Hangout texts via voice or canned text response in Android Wear. Best battery life on an Android Wear watch. Able to store music on the phone and with GPS you really can run without your phone. It is more of sportswatch, so good to workout with. Sony Lifelog is good for tracking daily calories. But you still need several less than efficient apps to track gym work outs. It would be good if Lifelog was better. Sleep tracking does not work in Lifelog with the Smartwatch 3. At first Sony told me it did and said I needed to update the firmware. When doing this it broke the watch and it would not reboot. Had to return it and get a new one. Then Sony told me that Life Log does not actually track sleep with the Smartwatch 3. The new one I ordred did not vibrate for some reason and returned it too. Decided to hold off on ordering a 3rd one. And now the price has come down..so this is definitely the Android Wear watch to get if you are going to go with that platform.
I would like to try the MS Band or maybe the Basis Peak, but neither are available now.

title: I really wanted to love this watch... But...
text:
Overall, this watch may be outstanding for most people, but it has one major issue that is a safety concern for me personally and so far the excuses I get is a sure sign of poor overall support and understanding of their watch and software. The concern: I have a dodge truck which has Uconnect and when I get text messages I can listen to them read by the Uconnect system, I don't have to take my eyes off the road. This worked just fine with a Samsung watch, and a Garmin Vivoactive 3 music watch with no issues at all. Once I loaded the Zepp software and synced my watch, no notifications go through to Uconnect. Supports answer to my issue was that it was the phone, not their software but when I delete the software it works fine. They are "highjacking notifications to only go to the watch, but other software for other watches doesn't appear to do that, so if they refuse to look into this at all, I'll send the watch back and go with another brand that I know will work.

The good: The display is beautiful and crisp. So far the battery is unbelievable. It looks good and seems well made. Overall it's a very good watch for the price and if it were not for the one issue that I personally can't live with, I'd give it 4.5 starts. Not 5 because they currently don't support apps that they don't integrate themselves.
The fitness tracking seems fairly accurate and there are plenty of activities included.

The not so good: Limited app support, and the issue above with notifications.

title: Worst generation samsung watch

title: Samsung refused to honor the phone's warranty
text:
I wanted to love the Samsung Gear Sport. It's a good smartwatch, but when it broke, Samsung won't fix it. They've been terrible about the warranty.

A couple of months after we bought the watch, a small window fell out of the back of the watch. I think it's the glass over the pulse sensor, and it's embedded in the watch back (or at least it was).

We sent the watch to Samsung for repair, but the fix only held for a few weeks before the window fell out again. We sent it to them for another attempt. This time they returned the watch unrepaired, saying it was out of warranty because we'd damaged it, somehow.

It's on the back of the watch. You know, the side next to your skin? So I looked. The watch is still brand new: there are no marks, no scratches, no dents, no bends, nothing. The Samsung rep insisted it must be broken since the repair shop listed it as 'damaged.' There was nothing they could do.

I insisted. They suggested I sent it to a 'Triage center,' although that seemed like a euphemism for the repair shop. I demurred and insisted. They transferred me to the second tier support, who also insisted that we'd damaged our new watch beyond any reasonable warranty. I suggested that any severe damage was either a pre-existing defect or the result of the first repair.

(Honestly, the watch is pristine. I haven't a clue what damage they believe we inflicted. There's a little glue left from Samsung's first repair attempt, but that's it. Moreover, a watch is a rugged package. Think for a second how difficult it might be to dent or bend the back of a watch.)

They wouldn't change or budge, insisting (sight unseen) that the watch was too damaged. I insisted that this was a simple warranty issue. I wasn't angry at them -- they're just reps following Samsung's script -- but this is an obvious warranty issue.

Eventually, they allowed me to ship the watch back to their repair shop. The ticket still said we may have damaged the watch, but they comped the repair. They also changed the problem to "Cradle will not hold phone," for some reason, but they insisted that I won't have to pay for the repair.

I probably should have held out longer. This will be Samsung's excuse later if there are any more issues. "Ah," they'll say, "nothing we can do. You voided your warranty." I know I'm buying myself more trouble.

Anyway, this was terrible service. Yes, the Samsung watch is a robust and well-integrated smartwatch. When it works, it's great. Just remember that it comes with an added risk: if it breaks, Samsung may deny the warranty.

title: Not great for fitness tracking
text: Overall, the watch is good for texts, emails, and other notifications from your phone, but pretty disappointing in terms of a fitness tracker. Calories burned aren't accurate, there's no feature for HIIT, it doesn't connect with WW, and has a habit of just turning off the workout feature in the middle of a workout. It's called a Samsung Active Watch, it should be better at tracking activity. Otherwise, it looks nice, it's comfortable, and the band kind of tucks into itself which is actually really helpful.

title: Okay but with faults
text:
After the frustration of several Fitbit products, I really really wanted to love this Samsung Gear Fit2. Unfortunately I don't for several reasons:
1) The battery only lasts about 2 days for me
2) The face fogs up during strenuous activity
3) The stair flight counter is completely useless
4) The step counter seems to be significantly less accurate than the Fitbit
5) It syncs with my phone but does not sync with my computer

Having said that, I do not find any problem with the device staying on my wrist. The strap is fine. The display is bright and easy to read, except in bright light. The retrieval of information by swiping right/left and up/down is innovative and effective.

title: Disappointed in Lack of Accuracy
text: I ended up having to order two of these but eventually sent both of them back due to a variety of problems. The first one wouldn't charge. I contacted the Samsung Gearfit Help line and they didn't want to try to help me figure out the problem at all. I eneded up just exchanging it. The second one charged but all the data was off by very large amounts. The device would say I had climbed 300 flights of stairs when I hadn't climbed a single step. When I knew I had run 4 miles, it recorded fewer than 3. New updates supposedly allowed the Samsung app (which is awful) to communicate with My Fitness Pal, but it double and triple recorded everything. Loading music never worked either. The only plus was the size of the device and the display face. In that regard, it beats the Fitbit and Garmin versions, but that doesn't matter if nothing it does is accurate. I guess if you simply wanted to wear it as a watch, that would be a selling point. Ultimately, I was highly disappointed.

title: Samsung lied about the blood pressure function of the Samsung Galaxy Watch Active
text:
I pre-ordered the Samsung Galaxy Watch Active because it was advertised to have blood pressure measuring. However, now it seems like Samsung has pulled the plug on that feature after the watch release.

That BP function was the only reason why I was willing to pay 2 hundred dollars for the Samsung Galaxy Watch Active!

I could have bought a much cheaper Garmin/Fitbit watch without that feature.

Samsung did a bait and switch here.

I will be sending it back!

title: Was skeptical about a inexpensive watch but they proved me wrong
text:
My first attempt to buy a smart watch was years ago as a samsung watch and there were just to many issues. It was bulky and batter life as maybe 2 days. So I went back to a real watch because that's all I really needed. Fast forward to 2022 and Black Friday. I picked up the Amazfit Bip 3 Pro for around 50.00.

My first impression was it was a good size. My wrists are small and it was in the size range I wanted. It is very light with the included band and a little heavier with my replacement SS band. Really in the range of any watch though.

My second thought was on the band. It was a rubberized substance which I really hate. I used it for about a week and decided it was not for me. I was worried but this uses a standard size and there are a lot of bands out there that will fit this on Amazon. I ended up getting a stainless steel replacement for 10.00. and just love it.

The Battery life is spot on their estimate. It claimed 14 days. I am past 14 days at 19% power. To me this is good. You can charge it every 2 weeks and not be worried you will run out towards the end.

The movement to turn on seems well done. It comes on when you move your wrist to view but really does not come on by just moving your wrist or arm. You will need to remember to turn this feature off at night. You can set times when it is active. I have mine set from 7AM to 11PM and if I need it after that just hit the button. If you don't it will come on when you don't want it to and its bright.

There are a minimal number of clock faces. It would be nice if there were more. I actually use the default and have one standard watch face if I want to look fancier.

You can get text and email notifications but they are not the best. You cannot reply to anything though.

You can answer calls but there is not mic or speaker so it really does you know good. Im not sure I want to look like Dick Tracey and talk into my arm.

I don't really use the measuring features as I really just wanted a watch. I have played with them and they seem to be right but I really have no way to tell that.

I bought this as a watch and the battery life and screen resolution make this a good choice.

Overall I bought this

title: Samsung Apps Only!
text: First off I am a big Samsung supporter but I often use Google apps and not Samsung apps.  That was my first problem with this watch.  While I loved the design and the screen display, the fact that I could only use Samsung apps was a huge let down.  The call forwarding feature was cool but that's where it ended.  So if you use any apps like Google Maps, Google Play Music, Hangouts or anything by someone else but Samsung, this product is not that great.  I instead returned mine and purchased a Samsung Gear Live, much happier with that purchase.

title: Useless "Fitness Tracker"
text:
Spo2 monitor doesn't even work throughout the day like a regular fitness tracker. You can't choose when to use it; only works when you sleep. Even $25 fitness trackers let you do that. You don't get to choose when to check it. What kind of $300 fitness tracker doesn't let you check your SPo2 on demand. I can see why they dropped the price $100 already. And it's supposed to be able to sync health data Google Fit via health connect. But it doesn't even do anything. Very disappointed. I bought this thinking this would be one of the best fitness trackers and is supposed to have way better life than my new smart watch.

Might as well switch back to my Samsung watch 5 with LTE. It syncs better and the battery lasts about the same. And I the GPS has an actual use on the Galaxy watch.

So much for the Google buyout.

title: Total Bust, Super Disappointment
text:
*****8-31-19 UPDATE TO ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW*****
We contacted Skagen customer service, and to their credit they were great. They said that the water intrusion is a known problem, and they promptly replaced the watch - and said to contact them again if the problem occurs again and they'll take care of it. The watch is still a 1 star item and not recommended, but Skagen was at least responsive.
****************************************************************
I bought a Samsung Gear S3 Frontier watch over two years ago, and I love it and have had zero problems with it. My wife wanted a smartwatch (not Apple as we are Android users) but didn't want something as big as the Samsung - so I got her this watch. I have two traditional Skagen watches I love, so I trusted the brand. The smartwatch is also smaller than some others, it looks good, and has a feminine style. I wasn't experienced with Google's Wear OS operating system, but assumed it would be good since it's a Google platform right? Wrong. I have been shocked by how bad it is from top to bottom.
Setting up the watch and pairing it with my wife's phone was easy - easy enough that she did it without my help, though I'm the techie and she doesn't typically have much patience for setting up devices. But what we noticed right off the bat was how slow and laggy the OS is. It consistently delays when you press the screen or side buttons, to the point of frustration and to the point that you sometimes wonder if it's broken or frozen. And several times it has frozen, with weird red lines across the screen and requiring a reboot to fix it. This is running Snapdragon's newest processor but you'd never know it, and the problem has to be Wear OS.
Not only is it slow, it also has absolutely terrible, completely unacceptable battery life. My two year old Samsung watch typically has about 60% battery charge left after a long day, and though I charge it every night it can last two days per charge. This one can't make it through a day at all, and that's doing everything you can to curb battery usage (having the screen auto-sleep when you're not using it, disabling features, setting the brightness very low, etc.). I wondered if we were doing something wrong so I searched the web and found that the terrible battery life is common to Wear OS in general and certainly common to this watch. I am amazed that Google hasn't optimized their OS better than this. So if you get this watch, plan to charge it several times per day to keep it going.
On top of the bad performance and battery, the watch is also poorly constructed. It's supposed to be waterproof, but after just 3 weeks of having it and wearing it in the pool a couple of times, my wife's watch had water get into the body and it died. We had never opened the watch, messed with it, or done anything with it outside of normal wear and usage to explain why or how the water got in. Now it's a paper weight.
What a shame. My wife loved the look of the watch and it has the right fitness and smart features (voice control, messaging, etc.). But between the software bugs, abysmal battery life, and structural failure, this Skagen was a total bust and super disappointment. Avoid it.

title: Great Watch, Average App
text:
I have a Samsung Gear S3 smartwatch but have been disappointed with it's performance as a fitness watch. The heart rate monitor always reads faster than my actual heart rate, distances don't seem spot on, and pace figures fluctuate wildly when I run. Got this ProSense 307 and it works more accurately. The heart rate readings are always accurate, the pace doesn't swing so much, and distance figures are precise. The battery life is also much better with the Epson. My Gear S3 needs a charge every 2 days at best, but the Epson can go twice that long easily. I also love the charging clip - it's smaller than the traditional cradle chargers used by Samsung and on previous Epson multisport watches and very portable. The Epson isn't quite as easy to navigate since it lacks a touch screen, but it's pretty easy to learn and overall the watch itself is great.

What keeps the watch from being a 5 star item is the Epson View software. Getting it up and running was a pain because the app kept giving me a cryptic warning whenever I ran it (something about going to settings and allowing automatic background updates, which wasn't even an option in my phone's settings). After much consternation and frustration, I finally figured out that what the app really wanted was for me to turn on my phone's gps during the app's setup process. Once I finally go it going, it syncs easily with the watch, but the app itself is a little schizophrenic. It doesn't quite have the polish and feng shui you'd expect from an Android app in 2018 (it feels like an app that's 8 or 10 years old). It's not terrible, but not as easy to navigate and find helpful info as it should be.

In the end, I like the watch a lot and it makes a great fitness tool. It also looks nice in a sporty, low key kind of way. It features great accuracy from the heart monitor and gps and is pretty easy to navigate once you get used to it. If it had a better app, I'd give it 5 stars. Until Epson reboots the app, however, the whole experience isn't all it could be. Great watch, average app.

title: Throwing In the Towel
text:
[[VIDEOID:7396f10bf219715a39a0e13c0a3236d7]] Have you ever heard of a company called Google? They're this tech BEHEMOTH. Their rival is this other company that goes by the name Apple. Apple came out with a smartwatch in 2015, and have made revisions to it every year since. Well, Google decided to work on it for 7 more years before launching THIS........ *crickets*........ this is it?

TL;DR: Google took the Apple Watch, made it a fragile circle, and injected it with some terrible software (some of which is literally paywalled) before tacking on a ludicrous price tag. In a word, PATHETIC. I wasted over 6 years of my life on Google Hardware and this was the final nail in the coffin for me. My next upgrades I'll be ditching my Pixels for Samsung or (more likely) Apple.

On the outside, you have a simply beautiful looking domed-glass "timepiece" in the shape of a circle. Perfect. Simple. Some might say simply perfect. Geometry....

Crafted with a mix of both premium and not-so-premium materials, when you are trying to hit a certain price point, you need to make some tradeoffs. Understandable, but the prioritization that Google did here is simply insane. Instead of going for a more durable glass (i.e. Sapphire) for the most exposed and fragile part of the watch, they picked good ol' soft'n'scratchy Gorilla Glass. Why? Because they blew the budget on utilizing a stainless steel chassis that is frankly at most barely an accent piece to the screen. Because of this, you can see how already scuffed the watch face is from a month of use in the winter. Winter! You know, that season where watches are covered up by sleeves...

The lugs are another interesting curveball. They went proprietary because it really is annoying dealing with spring clips. Google's solution? A slightly more complicated system that will only ever work on 41mm watches because of geometry. That's a big bet to take, considering that every other smartwatch on the market comes in two sizes. So now even if Google does want to make a larger version with the same lug design, they need to pay for engineering to scale it up, and then pay for new tooling to manufacturer the new lugs since they didn't come up with a universal design. This is basic engineering fundamentals, Google, what is going on?!

Also, I paid an extra $50 for mine for LTE. Why? Because I have Google Fi as a carrier and this is the only compatible smartwatch on their "network". I love the idea of not having to worry if I forgot my phone at home. But don't try to make any phone calls because the built in speaker is so weak I still can barely hear with the watch shoved up to my ear, and even then it's tin-y to the point of rattling. Also, the battery does not last a whole work day if you actually use your watch for things... so was the LTE worth it? Not really...

If you look past the roundedness and Android innards, this is pretty much an Apple Watch knockoff. Now, there's nothing wrong with copying success... but.... if I wanted a cheap imitation of an Apple Product, I'd type "Smartwatch" into Amazon and get the cheapest lookalike from a no-name Chinese brand (<$100), or buy an Amazfit GTS ($120 on Amazon), or heck even a Fitbit Versa 4 ($230 on Amazon) or Sense 2 ($300 on Amazon) versus shelling out for the Pixel Watch (LTE, $400 on Amazon). Yes, mockery is the sincerest form of flattery... but does Google really think it can stand out by blending in?

Since Google also had to justify it's acquisition of Fitbit in the same way Elon Musk is trying to justify having bought Twitter, some buffoon tried thought it would be a good idea to mate a Versa 3 with the original Moto 360 from 2014. Please stop trying to force-feed fake fitness down our throats. Watching my heart rate change by the second is a fun trick, but unless you're in the ICU it's completely useless. Proper health and nutrition can't be narrowed down to "did you take 250+ steps an hour?" And guess what, I don't need Fitbit to tell me I didn't sleep well! I already knew that from the second I woke up! But instead of using brain cells to come up with a novel use for wearable technology, Google decided to cram sensors in (rather than a larger battery) that they REALLY want to try to convince you are somehow necessary. It's probably because it's the software is behind a paywall and all they really want is your money.

But here's something that stung the most, and I never thought it would. There is a massive lack of any kind of Pixel Watch accessories. Sure, you have first party watch straps, some aftermarket ones, and two basic types of cases on Amazon. But, just look up Apple or Samsung watch accessories here on Amazon. You'll find charging stations you've never dreamed of, things integrated into furniture not just for the watch but for the whole family of alike products, and a whole SLEW of accessories you can't even imagine. Want to know what you can find for your Google products? A giant empty black hole. I know this technically isn't Google's fault, but they're not doing anything to help it especially with the whole proprietary wireless charging thing....

Back when WearOS was known as Android Wear, it had this cool little gesture trick. You could flick your wrist in different directions to move around the interface literally one-handed. That one feature was so inspired and useful and is completely gone. I do not think I will ever finish mourning its loss.

I don't even mind the massive bezels, or the slow and old (2018) processor (again, $400! How?!) that very much still DOES cause hiccups while navigating the WearOS interface and especially summoning the Google Assistant and will likely not age well. I don't mind that the battery lasts me a full day only if I don't use the watch at all for anything other than checking the time occasionally. What I do mind, however, is championing Google and Android for a very long 6+ years, being beat down by the smug Apple-bros, only to be handed some feces. Does Google really expect me to be happy about this?

Back in a few years ago (like 2014 to 2017-ish), Google was known as a hardware leader, a true innovator. Google Cardboard brought VR into the homes of thousands for a price that the masses could literally afford. Chromecast Audio made speakers so much more useful. Google Glass! A heads up display for your face that didn't make your head look like a padawan training helmet. The Google Home speaker completely outshone the Alexa counterpart and made the statement that Google is here to WIN the smart home game (until they gave up). They even made a Smoke + CO2 alarm that wasn't super annoying! These products were pioneers (ironically, Google killed them all). Now all Google does is churn out crud, and I really wish someone would help them #GetTheMessage. In comparison, this watch is a classless piece of junk hoping to scrape a few remaining dollars together to finish burying the casket that holds what is left of the innovative passion of Google. RIP.
