A slightly more complicated system that will only ever work on 41mm watches because of geometry. That's a big bet to take, considering that every other smartwatch on the market comes in two sizes. So now even if Google does want to make a larger version with the same lug design, they need to pay for engineering to scale it up, and then pay for new tooling to manufacturer the new lugs since they didn't come up with a universal design. This is basic engineering fundamentals, Google, what is going on?!

Also, I paid an extra $50 for mine for LTE. Why? Because I have Google Fi as a carrier and this is the only compatible smartwatch on their "network". I love the idea of not having to worry if I forgot my phone at home. But don't try to make any phone calls because the built in speaker is so weak I still can barely hear with the watch shoved up to my ear, and even then it's tin-y to the point of rattling. Also, the battery does not last a whole work day if you actually use your watch for things... so was the LTE worth it? Not really...

If you look past the roundedness and Android innards, this is pretty much an Apple Watch knockoff. Now, there's nothing wrong with copying success... but.... if I wanted a cheap imitation of an Apple Product, I'd type "Smartwatch" into Amazon and get the cheapest lookalike from a no-name Chinese brand (<$100), or buy an Amazfit GTS ($120 on Amazon), or heck even a Fitbit Versa 4 ($230 on Amazon) or Sense 2 ($300 on Amazon) versus shelling out for the Pixel Watch (LTE, $400 on Amazon). Yes, mockery is the sincerest form of flattery... but does Google really think it can stand out by blending in?

Since Google also had to justify it's acquisition of Fitbit in the same way Elon Musk is trying to justify having bought Twitter, some buffoon tried thought it would be a good idea to mate a Versa 3 with the original Moto 360 from 2014. Please stop trying to force-feed fake fitness down our throats. Watching my heart rate change by the second is a fun trick, but unless you're in the ICU it's completely useless. Proper health and nutrition can't be narrowed down to "did you take 250+ steps an hour?" And guess what, I don't need Fitbit to tell me I didn't sleep well! I already knew that from the second I woke up! But instead of using brain cells to come up with a novel use for wearable technology, Google decided to cram sensors in (rather than a larger battery) that they REALLY want to try to convince you are somehow necessary. It's probably because it's the software is behind a paywall and all they really want is your money.

But here's something that stung the most, and I never thought it would. There is a massive lack of any kind of Pixel Watch accessories. Sure, you have first party watch straps, some aftermarket ones, and two basic types of cases on Amazon. But, just look up Apple or Samsung watch accessories here on Amazon. You'll find charging stations you've never dreamed of, things integrated into furniture not just for the watch but for the whole family of alike products, and a whole SLEW of accessories you can't even imagine. Want to know what you can find for your Google products? A giant empty black hole. I know this technically isn't Google's fault, but they're not doing anything to help it especially with the whole proprietary wireless charging thing....

Back when WearOS was known as Android Wear, it had this cool little gesture trick. You could flick your wrist in different directions to move around the interface literally one-handed. That one feature was so inspired and useful and is completely gone. I do not think I will ever finish mourning its loss.

I don't even mind the massive bezels, or the slow and old (2018) processor (again, $400! How?!) that very much still DOES cause hiccups while navigating the WearOS interface and especially summoning the Google Assistant and will likely not age well. I don't mind that the battery lasts me a full day only if I don't use the watch at all for anything other than checking the time occasionally. What I do mind, however, is championing Google and Android for a very long 6+ years, being beat down by the smug Apple-bros, only to be handed some feces. Does Google really expect me to be happy about this?

Back in a few years ago (like 2014 to 2017-ish), Google was known as a hardware leader, a true innovator. Google Cardboard brought VR into the homes of thousands for a price that the masses could literally afford. Chromecast Audio made speakers so much more useful. Google Glass! A heads up display for your face that didn't make your head look like a padawan training helmet. The Google Home speaker completely outshone the Alexa counterpart and made the statement that Google is here to WIN the smart home game (until they gave up). They even made a Smoke + CO2 alarm that wasn't super annoying! These products were pioneers (ironically, Google killed them all). Now all Google does is churn out crud, and I really wish someone would help them #GetTheMessage. In comparison, this watch is a classless piece of junk hoping to scrape a few remaining dollars together to finish burying the casket that holds what is left of the innovative passion of Google. RIP.

title: These are the real deals but may not last
text:
**update: one day after I wrote this, the left earbud stopped working and none of the troubleshooting steps worked so I'm reducing from 5 to 4 stars**

For the price, I really don't think you can beat these. I've been using them for over 6 months, and I'm so pleased with the fit, sound quality, and battery life. I used
them for running, walking, around the house, in, around the house, and can even wear them to sleep. I love the charging case, and various bud sizes to get the right fit. It's been hard for me to find earbuds that I can use while running running, and these give me no problems. My only complaint is that they are very touch sensitive so it is easy to accidentally pause what you're listening to, but seems less easy to hit play to get it going again.. Very minor issue.. These pair well with everything from my phone, to my Bluetooth speaker any Samsung watch.

title: So much more than EarPods with the cables cut...
text:
I've seen a lot of bad professional reviews on the internet for these earbuds. I'd say about half of them mock the concept of AirPods - "they look like Q-tips in your ear! You'd lose them all the time!" I'd say 80% say that the sound quality is sub-par or "identical to EarPods" or "only slightly better than EarPods, not worth 5 times the price!" Just for fun I decided to try them for myself while I walked past an Apple Store - an employee kindly set me up in a quiet corner of the store so I could listen to them.

I don't know what the angry reviewers were smoking. None of their grievances are necessarily true, and that's coming from someone generally skeptical of Bluetooth headphones. If anything I did not expect these to be an upgrade in sound quality from my Bose MIE2i buds, but they were.

Firstly, the sound quality - miles above and beyond the EarPods GIVEN that they fit in your ear, not just "slightly better". You have to be deaf to not appreciate the difference. The upgrade over the EarPods in sound quality is night-and-day - I'm really confused about why so many reviewers are disappointed by the sound (my suspicion is that they didn't fit them snugly enough?). Everyone who's tried mine has noted the same thing - they genuinely sound good. On top of that - given how difficult it has been to even get a pair of these, I don't understand why internet minions who clearly have never even tried a pair are already giving them bad marks. It's almost a DISADVANTAGE to Apple that they made them look like EarPods - because while they look like EarPods, they sound nothing like EarPods. I find them much more pleasant to listen to than even my Bose MIE2i buds, which I've cherished for years. I did all my listening just using Apple Music's 256kbps AAC files. For those audiophiles out there, I would say that the sound profile is "scooped" - the midrange is underrepresented, but otherwise they sound really surprisingly nice.

The low ends are punchier and tight, the highs are crisp, and the soundstage produced is nice and wide and easy to get lost in. In comparison, the old wired EarPods had a subdued low-end and airier highs. No, I don't get the level of detail in the highs I do from my $400 over-the-ears, but for casual-to-intermediate listening these are more than sufficient. In jazz the hi-hats and cymbals are finely detailed and ring as they should, and you can appreciate the subtle "twang" of a double bass, which I'd say the EarPods certainly did not deliver. In classical the sound is detailed enough to detect the wispy scratching of a bow gliding gently over the strings of a violin. You can relaxedly tell all the instruments apart in an orchestral piece. Pop music does well with a satisfying, thumping bass that doesn't distort much at high volumes and clear vocals. On top of all that - these get noticeably louder than the EarPods - definitely ear-damagingly loud if you so desire.

Obviously all of this holds true only if they actually fit snugly in your ear - if they don't, they'll sound worse and they'll fall out. I was glad I tried them out in-store before deciding on them.

I've heard a lot of people say "they look like Q-tips in your ear! They look so silly!" Perhaps I'm missing something but if they look just like EarPods and those had a long windy cable, why were the EarPods not "silly"? I also don't understand why "but you'll lose them!" is a reason to make fun of the product. Isn't the future necessarily predictive of a world in which we are fully wireless? Do people not buy smartphones because they're afraid they'll lose them? Sure, at one point. We get used to our wireless products and we adapt to not losing them. Furthermore, many of the the comparisons people make to the AirPods don't even make sense - like the Bose bluetooth earbuds (which I actually think sound notably worse), which also aren't even fully wireless earbuds. There are so few, true AirPod alternatives on the market in there are truly no wires at all between earbuds; admittedly I have not tried them, but I haven't heard the best marks about those either. I considered of course the Earins (connectivity issues apparently?) and the Samsung Gear IconX (shorter battery life), but even those aren't the same category of buds because of their in-ear-canal design, whereas the AirPods resemble more classic earbuds. I personally detest the in-ear-canal feeling of squishing into my ears (I perceive the soundstage produced to be much narrower and literally inside my head), but I realize this is PURELY personal preference.

The AirPods have great battery life, and the charging is so fast I just stop listening to them to use the restroom, make a cup of coffee, come back, and they're ready to go again for hours. I have yet to exhaust their full 4-5 hour run in one sitting. The pairing was ridiculously easy and fun to do, and the transitioning between iCloud-linked devices is seamlessly albeit still laggy (fingers crossed for an update).

Try the AirPods before hating on them. It's an easy time to hate on Apple - the removal of headphone jacks from the iPhone, the death of MagSafe chargers, a world full of dongles, etc. Just know that if you join the hate bandwagon you'll be missing out on some genuinely good audio and handy wireless technology. These are not just EarPods with the cables cut.

title: Doesn't count Floors correctly (and the "90-day Amazon Renewed Guarantee" is useless)
text:
I recently returned this "Renewed" Samsung Galaxy Watch Active because it was defective; it would not count Floors correctly (or at all). So I immediately sent the watch to Samsung's repair facility. They said they ran their tests and could not find a problem and returned the watch to me. I tried it again and it still did not count Floors. So I then called the Samsung technical support office (855-795-0509) and talked to a technician who walked me through some diagnostic steps and finally told me I must have a "lemon" and should return the watch. Which I did. I was not given the option of a replacement, only a refund. So I took the refund and sent the watch back using the supplied return label. I was expecting a full refund per the "Amazon Renewed Guarantee Coverage" which states: "If you are unsatisfied with your purchase, return the product within 90 days of receipt of your original purchase (or within 1 year of receipt of a Renewed Premium iPhone) for a full refund or a replacement.". So I was expecting a full refund but instead $10.50 was deducted for shipping charges (to and from) and $18.34 deducted for a 20% restocking fee. This seems wrong and in conflict with the "Amazon Renewed Guarantee Coverage" I referenced above. So I'm unhappy about that.

On the other hand, except for the floors not counting correctly, the watch was pretty cool. It counted steps, it reminded me to get up and going when it detected I was sitting around too much and it kept pretty good track of my heart rate. If it had only counted Floors correctly (like my old, cheap fitbit) I would have kept it and be using it now.

title: Disappointed by Fitbit again.
text: It's ok, which is why it gets a 3 stars. Nothing makes it stand out from the other watches I've used. Battery life is better then my Samsung watch, but nothing else. This should probably be priced a lot lower to match it's real competition. Biggest disappointment is the ecg (main reason I decided to try this watch) won't ever give me a reading. Always says "ECG Results inconclusive". I do have heart palpitations, but at least give me the results with a disclaimer saying it can't diagnose anything based on the results it's getting (manual says you may get inconclusive if it detects anomalies...). Even the cheap china brand watch I had from 3 years ago gave me ecg/ekg readings. Hmm guess your supposed to read the manual before buying these things to find out your health issue that's related to the purpose of the function will make the function not function at all? Not looking for it to be my doctor just give me readings that my doctor and I can look over... But hey at least this one can be turned off to sit in the drawer unlike my other fitbit (which I also didn't like well enough to keep using - guess I'm a slow learner that I just don't like fitbits?). For battery life and basic fitness tracking my garmin is much better, and for extra "smart" features my samsung watch 3 is head and shoulders better, and I got them both for about the same price when the samsung was on sale, so not sure where this watch fits. Even for a bit more I'd recommend the samsung over this.

title: Excellent Looking Watch - With a Major Drawback
text:
The watch appearance is excellent: thin, stylish, and beautiful. Battery life is amazing - 5 days with AOD.
The Major Drawback is the deal breaker for me - almost NO Extra Watchfaces and only one extra watchface at a time.
Plus you need to get a Chinese Login for the Health App and a Beta Version to load the watchface.
Plus it was a technical challenge to make this happen. Huawei seems uninterested in providing the ability to get more watchfaces. Plus you cannot delete original watchfaces and memory only allows one new watchface at a time.
So I finally decided to return the watch and get a Samsung Gear S3 Classic -- a much better choice.

title: No GoogleAssistant No GPay No GoogleFit. Locked in with Samsung. Endless notifications 1 day battery
text:
If you're into the samsung bloatware experience then by all means go for it. There are absolutely no pros about this watch but here are the cons:

1) battery won't last a day
2) Does not work with Wear OS, it will get detected and the progress is stuck. Watch is connected with limited usability
3) requires the full bloatware from Samsung so be prepared to create samsung accounts and have all your vital stats at the mercy of a Korean giant.
4) First install the Samsung Wearable app and make sure you give it ALL the permissions else it will fail and you're get constant notifications even after you;'ve corrected the permissions. Then install the Samsung Watch 4 Plugin app and agains give it all permissions. Then you need to install the Samsung Health app and even that needs access to your phone.
5) After installing 3 Samsung apps and 2 samsung accounts, you will now experience degraded battery life on your phone
6) Samsung Health does not sync with Google Fit. So forget about trying to get that to work without installing another 3rd party app.
7) No Google Assistant on the watch
8) No Google Pay on the watch

So basically nothing Google works on this watch. So what's even the point and all the fuss about?

title: It's a good watch, not great. / Failed
text:
I was a previous Samsung Galaxy Watch user, and before that i had a Moto 360.

The first thing i notice when i got the watch is that the feeling wasn't that premium like my previous smartwatches, mainly because this one have a plastic body and those had a Stainless steel construction.

I have several watchbands to switch from my previous ones, but i feel like i cant be switching them that much, because the pin holes are plastic and the border can be broken and that's the end of the watch.

The addition is the second secreen it's a great feature because you can save a lot of battery and still be able to look the time all the time instead of an empty black screen, or the need to use an Always On display face. The only downside that I've found with it, is that it adds a silver like layer on top of the amoled screen, so blacks don't look deep black like they have to, but more like satin black. Still, that's nitpicking for a downside.

Battery life is good, not great. I'm use to a 3 days battery life with my previous Galaxy Watch, and this one have around the same duration. This was the first reason why I don't upgrade to a Galaxy Watch 4 Classic; that and because it's smaller and I like big watches.

WearOS is great, you have a lot of apps and watchfaces, and I was able to use my previous ones with the same facer and watchmaker apps that I was using on the GW with Tizen instead of Wear OS.

Call quality is good, and the ability to record voice notes on it is great. Volume is adequate and the quality of the mic is good. You can hear and be heard without a problem.

Notifications are customisable and your able to answer messages from the watch via predefined texts, writing your own, or using google voice typing wich is the best, since the keyboard on it, it's pretty small and I have big hands.

Another downside is that it uses a magnetic charging cable instead or QI charging, so no wireless charging on the go with my phone. But the cable is good and easy to find 3rd party cables that fits.

So far so good. I installed a stainless steel band and it looks more casual that sporty,

Besides that, i think is a nice watch with a cool design, and I'm waiting for the Wear OS 3 update once Samsung's embargo finish.

Can't tell that much about the sensors, because I don't use them a lot, but on my testings they were accurate, also the GPS.

Updatev 07/08/2022

Only downside so far its that it doesn't show WhatApp calls on the watch screen. Seem like call support it's only available to regular operator calls and not apps calls like Meet, Teams or whatsapp calls.

Also that we're half way 2022, and we're still waiting for the Wear OS 3.0 update.

Update : April 7.

Since last week, the essential mode it's not working anymore, so i had to activate the ambient mode to have a constant hour face.

2023 and Still, no Wear Os 3...

Just like MKBHD says: NEVER BUY A PRODUCT BASED ON A PROMISE OF A FUTURE FEATURE.

Mi 1st and last TicWatch. Going back to Samsung... At least the last one died after almost 3 years of use. This one doesn't have a year and it's already failing. Plus, the charging cable it's a little finicky. It connects and sometimes won't charge.

title: Don't Bother
text:
Don't bother with this band. I saw it and thought "Oh wow, a Samsung watch I can afford!" I spent a four maybe five hours trying to get it to connect to my devices, one of them a Samsung, and it just would not work. If you have Android 10 on your phone, forget it. This isn't even compatible with Android 10 yet.

The one device it would connect to kept losing the connection and the band resets itself when you try to reconnect... Then it never reconnects. Just save yourself the frustration and your money... Pass this one by.

title: I love my Samsung gear
text:

Now the battery is completely dead, won't charge, and now I'll have to buy another tracker. Definitely wont be buying this one again.

I love my Samsung gear. It fits great...although it
comes off at times on its own. I think is very accurate when counting steps and stairs. I gAve it 4 stars because it dies rather quickly....If I have it at 100% battery when I first start my day, by 4 or 5 hours in is at 60-65%. Also, it freezes up a lot when the battery is at 30-40% which is very frustrating.

title: It's a reliable sports GPS watch but so far behind on smart watch features
text:
The similarities between Suunto and Nokia are uncanny but Suunto could suffer the same fate.

I had a Fenix 3 (horrible Smart watch), VivoActive 3 (unreliable Bluetooth connection) and an Ambit3 Sapphire. I also have the Samsung Gear Sport as a day watch.

Suunto is trying to catch up but it is soooo far behind:
1. You only get SMS and call notifications but that is soooo 2016.
2. Finally, vibration alert. But it is so weak.
3. Colored screen is just right though. Not too vibrant like Samsung that the battery barely lasts day.

I had a Fenix 3 (horrible Smart watch), VivoActive 3 (unreliable Bluetooth connection) and an Ambit3 Sapphire. I also have the Samsung Gear Sport as a day watch.

Suunto is trying to catch up but it is soooo far behind:
1. You only get SMS and call notifications but that is soooo 2016.
2. Finally, vibration alert. But it is so weak.
3. Colored screen is just right though. Not too vibrant like Samsung that the battery barely lasts day.
4. Always on display.

title: Worst battery life out of all smart watches!!!! Don't waste your time and money!! X
text: I am very unhappy with this watch and it's because of the battery life. I've had it for two days now. In 8 hours the battery was dead. No matter what settings I set it on I can't get more then 8 hours or less. I had the galaxy watch and switched to ticwatch pro cause of Google. I am happy with the watch other than battery life. It is an awesome watch and ties in perfectly with my pixel 2 xl and the galaxy doesn't tie in so well. I'm going to have to return it and get a refund and go back to the galaxy watch. I really wish I could at least get 24 hours out of it but the only way to get that is to not receive any notifications and have EVERYTHING off essential mode. When the watch face is on I can watch the battery drop. It's a time peice and I should still be able to get at least a day even if it was in always on mode but I can't even get it when it's off. Galaxy watch gets two days with always on mode. With the ticwatch after a few hours I have to toggle essential mode on and off if I want it to last me just one day. I'm very unhappy with this I bought it for two reasons one and main reason wear os google and it was bosted about amazing battery life and I've come to find out its got worse battery life then any other watch I've set my hands on. You have to keep it on essential mode to get a day or more. But what's the point of having a smart watch if all you can do is tell the time and if you wanna use it you have to turn. It on and off every time? I am actually shocked at how bad this battery life is and I'm absolutely returning it for a refund so I can go back to the galaxy watch and that really sucks cause I really like wear os and having google. Voice assistant on the galaxy watch is useless 100% and because I don't have a Samsung phone I loose a lot of the capabilities. Using the pixel with the ticwatch pro is amazing and I'm really upset I have to get rid of it but it has to go. Sorry but 8 hours is a joke even with always on off. I don't even understand how it drains the battery so fast. When the second display the "battery saving" display is on the battery still drains fast. Two hours of not touching it and I lost 30% of battery. How??? Great watch but because of the battery this watch is useless to anyone that has a job and Doesn't sit around all day right next to the proprietary charger. Don't bother buying this watch it's a waste of money and time.

title: Disappointed, sub-$200 performance in a $1,200+ watch
text:
UPDATE: Reduced rating from three stars to two after having to send it back because it vibrates when vibrations are turned off and there is no way to back up the rather expansive configuration, even though the Connect app reads every part of the watch.

This watch does not have a touchscreen. I thought at this price point Garmin would have found a way to make the user interface easy to use without it, but it's clumsy and trying to remember all the combinations of buttons and long-press options is tiring. Spinning the bezel and tapping the screen, like the Samsung watches, would be a much better option. CS rep said this was done to make the watch more rugged, and I'm not a watchmaker so I don't know if I'm competent to argue that point, but it's still disappointing.

The price of bands for this watch are beyond ridiculous, $45 for a silicone band and over $200 for a replacement for the titanium band. They are easy to swap, but not particularly comfortable, especially the titanium band, which has sharp edges next to the skin; I guess they saw the necessity to chamfer the top side for looks but not the bottom side for comfort. It scrapes my wrist through the day and occasionally draws blood, whether loose, fitted, or snug.

And I think that's my biggest disappointment. I've used Garmin products for a VERY long time, and while this is the most expensive Garmin product I have purchased, it's also the most poorly thought-out and difficult to use, and I say that as someone who doesn't even use some of the advanced features like the golf courses and navigation maps; just the every day stuff like health monitoring and timers. UPDATE: My biggest disappointment just got bigger. The watch kept vibrating on every notification even though vibrations are turned off. CS rep said it was a hardware fault and the watch needs to be replaced, which they are doing, at the expense of a $450 refundable deposit and I have to pay to ship it to them, but the big let-down is...wait for it...the Connect app does not back up the watch configuration. It reads every part of the watch but can't back up the config? It took a bloody month of fiddling around to get it set up the way I wanted. Laziness, stupidity, or the arrogance to think a backup would never be needed, I don't know, but I find this inexcusable.

The main reason I bought this watch over something like a Samsung S3 is the battery life, which does seem good. With my current settings, which include SpO2 sensing while I sleep, I get about two weeks on a charge, compared to a day or two on common Android watches. But it could have been so much more with so little work, and to charge so much for such uncomfortable watch bands is both arrogant and foolish. Unfortunately, by the time I was ready to admit my disappointment, I was outside of the return window, and if I had the choice again knowing what I know today, I would buy something else.

title: Battery has malfunctioned after 6 months. I feel helpless.
text:
Summary: Do not buy the Venu 2 plus, the battery malfunctions.

I bought this watch from amazon on May 27th. For context, the watch has never fallen or been harmed. I keep a screen protector on it and it is in excellent new condition. Initially the watch worked like a dreas. I had purchased the samsung watch 4 and venu 2S and returned both to get this Venu 2 plus because of the phone call feature and great battery life. I could answer calls easily, it faithfully tracked everything I needed, played music, helped me improve my fitness and most of all it gave me 7 (seven) days of battery life easily. I felt so happy and pleased with my purchase, I gushed about the watch to every and anyone.

From September 1st the battery life began to steadily decrease. First it dropped to four days. Then to two days. Now on November 6th, it is at six hours. It took me so long to address the issue because my father is fighting colon, liver and prostate cancer so since June I have been focusing on him. Now on top of everything I have a crappy $450 paper weight on my wrist and I must find time to run around trying to contact Garmin (while I am in Barbados) in hopes of getting this mess sorted out. I am truly disappointed. So much so that I just regret ever choosing Garmin. I don't know if having it replaced would be better or if just getting my money back would be better. I am frankly too frazzled to process this right now. I don't have the energy to be angry, all I feel is helpless regret.

title: Good Watch, But Don't Buy (Tizen OS Is Dead)
text:
I really like this watch but returned it when I found out Tizon OS is no longer Samsung Watch OS. All watches starting with Samsung Watch 4 will have Wear OS. So although Samsung says they will support and upgrade OS for 3 years, App developers probably won't do same for apps and definitely no new apps.

For $50 more you can get the new Samsung Watch 4 instead of Samsung Active 3.

title: Completely unusable.
text:
I see some other reviewers here with varying issues and complaints, but all seem to have gotten further in the setup process than I did. Whether my issues are anecdotal, a result of my own incompetence, or just the sad end of a defective lot# I'll never know, because I'm taking this small piece of wearable blech back to the store TODAY!

I bought this watch after disatisfaction with my Samsung GearS3. It (the GearS3) had issues with playing music off-line and with SamsungPay consistency. Right out of the box, trouble started with this Garmin: went to set up my credit cards and none of them were supported! National banks, local banks... Visa, Discover, MasterCard... chipped cards, debit or credit; all told me they were unsupported.

Bluetooth connection seems okay. It connects and syncs with my Samsung Galaxy S8, and with my Beats headphones, though in the later case, I have no confirmation that the headphones work, because I've yet to download any music onto it.

Three music services are available; KKBOX, AWA, and iHeartRadio. Nope, no Spotify, no Pandora...nothing I currently use. KKBOX hit a dead end when I tried to set up a new account. It repeatedly sends me to an unloadable webpage. AWA might be usable if I understood the language it supports...Japanese...and I don't. iHeartRadio kind of works, in that it seems to be set up, and the watch and phone seem to go through the motions of syncing. But alas, at the end of the process of syncing any music or playlists, the message "Some files did not sync" invariably displays and I'm kicked out into an empty music library.

I cannot speak to the workout functions or GPS performance. I've not used them and I won't be wasting any more time on what may be, for others, perfectly delightful features. Unfortunately, the prior issues, of which I've spoken, make this product a tiny, glitchy, steaming fraction of what was advertised and vastly unsuited to my needs.

title: I got this from best buy and returned it in few hours
text:
I got this from best buy and returned it in few hours.This is not worth the price we pay for this. It's almost 285 including tax. The display is poor. The material feels cheap.The functions are limited. But the price is premium. The price might be for the overall looks, which is good. The display matches the color nokia phones in early 2000's. It gets disconnected frequently and tries to connect again with my note8.

I would have kept this if its priced around 150 or less. But not worth for 269. My advise would be if using samsung phone then spend few bucks more and get a samsung gear or if using apple then the apple watch price is almost same as this one. Though they are all in same price range, vivoactive 3 is not even comparable to the competition.

title: nice device / inadequate software
text:
It's my own fault for not doing a better job of reading the description, but I really do not understand the limitations of the Samsung Gearfit software. I made the admittedly bad assumption that since the software runs on android, I could lrun it on my brand new Samsung Galaxy Tab A tablet. I don't care at all about being able to screen incoming phone calls from my watch. I just want to be able to keep a log of my physical activity and calories burned and heart rate, etc. These are all things that could easily be done on a tablet, and I do not understand why Samsung has crippled the software so that it will only run on a smartphone.

Aside from that major disappointment, the Gearfit2 device has been great so far. I am only one week into ownership, so I am still learning to use all the functions but I'm happy with it so far.

So, to sum up - nice device / inadequate software.

title: Get a screen protector.  Otherwise, fantastic.  (Coming from Samsung Gear Watch)
text:
I'm coming from a Samsung Gear s3 Frontier watch that I have been using for 2.5 years. The experience on that watch was slowing getting worse. It was getting very laggy and the battery was starting to die quickly. It served me well, however. In my pursuit of a new watch, I almost didn't even look at Garmin. I was figuring I'd upgrade to a newer Samsung watch. I'm glad I took the time to research and try a Garmin.

I'm a techie and quasi-athlete. In other words, I like gadgets and cool tech, and I take exercise somewhat seriously. I'm not a hardcore athlete or daily runner by any means. I was originally looking heavily at the Garmin Forerunner 740. It had a lot of cool features and health tracking data for me to nerd over. One thing I didn't care for was the lack of a touch screen and some health and exercise features were probably overkill for my needs. Enter Venu 2.

Pros

The Venu 2, in my opinion, is the perfect balance between a modern smart watch (like a Samsung or an apple watch) and a health tracker. It has a beautiful, bright color touch screen, and an intuitive user interface. Coming from the Samsung gear there are two main things that I noticed. 1. ) The weight is significantly lighter on the Garmin. Noticeably. 2.) The battery life on the Garmin is amazing. My Samsung needed to be charged every night. That meant no sleep tracking for me. It's weird, but awesome to not have to worry about battery for days on end. It's a game changer in my opinion. How Garmin pulls that off on a lighter watch, I have no idea.

Garmin obviously is one of the best in the business when it comes to health tracking. I've found to the stats to be pretty accurate. I have noticed, that exercising on my elliptical, the calories burned is under reported on the watch, but that can be adjusted after the fact in the Garmin Connect app. Otherwise, health tracking is great.
Have respiration and oxygen saturation stats are cool features I didn't have before. I also like the body battery feature. On to the app. The Garmin Connect App is one of the nicest looking health tracking interfaces I've seen. Samsung health has gotten cluttered and sometimes it's hard to find historical data. The Garmin app interfaces is beautiful and motivating.

Cons
The Venu 2 is not without it's faults. I think it could have a better build quaility, particularly on the glass. My Samsung after 2.5 years didn't have a scratch on it. My Venu, scratched on day 1. I was not pleased. I have since learned that they do sell screen protectors for the Venu 2 which I would highly recommend. Also, on Amazon, Asurion insurance is only about 30 bucks which covers screen damage. Anyway, for $400, I feel like Garmin could use higher quality glass that is more resistant to scratching. They could also raise the outer bezel just a bit, to make that a more prominent contact point over the screen. This is where I dock the Venu 2 just 1 star. I do also wish that Garmin Connect allowed for more third party integration. My Withings scale for example. It would be nice if these companies would allow some back and forth with health data. But I get why they do it.

Would I buy it again? Absolutely. I love this watch so far. The weight, the battery life, the interface, the tracking, and the app are all fantastic. I recommend it with the one caveat of protecting it with a screen protector and possibly insurance.

title: Not impressed
text:
I decided to break from old Samsung watch and upgrade into this new Fitbit because I'm really more into the "fitness" functions of these smart watches lately.

I thought I was buying the premier Fitness watch. I was wrong.

I spend 45 minutes a day on the treadmill and 45 minutes a day on a rowing machine. This watch does not track rowing at all. It doesn't exist on their ap to even add it manually. On the Ap and on the watch itself, rowing does not exist at all. I have (had) a "fitness" watch that only can track half my activities. In other words......useless to me.

My THREE YEAR OLD Samsung can auto-detect rowing but the NEW Flagship Fitbit cannot and their forums say they have no plans to add it. I have no idea why

Unfortunately, this makes it completely worthless to me as a fitness watch. It is completely worthless to anybody who uses a rower that wants a fitness tracker watch.

I wanted to love it but I had to send it back

title: Terrible but has a good battery
text:
The only reasons this is even getting a 2 is the battery and water resistance.

The app is terrible and difficult to use. There are very few features regarding premade plans and routes. The trackers on there are terrible as a whole. It said I slept for 10.5 hours because I was at dinner for two hours and didn't move much whole I was there.

The steps are almost always wrong. I wore another tracker to compare the steps and the other (Samsung gear) was consistent on known distance runs. The GPS takes several minutes to acquire. But, it is pretty good as long as you have good reception (it said I did 1.5 miles on the Manitou Incline ascent. It's only .9)

The unit is much thicker than it look and I find that the heart rate monitor is only accurate if it a is tight. So it is either bump stuff with it or put a dent in your arm from the HR monitor.

The saving graces are the good battery life (if you turn off all the auto sync features) and that once it is on, it stays on. Unlike Fitbit where you have to take it off to shower.

title: Great watch, not sure the extra cost for the band is worth it.
text: 1 year review.  I will not take a lot time on the functions and features of this watch other than to say it has damn near everything I need, but is not the most intuitive so expect to spend some time with instructions learning how to get around menus and the like.  In comparison I have a Samsung Gear S3 Frontier.  While the Samsung has far less features and battery life is atrocious, Garmin could spend some time with that watch software to understand usability better.  Now on to the  unique and expensive add on to this watch - the red and black nylon band.  It is a great looking band but for something that adds $150 or more to the price of the watch, I'd have to say a year in I'm disappointed.  The end starts to fray a bit, making it harder and harder to get through the loops that hold the strap down, and as a result starts to fray the little loop itself which I know will fail not too far down the road.  I tried using flame to clean up the frayed end with some success but it still is way to hard to get into the loops each time I put the watch back on.  The saving grace is with this watch's great batter life, I only end up taking it off every 4 or 5 days for a charge.  Regardless, i pulled out the second black strap that came with the watch out and find I'm using that more often because it is so much faster to get on.  For a band costing $150+, I would have expected a solid metal end on the nylon strap that would keep it tight and easy to get on for more than a year. My advice would be to skip this expensive strap unless they fix the issue with fraying

title: Awesome fitness-focused smartwatch
text:
Update:
I had to change my review to three stars because this watch is so big, bulky and uncomfortable. I found myself having to make it really tight because otherwise the weight of it was constantly sliding around my wrist and was uncomfortable because of how heavy it is. If you have bigger wrists, this might not be as much of an issue for you. For my small wrists, I couldn't deal with how comfortable this is. I also think that for the price, there should be more features available for this watch. I decided to go with the Samsung Gear sport 2 Pro. It is so much more comfortable and easy to wear everyday.

Original review:
I really like this fitness tracking smartwatch and I have compared a lot of them, including the LG, FitBit and Garmin. I chose the Samsung one because I think having the S Health app is great because it has sports activity tracking for running and a lot of activities or you can choose "other" if it doesn't fit into any category. The S Health integration lets you track steps, calories, water, caffeine, etc. It has a heart rate monitor and is water proof!!! I like being able to wear it in the shower too. It is a little bit heavy, but it is really adjustable so as long as you get it on your wrist correctly, it isn't very bothersome. I am a female with small wrists and it looks a little bit big, but it's not too bad for the amount of features that are packed into it. I love being able to get notifications and being able to choose a quick reply, which you can edit. Fitbit doesn't allow you to do that, which really influenced my decision. It's important to keep in mind that this is definitely fitness focused with notifications. If you're looking for more of a smartwatch focus and not fitness, this probably isn't the best one for you.

title: Cannot wear it
text:
I always wear watches and decided to switch to the smarwatches when saw the 42 Classic Samsung watch 4 LTE. The idea was not to take the phone while bike riding or hiking. First - Xfinity does not work with it. OK I still wanted to keep them. But I got another problem - much more serious: After two days of wear I got a severe allergy reaction or burn right under the watch body. Not the band, just under the watch. I tried another hand - same reaction after just 40 minutes. Heart rate measurement - once in 10 min, nothing else. Very strange. Got a one day rest and tried again with the same result - red itching spot on the hand (see the pic)
I definitely do not have an allergy on steel, glass, titanium or sapphire. Since did never have any reaction on regular watches. I do not know what is the material of the lense at the bottom. May this is a culprit, may be measurement is two powerful, I do not know.
Besides of this, these are cool watches with pretty good design, kind of limited functionality. Body and band look cheap, though.

I definitely do not have an allergy on steel, glass, titanium or sapphire. Since did never have any reaction on regular watches. I do not know what is the material of the lense at the bottom. May this is a culprit, may be measurement is two powerful, I do not know.
Besides of this, these are cool watches with pretty good design, kind of limited functionality. Body and band look cheap, though.

title: Too expensive for buggy and subpar results
text: I ordered Fitbit Sense for all the advanced tracking and measuring of SpO2 levels, scared I may have gotten infected, but it's not as good as I expected.  SpO2 tracking supposedly only works when you sleep (WTH Fitbit, can't you help during COVID-19 times?) but it never showed anything on my watch. I wanted this instead of an Apple or Samsung watch because I like the fact that I don't have to charge it every night. Fortunately, I don't have COVID-19, but a $300+ watch shouldn't give intermittent but way too frequent troubles with screen not turning on upon wrist turn. This functionality works much better on my cheap, old faithful Amazfit Bip and I'm going back to it, using Runkeeper on myphone for exercise tracking. This is so going back.

title: Could use some work...
text:
The only thing that makes this even remotely better than other smart watches is Wear OS. Even then, its sometimes laggy and unresponsive. Ticwatch Pro claims that you can get 4-5 days on a single charge but I never made it past 2 days with regular use. I had to turn off many features to even make it to 2.5 days without charging. The screen resolution is nowhere near as good as Samsung Galaxy watch. Feels more like a $150 smartwatch, while looking at the display. You cant even make a call from the watch itself. If u ask google assistant to call someone, it will just make the call from your phone. Which is completely useless. Other smartwatches let you call directly from the watch.

Its not all that bad, the band that comes with it is pretty comfortable. The watch itself doesn't weight that much making it more comfortable to wear. The dual display is nice, lets you show the time at all times, without wasting much battery life.

title: Poor Battery Life
text: It becomes difficult to trust reviews: the merchandiser states that the battery is good for 2-5 days. My watch barely barely lasted 12 hours. I charged it fully before the initial startup. I adjusted the brightness to a lower setting, shut off notifications, etc. Returned the watch after about 9 days. It was my second smart watch (the first was much, much cheaper and looked cheezy). Bought a Samsung watch - 79% battery life remaining after 12 hours! Big difference!

title: NOT 100% compatible with iPhone
text:
Prior to purchase, I researched whether this watch would work with an iPhone and found information staying that it would. This is not true. The app that is available for download to iPhone is not the full app. "Find my phone" and "fall detection" will not work using this Samsung watch with an iPhone.

(I have reasons for wanting a Samsung watch to work with an iPhone. I looked into it in advance and was misinformed. This makes me further unhappy with APPLE. But Samsung should have been more forthcoming.)

title: Great watch ! Only 2 things bad.
text:
So I used a Samsung Galaxy watch before this. I lost my Samsung while hunting. This watch I used the rest of hunting season. Tracked all my routes while hunting in the remote back county of the Wyoming Range. Love all the features except two things. The heart rate tracker does not show my true heart rate when my arm is really sweaty, I constantly have to dry my arm and the bottom side of the watch to get it to read correctly. This throws off the PAI and makes it read lower than it should. The second thing is minor, but the little band that holds the tail of the watch band down just past the buckle broke after about 4 months of wear.

Other than those two things I love this watch.

title: I liked it but I didn't love it.
text: I was so excited to get the champagne gold 18mm band Moto 360.  However, I ended up returning it.  I have carpal tunnel and the weight of it bothered my wrist.  Maybe it would have been okay with a leather or silicone band.  I loved the different watch faces that you could get for it.  I especially loved the weather ones.  One of the reasons that I bought it was to count my steps.  It was terrible at doing that.  I continued to wear my Fitbit One at the same time and the steps were always under by thousands by the end of the day.  I also didn't like that I couldn't see the time without waking up the screen.  I really would like to see the time all of the time.  The battery life was not so good; not even a full day.  Sometimes I couldn't make it past around 8:30 in the evening.  Another thing that bothered me was that it kept disconnecting from my phone and from Wifi.  I'm not sure if that was an issue of the watch or an Android Wear issue, but it was really annoying.  I am going to try a Asus Zenwatch 2.  If I end up still not liking Android Wear, then I will probably going with a Pebbletime or a Samsung Gear 2 Neo.
